# Benchmarks

## Historical A10G BF16 Tensor Core Peak

This is a historical reference for the accepted local A10G dense BF16 ceiling.
The old raw-MMA peak microbenchmark has been removed from the project path; do
not rebuild or reintroduce it. Future experiments should use CUDA Tile kernels
and compare against the accepted local reference of about 70 TFLOP/s.

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
GPU: NVIDIA A10G, SM 8.6, SMs 80, device clock 1.710 GHz
Work: 67.109 TFLOP per measured launch
Time: best 960.014 ms, median 960.103 ms, p90 960.112 ms
Measured BF16 Tensor Core: best 69.90 TFLOP/s, median 69.90 TFLOP/s
A10G SM86 dense BF16 estimate at reported clock: 70.04 TFLOP/s
Best/estimate: 99.8%
```

Rechecked on 2026-06-02 before the raw-MMA bench cleanup, after confirming no
other compute process was active:

```text
default, 3 serial runs:
  best 69.90-69.91 TFLOP/s, median 69.90 TFLOP/s
long repeats=400000:
  Work: 134.218 TFLOP per measured launch
  Time: best 1919.977 ms, median 1920.142 ms
  Measured BF16 Tensor Core: best 69.91 TFLOP/s, median 69.90 TFLOP/s
```

Interpretation:

- The current A10G dense BF16 Tensor Core ceiling is about 70 TFLOP/s.
- The AWS/NVIDIA A10G datasheet lists BF16 Tensor Core as 70 TF and 140 TF with
  sparsity.
- A dense BF16 MBR kernel cannot reach 100 TFLOP/s on this GPU. Hitting that
  range requires 2:4 structured sparsity or a different advertised GPU ceiling.

## Current Forbidden-Path Audit

The active project goal forbids cuBLAS, cuFFT, cuDNN, raw WMMA, inline MMA, and
inline assembly in the inference/bench CUDA path. The reusable audit gate is:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
./bench/audit_cuda_tile_first.sh build-min/cudasep_infer
```

Current result from 2026-06-05:

```text
[audit] source/CMake/bench forbidden path scan
[audit] OK: no forbidden source/CMake/bench matches
[audit] dynamic dependency scan: build-min/cudasep_infer
  NEEDED               libcudart.so.13
  NEEDED               libstdc++.so.6
  NEEDED               libm.so.6
  NEEDED               libgcc_s.so.1
  NEEDED               libc.so.6
[audit] OK: no cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN dynamic dependency
[audit] dynamic symbol scan: build-min/cudasep_infer
[audit] OK: no forbidden dynamic symbols
[audit] binary string scan: build-min/cudasep_infer
[audit] OK: no forbidden binary strings
[audit] PASS: CUDA Tile-first purity gate passed for build-min/cudasep_infer
```

Interpretation:

- The current `cudasep_infer` binary links CUDA Runtime only, not cuBLAS, cuFFT,
  or cuDNN.
- The current source and bench paths have no raw WMMA, inline `mma.sync`, or
  inline `asm` token matches outside this README and the audit script itself.
- This is a current-state audit, not a final completion claim. Continue rerunning
  it after adding new CUDA Tile kernels or changing CMake linkage.

## Current Runtime BF16 DType-Conversion Audit

This audit measures real inference kernel launches instead of adding a debug
counter to the source path. It separates explicit dtype-conversion kernels from
the normal CUDA Tile pattern where BF16 values are loaded, converted to FP32
register values for arithmetic, and written back as BF16.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none \
  --force-overwrite=true -o outputs/nsys_default_bf16_dtype \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/nsys_default_bf16_dtype_audio \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ALL_BF16_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
CUDASEP_REMAINING_BF16_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none \
  --force-overwrite=true -o outputs/nsys_all_bf16_dtype \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/nsys_all_bf16_dtype_audio \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys export --type sqlite --force-overwrite=true \
  --output outputs/nsys_default_bf16_dtype.sqlite \
  outputs/nsys_default_bf16_dtype.nsys-rep
nsys export --type sqlite --force-overwrite=true \
  --output outputs/nsys_all_bf16_dtype.sqlite \
  outputs/nsys_all_bf16_dtype.nsys-rep
```

End-to-end:

```text
default --bf16:
  test_clean.wav chunked 4 chunks, infer 1000.2 ms, RTF 39.58x

all BF16 experiment flags:
  test_clean.wav chunked 4 chunks, infer 1002.0 ms, RTF 39.51x
```

Explicit conversion-like kernels in the default `--bf16` trace:

```text
kernel                                             calls   total_ms
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel                        288     35.789
apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_cutile    96     23.562
tanh_bf16_to_bf16_kernel                            960      6.274
f32_to_bf16_kernel                                 1032      3.530
glu_last_dim_bf16_to_bf16_kernel                    480      1.402
rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel                             240      1.159
gather_freqs_fold_complex_to_bf16_kernel              4      0.466
```

Explicit conversion-like kernels in the all-BF16 trace were effectively the
same:

```text
kernel                                             calls   total_ms
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel                        288     35.946
apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_cutile    96     23.562
tanh_bf16_to_bf16_kernel                            960      6.291
f32_to_bf16_kernel                                 1032      3.531
glu_last_dim_bf16_to_bf16_kernel                    480      1.410
rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel                             240      1.167
gather_freqs_fold_complex_to_bf16_kernel              4      0.466
```

Top default `--bf16` kernels by total GPU time:

```text
kernel                                      calls   total_ms   avg_us
ffn12_fused256_split2_cutile_kernel            96    213.476  2223.707
time_attention1301_main1280_cutile_kernel      48    165.232  3442.339
linear_cutile_static_full_bkn_bf16_kernel      96    144.348  1503.622
linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel     964     55.295    57.360
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel          96     48.905   509.423
freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel    48     38.646   805.117
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64   48     36.860   767.924
linear_cutile_static_masked_mn_bf16_kernel    568     36.850    64.877
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_time1301_d64   48     35.960   749.160
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel                  288     35.789   124.267
binary_inplace_same_shape_kernel              192     35.285   183.776
```

Interpretation:

- The standalone explicit `f32_to_bf16_kernel` cost is only about `3.5 ms` over
  the full `test_clean.wav` run, while total kernel time is about `965 ms`.
  Independent dtype-conversion kernels are not the main remaining bottleneck.
- The all-BF16 environment flags do not materially change the kernel mix or
  runtime in this build. The current default `--bf16` path already takes the
  BF16 custom path for the model-body experiments that matter here.
- The bigger remaining FP32/BF16 issue is inside fused CUDA Tile kernels:
  BF16 data is commonly loaded, promoted to FP32 register arithmetic for
  reductions, exp/sigmoid/tanh/GELU, RMSNorm, and softmax, then written as BF16.
  That does not show up as a separate `bf16_to_f32_kernel` launch.
- The next performance work should focus on high-time source kernels and global
  memory/layout boundaries: FFN12 fused split2, time attention main1280,
  long-QKV BKN linear, QKV rotary split/pad, and BTF/BFT layout copies.

## Negative Probe: Time Attention QKV-Direct Rotary-On-Load

The previous runtime audit showed about `35.9 ms` in the time split/rotary
kernel over `test_clean.wav`. This bench-only probe checks whether attention can
skip q/k/v materialization by reading packed QKV projection output directly and
applying q/k rotary while loading Q and K tiles.

The benchmark packs the existing deterministic q/k/v inputs into the real
projection output layout:

```text
packed qkv shape: [B=60, N=1301, 3 * heads=8 * D=64]
attention shape:  [BH=480, N=1301, D=64]
variant:          main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary
rotary table:     cos=1, sin=0
```

Using identity rotary keeps the expected output identical to the current
`q64/k32 exp2` baseline while still forcing global cos/sin loads and rotary
arithmetic in the kernel.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2 --warmup 5 --iters 20
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary \
  --warmup 5 --iters 20 --compare-baseline
```

Results:

```text
baseline main1280_q64k32_exp2:
  best=3.405 ms, median=3.410 ms
  real_math=60.10 TF/s, roof70=85.9%

qkv-direct rotary-on-load:
  best=19.075 ms, median=19.246 ms
  real_math=10.73 TF/s, roof70=15.3%
  compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2:
    max_abs=0, rms=0, rel_rms=0
```

Resource and static SASS evidence:

```text
baseline time_attention1301_main1280_kernel<64,32,true,true,false,false>:
  REG=237 STACK=0 SHARED=40960
  HMMA=160, MUFU.EX2=90, LDG=32, LDSM=64
  IMAD=63, IADD3=128, F2F=56, STS=60, STG=4

qkv-direct rotary-on-load:
  REG=224 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
  HMMA=64, MUFU.EX2=36, LDG=268, LDSM=0
  IMAD=515, IADD3=430, F2F=63, STS=20, STG=32
```

Interpretation:

- The output identity check proves the qkv-direct indexing and rotary math are
  functionally equivalent for this controlled case.
- The performance collapse is not caused by register spilling: the direct
  kernel has `REG=224` and `STACK=0`.
- The bottom-level cause is the load/gather schedule. Direct qkv indexing
  increases global loads from `LDG=32` to `LDG=268`, integer address arithmetic
  from `IMAD=63` to `IMAD=515`, and removes the baseline's `LDSM` path.
- It also recomputes key rotary for every query block. With `QRows=64`, the
  main1280 path has 20 query blocks, so packed K is reloaded and rerotated many
  times instead of being materialized once.
- Do not promote qkv-direct rotary-on-load to the source path. Future fusion work
  should preserve an attention-friendly K/V tile layout, or fuse around the
  split/rotary boundary without making attention repeatedly gather and rerotate
  packed QKV.

## CUDA Tile BF16 MMA GEMM

`bf16_cutile_mma_gemm.cu` is the first CUDA Tile-first BF16 Tensor Core GEMM
probe for the current MBR long-linear shapes. It uses `__tile_global__`,
`ct::tensor_span`, `ct::partition_view`, and `ct::mma`; it does not use cuBLAS,
cuFFT, raw WMMA, or inline MMA. Inputs and output are BF16, while `ct::mma`
accumulates into an FP32 tile. Official CUDA Tile matrix multiplication docs
were checked before implementation.

Build and run:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --warmup 2 --iters 4
```

The generated SASS contains `HMMA.16816.F32.BF16`, so these CUDA Tile kernels do
reach A10G Tensor Cores.

Static fixed-shape result:

```text
variant: t32x64x32s
static full tile M=78048, BF16 input/output, FP32 accumulate

shape           best time  TF/s   roof   current raw-WMMA comparison
infer_qkv       1.914 ms   32.07  45.8%  31.02 TF/s
infer_ffn1      1.271 ms   32.21  46.0%  30.57 TF/s
infer_ffn2      1.073 ms   38.15  54.5%  38.74 TF/s
infer_attn_out  0.600 ms   34.12  48.7%  34.70 TF/s
```

This is the first strong CUDA Tile-first positive result: the fixed-shape
`ct::mma` path is essentially tied with the current raw-WMMA long-linear
reference while using no raw WMMA.

Initial tile sweep:

```text
shape           best CUDA Tile variant   best time   TF/s   roof
infer_qkv       t32x64x16                5.013 ms   12.25  17.5%
infer_ffn1      t32x64x16                3.384 ms   12.09  17.3%
infer_ffn2      t64x64x16                4.844 ms    8.45  12.1%
infer_attn_out  t64x64x16                2.361 ms    8.67  12.4%
```

Current raw-WMMA comparison point from `bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset
infer_linear --warmup 2 --iters 4`:

```text
infer_qkv       best raw WMMA c32x256r2c4  1.979 ms 31.02 TF/s
infer_ffn1      best raw WMMA c32x256r2c4  1.339 ms 30.57 TF/s
infer_ffn2      best raw WMMA c32x256r2c4  1.056 ms 38.74 TF/s
infer_attn_out  best raw WMMA c32x256r2c4  0.590 ms 34.70 TF/s
```

K-tile sweep for the best `32x64` output tile:

```text
variant      qkv TF/s  ffn1 TF/s  ffn2 TF/s  attn_out TF/s
t32x64x16      12.26      12.25       8.09          8.37
t32x64x32      10.39      10.40       8.38         10.20
t32x64x64      11.34      11.35       5.95          8.68
```

`ct::matmul` did not improve the dynamic path:

```text
variant        qkv TF/s  ffn1 TF/s  ffn2 TF/s  attn_out TF/s
t32x64x16      12.26      12.26       8.09          8.37
t32x64x16m      9.56       9.58       6.25          8.50
t32x64x32      10.39      10.40       8.39         10.20
t32x64x32m     10.12      10.13       8.27          9.95
```

Static-shape `32x64` K-tile sweep:

```text
variant        qkv TF/s  ffn1 TF/s  ffn2 TF/s  attn_out TF/s
t32x64x16s     29.51      29.58      22.15         30.47
t32x64x32s     32.07      32.21      38.15         34.12
t32x64x64s     32.04      32.18      37.10         33.43
```

For `N=256,K=1024` ffn2, static output-tile sweep:

```text
t32x64x16s    22.18 TF/s
t32x128x16s   25.36 TF/s
t32x256x16s   24.96 TF/s
t64x64x16s    20.39 TF/s
t32x64x32s    38.15 TF/s
t32x128x32s   21.59 TF/s
```

For QKV `M=78048,N=1536,K=256`, static `TK=32` N-width sweep:

```text
t32x128x32s   1.773 ms 34.62 TF/s 49.5% roof
t32x256x32s   1.527 ms 40.20 TF/s 57.4% roof
t32x512x32s   2.173 ms 28.25 TF/s 40.4% roof
```

Follow-up QKV-only TM/TK probes:

```text
t32x256x32s       1.525 ms 40.25 TF/s 57.5% roof
qkv_t16x256x32s   2.111 ms 29.08 TF/s 41.5% roof
qkv_t64x256x32s   2.046 ms 29.98 TF/s 42.8% roof
qkv_t32x256x64s   2.043 ms 30.04 TF/s 42.9% roof
qkv_t32x256x32mat 2.255 ms 27.21 TF/s 38.9% roof
qkv_t32x256x32splitn128
                   2.288 ms 26.83 TF/s 38.3% roof
qkv_t32x256x32manual
                   7.722 ms  7.95 TF/s 11.4% roof
qkv_t32x256x32bkn 1.471 ms 41.72 TF/s 59.6% roof
```

Masked vs full-tile-only unmasked `32x64x16`:

```text
masked:    qkv 12.26 TF/s, ffn1 12.25, ffn2 8.09, attn_out 8.37
unmasked:  qkv 12.20 TF/s, ffn1 12.19, ffn2 8.12, attn_out 8.38
```

Resource evidence:

```text
dynamic t32x64x16:               REG=156 SHARED=7168
dynamic t32x64x16 unmasked:      REG=158 SHARED=7168
dynamic t32x64x32:               REG=190 SHARED=10240
dynamic t32x64x64:               REG=228 SHARED=16384
dynamic t32x128x16:              REG=238 SHARED=13312
dynamic t32x256x16:              REG=243 SHARED=25600

static t32x64x32 qkv/ffn1:       REG=77  SHARED=53248
static t32x64x32 ffn2:           REG=168 SHARED=98304
static t32x64x32 attn_out:       REG=90  SHARED=98304
static t32x64x16 qkv/ffn1:       REG=74  SHARED=53248
static t32x64x16 ffn2:           REG=96  SHARED=36864
static t32x64x64 qkv/ffn1:       REG=96  SHARED=53248
static t32x64x64 ffn2:           REG=211 SHARED=98304
static t32x128x32 qkv/ffn1:      REG=110 SHARED=90112
static t32x128x32 ffn2:          REG=116 SHARED=98304
static t32x256x32 qkv/ffn1:      REG=217 SHARED=98304
static t32x512x32 qkv/ffn1:      REG=255 STACK=200 SHARED=81920
static qkv_t16x256x32:           REG=224 STACK=0 SHARED=90112
static qkv_t64x256x32:           REG=255 STACK=72 SHARED=98304
static qkv_t32x256x64:           REG=255 STACK=184 SHARED=81920
static qkv_t32x256x32mat:        REG=255 STACK=64 SHARED=98304
static qkv_t32x256x32splitn128:  REG=122 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
static qkv_t32x256x32manual:     REG=255 STACK=392 SHARED=88064
static qkv_t32x256x32bkn:        REG=206 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

raw WMMA c32x256r2c4 reference:  REG=118 SHARED=9216
```

Interpretation:

- CUDA Tile `ct::mma` reaches Tensor Cores on A10G and can match the current
  raw-WMMA long-linear reference when the model shape is made compile-time
  static.
- The first dynamic `partition_view` version was slow because dynamic extents
  produced a much worse generated schedule/resource shape. Static extents cut
  qkv/ffn1 registers from `REG=156` to `REG=77` for the best `TK=32` path and
  roughly tripled throughput.
- Tail masking is not the root cause; full-tile-only unmasked loads/stores are
  effectively tied with the masked variant.
- `ct::matmul` is not a fix here; it increased register pressure and was slower
  than `ct::mma` on the tested shapes.
- `TK=32` is the current best K tile for these static long-linear shapes.
  `TK=16` is too small for ffn2, while `TK=64` raises ffn2 register pressure
  (`REG=211`) and loses about 1 TF/s.
- Widening N beyond `32x64` is shape-sensitive and usually negative. For ffn2,
  `32x128x32s` falls to 21.59 TF/s even though it halves CTA count, because the
  static resource/schedule shape changes unfavorably.
- For QKV, `32x256x32s` is the current CUDA Tile static sweet spot. It halves
  CTA count versus `32x128x32s` and reaches 40.20 TF/s, but widening again to
  `32x512x32s` is negative: register allocation hits `REG=255` and adds
  `STACK=200`, so the lower CTA count is outweighed by spills/resource pressure.
- QKV TM/TK probes narrow the next search space. `16x256x32` avoids stack but
  still uses `REG=224` and doubles CTA count, so smaller M tiles are not the
  fix. `64x256x32` and `32x256x64` both hit `REG=255` and spill to stack, so
  larger M reuse or fewer K-loop iterations are also negative. The next QKV
  attempt should preserve the `32x256x32` macro shape while reducing generated
  accumulator/staging pressure or changing the internal schedule.
- Static `ct::matmul` is not the solution for this QKV shape. On the same
  `32x256x32` macro tile it falls to 27.21 TF/s and changes resource usage from
  `REG=217,STACK=0` to `REG=255,STACK=64`; the higher-level API gives worse
  generated code here than explicit `ct::mma`.
- Serial split-N is also negative. `qkv_t32x256x32splitn128` keeps the same
  macro CTA grid but computes two `32x128` accumulators in sequence. It cuts
  registers from `REG=217` to `REG=122`, but shared memory remains `98304`, so
  CTA residency does not improve; the serial sub-N schedule and repeated A loads
  drop throughput to 26.83 TF/s. This shows the QKV limiter is not register
  count alone. The next useful direction must reduce the generated shared-memory
  footprint or increase tile-internal parallelism without serializing the N
  work.
- Manual offset load/store is not a fix. `qkv_t32x256x32manual` avoids
  `partition_view` and reduces shared memory from `98304` to `88064`, but the
  unstructured address tiles drive `REG=255` and `STACK=392`; throughput falls
  to 7.95 TF/s. The structured `partition_view` path is important for codegen.
  The remaining target is a structured CUDA Tile expression that lowers shared
  memory without exploding address arithmetic or spills.
- A structured B(K,N) row-major weight layout is a small QKV positive.
  `qkv_t32x256x32bkn` keeps `partition_view` but replaces the current
  `[N,K] + layout_left` view with a row-major `[K,N]` weight view. It improves
  QKV from 40.31 TF/s to 41.72 TF/s and lowers registers from `REG=217` to
  `REG=206`, while shared memory stays `98304`. This does not solve the shared
  footprint, but it shows `layout_left` has a measurable codegen cost. The
  checksum is not comparable to the baseline because the benchmark initializes
  the same linear storage under a different logical layout. Next step: test
  B(K,N) on the other long-linear shapes before considering source-side
  pretransposed weights.

### Negative Probe: QKV BKN Shape Sweep

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t16x256x32bkn --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t64x256x32bkn --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x512x32bkn --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x64bkn --warmup 3 --iters 10
```

Results:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn  1.466 ms 41.87 TF/s 59.8% roof grid=(2439,6)
qkv_t16x256x32bkn  2.424 ms 25.32 TF/s 36.2% roof grid=(4878,6)
qkv_t64x256x32bkn  1.705 ms 35.99 TF/s 51.4% roof grid=(1219,6)
qkv_t32x512x32bkn  1.823 ms 33.67 TF/s 48.1% roof grid=(2439,3)
qkv_t32x256x64bkn  1.777 ms 34.55 TF/s 49.4% roof grid=(2439,6)
```

Resource evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn  REG=206 STACK=0  SHARED=98304
qkv_t16x256x32bkn  REG=157 STACK=0  SHARED=90112
qkv_t64x256x32bkn  REG=254 STACK=0  SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x512x32bkn  REG=255 STACK=16 SHARED=81920
qkv_t32x256x64bkn  REG=172 STACK=0  SHARED=81920
```

SASS static counts:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn  HMMA=256 instr=832
qkv_t16x256x32bkn  HMMA=128 instr=656
qkv_t64x256x32bkn  HMMA=512 instr=1280
qkv_t32x512x32bkn  HMMA=512 instr=1400
qkv_t32x256x64bkn  HMMA=256 instr=768
```

Follow-up N-width sweep:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x64x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x128x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x512x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Results:

```text
qkv_t32x64x32bkn   grid=(2439,24) best=1.943 ms median=1.956 ms 31.59 TF/s 45.1% roof
qkv_t32x128x32bkn  grid=(2439,12) best=1.748 ms median=1.764 ms 35.12 TF/s 50.2% roof
qkv_t32x256x32bkn  grid=(2439,6)  best=1.465 ms median=1.474 ms 41.89 TF/s 59.8% roof
qkv_t32x512x32bkn  grid=(2439,3)  best=1.821 ms median=1.826 ms 33.71 TF/s 48.2% roof
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x64x32bkn:
  REG=77,  STACK=0,  SHARED=53248
  instr=464,  HMMA=64,  F2F=8,  LDG=40,  LDSM=48,  STS=28

qkv_t32x128x32bkn:
  REG=110, STACK=0,  SHARED=90112
  instr=600,  HMMA=128, F2F=16, LDG=56,  LDSM=64,  STS=44

qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  REG=206, STACK=0,  SHARED=98304
  instr=832,  HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88,  LDSM=96,  STS=80

qkv_t32x512x32bkn:
  REG=255, STACK=16, SHARED=81920
  instr=1400, HMMA=512, F2F=64, LDG=152, LDSM=160, STS=152
```

Additional interpretation:

- `N64`, `N128`, `N256`, and `N512` all issue the same total HMMA count per
  full output row band after multiplying per-CTA HMMA by the N-grid. The
  performance gap is therefore not Tensor Core fallback.
- Narrow `N64/N128` tiles cut registers, but shared memory is still high enough
  that CTA residency does not improve on A10G. Their larger N-grid multiplies
  load/store/control overhead and loses to `N256`.
- Wide `N512` cuts the N-grid, but the accumulator and store schedule push the
  kernel to `REG=255` with stack usage. The lower CTA count is outweighed by
  resource pressure.
- For this QKV B(K,N) CUDA Tile family, `M32 x N256 x K32` remains the A10G
  sweet spot. The remaining gap below 70 TF/s is a Tile compiler generated
  schedule/resource issue, not a macro N-width issue.

Power-of-two completeness and generic-vs-specialized check:

The CUDA Tile programming guide requires tile extents to be powers of two, so
intermediate `N192` or `N384` tile widths are not valid CUDA Tile shapes. The
legal N-width sweep for this `N=1536` QKV shape is therefore `32/64/128/256/512`.
The `N32` point and a generic `N256` dispatch were added to close two remaining
questions: whether a narrower tile keeps improving pressure, and whether the
current `N256` result is helped by its older specialized kernel spelling.

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x32x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

```text
qkv_t32x32x32bkn        grid=(2439,48) best=3.440 ms median=3.442 ms 17.84 TF/s 25.5% roof
qkv_t32x256x32bkn       grid=(2439,6)  best=1.463 ms median=1.473 ms 41.95 TF/s 59.9% roof
qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled grid=(2439,6)  best=1.463 ms median=1.474 ms 41.97 TF/s 60.0% roof
```

```text
qkv_t32x32x32bkn:
  REG=50,  STACK=0, SHARED=34816
  instr=384, HMMA=32,  F2F=4,  LDG=32, LDSM=32, STS=20

qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80
```

Conclusion:

- `N32` lowers per-CTA pressure further, but `grid_y=48` multiplies CTA and
  non-HMMA overhead enough to collapse throughput to 17.84 TF/s.
- Generic `N256_tiled` and the older specialized `N256` variant produce the same
  resource usage, SASS counts, and runtime. The `N256` result is not an artifact
  of the specialized kernel spelling.
- Given CUDA Tile's power-of-two tile-shape rule, the QKV B(K,N) N-width macro
  search is now closed for this shape: `N256` is the best legal point tested.

K-depth sweep at `M32 x N256`:

With N-width closed, the next test fixed the best `M32 x N256` macro shape and
swept legal power-of-two K tile depths.

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x64bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x128bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn  grid=(2439,6) best=1.381 ms median=1.404 ms 44.45 TF/s 63.5% roof
qkv_t32x256x32bkn  grid=(2439,6) best=1.466 ms median=1.475 ms 41.88 TF/s 59.8% roof
qkv_t32x256x64bkn  grid=(2439,6) best=1.771 ms median=1.777 ms 34.65 TF/s 49.5% roof
qkv_t32x256x128bkn grid=(2439,6) best=1.701 ms median=1.722 ms 36.09 TF/s 51.6% roof
```

Longer confirmation:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn  warmup=20 iters=500 best=1.381 ms median=1.404 ms 44.46 TF/s 63.5% roof
qkv_t32x256x32bkn  warmup=20 iters=500 best=1.463 ms median=1.474 ms 41.96 TF/s 59.9% roof
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88

qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88,  LDSM=96, STS=80

qkv_t32x256x64bkn:
  REG=172, STACK=0, SHARED=81920
  instr=768, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=80,  LDSM=96, STS=80

qkv_t32x256x128bkn:
  REG=214, STACK=0, SHARED=81920
  instr=744, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=76,  LDSM=96, STS=80
```

Interpretation:

- `K16` is the first positive QKV BKN macro-shape result after the row-major
  B(K,N) change. It improves the standalone QKV shape from about `41.96` to
  `44.46 TF/s`, roughly `+6%`.
- The win is not from lower shared memory: `K16` and `K32` both use `98304`
  bytes. It is also not from fewer static instructions: `K16` has more
  instructions and more LDG/STS than `K32`.
- The strongest static clue is register pressure: `K16` lowers registers from
  `206` to `170` while preserving the same total HMMA count. This looks like a
  better generated MMA/staging schedule under the current CUDA Tile compiler,
  not an occupancy win from shared-memory reduction.
- `K64/K128` reduce shared memory and static instruction count, but throughput
  drops sharply. For this shape, larger K tiles make the generated schedule less
  efficient even though they look cheaper statically.
- The source-path and Nsight results below confirm `TK=16` in the real QKV
  B(K,N) path.

Source promotion: QKV B(K,N) TK16 default:

The source QKV B(K,N) branch now uses `TK=16` by default and keeps
`CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_BKN_QKV_TK16=1` as a same-binary rollback to the old
`TK=32` path. FFN B(K,N) paths still use the existing `kLinearCutileTileK=32`.

Source resource and SASS:

```text
TK16 source:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88

TK32 rollback:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88,  LDSM=96, STS=80
```

Same-binary runtime A/B:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  TK16 default:  957.1 ms
  TK32 rollback: 959.9 ms

test.wav:
  TK16 default:  5625.7 ms
  TK32 rollback: 5673.7 ms
```

Output diff, TK16 default versus TK32 rollback:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rms_diff=9.94957069e-09 rel_rms=5.39417972e-08 snr=145.361 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=2.98023224e-07 rms_diff=1.30559415e-08 rel_rms=5.42996741e-08 snr=145.304 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=2.98023224e-07 rms_diff=1.02510235e-08 rel_rms=5.42470683e-08 snr=145.312 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rms_diff=1.04214479e-08 rel_rms=5.43560701e-08 snr=145.295 dB
```

Nsight Systems on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
TK16 default:
  inference time:       973.9 ms
  total GPU kernels:    930.950 ms / 6465 calls
  QKV BKN kernel group: 135.289 ms / 96 calls, avg 1409.258 us
  resource:             REG=170, SHARED=98304

TK32 rollback:
  inference time:       981.3 ms
  total GPU kernels:    940.040 ms / 6465 calls
  QKV BKN kernel group: 144.615 ms / 96 calls, avg 1506.408 us
  resource:             REG=206, SHARED=98304
```

The QKV BKN group drops by `9.326 ms`, and total GPU kernel time drops by
`9.090 ms`. The kernel-level win therefore matches the bench prediction and is
not hidden by another CUDA kernel regression.

Decision:

- Keep source QKV B(K,N) on `TK=16` by default.
- Keep the rollback flag for same-binary A/B while the long-linear experiments
  are still active.
- The source result matches the bench result qualitatively: lower register
  pressure wins even though static instruction count is higher.

Interpretation:

- The earlier B(K,N) M/N sweep showed that `M32 x N256` is the best A10G CUDA
  Tile macro shape in that family. The later K-depth sweep above changes the
  current default from `TK=32` to `TK=16`.
- `16x256x32` lowers registers and shared memory, but doubles the M-grid from
  `2439` CTAs to `4878` CTAs and loses badly. Lower per-CTA pressure alone is
  not enough if total CTA scheduling work grows too much.
- `64x256x32` halves the M-grid, but reaches `REG=254` and carries twice the
  HMMA count per CTA. The bigger tile does not improve residency or throughput.
- `32x512x32` halves the N-grid from 6 CTAs to 3 CTAs and lowers shared memory,
  but it hits `REG=255`, spills to stack, and has the largest static instruction
  count in this probe. The reduced CTA count is outweighed by codegen/resource
  pressure.
- `32x256x64` lowers shared memory and registers versus the original `TK=32`
  baseline, but fewer K-loop iterations do not help. The generated schedule is
  less efficient for A10G than both `TK=32` and the current `TK=16` default.
- All tested BKN variants still emit `HMMA.16816.F32.BF16`, so these negative
  results are not Tensor Core fallback failures. The remaining QKV gap is in
  generated schedule/resource balance, not in whether CUDA Tile reaches Tensor
  Cores.
- The broader long-linear B(K,N) probe uses the same `32x256x32` macro tile:

```text
shape           layout_left        B(K,N) row-major     delta
infer_qkv       40.17 TF/s         41.93 TF/s           +4.4%
infer_ffn1      39.99 TF/s         41.72 TF/s           +4.3%
infer_ffn2      25.90 TF/s         30.88 TF/s          +19.2%
infer_attn_out  32.16 TF/s         31.82 TF/s           -1.1%
```

Resource comparison:

```text
infer_qkv/ffn1      REG 217 -> 206, SHARED 98304 -> 98304
infer_ffn2          REG 187 -> 174, SHARED 94208 -> 94208
infer_attn_out      REG 182 -> 176, SHARED 98304 -> 98304
```

Interpretation: B(K,N) does not reduce the shared-memory footprint, but it
consistently reduces register pressure and is positive for QKV, FFN1, and FFN2.

### Negative Probe: QKV BKN Explicit K-Loop Unroll

The current best QKV BKN macro shape is `M32 x N256 x K32`. This bench-only
probe keeps that shape and the row-major `B(K,N)` weight layout, but replaces
the `ct::irange` K loop with eight explicit `ct::mma` statements for the
fixed `K=256` / `TK=32` shape.

The hypothesis was that explicit K unrolling might reduce loop/control overhead
or expose a better A10G schedule while preserving the same Tensor Core work.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn --warmup 5 --iters 40
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn_unroll8 --warmup 5 --iters 40
```

Results:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  best=1.467 ms, median=1.474 ms, 41.83 TF/s, roof70=59.8%

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_unroll8:
  best=2.324 ms, median=2.326 ms, 26.41 TF/s, roof70=37.7%
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_unroll8:
  REG=212, STACK=0, SHARED=88064
  instr=2264, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=72, LDSM=0, STS=168
```

Interpretation:

- Explicit K unrolling is a strong negative result even though the static HMMA
  count stays at `256`, so this is not a Tensor Core fallback failure.
- The unrolled kernel lowers static shared memory from `98304` to `88064`, but
  throughput collapses. Lower shared memory alone is therefore not a useful
  predictor for this CUDA Tile QKV shape.
- The generated schedule changes qualitatively: `LDSM` disappears, `STS`
  doubles, and instruction count grows from `832` to `2264`. The likely bottom
  cause is that explicit statement unrolling prevents the CUDA Tile compiler
  from producing the better shared-load / MMA staging sequence it emits for
  `ct::irange`.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t32x256x32bkn_unroll8` as a benchmark-only negative result.
- Do not port explicit K-loop unrolling into the source QKV BKN path.

### Neutral Probe: QKV BKN Pair-K Loop Grouping

After full explicit unroll8 proved negative, this bench-only probe tested a
more conservative K-loop grouping. It keeps `ct::irange`, but each loop body
handles two adjacent `K32` tiles:

```text
for kpair in 0..3:
  mma(kpair * 2)
  mma(kpair * 2 + 1)
```

The macro shape remains the current QKV BKN default:
`M32 x N256 x K32`, row-major `B(K,N)`, full `M=78048`.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn_pairk --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Results:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  best=1.462 ms, median=1.474 ms, 41.98 TF/s, roof70=60.0%

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_pairk:
  best=1.462 ms, median=1.474 ms, 41.97 TF/s, roof70=60.0%
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_pairk:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80
```

Interpretation:

- Pair-K grouping compiles to the same resource and SASS shape as the baseline,
  and benchmark timing is effectively identical.
- The CUDA Tile compiler already finds the good staging pattern for the
  structured `ct::irange` K loop. Mild pair grouping is neutral; full explicit
  statement unroll is negative because it changes staging.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t32x256x32bkn_pairk` as a benchmark-only diagnostic result.
- Do not promote pair-K into source; it provides no measurable source-path win.
The large FFN2 gain suggests the row-major B view improves generated load/MMA
scheduling beyond the small register drop. It is slightly negative for
attention-output, so a source-side pretransposed-weight experiment should be
shape-gated rather than applied to every linear.

Source-side B(K,N) experiment:

```text
env: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1
implementation: opt-in pretranspose of QKV weight to [K,N], then QKV full-tile
B(K,N) CUDA Tile kernel; tail rows still use the original [N,K] weight.

QKV source resource:
  layout_left: REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
  B(K,N):      REG=206 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

test_clean.wav:
  default:      1108.1 ms
  QKV-only BKN: 1103.5 ms, 1101.8 ms

test.wav:
  default:      6573.1 ms
  QKV-only BKN: 6542.2 ms

test_clean.wav output diff vs default:
  Vocals       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rel_rms=5.45462953e-08
  Instrumental max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rel_rms=5.48287214e-08

test.wav output diff vs default:
  Vocals       max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rel_rms=5.47417690e-08
  Instrumental max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rel_rms=5.47250346e-08
```

An earlier attempt also routed FFN1/FFN2 through B(K,N) `32x256x32`, but it was
negative end-to-end (`1138.1/1137.7 ms` vs default `1114.1/1108.0 ms`). The
root cause is that the source FFN defaults are not the bench `32x256x32` shape;
FFN1 in source has narrower existing tile variants and fused GELU resource
behavior, so the B(K,N) bench win did not transfer. The retained source
experiment is therefore still QKV-only on the positive side. A later
source-default-tile FFN-only opt-in (`CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_FFN_LONG=1`)
was also tested and stayed negative end-to-end:
`test_clean.wav 1108.1 -> 1131.8 ms`, `test.wav 6565.2 -> 6684.4 ms`.
With both `CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1` and
`CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_FFN_LONG=1`, `test_clean.wav` was `1131.5 ms`,
so the QKV positive does not overcome the FFN cost.
Its FFN1 B(K,N) instantiation for `78048x1024x256` uses
`REG=188 SHARED=98304` versus the default `REG=95 SHARED=61440`, so the row-
major B view increases resource pressure enough to offset any load-side gain.
The workspace filesystem was full during the first `test.wav` attempt, so old
generated experiment WAV directories were cleaned before rerunning the full
default/BKN diff.
- The early source-port note that started from `32x64x32s` is superseded for
  QKV by the later `32x256x32` evidence. Future linear source changes should
  stay shape-specific; QKV should keep the current `32x256x32` candidate unless
  a new probe beats it with resource evidence.

Nsight Compute counter collection was attempted for `t32x64x16`, but this
machine denies performance-counter access with `ERR_NVGPUCTRPERM`; resource and
SASS evidence above are currently the available low-level evidence.

## MBR BF16 GEMM Shape Baseline

`bf16_gemm_shapes.cu` is the first cuBLAS-free GEMM shape probe for the current
MelBandRoformer model. It uses WMMA directly with BF16 inputs and FP32
accumulation. Weights are laid out as `B[N, K]` so the kernel computes
`A[M, K] @ B[N, K]^T`.

Current variants:

- `warp16`: one warp computes one `16x16` output tile.
- `c16x64`: one 4-warp CTA computes one `16x64` output tile; each warp computes
  one `16x16` column tile. This probes skinny-N attention output where more
  CTAs can be better than larger per-warp accumulator tiles.
- `c32x64`: one 4-warp CTA computes one `32x64` output tile; each warp computes
  a `16x32` region with two `16x16` accumulators.
- `cta64`: one 4-warp CTA computes one `64x64` output tile; each warp computes a
  `32x32` quadrant with four `16x16` accumulators. This variant still does not
  use shared memory.
- `cta64s`: `cta64` plus naive shared-memory staging for each `64x64x16` K tile.
- `cta64x128`: one 8-warp CTA computes one `64x128` output tile; each warp still
  computes a `32x32` quadrant.
- `cta64x128w`: one 4-warp CTA computes one `64x128` output tile; each warp
  computes a wider `32x64` tile with eight `16x16` accumulators.
- `cta64x256w`: one 8-warp CTA computes one `64x256` output tile; each warp
  still computes a `32x64` tile. This tests whether wider-N CTAs improve large
  transformer linear shapes by reducing CTA count.
- `cta128x64`: one 8-warp CTA computes one `128x64` output tile.
- `cta128x128`: one 16-warp CTA computes one `128x128` output tile.

Build and run:

```bash
ninja -C build-bench bench_bf16_gemm_shapes -j8
./build-bench/bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset all --warmup 1
```

Shape source:

- Model config: `dim=256`, `depth=12`, `num_bands=60`, `heads=8`,
  `dim_head=64`, `stft_n_fft=2048`, `chunk_size=573300`.
- Main transformer token rows per chunk: `60 * 1301 = 78060`.
- Time attention: `batch=60 * 8 = 480`, sequence length `1301`.
- Freq attention: `batch=1301 * 8 = 10408`, sequence length `60`.

Measured baseline:

```text
shape        best       TF/s   notes
linear_qkv   c64x256w   38.27  54.7% of A10G dense BF16 roofline
linear_ffn1  c64x256w   37.91  54.2% of A10G dense BF16 roofline
linear_ffn2  c64x256w   42.00  60.0% of A10G dense BF16 roofline
time_qk      cta64      16.84  K=64 attention still needs its own path
time_av      c16x64     16.86  skinny N prefers 16x64 over cta64
freq_qk      c16x64     9.05   N=60, M=60; 16x64 is only marginally better
freq_av      warp16     9.01   N=64, M=60; warp16 still wins
mask_ffn1    c64x128w   28.33  wider warp still beats cta64 and c64x256w
mask_out48   warp16     6.75   tiny N; wide tiles lose to padding
```

Interpretation:

- `cta64` was the first useful CTA step over `warp16`; multi-accumulator CTA
  tiles are the right direction for `K=256/1024` linear kernels.
- `cta64x256w` is currently the best long-M transformer linear variant. A
  `64x256` CTA with eight `32x64` warps beats `cta64x128w` for `linear_qkv`,
  `linear_ffn1`, and especially `linear_ffn2`, reaching about 38-42 TF/s.
- `cta64x128w` remains better for small-M mask MLP shapes. `mask_ffn1` drops
  from about 28.3 TF/s with `c64x128w` to about 21.9 TF/s with `c64x256w`, and
  `mask_out48` is still dominated by output-width padding.
- `cta128x64` and `cta128x128` do not beat `cta64x128w`; in this WMMA prototype,
  widening M is worse than widening per-warp N for the long-row transformer
  linear shapes.
- `cta64s` is slower than `cta64` on every tested shape except that it partially
  recovers `time_av` relative to `cta64`; naive shared-memory staging is not the
  next path to optimize.
- `c16x64` is now the best no-cuBLAS prototype for `time_av` and barely wins
  `freq_qk`, but it does not help `time_qk` and loses `freq_av`. Attention needs
  separate QK, AV, time, and freq kernels rather than one generic GEMM tile.
- `cta64x128` is bad for `freq_*` and skinny-N shapes because `N=60/64` pads to
  `128`; attention shapes need separate `K=64` / skinny-N kernels instead of the
  same tile as large linear.
- These kernels are still not replacement candidates for cuBLAS; large linear
  shapes need shared-memory reuse and better CTA swizzling.
- `K=64` attention shapes need dedicated kernels; padding `60 -> 64` costs about
  13.8% extra math for freq attention before any other overhead.

## Inference-Like Linear Shape Probe

The padded GEMM shape sweep above is useful for broad tile exploration, but it
does not predict the current inference linear path by itself. The inference
kernel writes BF16 output directly, keeps the real `M=78060` tail, and fuses the
linear post-op for FFN layers. Run the inference-like probe with:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset infer_linear --warmup 2 --iters 5
```

This preset compares several `64xN` CTA shapes using the current model's
long-M transformer linear shapes. The `t` suffix means the kernel is compiled
with the same N/K-tail `smem_b` resource shape as the current default
`c64x128w_tail`; plain `c64x256` is the earlier lightweight opt-in shape.

```text
shape            best variants from --preset infer_linear --warmup 2 --iters 5
infer_qkv        c64x128t 2.018 ms, 30.43 TF/s; c64x256t 2.085 ms, 29.44 TF/s
infer_ffn1_gelu  c64x256t 1.379 ms, 29.68 TF/s; c64x128t 1.394 ms, 29.36 TF/s
infer_ffn2_bias  c64x256t 1.130 ms, 36.21 TF/s; c64x128t 1.338 ms, 30.58 TF/s
infer_attn_out   c64x256t 0.625 ms, 32.74 TF/s; c64x128t 0.725 ms, 28.24 TF/s

negative variants:
  c64x64t is slower than c64x128t on every shape.
  c64x128ab stages every A/B K tile through shared memory to reduce repeated
    CTA-internal global loads; it is much slower than c64x128t
    (qkv 6.140 ms vs 2.018 ms, ffn1 4.185 ms vs 1.392 ms,
    ffn2 4.533 ms vs 1.336 ms, attn_out 2.333 ms vs 0.732 ms).
  c64x128s stores accumulator fragments through a shared-memory FP32 output
    tile before BF16 conversion; it is slower than c64x128t on every shape
    (qkv 2.400 ms vs 2.015 ms, ffn1 1.866 ms vs 1.394 ms,
    ffn2 1.533 ms vs 1.339 ms, attn_out 0.853 ms vs 0.720 ms).
  c64x128r1/c64x256r1 split each warp from a `32x64` output tile to `16x64`,
    cutting accumulator pressure from `REG=118` to `REG=64` but doubling the
    CTA warp count. They are slower on every long-M shape: qkv
    2.799/2.877 ms vs 2.017/2.093 ms, ffn1 1.776/1.823 ms vs
    1.393/1.384 ms, ffn2 1.701/1.750 ms vs 1.333/1.107 ms, and attn_out
    0.886/0.920 ms vs 0.721/0.625 ms.
  c64x192t loses badly on N=256 shapes because it pads 256 -> 384.
  plain c64x256 remains negative; c64x256t is a different resource/codegen shape.
  c64x320t is dominated by padding and larger CTA pressure.
  c64x384t/c64x512t reduce CTA count for qkv/ffn1 but are still slower
    (qkv 2.345/2.319 ms vs c64x128t 2.014 ms; ffn1 2.367/1.585 ms
    vs c64x256t 1.380 ms). For N=256 they pad badly and lose heavily.
  c128x64t/c128x128t/c128x256t increase CTA-M from 64 to 128 to reuse each B
    tile across four row-warp groups instead of two. This is also negative:
    qkv best c128 is 2.135 ms vs c64x128t 2.016 ms; ffn1 best c128 is
    1.494 ms vs c64x256t 1.386 ms; ffn2 best c128 is 1.259 ms vs c64x256t
    1.109 ms; attn_out best c128 is 0.714 ms vs c64x256t 0.624 ms.
```

Follow-up per-warp row/column layout probe, measured with
`./build-min/bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset infer_linear --warmup 3 --iters 10`:

```text
c64x128r4c2: one 4-warp CTA computes 64x128, each warp computes 64x32.
c64x128r2c2: one 8-warp CTA computes 64x128, each warp computes 32x32.
c64x256r4c2: one 8-warp CTA computes 64x256, each warp computes 64x32.
c64x256r1c8: one 8-warp CTA computes 64x256, each warp computes 16x128.

infer_qkv:
  c64x128t 2.014 ms
  c64x128r4c2 2.071 ms
  c64x128r2c2 2.433 ms

infer_ffn1_gelu:
  c64x256t 1.389 ms
  c64x256r4c2 1.472 ms
  c64x256r1c8 1.674 ms

infer_ffn2_bias:
  c64x256t 1.110 ms
  c64x256r4c2 1.100 ms
  c64x256r1c8 1.604 ms

infer_attn_out:
  c64x256t 0.627 ms
  c64x256r4c2 0.616 ms
  c64x256r1c8 0.855 ms
```

Resource usage:

```text
c64x256t:     REG=118 SHARED=10240 LOCAL=0
c64x256r4c2:  REG=116 SHARED=10240 LOCAL=0 in benchmark,
               REG=106 SHARED=10240 LOCAL=0 in the temporary inference port
c64x256r1c8:  REG=128 SHARED=10240 LOCAL=0
```

Interpretation: `c64x256r4c2` is a small microbenchmark positive only for the
`N=256` ffn2 and attention-output linears, but it regresses qkv/ffn1 and is not
a full inference win. A temporary `out_features == 256` inference port measured
`test_clean.wav` at `1718.9 ms` and `1716.4 ms`; disabling it measured
`1715.0 ms` and `1719.1 ms`. The average is effectively tied, so this remains
benchmark-only and was removed from `src`. `c64x128r2c2` and `c64x256r1c8` are
clear negative results.

Interpretation:

- The original padded/no-postop GEMM sweep made `c64x256w` look attractive for
  large transformer linears, but the inference-like probe agrees with the full
  Nsight result: current plain `c64x256w_tail` is negative for all four long-M
  linear shapes.
- `c64x256t` is the first new candidate from this corrected probe. It is
  slightly worse than `c64x128t` for qkv, roughly tied for ffn1, and clearly
  better for the N=256 ffn2 and attention-output linears.
- CTA-M=128 does not pay off for the inference-like BF16-output linears. It
  halves CTA count for the same N tile, but the larger row tile lowers residency
  and does not improve per-warp scheduling. Resource usage is `REG=108,
  SHARED=6144` for `c128x64t`, `REG=118, SHARED=8192` for `c128x128t`, and
  `REG=118, SHARED=12288` for `c128x256t`. These variants should stay
  benchmark-only.
- An aligned-bias `c64x128` experiment for the small-M `M=1301,N=1024` groups
  reduced resource use from `REG=118, SHARED=6144` to `REG=96, SHARED=2048`,
  but it was slower in Nsight. The dominant `gridX=168` group moved from about
  94.2 ms to 109.6 ms, and the total bias-linear group regressed. The
  implementation was removed; lower static shared memory alone is not a useful
  predictor for this A10G shape.
- Future linear shape searches should use this inference-like preset or an even
  closer full-kernel microbench before moving a tile shape into the CLI path.
- The current default long-M linear path is still only about 28-31 TF/s, or
  roughly 40-44% of the measured A10G dense BF16 roofline, so linear remains a
  valid target for deeper CTA/shared-memory/MMA scheduling work.
- A `c64x128s` shared-output experiment showed that WMMA
  `store_matrix_sync` to a CTA-local FP32 tile plus contiguous BF16 conversion
  is worse than the direct SM86 accumulator-fragment BF16 store. The likely
  cost is shared-memory round-trip and reduced occupancy, so output-store
  optimization should not use a full CTA FP32 staging tile.
- A `c64x128ab` A/B shared-staging experiment also regressed badly. Avoid
  naive per-K shared-memory staging for these long-M linears; the current direct
  global fragment loads are faster on A10G despite CTA-internal reuse loss.
- Wider `c64x384t` and `c64x512t` candidates also regress. They keep the same
  118-register per-thread pressure as `c64x128t/c64x256t`, increase static
  shared memory to 14-18KB, and reduce CTA count too far for the current
  `32x64` per-warp WMMA layout.
- The `r1` accumulator-pressure experiment shows that lower registers alone are
  not enough. `c64x128r1/c64x256r1` cut resource usage to `REG=64` with the same
  shared-memory footprint as the current `CtaM=64` tail kernels
  (`6144/10240 B`), but doubling CTA warp count makes all four long-M linear
  shapes slower. The current `32x64` per-warp tile remains the better A10G
  scheduling point for these WMMA linears.

The small-linear probe targets `M=1301` band-split and mask-estimator linears:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset infer_small_linear --warmup 2 --iters 8
```

Key results:

```text
shape           best useful variant
band_n256_k16   warp16t 0.0084 ms vs c64x128t 0.011 ms
band_n256_k32   warp16t 0.0092 ms vs c64x128t 0.012 ms
band_n256_k64   warp16t 0.0111 ms vs c64x128t 0.014 ms
mask_hid_k256   c32x256r2c4 0.043 ms vs c64x128t 0.045 ms
mask_hid_k1024  c32x256r2c4 0.147 ms vs c64x128t 0.154 ms
mask_out48      warp16t 0.0542 ms vs c64x128t 0.198 ms
mask_out128     warp16t 0.0673 ms vs c64x128t 0.118 ms
```

Interpretation:

- `warp16t` is the right current path for tiny output widths (`N<=128`) at
  `M=1301`, especially mask output projections.
- `c32x256r2c4` is now the best measured path for hidden `N=1024` mask MLP
  bias linears at `M=1301`. It uses a `32x256` CTA with four warps, each warp
  computing a `32x64` tile. It is negative for band `N=256` and mask output
  `N<=128`, so the inference port is restricted to
  `total_batch <= 2048 && out_features == 1024`.
- Resource usage for the inference port is `REG=104 SHARED=9216 LOCAL=0`,
  versus `REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0` for the old `c64x128t` bias kernel.
- Nsight before/after on `test_clean.wav`: the old `c64x128t` bias-linear group
  was 1448 calls / `159.219 ms`. After the restricted `c32x256` port it becomes
  960 calls / `85.778 ms` for `c32x256`, plus the remaining `c64x128t` bias
  calls at 488 calls / `68.546 ms`, for `154.324 ms` total.
- End-to-end validation after the port: `test_clean.wav` measured `1716.8 ms`
  versus `1718.7 ms` with `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_WMMA_C32X256_SMALL=1`;
  `test.wav` measured `10202.1 ms`. Output difference versus the disabled
  path on `test_clean.wav` stayed at BF16 write-noise scale:
  Vocals `rel_rms=5.247492134e-08`, Instrumental `rel_rms=5.340587398e-08`.

## Inference Experiment: Long-M CtaM=32 Linear WMMA

The long-M inference-like probe found a small positive `CtaM=32` follow-up for
the transformer linears. The retained inference port is restricted to measured
MelBandRoformer shapes and can be disabled with:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_WMMA_C32_LONG=1
```

Current dispatch:

- qkv no-bias: `M>=4096,N=1536,K=256` uses `32x128`, two warps per CTA, each
  warp computing `32x64`
- attention output no-bias: `M>=4096,N=256,K=512` uses `32x256`, four warps per
  CTA, each warp computing `32x64`
- ffn1 bias+GELU: `M>=4096,N=1024,K=256` uses the same `32x256` layout
- ffn2 bias: `M>=4096,N=256,K=1024` uses the same `32x256` layout
- the earlier small-mask `M<=2048,N=1024` bias path now reuses the same
  `32x256` RC template

Resource usage in `cudasep_infer`:

```text
c32x128 no-bias: REG=118 SHARED=5120 LOCAL=0
c32x256 no-bias/bias/bias+GELU: REG=118 SHARED=9216 LOCAL=0
old c64x128: REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0
old c64x256: REG=118 SHARED=10240 LOCAL=0
```

End-to-end A/B on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default c32 long: 1698.2 ms, 1697.6 ms
CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_WMMA_C32_LONG=1: 1713.6 ms, 1712.6 ms
```

`test.wav` default validation measured `10116.1 ms`.

Output diff versus the disabled path on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.251476458e-08
Instrumental: max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.321279538e-08
```

Nsight Systems kernel totals on `test_clean.wav` show the source of the win:

```text
qkv no-bias:       c32x128 193.544 ms vs c64x128 194.856 ms
ffn1 bias+GELU:    c32x256 130.292 ms vs c64x256 133.120 ms
ffn2 bias + small: c32x256 189.208 ms vs c64x256 ffn2 109.365 ms + small 84.995 ms
attn_out no-bias:  c32x256  57.385 ms vs c64x256  60.857 ms
```

Follow-up `CtaM=32` fragment-layout sweep:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset infer_linear --warmup 2 --iters 5
./build-min/bench_bf16_gemm_shapes --preset infer_small_linear --warmup 2 --iters 5
```

The existing `r2c4` layout remains the best inference candidate. For long-M
linears, all new `r1c4/r1c8/r2c8` variants are negative:

```text
shape            current best               closest new variant
infer_qkv        c32x128r2c4 1.989 ms 30.87 TF/s  c32x128r2c8 2.142 ms 28.66 TF/s
infer_ffn1_gelu  c32x256r2c4 1.325 ms 30.90 TF/s  c32x256r2c8 1.547 ms 26.46 TF/s
infer_ffn2_bias  c32x256r2c4 1.054 ms 38.83 TF/s  c32x256r2c8 1.149 ms 35.61 TF/s
infer_attn_out   c32x256r2c4 0.590 ms 34.67 TF/s  c32x256r2c8 0.649 ms 31.52 TF/s
```

Small-M shapes do not create a port candidate either. The band and mask-output
linears still prefer the existing warp16 path, and the mask-hidden `N=1024`
linears still prefer `c32x256r2c4`:

```text
mask_hid_k256   c32x256r2c4 0.044 ms 15.66 TF/s
mask_hid_k1024  c32x256r2c4 0.149 ms 18.33 TF/s
```

Benchmark resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
c32x128r2c4 REG=118 SHARED=5120 LOCAL=0
c32x128r1c4 REG=64  SHARED=5120 LOCAL=0
c32x128r1c8 REG=126 SHARED=5120 LOCAL=0
c32x128r2c8 REG=204 SHARED=5120 LOCAL=0
c32x256r2c4 REG=118 SHARED=9216 LOCAL=0
c32x256r1c4 REG=64  SHARED=9216 LOCAL=0
c32x256r1c8 REG=128 SHARED=9216 LOCAL=0
c32x256r2c8 REG=200 SHARED=9216 LOCAL=0
```

Interpretation: lowering register pressure with `r1c4` is not useful because it
adds warp scheduling work and splits the row tile into `16x64` warp tiles.
Widening each warp to `r2c8` reduces CTA warp count, but the accumulator set
pushes register pressure to about 200 registers/thread and loses throughput.
Keep these variants benchmark-only; do not port them into `src`.

## Inference Experiment: WMMA BF16 Linear Output

`--bf16` now enables the first inference-path replacement for BF16 linear GEMM
output by default. The current default mixes fixed-shape `32x128`/`32x256`
linears for the measured long-M transformer shapes, `32x256` for small
`N=1024` mask hidden linears, and the older `64x128`/`64x256` tail-capable
paths for the remaining linear calls. All of these use BF16 inputs and FP32
WMMA accumulators, then write BF16 output directly.

Current dispatch constraints:

- input activation and weight must already be BF16
- `total_batch`, `out_features`, and `in_features` may have tails
- full `64x128x16` tiles still load A/B directly from global memory
- M/N/K tail tiles zero-pad A/B through shared memory and do masked BF16 stores
- output is written directly from the SM86 accumulator fragment mapping; the
  old `64x128` shared-memory FP32 output tile has been removed

Useful validation commands on the current A10G build:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/nocublas_default_bf16_clean_final \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav \
  --output outputs/nocublas_default_bf16_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
  -o outputs/nocublas_default_bf16_nsys \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/nocublas_default_bf16_nsys_out \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Current results:

```text
direct-store linear output, time+freq fused:
  test_clean.wav: 2280.4 ms -> 2052.5 ms
  output diff vs old shared-output path:
    Vocals rel_rms=5.2149202e-08, Instrumental rel_rms=5.3206578e-08

tail-capable linear WMMA, time+freq fused:
  test_clean.wav: 1989.4 ms

default fixed BF16 build, no cuBLAS/cuFFT link:
  test_clean.wav: 2052.6 ms before CLI guard, 2074.1 ms after CLI guard rebuild
  test_clean.wav: 2057.7 ms after disabling aligned split by default
  test_clean.wav: 2017.9 ms after direct BF16 fragment stores in time/freq attention
  test_clean.wav: 1998.5 ms after fusing plain linear bias into WMMA output
  test_clean.wav: 2004.7 ms after the final unused-bias-kernel cleanup rebuild
  test_clean.wav: 1952.1 ms after fusing linear_gelu bias+GELU into WMMA output
  test_clean.wav: 1955.8 ms after the final unused-GELU-kernel cleanup rebuild
  test_clean.wav: 1954.0 ms after fusing linear_sigmoid bias+sigmoid into WMMA output
  test_clean.wav: 1956.6 ms after gating the negative qkv_rotary fusion experiment off by default
  test_clean.wav: 1932.0 ms after enabling threaded qkv split/rotary by default
  test_clean.wav: 1878.0 ms after enabling threaded gate merge by default
  test_clean.wav: 1864.3 ms after enabling D=256 strided-copy specialization by default
  test_clean.wav: 1831.7 ms after enabling c64x256t for N<=1024 linears by default
  test_clean.wav: 1783.7 ms after enabling small-N warp16 bias linear by default
  test_clean.wav: 2076.3 ms under Nsight Systems
  test_clean.wav: 2017.1 ms under Nsight Systems after linear bias fusion
  test_clean.wav: 1964.2 ms under Nsight Systems after linear_gelu fusion
  test_clean.wav: 1962.9 ms under Nsight Systems after linear_sigmoid fusion
  test_clean.wav: 2073.2 ms under Nsight Systems with historical qkv_rotary opt-in
  test_clean.wav: 1928.5 ms under Nsight Systems with threaded qkv split/rotary
  test_clean.wav: 1910.4 ms under Nsight Systems with threaded gate merge
  test_clean.wav: 1871.9 ms under Nsight Systems with D=256 strided-copy specialization
  test_clean.wav: 1844.8 ms under Nsight Systems with c64x256t default path
  test_clean.wav: 1795.7 ms under Nsight Systems with small-N warp16 default path
  test.wav:       12353.7 ms before attention direct-store
  test.wav:       12135.2 ms after attention direct-store
  test.wav:       11970.0 ms after linear bias fusion
  test.wav:       11685.7 ms after linear_gelu fusion
  test.wav:       11724.1 ms after linear_sigmoid fusion
  test.wav:       11684.4 ms after gating qkv_rotary fusion off by default
  test.wav:       11445.1 ms after enabling threaded qkv split/rotary by default
  test.wav:       11192.6 ms after enabling threaded gate merge by default
  test.wav:       11054.7 ms after enabling D=256 strided-copy specialization by default
  test.wav:       10874.4 ms after enabling c64x256t for N<=1024 linears by default
  test.wav:       10587.2 ms after enabling small-N warp16 bias linear by default

unsupported probes:
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_BF16_CUSTOM_PATH=1 fails at linear_gemm_f32_output
  --fp16 fails at CLI argument validation

historical aligned split experiment, removed:
  test_clean.wav: 2292.9 ms, negative
  output diff vs default tail-only path: rel_rms about 5e-8
  Nsight:
    aligned kernel: 1396 calls, 882.6 ms total
    tail kernel:     764 calls, 144.0 ms total
```

Resource usage:

```text
direct-store before N/K tail support:
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_kernel: REG=96 SHARED=2048 LOCAL=0

tail-capable version:
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_kernel: REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0

historical aligned split experiment, removed:
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_aligned_kernel: REG=96 SHARED=2048 LOCAL=0
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_tail_kernel:    REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0

attention direct-store:
  time_attention1301_q32_fragscale_kernel:   REG=40 SHARED=9600 LOCAL=0
  freq_attention60_q16_fragprob_regout:      REG=40 SHARED=6848 LOCAL=0

linear bias fusion:
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_tail_kernel<false>: REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_tail_kernel<true>:  REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0

linear_gelu fusion:
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_tail_kernel<false,false>: REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x128w_tail_kernel<true,false>:  REG=118 SHARED=6144 LOCAL=0

  split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_threaded_kernel: REG=28 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
  split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_d64_threaded:    REG=19 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0, removed

threaded gate merge:
  apply_gates_and_merge_heads_typed_kernel<bf16,bf16,bf16>: REG=40 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
  apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_threaded_kernel:         REG=26 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0

RMSNorm dim=256 threaded experiment:
  rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel<bf16>:         REG=33 SHARED=16 LOCAL=0
  rms_norm_bf16_dim256_threaded_kernel: REG=14 SHARED=1024 LOCAL=0

historical BF16 binary inplace threaded experiment:
  binary_inplace_same_shape_kernel<bf16>:        REG=27 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
  binary_inplace_same_shape_bf162_threaded:      REG=11 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0

BF16 D=256 strided-copy specialization:
  strided_copy_value_kernel<bf16>:       REG=38 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
  strided_copy_bf16_*_d256_kernel:       REG=28 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0

linear c64x256t default:
  linear_wmma_bf16_c64x256w_tail_kernel<*,*,*,true>: REG=118 SHARED=10240 LOCAL=0
```

```text
qkv no-bias c64x128 aligned experiment,
`CUDASEP_LINEAR_WMMA_ALIGNED_EXPERIMENT=1`:
  The aligned no-bias kernel avoids N/K tail handling and reduces resource use
  from `REG=118, SHARED=6144` to `REG=96, SHARED=2048`, but it is much slower
  for the real qkv shape. On `test_clean.wav`, end-to-end time regressed from
  about 1741.3 ms to 1797.8 ms. Nsight moved qkv from the default c64x128 tail
  kernel at 96 calls / 195.233 ms to the aligned kernel at 96 calls /
  269.571 ms. Output drift stayed around `5e-8` relative RMS. This opt-in path
  was removed; lower register/shared usage alone is not a reliable predictor
  for the A10G qkv projection.
```

Interpretation:

- Direct accumulator-fragment BF16 stores are a clear win and should stay.
- N/K tail support removes the residual linear GEMM cuBLAS calls in the
  all-BF16 output experiment, but it raises register pressure from 96 to 118.
- Splitting out a lower-resource aligned kernel is a negative result on A10G:
  lower register/shared usage did not translate into lower time for the current
  mixture of linear shapes, so it remains opt-in only.
- Direct BF16 stores from the time/freq attention accumulator fragments are a
  positive result. They remove the shared FP32 output tile and reduce attention
  shared memory without changing output beyond float-to-BF16 write noise.
- Fusing the plain `linear()` bias add into the WMMA output store is a positive
  result. It removes 1776 CUDA launches per `test_clean.wav` inference, deletes
  the standalone BF16 inplace bias kernel, keeps the same 118-register tail
  kernel footprint, and only changes output at float-to-BF16 write-noise scale.
- Fusing `linear_gelu()` bias and GELU into the WMMA output store is also a
  speed-positive result: it removes 96 CUDA launches and the standalone BF16 GELU
  postprocess kernel. The fused GELU instantiation keeps the same reported
  resource footprint, but it changes final audio by about 0.003 relative RMS
  against the plain-bias-fused path. Replacing `__expf` with `expf` did not
  change the diff, so this is recorded as a BF16/fusion-path numerical
  difference rather than a fast-exp-only artifact.
- Fusing `linear_sigmoid()` bias and sigmoid into the WMMA output store removes
  the last BF16 linear activation postprocess launch group. It is performance
  neutral in Nsight because the old standalone sigmoid kernel was only about
  0.55 ms, but it removes 96 launches and keeps output differences at BF16 write
  noise scale relative to the `linear_gelu`-fused path.
- Fusing qkv projection, q/k/v layout conversion, and rotary directly into the
  WMMA qkv output store is a negative A10G result in this implementation. It
  removes the standalone split/rotary launch, but the qkv GEMM kernel grows from
  about 197 ms to about 422 ms because the accumulator store path now performs
  paired q/k rotation and scattered q/k/v writes. The implementation and opt-in
  environment flag were removed; default inference uses the faster qkv GEMM plus
  split/rotary path.
- Replacing the cuTile BF16 qkv split/rotary kernel with a conventional
  256-thread CUDA kernel is a positive A10G result. It keeps the operation local
  to split/rotary instead of slowing qkv GEMM, drops the split kernel from about
  96.5 ms to 71.1 ms per `test_clean.wav`, and preserves output at BF16 write
  noise scale. This path is now default; `CUDASEP_DISABLE_SPLIT_QKV_THREADED=1`
  restores the older tile split kernel for comparison.
- Specializing split/rotary further for `heads=8, dim_head=64` with
  `__nv_bfloat162` pair loads/stores is a negative result. It lowers registers
  from 28 to 19, but Nsight still reports about 71.2 ms for the 96-call group
  and final audio moves by about 0.0033-0.0035 relative RMS. The implementation
  and opt-in environment flag were removed.
- Grouping one `heads=8, dim_head=64` token block per launch block was tested
  behind `CUDASEP_SPLIT_QKV_GROUPED_D64_EXPERIMENT=1`, but it was slower
  (`test_clean.wav` about 1815.5 ms) and produced the same large output drift
  as the d64 specialization. The implementation was removed instead of keeping
  another invalid opt-in path.
- A scalar layout-only microbench now compares the current split/rotary indexing
  against a token-block layout without changing math:
  `./build-min/bench_bf16_split_qkv_rotary --warmup 3 --iters 10`.
  The token-block version is bit-identical but does not win on A10G:
  time shape `0.7751 ms` vs current `0.7411 ms`, freq shape `0.7435 ms`
  vs current `0.7424 ms`. A `head8_block` variant keeps the current same-head
  / 8-token grouping but maps `b,h,n` from `blockIdx` instead of a global
  element index; it is also bit-identical and effectively tied, with time
  `0.7422 ms` and freq `0.7428 ms`. Resource use is lower for token/head8
  (`REG=22/23` vs `REG=26`), but removing the index arithmetic does not improve
  the A10G split/rotary path, so neither variant is ported.
- A follow-up block-size sweep on the same current indexing is also not a port
  candidate. The `128`, `256`, and `512` thread launches are bit-identical and
  effectively tied for both stable split/rotary shapes:

  ```text
  time B=60,N=1301:
    current256 best=0.7421 ms median=0.7424 ms
    current128 best=0.7423 ms median=0.7426 ms mismatch=0
    current512 best=0.7418 ms median=0.7426 ms mismatch=0

  freq B=1301,N=60:
    current256 best=0.7427 ms median=0.7441 ms
    current128 best=0.7431 ms median=0.7438 ms mismatch=0
    current512 best=0.7434 ms median=0.7441 ms mismatch=0
  ```

  Resource use for the current indexing kernel is unchanged at
  `REG=26 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0`. The default 256-thread launch remains in `src`;
  block size is not the current split/rotary bottleneck.
- Replacing the cuTile BF16 gate-merge kernel with a conventional 256-thread
  CUDA kernel is also positive. It drops gate merge from about 99.8 ms to
  58.0 ms per `test_clean.wav`, preserves output at BF16 write-noise scale, and
  is now default. `CUDASEP_DISABLE_GATE_MERGE_THREADED=1` restores the older
  tile gate-merge kernel for comparison.
- Specializing BF16 gate merge for the fixed `heads=8, dim_head=64` path with
  one block per `(B,N)` token is another positive result. It writes the merged
  512-wide token contiguously, cuts the gate-merge block count in half, lowers
  resource use from `REG=26` to `REG=19`, and drops the 96-call Nsight total
  from about 58.0 ms to 26.7 ms. `test_clean.wav` final audio differs from the
  threaded default by only about `5e-8` relative RMS. This path is now default;
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_GATE_MERGE_TOKEN_D64=1` restores the older threaded default.
- Replacing the one-block-per-row cuTile RMSNorm path with a conventional
  256-thread CUDA reduction is a negative A10G result. It lowers register usage
  but raises shared memory to 1024 bytes per block, changes the reduction order,
  slows the main dim=256 RMSNorm group from about 56.7 ms to 89.1 ms, and moves
  `test_clean.wav` output by about 0.0034 relative RMS. The implementation and
  `CUDASEP_RMS_NORM_THREADED_EXPERIMENT` env flag have been removed.
- Replacing BF16 same-shape inplace add/mul with a conventional 256-thread
  kernel is also not a positive result. The threaded prototypes lower register
  usage from 27 to 11 and use packed `bf16x2` arithmetic, but Nsight still shows
  about 35.7 ms for the 192-call group versus 35.6 ms for the cuTile kernel.
  The implementation and `CUDASEP_BINARY_INPLACE_THREADED_EXPERIMENT` env flag
  have been removed.
- Specializing the hot BF16 strided-copy path for `D=256` transformer layout
  materialization is a positive result. The fixed kernels cover the stable
  `[B,T,F,256] <-> [B,F,T,256]` transposes and the mask-estimator
  `[B,T,F,256] -> [F,B,T,256]` pack. The 104-call large-copy group drops from
  about 27.1 ms to 13.0 ms while preserving output at BF16 write-noise scale.
  This path is now default; `CUDASEP_DISABLE_STRIDED_COPY_D256=1` restores the
  generic cuTile strided copy.
- `cudasep_infer` no longer links cuBLAS/cuFFT. Generic GEMM fallback call sites
  were replaced with explicit unsupported errors, so the fixed target path is
  BF16-only and library-free for GEMM/FFT.
- Linear is now the main no-cuBLAS bottleneck. The next useful split is separate
  kernels for large full-tile QKV/FFN shapes and tiny/skinny tail shapes. The
  obvious BF16 linear postprocess launches for plain bias, GELU, and sigmoid
  have now been fused into the WMMA output path.

## Historical Inference Experiment: WMMA BF16 Attention AV

The removed `CUDASEP_ATTENTION_AV_WMMA_BF16_OUTPUT=1` experiment enabled a
time-attention AV replacement for the long `D=64` shape. The kernel computed:

```text
scores_lowp[BH, N, N_k] @ v[BH, N_k, 64] -> out[BH, N, 64]
```

It is intentionally restricted to `D == 64`, `N >= 512`, and `N_k >= 512`, so it
targets time attention and does not take over the `N=60` frequency path. It uses
one CTA per `16x64` output tile, four warps per CTA, shared-memory zero padding
for `M/K` tails, FP32 accumulator fragments, and direct BF16 output.

Historical validation command:

```bash
CUDASEP_ATTENTION_AV_BF16_OUTPUT=1 CUDASEP_ATTENTION_AV_WMMA_BF16_OUTPUT=1 \
  ./build-bench/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/av_wmma_plus_bf16_test_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
test_clean.wav, cuBLAS AV BF16: 2103.6 ms, RTF 18.82x
test_clean.wav, WMMA  AV BF16: 2606.9 ms, RTF 15.19x
test.wav,       cuBLAS AV BF16: 11869.6 ms, RTF 26.25x
test.wav,       WMMA  AV BF16: 14854.3 ms, RTF 20.98x
```

Retested after the default no-cuFFT STFT/iSTFT path and default BF16 linear
WMMA path were enabled:

```text
test_clean.wav, default BF16 linear WMMA:        2439.0 ms, RTF 16.23x
test_clean.wav, + attention AV WMMA experiment: 2916.0 ms, RTF 13.58x
```

Output difference against the cuBLAS AV BF16 baseline for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0044256523 rms=0.00029814523 rel_rms=0.0016158159
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0031312406 rms=0.00031810956 rel_rms=0.0013230213
```

Output difference against the current default BF16 linear-WMMA path:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.012404896 rms=0.00069050986 rel_rms=0.0037394956
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.011785991 rms=0.00086159894 rel_rms=0.0035836985
```

Interpretation:

- The kernel is stable on both test files and emits BF16 HMMA instructions.
- It is not a cuBLAS replacement candidate in this form: about 24% slower on
  `test_clean.wav` and about 25% slower on `test.wav` in the earlier stack; in
  the current stack it is still about 19.6% slower on `test_clean.wav`.
- The audio-level difference is larger than the linear WMMA experiment. Some of
  this can be normal reduction-order variance, but this path should not be used
  as a quality-neutral replacement without a tensor-level correctness probe.
- The implementation and `CUDASEP_ATTENTION_AV_WMMA_BF16_OUTPUT` env flag were
  removed; default inference uses the fixed fused time/freq attention paths.
- A standalone AV GEMM kernel still pays global-memory traffic for
  `scores_lowp`. The next attention direction should fuse QK, softmax, and AV
  for the fixed `N=1301, D=64` time-attention shape, so probabilities do not
  round-trip through global memory.

## Inference Experiment: BF16 Attention QK Scores and Accumulation

Two QK precision experiments were retested after the default no-cuFFT
STFT/iSTFT path and default BF16 linear WMMA path were enabled:

- `CUDASEP_ATTENTION_QK_BF16_SCORES=1`: keep cuBLAS QK with FP32 accumulation,
  but write the QK scores buffer as BF16 before softmax.
- `CUDASEP_ATTENTION_QK_BF16_SCORES=1 CUDASEP_ATTENTION_QK_BF16_ACCUM=1`: bypass
  cuBLAS QK and use the scalar tile kernel that rounds the running QK dot-product
  accumulator to BF16 at every `D=64` step.

Validation commands:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/qk_accum_current_default_clean_20260602 \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ATTENTION_QK_BF16_SCORES=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/qk_scores_bf16_scores_clean_20260602 \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ATTENTION_QK_BF16_SCORES=1 CUDASEP_ATTENTION_QK_BF16_ACCUM=1 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/qk_accum_bf16_accum_clean_20260602 \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
test_clean.wav, default BF16 linear WMMA:       2433.9 ms, RTF 16.27x
test_clean.wav, QK scores BF16 only:            2424.6 ms, RTF 16.33x
test_clean.wav, QK scores + BF16 accum scalar: 22307.4 ms, RTF  1.77x
```

Output difference against the current default BF16 linear-WMMA path:

```text
QK scores BF16 only:
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0092277527 rms=0.00057544193 rel_rms=0.0031163386
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.009630084  rms=0.00064682239 rel_rms=0.0026903659

QK scores + BF16 accum scalar:
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.04126808  rms=0.0023720798 rel_rms=0.012846134
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.032676876 rms=0.0023358837 rel_rms=0.0097157767
```

Interpretation:

- Storing QK scores in BF16 is performance-neutral in this stack, but already
  causes about 0.27-0.31% output rel RMS drift on `test_clean.wav`.
- Rounding the QK dot-product accumulator to BF16 increases drift to about
  0.97-1.28% rel RMS and is about 9.2x slower end to end because the current
  experiment is scalar per score, not Tensor Core MMA.
- The BF16-accum scalar path should remain an opt-in hypothesis test only. A
  performance path must use a fused time-attention MMA kernel that keeps QK,
  softmax, and AV inside the kernel instead of writing a global BF16 scores
  buffer.

## Inference Experiment: Fused Time Attention1301 Regout

`CUDASEP_TIME_ATTENTION_FUSED_FRAGSCALE=1` enables the first inference-path
fixed-shape fused time-attention kernel for:

```text
BH=480, N=1301, D=64
```

It computes QK, online softmax, and AV inside one BF16 WMMA kernel and writes
BF16 output directly. The kernel uses the A10G accumulator-fragment row mapping
from `bench_wmma_fragment_map` so the online-softmax row rescale happens in
accumulator registers. The current implementation also keeps each warp's output
accumulator fragment live in registers across the full K loop and writes the
output tile to shared memory only once at the end.

Validation commands:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/time_fused_current_default_clean_20260602 \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_TIME_ATTENTION_FUSED_FRAGSCALE=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/time_fused_fragprob_clean_20260602 \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
test_clean.wav, default BF16 linear WMMA:            2437.1 ms, RTF 16.24x
test_clean.wav, + old shared-output fragscale:       2818.1 ms, RTF 14.05x
test_clean.wav, + regout implementation:             2579.8 ms, RTF 15.35x
test_clean.wav, + fragprob-regout implementation:    2314.8 ms, RTF 17.10x
test.wav,       default BF16 linear WMMA:           14232.7 ms, RTF 21.89x
test.wav,       + fragprob-regout implementation:   13462.4 ms, RTF 23.15x
```

Output difference against the current default BF16 linear-WMMA path for the
current fragprob-regout implementation:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.012140632 rms=0.00061686587 rel_rms=0.0033406724
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.01406005  rms=0.00075301097 rel_rms=0.0031320423
```

For `test.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.017488092 rms=0.00055778155 rel_rms=0.0029516853
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.02020514  rms=0.00061040436 rel_rms=0.0031823139
```

Resource usage in `cudasep_infer`:

```text
time_attention1301_q32_fragscale_kernel: REG=46 SHARED=19968
```

Nsight Systems comparison on `test_clean.wav`:

```bash
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none \
  --force-overwrite=true -o outputs/time_fused_default_nsys \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/time_fused_default_nsys_out \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_TIME_ATTENTION_FUSED_FRAGSCALE=1 \
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none \
  --force-overwrite=true -o outputs/time_fused_regout_nsys \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/time_fused_regout_nsys_out \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```
