Profile summary:

```text
default total CUDA kernel time: 2344.9 ms
regout total CUDA kernel time:  2492.1 ms
fragprob total CUDA kernel time: 2227.1 ms

default replaced time-attention work:
  cutlass_75_tensorop_s1688gemm_bf16_64x128_tn_align1: 48 calls, 257.5 ms
  cutlass_75_tensorop_s1688gemm_bf16_64x128_nn_align1: 48 calls, 249.6 ms
  scale_softmax_to_bf16_kernel:                         48 calls, 356.2 ms
  combined:                                                        863.3 ms

regout fused replacement:
  time_attention1301_q32_fragscale_kernel:              48 calls, 1034.6 ms

fragprob-regout fused replacement:
  time_attention1301_q32_fragscale_kernel:              48 calls, 769.2 ms
```

Interpretation:

- This is the first opt-in inference path that removes the separated cuBLAS QK
  and AV calls for fixed `N=1301,D=64` time attention.
- The regout update improves the opt-in path from 2818.1 ms to 2579.8 ms on
  `test_clean.wav`, but it is still about 5.9% slower than the default path.
- The fragprob-regout update improves the opt-in path again to 2314.8 ms on
  `test_clean.wav`, about 5.0% faster than the same-build default path
  measured at 2437.1 ms.
- On `test.wav`, the same opt-in path improves from 14232.7 ms to 13462.4 ms,
  about 5.4% faster than the same-build default path.
- Output drift is in the same range as other BF16 attention experiments. This is
  acceptable for experimentation, but not a default replacement.
- The opt-in path replaces about 18.0 ms of default time-attention work per call
  with a 21.6 ms fused kernel. To beat the current cuBLAS-separated path, the
  fixed-shape fused time-attention kernel needs to fall below about 18 ms per
  call, not merely remove global score/probability buffers.
- The standalone `mma_q32_raw_regout` lower-bound probe is about 15.7 ms, below
  the 18.0 ms/call replacement target. That means the q32/k16 QK/AV Tensor Core
  schedule is not the fundamental blocker; the remaining gap in the valid
  21.5 ms regout kernel is mainly softmax, row-state, and probability staging.
- The follow-up `mma_q32_exp_fragprob_regout` microbenchmark removes the
  `scores_s` shared-memory tile while keeping bit-identical BF16 output versus
  `q32_exp1`. With the required loop-tail barrier it runs in about 16.0 ms and
  the inference-path fused kernel profiles at 769.2 ms over 48 calls, or
  16.0 ms/call.

Full-tile specialization update:

```text
bench, --warmup 2 --iters 8:
  mma_q32_exp_fragprob_regout   best=16.005 ms median=16.017 ms
  mma_q32_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=15.784 ms median=15.794 ms

validate q32_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1:
  bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

inference after port:
  test_clean.wav: 1772.5 ms
  test.wav:       10553.1 ms

Nsight test_clean.wav:
  before fulltile: time_attention1301_q32_fragscale_kernel 48 calls, 755.949 ms
  after fulltile:  time_attention1301_q32_fragscale_kernel 48 calls, 746.962 ms

resource usage after fulltile:
  REG=40 SHARED=9600 LOCAL=0
```

The full-tile version splits the fixed `N=1301` K loop into 81 complete
`K=16` tiles plus one 5-row tail tile, and skips per-fragment row bounds checks
for the 40 complete `Q=32` query blocks. Output difference versus the previous
default inference output stays at numerical-noise level:

```text
clean Vocals       max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.2478997e-08
clean Instrumental max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.3330312e-08
test Vocals        max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.2582339e-08
test Instrumental  max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.3130378e-08
```

## Inference Experiment: Fused Frequency Attention60

`CUDASEP_FREQ_ATTENTION60_FUSED=1 CUDASEP_FREQ_ATTENTION60_BF16=1` enables the
fixed `N=60,D=64` frequency-attention fused path. It avoids the separate QK,
softmax, and AV cuBLAS path for frequency attention, but the current tiled kernel
does not use Tensor Core MMA and remains a negative result.

Retested after the default no-cuFFT STFT/iSTFT path and default BF16 linear WMMA
path were enabled:

```text
test_clean.wav, default BF16 linear WMMA:          2439.0 ms, RTF 16.23x
test_clean.wav, + freq attention60 fused BF16:    3630.0 ms, RTF 10.91x
```

Retested again after the time-attention fragprob-regout path was enabled:

```text
test_clean.wav, time fragprob-regout:              2314.8 ms, RTF 17.10x
test_clean.wav, + scalar freq fused BF16:          3492.0 ms, RTF 11.34x
test_clean.wav, + WMMA  freq fused BF16:           2281.9 ms, RTF 17.35x
test.wav,       time fragprob-regout:             13462.4 ms, RTF 23.15x
test.wav,       + WMMA  freq fused BF16:          13269.4 ms, RTF 23.48x
```

Output difference against the time fragprob-regout baseline for
`test_clean.wav` with WMMA frequency fused:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0070934892 rms=0.00050822349 rel_rms=0.0027545827
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0086916611 rms=0.00060956163 rel_rms=0.0025350603
```

For `test.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.011621714 rms=0.00043087953 rel_rms=0.0022802142
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.013534814 rms=0.00046733941 rel_rms=0.0024364369
```

Interpretation:

- This path should remain opt-in. It removes some cuBLAS calls, but is about 49%
  slower end to end in the earlier stack and about 51% slower on top of the
  current time fragprob-regout path when using the old scalar q4 kernel.
- The WMMA q16 frequency fused kernel replaces that scalar path and is now a
  positive no-cuBLAS result. With time fragprob-regout also enabled,
  `test_clean.wav` improves from 2314.8 ms to 2281.9 ms, and `test.wav`
  improves from 13462.4 ms to 13269.4 ms.
- Nsight reports the inference-path `freq_attention60_q16_fragprob_regout_kernel`
  at 48 calls, 70.7 ms total, or 1.47 ms/call. The previous separated frequency
  QK/softmax/AV target was about 103 ms total over 48 calls, or 2.16 ms/call.

Full-tile specialization update after the time-attention full-tile port:

```text
bench, --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate:
  validate q16_fragprob_regout vs scalar_ref_bh0:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q16_fragprob_fulltile vs scalar_ref_bh0:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q16k32_fragprob_regout vs scalar_ref_bh0:
    bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898
  freq_q16_fragprob_regout   best=1.465 ms median=1.466 ms
  freq_q16_fragprob_fulltile best=1.427 ms median=1.431 ms
  freq_q16k32_fragprob_regout best=1.909 ms median=1.913 ms

inference after port:
  test_clean.wav: 1777.2 ms
  test.wav:       10570.4 ms

Nsight test_clean.wav:
  before fulltile: freq_attention60_q16_fragprob_regout_kernel 48 calls, 56.733 ms
  after fulltile:  freq_attention60_q16_fragprob_regout_kernel 48 calls, 55.332 ms

resource usage after fulltile:
  REG=40 SHARED=6848 LOCAL=0
```

Output difference versus the time-attention full-tile default output stays at
numerical-noise level:

```text
clean Vocals       max_abs=1.7881393e-07 rel_rms=5.2625139e-08
clean Instrumental max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.3232703e-08
test Vocals        max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.2535037e-08
test Instrumental  max_abs=2.3841858e-07 rel_rms=5.3082637e-08
```

## Microbenchmark: WMMA BF16 Frequency Attention60

`bf16_freq_attention_fused.cu` is the Tensor Core replacement probe for the
fixed frequency-attention shape observed in the current profile:

```text
BH=10408, N=60, D=64
```

The first variant, `freq_q16_fragprob_regout`, uses one CTA per `16x64` output
tile, four warps per CTA, BF16 WMMA for QK and AV, and the same A10G
accumulator-fragment probability path as the optimized time-attention kernel.
It avoids cuBLAS/cuFFT and writes BF16 output directly.

Build and run:

```bash
ninja -C build-bench bench_bf16_freq_attention_fused -j8
./build-bench/bench_bf16_freq_attention_fused --warmup 1 --iters 4 --validate
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
validate q16_fragprob_regout vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q16_fragprob_fulltile vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q16_fragprob_lfp32_fulltile vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898
validate q32_fragprob_fulltile vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q32_fragprob_lfp32_fulltile vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898
validate q32_lfp32_direct vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898
validate q64_lfp32_direct vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898
validate q16k32_fragprob_regout vs scalar_ref_bh0: bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898
freq_q16_fragprob_regout best=1.466 ms median=1.466 ms math=6.54 TF/s
freq_q16_fragprob_fulltile best=1.427 ms median=1.431 ms math=6.72 TF/s
freq_q16_lfp32_fulltile best=1.424 ms median=1.428 ms math=6.74 TF/s
freq_q32_fragprob_fulltile best=1.071 ms median=1.071 ms math=8.96 TF/s
freq_q32_lfp32_fulltile best=1.068 ms median=1.069 ms math=8.98 TF/s
freq_q32_lfp32_direct best=1.043 ms median=1.044 ms math=9.19 TF/s
freq_q64_lfp32_direct best=1.109 ms median=1.115 ms math=8.65 TF/s
freq_q16k32_fragprob_regout best=1.909 ms median=1.913 ms math=5.02 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
freq_attention60_q16_fragprob_regout_kernel: REG=40 SHARED=6848 LOCAL=0
bench q16/fp32-row_l fulltile: REG=40 SHARED=10944 LOCAL=0
bench q32/fp32-row_l fulltile: REG=40 SHARED=17792 LOCAL=0
inference q32/fp32-row_l direct-store: REG=40 SHARED=9600 LOCAL=0
bench q64/fp32-row_l direct-store: REG=40 SHARED=15104 LOCAL=0
bench q16/k32 probe: REG=40 SHARED=13568 LOCAL=0
```

Interpretation:

- This is the first positive no-cuBLAS frequency-attention result. The
  separated frequency QK + softmax + AV target is about 2.16 ms/call in the
  current profile, while the q16 Tensor Core fused probe is about 1.47 ms/call.
- The output is bit-identical to a scalar online-softmax BF16-probability
  reference for BH=0, including the `N=60` tail handling.
- Splitting fixed `N=60` into three complete K tiles plus one tail tile trims
  the microbenchmark from about 1.47 ms to about 1.43 ms and reduces the
  inference profile from 56.7 ms to 55.3 ms over 48 calls.
- Expanding K tile from 16 to 32 is negative for this frequency shape. It
  reduces the K loop from four tiles to two, but increases shared-memory
  staging, changes the online-softmax accumulation path, loses bit-identical
  output, and slows the benchmark to about 1.91 ms.
- Updating `row_l` from the FP32 probability before BF16 rounding is a very
  small positive result for the q16 fulltile frequency kernel. The benchmark
  moves from `1.429 ms` to `1.424 ms` in the same warmup=3/iters=10 run with
  unchanged `REG=40` and static shared memory. Like the q16/k32 experiment, it
  changes the BH=0 attention output by about `0.00117 rel_rms`, because the AV
  numerator still uses BF16 probabilities while the denominator uses unrounded
  probabilities. This was ported into the inference-path frequency fused kernel.

  End-to-end validation after the inference port, on top of the time-attention
  FP32-probability denominator experiment:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav: 1722.5 ms
  test.wav:      10262.0 ms

  diff vs outputs/time_q64_lfp32_clean:
    Vocals       max_abs=0.007982760668 rel_rms=0.003683980454
    Instrumental max_abs=0.009689830244 rel_rms=0.003541009876

  diff vs outputs/time_q64_lfp32_test:
    Vocals       max_abs=0.01309668832 rel_rms=0.003234638129
    Instrumental max_abs=0.01177228987 rel_rms=0.004035864036
  ```
- Expanding the query tile from `q16/k16` to `q32/k16` is a positive A10G
  result for this fixed `N=60` frequency shape. It halves query CTAs per
  `(batch,head)` from four to two while keeping the same K tile and
  fragment-probability path. The benchmark q32 lfp32 variant is bitwise aligned
  with q16 lfp32 against the scalar BF16 reference drift
  (`rel_rms=0.0011726898`) and improves from `1.425 ms` to `1.068 ms`.
  The direct-store variant used by inference is faster still at `1.043 ms`,
  with `REG=40, SHARED=9600` instead of the benchmark shared-output `17792 B`.

  End-to-end validation after the inference port:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav: 1717.3 ms
  test.wav:      10253.8 ms

  Nsight test_clean.wav:
    freq_attention60_q32_fragprob_regout_kernel: 48 calls, 50.626 ms
    previous q16 lfp32 profile:                  48 calls, 55.018 ms

  diff vs previous q16-lfp32 outputs:
    clean Vocals       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.280249133e-08
    clean Instrumental max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.361318131e-08
    test Vocals        max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.249615485e-08
    test Instrumental  max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.324030764e-08
  ```
- A q48 direct-store follow-up is a negative result. It keeps the same lfp32
  denominator/direct-output path as q32/q64 and validates with the same scalar
  BF16-reference drift, but it still needs two query CTAs for `N=60` while
  raising each CTA from 256 to 384 threads. The second CTA only covers 12 query
  rows, so the extra row groups are mostly wasted:

  ```text
  validate q48_lfp32_direct vs scalar_ref_bh0:
    bit_mismatch=150 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011726898

  freq_q32_lfp32_direct best=1.042 ms median=1.043 ms math=9.21 TF/s
  freq_q48_lfp32_direct best=1.424 ms median=1.425 ms math=6.74 TF/s
  freq_q64_lfp32_direct best=1.104 ms median=1.106 ms math=8.69 TF/s
  ```

  Resource usage confirms q48 is not register-bound but pays extra shared
  memory and underfilled-CTA cost:

  ```text
  q32 direct: REG=40 SHARED=9600  LOCAL=0
  q48 direct: REG=40 SHARED=12352 LOCAL=0
  q64 direct: REG=40 SHARED=15104 LOCAL=0
  ```

  q48 stays benchmark-only; the current q32 direct path remains the frequency
  attention default.
- Expanding further to `q64/k16` is negative. It covers all 60 query rows with
  one CTA per `(batch,head)`, but raises the direct-store shared footprint to
  `15104 B` and doubles the block thread count to 512. The benchmark slows from
  q32 direct `1.043 ms` to q64 direct `1.109 ms`, so q64 stays benchmark-only.
- This q32 Tensor Core frequency kernel has been ported into the inference-path
  `CUDASEP_FREQ_ATTENTION60_FUSED` implementation for BF16 input and BF16
  output. Non-BF16/f32-output fallbacks still use the old scalar q4 path.

## Microbenchmark: Row-Wise Fused Time Attention

`bf16_time_attention_fused.cu` is a deliberately simple no-cuBLAS/no-cuFFT
baseline for fixed time attention:

```text
BH=480, N=1301, D=64
q[BH,N,D], k[BH,N,D], v[BH,N,D] -> out[BH,N,D]
```

It computes one query row per CTA, keeps the `N=1301` scores in shared memory,
performs softmax, and immediately applies V. This removes the global-memory
round trip for scores/probabilities, but it rereads K/V for every query row and
uses scalar FFMA rather than Tensor Core MMA.

Build and run:

```bash
ninja -C build-bench bench_bf16_time_attention_fused -j8
./build-bench/bench_bf16_time_attention_fused --warmup 1 --iters 3
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
best=419.849 ms median=419.960 ms math=0.50 TF/s
```

Interpretation:

- Avoiding the score/probability global write is not enough by itself.
- A row-wise scalar streaming implementation is roughly two orders of magnitude
  below the A10G dense BF16 roofline.
- The viable fused-attention path must be block/tile based, reuse K/V across
  multiple query rows, and use BF16 Tensor Core MMA, i.e. a fixed-shape
  FlashAttention-style kernel for `N=1301,D=64`.

## Microbenchmark: Block-Wise Fused Time Attention

`bf16_time_attention_block_fused.cu` is the next fixed-shape fused attention
probe. It processes 16 query rows per CTA for the same time-attention shape:

```text
BH=480, N=1301, D=64
```

For each 16-key tile it computes QK with BF16 WMMA, stores only the local
`16x16` score tile in shared memory, performs online softmax updates, and
accumulates AV into shared output. The benchmark now reports these variants:

- `scalar_av`: QK uses Tensor Core, AV is scalar FFMA over the 16-key tile.
- `mma_av`: `16` query rows per CTA; QK uses Tensor Core, and AV converts the
  local probability tile to BF16 and uses WMMA for `P[16,16] @ V[16,64]`. The
  current version loads the existing output tile into an accumulator fragment,
  applies AV MMA, and writes it back, avoiding an extra add buffer.
- `mma_av_q32`: `32` query rows per CTA with 8 warps; it computes two query row
  groups and reuses each K/V tile across both groups. AV converts the local
  probability tile to BF16 and uses WMMA for `P[16,16] @ V[16,64]`.
- `mma_q32_raw`: profiling-only q32 variant. It keeps the same QK/AV WMMA and
  shared-memory structure, but replaces online softmax with a simple BF16 cast
  of the scaled score tile. This is not numerically valid attention; it isolates
  softmax/rescale overhead.
- `mma_q32_raw_regout`: profiling-only q32 raw variant that keeps each warp's
  output accumulator fragment live in registers across the full K loop. This is
  not numerically valid attention; it isolates the lower bound for the current
  q32/k16 QK plus AV Tensor Core schedule without softmax or per-tile output
  shared-memory traffic.
- `mma_q32_exp1`: q32 variant that computes the probability exp once, stores it
  to BF16 `p_s`, and forms the denominator by summing BF16 probabilities. This
  is an aggressive BF16 softmax experiment.
- `mma_q32_exp_noscale`: profiling-only `mma_q32_exp1` variant that keeps the
  parallel exp, BF16 probability tile, denominator update, and AV MMA, but skips
  the per-tile `out_s *= row_alpha` rescale. This is not numerically valid
  attention; it isolates shared-output rescale cost.
- `mma_q32_exp_fragscale`: q32 variant that uses the A10G WMMA accumulator
  fragment row mapping to multiply `out_frag.x[]` by the per-row online-softmax
  `row_alpha` in registers, replacing the separate shared-memory
  `out_s *= row_alpha` pass.
- `mma_q32_exp_regout`: q32 variant that keeps each warp's output accumulator
  fragment live in registers across the full K loop. It applies online-softmax
  row rescale directly to the accumulator fragment, performs AV MMA in-place,
  and writes the output tile to shared memory only once at the end.
- `mma_q32_exp_fragprob_regout`: q32 regout variant that uses the A10G
  accumulator fragment row/column mapping to compute tile maxima, BF16
  probabilities, and denominator updates directly from the QK accumulator
  fragment. It avoids the `scores_s` shared-memory tile entirely.
- `mma_q32_exp_fragprob_fulltile`: q32 fragprob-regout variant specialized for
  fixed `N=1301`. It runs the 81 complete K tiles without key-tail bounds
  checks and handles the final 5-key tail separately.
- `mma_q64_lfp32_fulltile`: q64 fulltile variant that still stores BF16
  probabilities for AV Tensor Core, but updates the online-softmax denominator
  from the FP32 probability before BF16 rounding. It tests whether removing the
  BF16 round-trip from `row_l` is useful.
- `mma_q64_fullq_runtime` / `mma_q64_fullq_static`: profiling-only q64 probes
  that run only the first 20 complete Q blocks. They isolate whether making the
  complete-Q path compile-time static is worth splitting the tail block.
- `mma_q64_split_fulltail`: profiling-only q64 probe that launches the static
  complete-Q path for the first 1280 query rows, then launches the normal q64
  path once more with an offset for the final 21-row tail.
- `mma_q32_exp_row`: q32 variant where each row thread computes its 16 local
  probability values, writes `p_s`, and updates the denominator in one pass. It
  tests whether removing one `p_s` readback/synchronization is worth reducing
  exp parallelism.
- `mma_q32k32`: `32` query rows and `32` key rows per CTA step. It halves the
  number of online softmax/rescale iterations, but doubles the local score/K/V
  tile footprint.
- `mma_q32k32_regout`: q32/k32 plus register-resident output accumulator. It
  tests whether the k32 iteration-count reduction becomes useful after removing
  per-tile `out_s` shared-memory load/store.
- `mma_av_q64`: `64` query rows per CTA with 16 warps; it computes four query
  row groups and reuses each K/V tile across all four groups. This variant loads
  each output tile from shared memory into an accumulator fragment, applies the
  AV MMA, and stores it back, avoiding an extra `add_s` shared buffer.

Build and run:

```bash
ninja -C build-bench bench_bf16_time_attention_block_fused -j8
./build-bench/bench_bf16_time_attention_block_fused --warmup 1 --iters 3
./build-bench/bench_bf16_time_attention_block_fused --warmup 1 --iters 1 --validate
ninja -C build-bench bench_wmma_fragment_map -j8
./build-bench/bench_wmma_fragment_map
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
scalar_av      best=107.017 ms median=107.119 ms math=1.94 TF/s
mma_av         best=36.508 ms  median=36.520 ms  math=5.70 TF/s
mma_av_q32     best=31.983 ms  median=31.984 ms  math=6.50 TF/s
mma_q32_raw    best=20.469 ms  median=20.473 ms  math=10.16 TF/s
mma_q32_raw_regout best=15.668 ms median=15.671 ms math=13.27 TF/s
mma_q32_exp1   best=29.824 ms  median=29.826 ms  math=6.97 TF/s
mma_q32_exp_noscale best=26.110 ms median=26.118 ms math=7.97 TF/s
mma_q32_exp_fragscale best=26.525 ms median=26.528 ms math=7.84 TF/s
mma_q32_exp_regout best=21.521 ms median=21.530 ms math=9.66 TF/s
mma_q32_exp_fragprob_regout best=16.010 ms median=16.012 ms math=12.99 TF/s
mma_q16_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=22.096 ms median=22.117 ms math=9.41 TF/s
mma_q32_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=15.784 ms median=15.794 ms math=13.18 TF/s
mma_q48_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=16.207 ms median=16.218 ms math=12.83 TF/s
mma_q64_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=15.020 ms median=15.023 ms math=13.85 TF/s
mma_q80_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=16.466 ms median=16.472 ms math=12.63 TF/s
mma_q96_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=15.910 ms median=15.918 ms math=13.07 TF/s
mma_q32_exp_row best=33.095 ms median=33.101 ms math=6.28 TF/s
mma_q32k32     best=35.623 ms  median=35.628 ms  math=5.84 TF/s
mma_q32k32_regout best=31.166 ms median=31.191 ms math=6.67 TF/s
mma_av_q64     best=32.380 ms  median=32.384 ms  math=6.42 TF/s
```

Follow-up q64 full-Q branch split probe, measured with
`./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_block_fused --warmup 2 --iters 8`:

```text
mma_q64_exp_fragprob_fulltile best=14.764 ms median=14.766 ms math=14.09 TF/s
mma_q64_lfp32_fulltile        best=14.707 ms median=14.713 ms math=14.14 TF/s
mma_q64_lfp32_exp2_fulltile   best=15.278 ms median=15.286 ms math=13.61 TF/s
mma_q64_lfp32_inv_fulltile    best=14.714 ms median=14.719 ms math=14.14 TF/s
mma_q64_lfp32_regrow_fulltile best=16.731 ms median=16.738 ms math=12.43 TF/s
mma_q64_fullq_runtime         best=14.049 ms median=14.053 ms math=14.57 TF/s
mma_q64_fullq_static          best=14.101 ms median=14.104 ms math=14.51 TF/s
mma_q64_split_fulltail        best=14.872 ms median=14.875 ms math=13.98 TF/s
```

The A10G accumulator-fragment probe reports
`num_elements=8` for `wmma::fragment<accumulator,16,16,16,float>`. For this
SM86 WMMA layout, the local row and column for each fragment element are:

```text
row = lane / 4 + ((element & 2) ? 8 : 0)
col = (lane & 3) * 2 + (element & 1) + ((element & 4) ? 8 : 0)
```

`bench_wmma_fragment_map` now validates both formulas against
`wmma::store_matrix_sync` output on the current A10G. This mapping is used only
for the A10G fixed-shape experiment. The `mma_q32_exp_fragprob_regout` variant
uses both formulas to compute tile maxima/probabilities directly from QK
accumulator fragments instead of first storing the full `scores_s`
shared-memory tile.

`--validate` compares the full BF16 output tensor from `mma_q32_exp_fragscale`
against the original `mma_q32_exp1` path:

```text
validate q32_exp_fragscale vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q32_exp_regout vs q32_exp1:    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q32_exp_fragprob_regout vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q16_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q32_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q48_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q64_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q64_exp_fragprob_lfp32_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=1615350 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011051061
validate q64_exp_fragprob_lfp32_exp2_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=1615350 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011051061
validate q64_exp_fragprob_lfp32_inv_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=1615350 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011051061
validate q64_exp_fragprob_lfp32_regrow_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=1615350 max_abs=0.001953125 rel_rms=0.0011051061
validate q80_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
validate q96_exp_fragprob_fulltile vs q32_exp1: bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
kernel       threads  registers/thread  static shared  rough active blocks/SM limit
scalar_av    128      40                9472 B         10 by shared, 12 by threads/registers
mma_av_q16   128      40                12032 B        8 by shared, 12 by threads/registers
mma_av_q32   256      40                19968 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32_raw  256      38                19456 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32_raw_regout
              256      40                19456 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp1 256      40                19968 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp_noscale
              256      40                19968 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp_fragscale
              256      39                19968 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp_regout
              256      46                19968 B        5 by shared, 5 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp_fragprob_regout
              256      40                17792 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q16_exp_fragprob_fulltile
              128      40                10944 B        9 by shared, 12 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp_fragprob_fulltile
              256      40                17792 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q48_exp_fragprob_fulltile
              384      40                24640 B        4 by shared, 4 by threads/registers
mma_q64_exp_fragprob_fulltile
              512      40                31488 B        3 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
mma_q64_lfp32_fulltile
              512      40                31488 B        3 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
mma_q64_lfp32_exp2_fulltile
              512      40                31488 B        3 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
mma_q64_lfp32_inv_fulltile
              512      40                31744 B        3 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
mma_q64_lfp32_regrow_fulltile
              512      48                31232 B        3 by shared, 2 by threads/registers
mma_q64_fullq_static
              512      40                31488 B        3 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
mma_q80_exp_fragprob_fulltile
              640      40                38336 B        2 by shared, 2 by threads/registers
mma_q96_exp_fragprob_fulltile
              768      40                45184 B        2 by shared, 2 by threads/registers
mma_q32_exp_row
              256      40                19968 B        5 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32k32   256      39                27136 B        3 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q32k32_regout
              256      40                27136 B        3 by shared, 6 by threads/registers
mma_q64k32_regout
              512      40                46080 B        2 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
mma_av_q64   512      40                35840 B        2 by shared, 3 by threads/registers
```

A10G device limits used for the rough occupancy read:

```text
maxThreadsPerSM=1536, maxBlocksPerSM=16, sharedPerSM=102400 B,
sharedPerBlock=49152 B, regsPerSM=65536
```

SASS contains `HMMA.16816.F32.BF16`, but also scalar `FFMA` instructions from
softmax, rescaling, and remaining elementwise work.

Interpretation:

- Moving QK from scalar FFMA to Tensor Core improves the row-wise fused baseline
  by about 3.9x, from 419.8 ms to 107.1 ms.
- Moving AV to Tensor Core and eliminating the extra add buffer improves the
  block-wise fused prototype from 107.0 ms to 36.5 ms.
- Expanding the query tile to 32 rows improves it again to 32.0 ms, confirming
  K/V tile reuse across more query rows matters.
- The q32 raw probe runs in 20.5 ms with nearly the same resource footprint as
  q32. Keeping raw output fragments in registers drops the lower bound to
  15.7 ms, which is below the roughly 18.0 ms/call default separated QK +
  softmax + AV replacement target. The current q32/k16 QK/AV Tensor Core
  schedule can be fast enough if the valid softmax path is made cheaper.
- Computing each exp only once and summing the BF16 probability tile improves
  q32 from 32.0 ms to 29.8 ms without changing register/shared usage. Duplicate
  exp was measurable, but most remaining overhead is synchronization, row-state
  update, and shared-memory traffic around online normalization.
- Skipping the per-tile shared-output rescale improves q32 exp1 from 29.8 ms to
  26.1 ms with identical register/shared usage. This invalid variant explains
  about 3.7 ms, or about 40% of the remaining 9.3 ms gap between q32 exp1 and
  q32 raw. Register-resident output accumulation or a cheaper row-wise rescale
  mechanism is therefore a first-order fused-attention problem.
- Moving the row-wise rescale into the WMMA accumulator fragment keeps the same
  full BF16 output tensor as q32 exp1 and improves the valid q32 path from
  29.8 ms to 26.5 ms. It recovers most of the invalid `noscale` gain without
  increasing shared memory or register pressure. This validates A10G-specific
  fragment-register rescale as the next fused-attention direction.
- Keeping the output accumulator fragment live in registers across the K loop
  improves the valid q32 path again, from 26.5 ms to 21.5 ms, while staying
  bit-identical to q32 exp1. This removes the per-tile shared-memory
  load/store of `out_s`; the remaining gap to the 15.7 ms raw-regout lower
  bound is about 5.9 ms and is mostly softmax/row-state work rather than output
  accumulation traffic.
- Computing tile maxima, BF16 probabilities, and denominator updates directly
  from the QK accumulator fragment removes the `scores_s` shared-memory tile and
  reduces the valid q32 regout path from 21.5 ms to 16.0 ms. The output remains
  bit-identical to q32 exp1, register use drops from 46 to 40 per thread, and
  static shared memory drops from 19.5 KiB to 17.4 KiB. This is the first valid
  fused time-attention prototype below the roughly 18.0 ms/call replacement
  target for the current cuBLAS-separated path.
- Splitting fixed `N=1301` into 81 complete K tiles plus one tail tile trims
  the fragprob-regout microbenchmark from about 16.0 ms to about 15.8 ms without
  changing output or resource usage. This improvement was ported into the
  inference kernel.
- Shrinking the same valid fulltile path from `q32/k16` to `q16/k16` is a
  negative A10G result. It is bit-identical and uses less shared memory
  (`10.7 KiB`, rough active blocks/SM about 9), but it doubles the number of
  query CTAs and loses K/V reuse across 32 query rows. Runtime regresses from
  about 15.79 ms to 22.10 ms, so q16 was not ported into inference.
- Expanding the same valid fulltile path from `q32/k16` to `q48/k16` is a
  negative A10G result. It is bit-identical, but slows from about 15.79 ms to
  16.21 ms. Register use stays at 40 per thread, while static shared memory
  rises from 17.8 KiB to 24.6 KiB and rough active blocks/SM falls from about 5
  to 4. The reduced block count does not pay for the lower residency and larger
  shared-memory footprint, so q48 was not ported into inference.
- Expanding the same fulltile path to `q64/k16` is a positive A10G result. The
  microbenchmark remains bit-identical, increases static shared memory to
  31.5 KiB and rough active blocks/SM falls to about 3, but the smaller CTA
  count and better K/V reuse win: best time improves from about 15.79 ms to
  15.02 ms. The q64 inference port moves the 48-call time-attention Nsight
  total from 744.417 ms to 732.245 ms and keeps final audio at BF16 write-noise
  scale.
- Splitting q64 complete-Q blocks from the tail block is a negative result.
  Running only the first 1280 query rows showed no stable benefit from making
  the Q-bound path compile-time static (`14.101 ms` static versus `14.049 ms`
  runtime on the current run, normalized to 1280 rows). The complete two-launch
  split path is slower than the single q64 fulltile kernel (`14.872 ms` versus
  `14.764 ms` in the same warmup=2/iters=8 run), so the tail split should not be
  ported into inference.
- Updating `row_l` from the FP32 probability before BF16 rounding is a very
  small positive result for q64. The benchmark moves from `14.764 ms` to
  `14.707 ms` in the same warmup=2/iters=8 run with unchanged `REG=40` and
  static shared memory. The attention output changes by about `0.0011 rel_rms`
  versus q32_exp1, because the numerator still uses BF16 probabilities while
  the denominator uses unrounded probabilities. This was ported into
  `time_attention1301_q64_fragscale_kernel` for the current experimental BF16
  inference path.

  End-to-end validation after the inference port:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav: 1730.0 ms
  test.wav:      10288.5 ms

  diff vs outputs/time_q64_clean:
    Vocals       max_abs=0.01468872931 rel_rms=0.00459206362
    Instrumental max_abs=0.01337582245 rel_rms=0.004151478687

  diff vs outputs/time_q64_test:
    Vocals       max_abs=0.02045577765 rel_rms=0.003963085513
    Instrumental max_abs=0.01782265306 rel_rms=0.00471115488
  ```
- Changing q64 AV column ownership from four `16x16` output warps per row-group
  to fewer, wider output warps is a positive A10G result. The
  `mma_q64_c2_lfp32` and `mma_q64_c4_lfp32` variants keep the same BF16
  probability numerator and FP32-probability denominator as the current q64
  lfp32 path. `c2` launches 8 warps and each active output warp holds two
  accumulator fragments; `c4` launches 4 warps and each active output warp holds
  four accumulator fragments. The benchmark moves from `14.704 ms` to
  `12.988 ms` in the same warmup=2/iters=8 run:

  ```text
  mma_q64_lfp32_fulltile best=14.704 ms median=14.714 ms math=14.15 TF/s
  mma_q64_c2_lfp32       best=13.379 ms median=13.383 ms math=15.55 TF/s
  mma_q64_c4_lfp32       best=12.988 ms median=12.996 ms math=16.01 TF/s
  ```

  Validation:

  ```text
  validate q64_c2_lfp32_fulltile vs q64_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile vs q64_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  ```

  Resource usage in `cudasep_infer`:

  ```text
  ColFrags=1: REG=40 SHARED=15104 LOCAL=0, 512 threads
  ColFrags=2: REG=56 SHARED=15104 LOCAL=0, 256 threads
  ColFrags=4 old: REG=71 SHARED=15104 LOCAL=0, 128 threads
  ColFrags=4 regrow-warp-sync: REG=74 SHARED=14336 LOCAL=0, 128 threads
  ```

  `ColFrags=4` was first ported into `time_attention1301_q64_fragscale_kernel<4>`.
  The current default is the later
  `time_attention1301_q64_c4_k32_regrow_warpsync_kernel`. Use
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4_K32=1` to fall back to the K16
  regrow-warp-sync c4 kernel, `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4_REGROW_WS=1`
  to fall back to the old c4 template, `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4=1`
  to fall back to c2, and `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C2=1` to fall
  back to c1.
  End-to-end validation for the first c4 port:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav default c4: 1596.3 ms
  test_clean.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4=1: 1619.1 ms
  test_clean.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C2=1: 1696.4 ms
  test.wav default c4: 9556.6 ms

  diff vs c2 on test_clean.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.253749902e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.335501707e-08

  Nsight test_clean.wav:
    time_attention1301_q64_fragscale_kernel<4>: 48 calls, 632.234 ms
    previous ColFrags=2 profile:               48 calls, 651.093 ms
    previous ColFrags=1 profile:               48 calls, ~734 ms
  ```
- A `q64_c4` register-row-state plus warp-sync specialization is a positive
  A10G result and is now the default c4 inference path. In `ColFrags=4`, each
  row group is owned by exactly one warp, so `row_m`, `row_l`, and `row_alpha`
  do not need CTA-wide shared-memory state. The specialization keeps each
  warp's online-softmax row state in registers, uses `__syncwarp()` after
  writing that warp's BF16 probability tile, and keeps the CTA-wide syncs only
  around the shared K/V tile reuse. It is bit-identical to the old c4 path in
  benchmark validation:

  ```text
  validate q64_c4_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  ```

  Stable benchmark with `--warmup 2 --iters 10`:

  ```text
  mma_q64_c4_lfp32    best=12.983 ms median=12.996 ms math=16.02 TF/s
  mma_q64_c4_regrow_ws best=12.569 ms median=12.576 ms math=16.55 TF/s
  ```

  End-to-end A/B on `test_clean.wav`:

  ```text
  default regrow-warp-sync: 1581.9 ms
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4_REGROW_WS=1: 1595.6 ms

  diff vs old c4:
    Vocals       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.231385172e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.307887654e-08
  ```

  `test.wav` default regrow-warp-sync validation:

  ```text
  test.wav: 9379.7 ms
  ```
- Expanding the specialized c4 path from KTile=16 to KTile=32 is also a speed
  positive and is now the default c4 inference path. The kernel computes two
  `16x16` QK score fragments per row warp, stores a `16x32` BF16 probability
  tile, and performs two AV MMA groups before the next online-softmax update.
  This halves the number of K-loop CTA-wide K/V reload syncs, but increases
  shared memory from `14336` to `20480` bytes:

  ```text
  K16 regrow-warp-sync: REG=74 SHARED=14336 LOCAL=0
  K32 regrow-warp-sync: REG=74 SHARED=20480 LOCAL=0
  ```

  Benchmark validation against old c4 on the synthetic benchmark input remains
  bit-identical:

  ```text
  validate q64_c4_k32_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  ```

  Stable benchmark with `--warmup 2 --iters 10`:

  ```text
  mma_q64_c4_regrow_ws     best=12.561 ms median=12.576 ms math=16.56 TF/s
  mma_q64_c4_k32_regrow_ws best=12.315 ms median=12.350 ms math=16.89 TF/s
  ```

  Real inference is not bit-identical to K16 because the online-softmax row
  state is updated once per 32 keys instead of once per 16 keys. This changes
  FP32 softmax grouping while keeping the same BF16 probability/AV path. A/B
  results:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav default K32: 1568.2 ms
  test_clean.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4_K32=1: 1581.2 ms

  diff vs K16 regrow-warp-sync on test_clean.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=8.995652199e-03 rel_rms=3.886989913e-03
    Instrumental max_abs=9.351566434e-03 rel_rms=3.931924538e-03

  test.wav default K32: 9321.1 ms

  diff vs K16 regrow-warp-sync on test.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=2.129352093e-02 rel_rms=3.828245414e-03
    Instrumental max_abs=1.967543364e-02 rel_rms=4.607006828e-03
  ```
- Expanding the specialized c4 K32 path from q64 to larger query blocks is a
  positive result, with q112 now the default inference path. The specialization
  keeps one row-group warp per 16 query rows. This keeps register use flat while
  reducing CTA count and testing how much extra shared memory A10G can tolerate:

  ```text
  q64 c4 K32: REG=74 SHARED=20480 LOCAL=0
  q80 c4 K32: REG=74 SHARED=23552 LOCAL=0
  q96 c4 K32: REG=74 SHARED=26624 LOCAL=0
  q112 c4 K32: REG=74 SHARED=29696 LOCAL=0
  q128 c4 K32: REG=74 SHARED=32768 LOCAL=0
  ```

  Benchmark validation remains bit-identical against the q64 c4 fulltile
  baseline:

  ```text
  validate q80_c4_k32_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q96_c4_k32_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q128_c4_k32_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  ```

  Stable benchmark with `--warmup 2 --iters 5`:

  ```text
  mma_q64_c4_k32_regrow_ws  best=12.336 ms median=12.348 ms math=16.86 TF/s
  mma_q80_c4_k32_regrow_ws  best=11.241 ms median=11.252 ms math=18.50 TF/s
  mma_q96_c4_k32_regrow_ws  best=11.280 ms median=11.287 ms math=18.44 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_regrow_ws best=10.849 ms median=10.855 ms math=19.17 TF/s
  mma_q128_c4_k32_regrow_ws best=10.881 ms median=10.893 ms math=19.12 TF/s
  ```

  End-to-end same-build A/B:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav default q112: 1496.7 ms
  test_clean.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1: 1515.5 ms

  diff vs q80 K32 on test_clean.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.218887753e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.980232239e-07 rel_rms=5.303925256e-08

  test.wav default q112: 9013.5 ms
  test.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1: 9064.4 ms

  diff vs q80 K32 on test.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.253098512e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.980232239e-07 rel_rms=5.316174637e-08
  ```

  The fallback chain is q112 PStride40 -> q80 -> q64 K32 -> q64 K16. Use
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1` to fall back to q80, and
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q64_C4_K32=1` to disable the K32 path entirely.
- Splitting q112 c4 K32 into a static-full-q kernel for the first 11 full
  query blocks and a runtime-bounds tail kernel is a negative result. The split
  output remains bit-identical, but the static full-q instance raises register
  use from `REG=74` to `REG=75`, and even the full-block-only comparison slows:

  ```text
  validate q112_c4_k32_split_fulltail vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

  q112 c4 K32 runtime: REG=74 SHARED=29696 LOCAL=0
  q112 c4 K32 static full-q: REG=75 SHARED=29696 LOCAL=0

  mma_q112_c4_k32_regrow_ws  best=10.821 ms median=10.870 ms math=19.22 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_fullq_runtime  best=9.905 ms median=9.932 ms math=19.89 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_fullq_static   best=9.970 ms median=10.021 ms math=19.75 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_split_fulltail best=11.020 ms median=11.040 ms math=18.87 TF/s
  ```

  The extra launch plus the slightly higher static-full-q register pressure
  outweigh removing the full-block bounds checks, so this stays benchmark-only
  and the inference path remains a single q112 kernel.
- Removing the q112 c4 K32 `__syncwarp()` between writing the per-warp BF16
  probability tile and loading it for AV is not useful. The no-syncwarp variant
  remains bit-identical on the benchmark input and has the same resource usage,
  but stable timing is at best noise-level and the median is slower:

  ```text
  validate q112_c4_k32_no_syncwarp vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

  q112 c4 K32 syncwarp:    REG=74 SHARED=29696 LOCAL=0
  q112 c4 K32 no-syncwarp: REG=74 SHARED=29696 LOCAL=0

  mma_q112_c4_k32_regrow_ws  best=10.813 ms median=10.825 ms math=19.23 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_no_syncwarp best=10.815 ms median=10.850 ms math=19.23 TF/s
  ```

  Since it provides no clear speedup and weakens the warp-level shared-memory
  ordering guarantee, the inference path keeps the explicit `__syncwarp()`.
- Padding the q112 c4 K32 BF16 probability shared-memory tile from stride 32 to
  stride 40 is a positive intermediate result. The padding targets WMMA
  shared-memory load conflicts for the `P @ V` step:

  ```text
  validate q112_c4_k32_pstride40 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_pstride48 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_pstride64 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

  q112 PStride=32: REG=74 SHARED=29696 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40: REG=74 SHARED=31488 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=48: REG=74 SHARED=33280 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=64: REG=74 SHARED=36864 LOCAL=0

  mma_q112_c4_k32_regrow_ws best=10.832 ms median=10.839 ms math=19.20 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_pstride40 best=10.313 ms median=10.328 ms math=20.17 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_pstride48 best=12.155 ms median=12.206 ms math=17.11 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_pstride64 best=13.092 ms median=13.109 ms math=15.89 TF/s
  ```

  The first source port instantiated q112 as `QBlock=112, PStride=40,
  VStride=64`; q80 and q64 fallback instances still used stride 32. This was
  then superseded by the VStride=72 result below.

  End-to-end same-build A/B against the q80 fallback:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav default q112 PStride=40: 1472.5 ms
  test_clean.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1: 1515.4 ms

  diff vs q80 K32 on test_clean.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.230450888e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.343710832e-08

  test.wav default q112 PStride=40: 8784.3 ms
  test.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1: 9088.5 ms

  diff vs q80 K32 on test.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.253887875e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.332054079e-08
  ```

  PStride=48 and PStride=64 are negative despite identical benchmark output:
  the extra shared-memory footprint lowers the effective schedule quality more
  than any bank-conflict reduction helps.
- Padding the q112 c4 K32 BF16 V shared-memory tile while keeping `PStride=40`
  is also a positive result and is now the default inference q112 path. This
  targets the row-major WMMA load of `V` in the `P @ V` step. VStride=72 wins;
  VStride=80 is close but slower, VStride=88 is noise-level tied but uses more
  shared memory, and VStride=96/104 are negative:

  ```text
  validate q112_c4_k32_p40_v72 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_p40_v80 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_p40_v88 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_p40_v96 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_p40_v104 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_p32_v72 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q112_c4_k32_p48_v72 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

  q112 PStride=32 VStride=72: REG=74 SHARED=30208 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40 VStride=64: REG=74 SHARED=31488 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40 VStride=72: REG=74 SHARED=32000 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40 VStride=80: REG=74 SHARED=32512 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40 VStride=88: REG=74 SHARED=33024 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40 VStride=96: REG=74 SHARED=33536 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=40 VStride=104: REG=74 SHARED=34048 LOCAL=0
  q112 PStride=48 VStride=72: REG=74 SHARED=33792 LOCAL=0

  mma_q112_c4_k32_pstride40 best=10.328 ms median=10.341 ms math=20.14 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v72  best=8.628 ms median=8.634 ms math=24.11 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v80  best=8.718 ms median=8.728 ms math=23.86 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v88  best=8.623 ms median=8.638 ms math=24.12 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v96  best=10.965 ms median=10.979 ms math=18.97 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v104 best=10.703 ms median=10.735 ms math=19.43 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p32_v72  best=8.840 ms median=8.844 ms math=23.53 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p48_v72  best=10.744 ms median=10.784 ms math=19.36 TF/s
  ```

  A 10-iteration follow-up shows V88 is too close to V72 to justify the extra
  shared memory: V72 has the better best time (`8.619 ms` vs `8.623 ms`), while
  V88 has only a `0.001 ms` median edge (`8.638 ms` vs `8.639 ms`). The default
  therefore stays at VStride=72.

  A follow-up P/V stride interaction sweep shows PStride=40 is still the best
  probability-tile stride after VStride=72 is added. PStride=32 saves shared
  memory but loses about 2.4%, while PStride=48 is dominated by the larger
  probability tile.

  A q128 follow-up with the same P/V padding remains negative. Padding improves
  q128 versus its unpadded form, but the larger query block still loses to q112
  P40/V72:

  ```text
  validate q128_c4_k32_p40_v72 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q128_c4_k32_p40_v80 vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

  q128 PStride=32 VStride=64: REG=74 SHARED=32768 LOCAL=0
  q128 PStride=40 VStride=72: REG=74 SHARED=35328 LOCAL=0
  q128 PStride=40 VStride=80: REG=74 SHARED=35840 LOCAL=0

  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v72 best=8.629 ms median=8.643 ms math=24.10 TF/s
  mma_q128_c4_k32_regrow_ws best=10.877 ms median=10.886 ms math=19.12 TF/s
  mma_q128_c4_k32_p40_v72 best=9.995 ms median=10.017 ms math=20.81 TF/s
  mma_q128_c4_k32_p40_v80 best=10.035 ms median=10.053 ms math=20.73 TF/s
  ```

  The current source port instantiates q112 as `QBlock=112, PStride=40,
  VStride=72`; q80 and q64 fallback instances still use stride 32/64. Current
  `cudasep_infer` resource usage confirms `REG=74 SHARED=32000 LOCAL=0` for the
  q112 P40/V72 kernel.

  End-to-end same-build A/B against the q80 fallback:

  ```text
  test_clean.wav default q112 P40/V72: 1391.9 ms
  test_clean.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1: 1515.8 ms

  diff vs q80 K32 on test_clean.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.194562148e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.319217832e-08

  test.wav default q112 P40/V72: 8351.4 ms
  test.wav with CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_Q112_C4_K32=1: 9082.0 ms

  diff vs q80 K32 on test.wav:
    Vocals       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.251446721e-08
    Instrumental max_abs=2.980232239e-07 rel_rms=5.329183474e-08
  ```

  Current profile refresh after the q112 P40/V72 time-attention path and the
  `CtaM=32` long-linear port:

  ```bash
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/current_bf16_reprofile_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

  nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none --stats=false \
    --force-overwrite=true -o outputs/current_bf16_reprofile_nsys \
    ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/current_bf16_reprofile_nsys_out \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

  nsys stats --report cuda_gpu_kern_sum --format csv --force-export=true \
    outputs/current_bf16_reprofile_nsys.nsys-rep
  ```

  ```text
  plain test_clean.wav inference: 1397.5 ms
  Nsight Systems test_clean.wav inference: 1407.7 ms

  time_attention1301 q112 P40/V72:       48 calls, 423.595 ms total
  c32x128 qkv no-bias linear:            96 calls, 195.189 ms total
  c32x256 bias linear group:           1056 calls, 190.158 ms total
  c32x256 bias+GELU ffn1:                96 calls, 130.823 ms total
  split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16:       96 calls,  71.101 ms total
  c64x128 remaining bias linears:       488 calls,  68.959 ms total
  rms_norm_to_bf16:                     528 calls,  58.517 ms total
  c32x256 no-bias attention-out linear:  96 calls,  57.730 ms total
  freq_attention60 q32 fused:            48 calls,  51.282 ms total
  ```

  Interpretation: time attention is still the largest single kernel, while
  linear WMMA remains the largest group. Since the latest linear fragment-layout
  sweep was negative, the next high-impact attention work should target the
  single-kernel q112 schedule rather than another broad linear tile sweep.

  A follow-up complete-Q/static-tail experiment was run on the current
  P40/V72 layout:

  ```text
  mma_q112_c4_k32_p40_v72          best=8.619 ms median=8.648 ms math=24.13 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_fullq_p40v72_runtime best=7.946 ms median=7.957 ms math=24.79 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_fullq_p40v72_static  best=8.003 ms median=8.020 ms math=24.61 TF/s
  mma_q112_c4_p40v72_split_tail    best=8.867 ms median=8.872 ms math=23.46 TF/s
  ```

  The full-Q rows alone are faster because they skip the final partial Q block,
  but the two-launch full+tail path is slower than the current single kernel.
  The static full-Q specialization also increases resource use from
  `REG=74 SHARED=32000 LOCAL=0` to `REG=79 SHARED=32000 LOCAL=0`. Keep this
  split-tail path benchmark-only; the tail branch in the single q112 P40/V72
  kernel is cheaper than an extra launch plus the static specialization cost.
- Expanding the same specialized c4 path further to KTile=48 or KTile=64 is a
  negative result. Both remain bit-identical to old c4 on the synthetic
  benchmark input, but resource pressure rises quickly. K48 uses
  `REG=84 SHARED=26624 LOCAL=0`, while K64 jumps to
  `REG=106 SHARED=32768 LOCAL=0`. The reduced K-loop sync count does not
  compensate for lower residency and larger fragment pressure on A10G:

  ```text
  validate q64_c4_k48_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
  validate q64_c4_k64_regrow_warpsync vs q64_c4_lfp32_fulltile:
    bit_mismatch=0 max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

  mma_q64_c4_k32_regrow_ws best=12.339 ms median=12.345 ms math=16.86 TF/s
  mma_q64_c4_k48_regrow_ws best=14.574 ms median=14.592 ms math=14.27 TF/s
  mma_q64_c4_k64_regrow_ws best=14.988 ms median=15.004 ms math=13.88 TF/s
  ```

  KTile=48 and KTile=64 stay benchmark-only. The current A10G sweet spot for this c4
  register-row-state path is KTile=32.
- A `q64_c4` score-AV lower-bound diagnostic was added to test whether the
  remaining gap is mostly online softmax. It keeps the same 4-warp `q64_c4`
  mapping and the same QK+AV BF16 WMMA count, but replaces the online softmax
  with a BF16 cast of the scaled score before AV. The output is intentionally
  not equivalent (`checksum=154` instead of `0.242188`), so this is not a port
  candidate. Stable run with `--warmup 2 --iters 10`:

  ```text
  mma_q64_c4_lfp32       best=12.983 ms median=12.996 ms math=16.02 TF/s
  mma_q64_c4_score_av_lb best=12.343 ms median=12.361 ms math=16.85 TF/s
  ```

  Resource usage for the lower-bound kernel is `REG=64 SHARED=14336 LOCAL=0`,
  versus `REG=71 SHARED=15104 LOCAL=0` for the valid `q64_c4_lfp32` kernel. The
  lower bound removes only about `0.64 ms` per benchmark launch, or about a
  `4.9%` time reduction. This means the current valid path's gap to the A10G
  dense BF16 roofline is not primarily `exp` or row-state arithmetic; even the
  invalid no-softmax lower bound reaches only about `24%` of the measured
  `69.9 TF/s` Tensor Core ceiling. The next time-attention direction should
  focus on MMA scheduling, CTA/warp mapping, shared-memory traffic, and sync
  structure rather than more scalar softmax micro-optimizations.
- Replacing q64 softmax `__expf(x)` with inline
  `ex2.approx.ftz.f32(x * log2e)` is a negative result. It keeps exactly the
  same benchmark output drift as the FP32-probability denominator variant
  (`rel_rms=0.0011051061`) and the same `REG=40`, `SHARED=31488` resource
  footprint, but slows the stable run from `14.707 ms` to `15.278 ms`. On this
  CUDA 13.3/A10G build, the manual exp2 approximation does not improve the
  softmax path, so it remains benchmark-only.
- Precomputing one reciprocal per row for the final `out_frag / row_l`
  normalization is a negative result. It keeps the same output drift as the
  FP32-probability denominator variant, but adds 256 B of shared memory and
  moves the q64 benchmark from `14.707 ms` to `14.714 ms`. The extra shared
  write/sync does not pay for replacing final per-fragment divides with
  multiplies, so this stays benchmark-only.
- Keeping q64 online-softmax row state in registers is also a negative result.
  It keeps the same output drift as the FP32-probability denominator variant
  (`rel_rms=0.0011051061` versus q32_exp1), but raises register use from
  `REG=40` to `REG=48` and slows the benchmark from `14.707 ms` to
  `16.732 ms` in the same warmup=2/iters=8 run. Trading shared row-state arrays
  for per-thread register state increases pressure and scheduling cost on this
  A10G shape, so it was not ported into inference.
- Expanding K tile from `q64/k16` to `q64/k32` is a negative result in the
  existing q32k32-style regout kernel. It slows to about 28.65 ms, while static
  shared memory rises to 46.1 KiB. Halving the number of K-loop iterations does
  not pay for the larger score/prob staging and lower residency.
- Expanding the generic fulltile path beyond q64 is negative on this A10G shape.
  q80 and q96 are bit-identical, but q80 slows to about 16.47 ms and q96 to
  about 15.91 ms.
  Their shared-memory footprints rise to 38.3 KiB and 45.2 KiB, and active
  blocks/SM falls to about 2, so the extra K/V reuse no longer pays for the
  lower residency. This makes q64 the current fulltile query-block sweet spot.
- Specializing the inference-path time-attention scale for the fixed `D=64`
  shape replaces the runtime scale multiply with the constant `0.125f`. Nsight
  moves the 48-call time-attention total from about 745.7 ms to 745.0 ms with
  unchanged `REG=40, SHARED=9600`. This is a very small positive result, but it
  keeps the fixed-shape assumption explicit and guarded by the host scale check.
- Replacing `__expf(x)` with `exp2f(x * log2(e))` in the inference-path
  time-attention online softmax was tested and removed. On the same current
  binary, the 48-call time-attention total moved from 744.846 ms to 744.879 ms
  with unchanged `REG=40, SHARED=9600`; output drift versus default was about
  `5.3e-8` relative RMS. This is within noise and slightly slower, so exp2 is
  not a useful A10G shape-specific optimization here.
- Rounding the online-softmax row state (`row_m`, `row_l`, `row_alpha`) to BF16
  was tested as a full-BF16-arithmetic hypothesis. It was slower on
  `test_clean.wav` (about 1748.8 ms versus 1737.5 ms) and moved final audio by
  about 0.012 relative RMS, so the implementation was removed and the default
  keeps row state in FP32.
- The row-owned probability/denominator variant is slower than `mma_q32_exp1`
  despite identical register/shared usage. On this A10G shape, keeping the 512
  per-tile exp operations parallel is more important than saving one
  probability readback/synchronization.
- Expanding the key tile to 32 rows is worse: it reduces softmax/rescale loop
  count, but shared memory rises to 27 KiB and active blocks/SM fall to about 3.
  On this A10G prototype, `q32/k16` is better than `q32/k32`.
- The q32/k32 regout variant remains much slower than q32/k16 regout
  (31.2 ms vs 21.5 ms). Removing output shared-memory traffic does not rescue
  k32; the larger score/K/V/probability tile and lower active blocks/SM dominate
  the lower K-loop count.
- Expanding to 64 rows no longer wins after the add buffer removal: q64 is
  slightly slower than q32. The original q64 attempt exceeded the 48 KiB static
  shared-memory limit until the extra add buffer was removed, so shared-memory
  footprint and occupancy are already real design constraints.
- The valid block-level fused prototype is now faster than the separated
  cuBLAS-path replacement target in isolation, and the structure has been
  ported into the inference-path fixed `N=1301,D=64` time-attention opt-in
  kernel. The next work is to use the new 16.0 ms/call profile as the baseline
  for remaining time-attention improvements.

## Microbenchmark: Custom 2048 FFT Baseline

`fft2048_custom.cu` is the first no-cuFFT probe for the fixed MBR STFT shape.
The current inference path still calls cuFFT in `src/stft_cuda_tile.cu`:

```text
STFT:  cufftPlan1d(n_fft=2048, batch=B*T, CUFFT_R2C)
iSTFT: cufftPlan1d(n_fft=2048, batch=B*T, CUFFT_C2R)
```

For the current chunk shape, `T=1301`. The relevant batch counts are therefore
`2602` for stereo STFT-like work and `5204` for the larger iSTFT-like stem /
channel batch.

Build and run:

```bash
ninja -C build-bench bench_fft2048_custom -j8
./build-bench/bench_fft2048_custom --batch 2602 --warmup 2 --iters 5
./build-bench/bench_fft2048_custom --batch 5204 --warmup 2 --iters 5
```

The target links only `CUDA::cudart`; `ldd build-bench/bench_fft2048_custom`
shows no cuFFT or cuBLAS dependency.

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
batch=2602
sincos   best=0.281 ms median=0.281 ms throughput=9264270 FFT/s
         max_abs=3.425401e-05 rel_rms=1.604645e-07
const    best=0.534 ms median=0.534 ms throughput=4875142 FFT/s
         max_abs=5.796379e-06 rel_rms=3.715204e-08
shared   best=0.374 ms median=0.375 ms throughput=6959304 FFT/s
         max_abs=5.796379e-06 rel_rms=3.715204e-08
r2c      best=0.106 ms median=0.107 ms throughput=24462245 FFT/s
         max_abs=3.116474e-05 rel_rms=1.193213e-07
r2cwin   best=0.109 ms median=0.109 ms throughput=23901381 FFT/s
         max_abs=5.132074e-05 rel_rms=2.593078e-07
c2r      best=0.106 ms median=0.106 ms throughput=24640152 FFT/s
         max_abs=4.730225e-04 rel_rms=2.449651e-07
c2rola   best=0.113 ms median=0.114 ms throughput=22989116 FFT/s
         max_abs=3.937864e-07 rel_rms=2.647217e-07
speedup vs sincos: const 0.53x, shared 0.75x
r2c speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.64x
r2cwin speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.58x, vs r2c 0.98x
c2r speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.66x
c2rola speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.48x, vs c2r 0.93x

batch=5204
sincos   best=0.552 ms median=0.553 ms throughput=9431911 FFT/s
         max_abs=3.425401e-05 rel_rms=1.604645e-07
const    best=1.058 ms median=1.058 ms throughput=4919533 FFT/s
         max_abs=5.796379e-06 rel_rms=3.715204e-08
shared   best=0.739 ms median=0.740 ms throughput=7043702 FFT/s
         max_abs=5.796379e-06 rel_rms=3.715204e-08
r2c      best=0.206 ms median=0.206 ms throughput=25291602 FFT/s
         max_abs=3.116474e-05 rel_rms=1.193213e-07
r2cwin   best=0.211 ms median=0.211 ms throughput=24685034 FFT/s
         max_abs=5.132074e-05 rel_rms=2.593078e-07
c2r      best=0.205 ms median=0.205 ms throughput=25445940 FFT/s
         max_abs=4.730225e-04 rel_rms=2.449651e-07
c2rola   best=0.218 ms median=0.218 ms throughput=23862803 FFT/s
         max_abs=3.937864e-07 rel_rms=2.647456e-07
speedup vs sincos: const 0.52x, shared 0.75x
r2c speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.68x
r2cwin speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.62x, vs r2c 0.98x
c2r speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.70x
c2rola speedup vs c2c sincos: 2.53x, vs c2r 0.94x
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
sincos kernel: 1024 threads/block, 31 registers/thread, 16384 B static shared
const  kernel: 1024 threads/block, 30 registers/thread, 16384 B static shared
shared kernel: 1024 threads/block, 32 registers/thread, 16384 B static shared
               plus 16376 B dynamic shared for the twiddle table
r2c    kernel: 512 threads/block, 24 registers/thread, 8192 B static shared
r2cwin kernel: 512 threads/block, 26 registers/thread, 8192 B static shared
c2r    kernel: 512 threads/block, 23 registers/thread, 8192 B static shared
c2rola kernel: 512 threads/block, 30 registers/thread, 8192 B static shared
```

Interpretation:

- This is a correctness-oriented radix-2 C2C baseline, not a final STFT kernel.
  It computes a full 2048-point complex output from real input, while the real
  STFT path only needs 1025 frequency bins from an R2C transform.
- Even with `__sincosf` inside every butterfly stage, the `sincos` variant
  reaches about 9.3-9.4 million 2048-point FFTs per second for the current batch
  sizes.
- Naive precomputed twiddles are a negative result on this A10G shape. Constant
  memory twiddles are about 0.52x of `sincos`, likely because each warp issues
  divergent constant-cache addresses rather than broadcast loads. Loading all
  twiddles through shared memory improves over constant memory but is still only
  about 0.75x of `sincos`, because it adds about 16 KiB dynamic shared memory and
  extra shared loads.
- The fixed-shape R2C/C2R prototypes are the first positive FFT direction. R2C
  uses one 1024-point complex FFT over even/odd samples plus the standard
  real-FFT postprocess and emits the 1025 STFT frequency bins directly. C2R
  reconstructs the 1024-point complex spectrum from the 1025 R2C bins, performs
  the inverse 1024-point FFT, and emits cuFFT-compatible unnormalized real
  output. Both are about 2.6-2.7x faster than the full 2048-point C2C baseline.
- R2C and C2R also drop to 512 threads/block and 8 KiB shared memory. On A10G,
  that gives much better occupancy headroom than the 1024-thread C2C baseline.
- Fusing the Hann window into R2C is close to free: the windowed R2C variant is
  about 0.98x of plain R2C while keeping the same 8 KiB shared footprint. This
  is the right direction for removing the current `extract_windowed_frames`
  global-memory write before cuFFT.
- Fusing Hann-window multiply, `1/N` scaling, and overlap-add into C2R is also
  viable: `c2rola` is about 0.93-0.94x of plain C2R while avoiding the full
  intermediate frame write before overlap-add. It still uses atomics, matching
  the current overlap-add strategy.
- The next no-cuFFT direction should integrate the windowed R2C and C2R
  overlap-add kernels into an experimental `stft_cuda_tile.cu` path, then handle
  window-sum normalization. Naive global/constant/shared twiddle tables should
  be avoided unless a later stage-specialized or radix-4/8 plan changes the
  access pattern.

## Inference Experiment: Default No-cuFFT STFT/iSTFT

`src/stft_cuda_tile.cu` now uses the fixed-shape custom STFT/iSTFT kernels as
the only runtime path:

- STFT bypasses `extract_windowed_frames -> cuFFT R2C -> layout conversion` and
  directly writes `[B, 1025, T, 2]`.
- iSTFT bypasses `layout conversion -> cuFFT C2R -> frame buffer -> overlap-add`
  and directly does fixed C2R with Hann-windowed overlap-add. Window-sum
  normalization still uses the existing kernel.
- `cudasep_infer` no longer links `CUDA::cufft`; `ldd build-min/cudasep_infer`
  shows `libcublas`, `libcublasLt`, and `libcudart`, but no `libcufft`.

Validation commands:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/stft_nocufft_default_clean \
  --fp16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav --output outputs/stft_nocufft_default_test \
  --fp16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
test_clean.wav old cuFFT baseline:      2405.2 ms, RTF 16.46x
test_clean.wav default no-cuFFT:        2398.6 ms, RTF 16.51x
test.wav       default no-cuFFT:        13797.7 ms, RTF 22.58x
```

Output difference against the historical cuFFT STFT/iSTFT baseline for
`test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0015105307 rms=0.00010496175 rel_rms=0.00056927941
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0014211684 rms=0.00011823861 rel_rms=0.00049164266
```

Interpretation:

- The default no-cuFFT STFT/iSTFT path is end-to-end stable on both validation
  WAVs.
- Previous C2R-only testing was almost identical to cuFFT at the final audio
  level. The remaining difference comes from the custom R2C operation order,
  matching the earlier R2C-only experiment.
- End-to-end speed is roughly neutral because the transformer dominates this
  run. The value of this path is that cuFFT is removed from the main binary and
  the STFT/iSTFT boundaries are now open for further fusion.

## Inference Experiment: CUDA Tile Static Long Linear

`src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu` now tries a CUDA Tile static BF16 Tensor Core path before
the older raw-WMMA fallback for the four fixed long-linear MBR shapes:

```text
qkv:       M=78060 N=1536 K=256  no bias
ffn1:      M=78060 N=1024 K=256  bias + GELU
ffn2:      M=78060 N=256  K=1024 bias
attn_out:  M=78060 N=256  K=512  no bias
```

The full `M=78048` rows use static `ct::mma` with `tile=32x64x32`; the last
12 rows use a separate CUDA Tile scalar tail kernel. This keeps the high-volume
full-tile kernel static, while avoiding raw WMMA for these long-linear default
paths. Disable for comparison with:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_CUTILE_STATIC_BF16=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
```

Validation commands:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/cutile_static_linear_clean_final \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav --output outputs/cutile_static_linear_test_final \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
bench static ct::mma t32x64x32s:
  qkv       1.917 ms, 32.02 TF/s, 45.7% of 70 TF/s
  ffn1      1.325 ms, 30.89 TF/s, 44.1% of 70 TF/s
  ffn2      1.074 ms, 38.12 TF/s, 54.5% of 70 TF/s
  attn_out  0.600 ms, 34.11 TF/s, 48.7% of 70 TF/s

test_clean.wav CUDA Tile static long-linear: 1439.1 ms, RTF 27.51x
test_clean.wav raw-WMMA fallback:            1399.2 ms, RTF 28.29x
test.wav       CUDA Tile static long-linear: 8590.2 ms, RTF 36.28x
```

SASS confirms the new static kernel lowers to BF16 Tensor Core instructions:

```text
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1536,256,...>
  HMMA.16816.F32.BF16
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
qkv       static full: REG=77  SHARED=53248
ffn1      static full: REG=113 SHARED=61440
ffn2      static full: REG=152 SHARED=98304
attn_out  static full: REG=90  SHARED=98304

qkv       tail: REG=40 SHARED=0
ffn1      tail: REG=38 SHARED=0
ffn2      tail: REG=33 SHARED=0
attn_out  tail: REG=32 SHARED=0
```

Output difference against the raw-WMMA fallback for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0092458501 mae=0.00033468063 rmse=0.0006108921 snr=49.60 dB
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0099793971 mae=0.0005231282  rmse=0.0008169154 snr=49.38 dB
```

Negative tail-fusion result:

- Folding the last 12 rows into the same static full kernel removed one launch,
  but polluted the full-tile resource shape.
- Resource usage regressed to `qkv REG=142` and `attn_out REG=141`.
- `test_clean.wav` regressed to `1507.5 ms`.
- Current conclusion: keep full static `ct::mma` and tail handling separate until
  a better no-extra-launch tail strategy avoids raising full-kernel register
  pressure.

Remaining work:

- Raw WMMA still exists as fallback and in the fused attention kernels.
- The static CUDA Tile linear kernels use up to 98 KiB shared memory, which is a
  likely bottom-level reason they are at about 44-55% of the accepted 70 TF/s
  A10G dense BF16 ceiling instead of closer to the roofline.
- Next A10G direction: tune CUDA Tile static GEMM shapes to reduce shared memory
  and register pressure, then port the attention raw-WMMA kernels to CUDA Tile.

## Inference Experiment: CUDA Tile Static Small Linear

The small-linear probe targets the fixed `M=1301` band-split and mask-estimator
linears. The first source attempt used `M=1280` static full tiles plus a scalar
CUDA Tile tail for the remaining 21 rows. That was a negative result:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_CUTILE_STATIC_SMALL_BF16=1 test_clean.wav: 1480.4 ms

Nsight opt-in bottleneck:
  linear_cutile_tail_bf16_kernel<1024,1024,true,false>
    480 calls, 160.965 ms total, 335.345 us avg
```

The follow-up experiment avoids the scalar tail. It uses compile-time static
CUDA Tile `ct::mma` for the fixed `M=1301` shapes and handles the final partial
M tile with `load_masked` / `store_masked` instead of a per-output K loop.

Best small-shape bench results on the current A10G:

```text
mask_hid_k1024  t32x64x64sp  best=0.084 ms useful=32.35 TF/s roof=46.2%
mask_hid_k256   t32x64x64sp  best=0.027 ms useful=25.26 TF/s roof=36.1%
mask_out48      t32x64x64smn best=0.020 ms useful=6.33 TF/s  roof=9.0%
mask_out128     t32x64x64sm  best=0.023 ms useful=14.66 TF/s roof=20.9%
mask_out400     t32x64x32smn best=0.051 ms useful=20.87 TF/s roof=29.8%
mask_out1040    t32x64x32smn best=0.115 ms useful=24.13 TF/s roof=34.5%
gate_n8_k256    t32x16x32smn best=0.179 ms useful=1.78 TF/s  roof=2.5%
band_n256_k16   t64x64x16sp  best=0.007 ms useful=1.63 TF/s  roof=2.3%
band_n256_k32   t64x64x16sp  best=0.007 ms useful=2.92 TF/s  roof=4.2%
band_n256_k64   t64x64x16sp  best=0.010 ms useful=4.27 TF/s  roof=6.1%
```

Source opt-in validation:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_CUTILE_STATIC_SMALL_BF16=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/linear_cutile_static_small_padded_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_CUTILE_STATIC_SMALL_BF16=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav --output outputs/linear_cutile_static_small_padded_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured:


Interpretation:

- Static non-divisible `M=1301` with `partition_view.load_masked` works, but it
  is slower on `N=1024` than a padded-M full-tile view with only the final M tile
  manually masked.
- The scalar `linear_cutile_tail_bf16_kernel<1024,1024,true,false>` no longer
  appears in the opt-in source profile.
- Small `N=256,K=16/32/64` band linears are too small to approach the 70 TF/s
  dense BF16 ceiling; launch/scheduling overhead dominates even though the path
  still uses CUDA Tile Tensor Core MMA.
- `N=8` gate sigmoid is even more dimension-starved: it only reaches about
  1.78 useful TF/s in bench, but replacing the raw-WMMA sigmoid still lowers the
  profiled gate kernel total from about 19.2 ms to about 15.8 ms on
  `test_clean.wav`.
- Raw-WMMA small warp16 calls drop from 224 to 112 after the `N=48` mask-output
  path is added. The remaining 112 calls need another shape pass before the
  small-linear opt-in should become default.
- The first wide-N batch removes the top 8 raw-WMMA `K=1024` mask-output shapes
  from the shape probe. The second wide-N batch removes `N=608` through `N=376`.
  The third batch removes `N=352` through `N=224`, reducing the remaining
  c64x128 raw-WMMA bias path to 292 calls on `test_clean.wav`.
- The third batch is farther from the 70 TF/s dense ceiling because `N` is now
  narrow: `N=224` launches only `41 x 4 = 164` CTAs, or about 2.3 CTAs per A10G
  SM before scheduling waves, and the `TN=64` tail tile wastes useful columns.
  The lower roof fraction is therefore dimension-starvation plus masked-tail
  overhead, not high register/shared pressure.
- The newly added `N=224..352` kernels keep the same resource shape as the
  earlier productive wide-N batch (`REG=72, SHARED=9216`), unlike the `N=48`
  path (`REG=148, SHARED=81920`).
- The dynamic-K `N=256` band-split CUDA Tile path removes 236 c64 raw-WMMA
  calls without statically instantiating every band K shape. It is not an A10G
  utilization win by itself: the useful rate is only about 3.08 TF/s because
  each launch has only `21 x 4 = 84` CTAs, many band K values are tiny
  (`K=24/28/36/...`), and the dynamic masked-K kernel is resource-heavy
  (`REG=124, SHARED=36864`). The bottom-level fix is deeper band-split fusion
  across bands, ideally fusing per-band RMSNorm + linear and increasing per
  launch work instead of running many small GEMMs.
- Adding the final medium mask-output shapes (`N=136..208,K=1024`) removes the
  remaining c64 raw-WMMA bias path entirely. These kernels are still low roof
  fraction because the output width is too narrow for enough CTA waves, but
  they are clean CUDA Tile replacements with the same `REG=72, SHARED=9216`
  resource shape as the wider mask-output batch.
- The final tiny `N=56/72/80/88/104/120` pass removes the remaining warp16
  raw-WMMA runtime path. After default-enabling the small-linear CUDA Tile path,
  this leaves no `linear_wmma_bf16_*` kernels in the default Nsight profile.
- The tiny-N CUDA Tile kernels are intentionally low-roof: `N=56` launches only
  `41 x 1 = 41` CTAs, less than one CTA per A10G SM, and `N=72/80/88/104/120`
  launch only `41 x 2 = 82` CTAs. The low 6-11% roof fraction is CTA-count and
  output-width starvation, not a failure to use Tensor Core MMA.
- The source no longer contains raw WMMA (`wmma::`, `nvcuda::wmma`, or
  `<mma.h>`) in `src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu`. The default validated
  MelBandRoformer `.csm` path also has no old raw-WMMA kernel launches in the
  Nsight trace. Attention fallback coverage is now intentionally strict: fixed
  time/freq attention goes through CUDA Tile, and unsupported shapes fail rather
  than falling back to raw WMMA.
- Static-instantiating many shapes has a real NVCC compile-time cost, so further
  rollout should continue in measured batches or move to a generated dispatch
  table instead of hand-written branches.
- Even with lower per-kernel TF/s for small `N`, this batch is aligned with the
  CUDA Tile-first goal because it removes another 64 raw-WMMA calls and improves
  both validation WAVs.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Fixed Freq Attention60

`bench/bf16_freq_attention_cutile.cu` is a no-raw-WMMA CUDA Tile experiment for
the fixed frequency attention shape:

```text
BH=10408, N=60, D=64
QK + row-wise softmax + AV in one CUDA Tile kernel
BF16 Q/K/V/prob/output, FP32 Tensor Core accumulation
```

The kernel uses two `ct::mma` operations:

- `Q[QRows,64] x K^T[64,64] -> scores[QRows,64]`
- `P[QRows,64] x V[64,64] -> out[QRows,64]`

Validation against the CPU BF16-rounded reference for `BH=0` is exact for all
measured variants:

```text
validate BH0 max_abs=0 rms=0
```

Measured on the current A10G:

```text
CUDA Tile padded input, static 64 rows:
  q16  best=0.785 ms, 12.21 TF/s, REG=93,  SHARED=22528
  q32  best=1.427 ms,  6.72 TF/s, REG=100, SHARED=28672

CUDA Tile unpadded masked loads, safe for current q/k/v tensors:
  q16m best=1.986 ms, 4.83 TF/s, REG=141, SHARED=22528
  q32m best=2.027 ms, 4.73 TF/s, REG=186, SHARED=28672

CUDA Tile pad 60->64 + padded q16 attention + unpadded output:
  q16p best=1.554 ms, 6.17 TF/s
  pad3 kernel: REG=30, SHARED=0
  padded attention out60: REG=92, SHARED=22528

Raw-WMMA fused benchmark reference:
  freq_q32_lfp32_direct best=1.043 ms, 9.20 TF/s
  freq_q32_fragprob_fulltile best=1.070 ms, 8.96 TF/s
```

Interpretation:

- CUDA Tile can beat the current raw-WMMA frequency fused attention when q/k/v
  are already padded to 64 rows: q16 padded is `0.785 ms` versus raw-WMMA
  `1.043 ms`.
- The direct unpadded CUDA Tile version is a negative result. Safe masked
  pointer-tile loads raise register pressure from `REG=93` to `REG=141` for q16,
  and throughput drops to `4.83 TF/s`.
- Padding inside attention is also a negative result. The attention kernel stays
  fast, but the extra global-memory pad of three tensors makes the whole q16p
  pipeline `1.554 ms`, slower than the raw-WMMA fused reference.
- Best next source-level direction is not "pad inside attention". It is to emit
  q/k/v as padded `NPad=64` directly from the freq `split_qkv_heads_rotary`
  stage, then use the static padded CUDA Tile attention and return only the first
  60 rows. That avoids both masked-load register inflation and the separate pad
  global-memory pass.

Source integration result:

- `split_qkv_heads_rotary()` now emits padded BF16 freq q/k/v tensors with
