  `NPad=64` directly when `N=60,D=64` and BF16 inference is active.
- `scaled_dot_product_attention()` now dispatches the padded `N=64` BF16 freq
  path to `freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16>()`, returning only the
  first 60 rows to preserve downstream gate shape.
- `CUDASEP_DISABLE_FREQ_ATTENTION60_CUTILE_PADDED=1` disables this source path
  and falls back to the previous raw-WMMA fused freq attention branch.
- Resource usage in `cudasep_infer`:

```text
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel:
  REG=40, SHARED=0
freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16>:
  REG=92, SHARED=22528
```

- SASS for `freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16>` contains
  `HMMA.16816.F32.BF16`, confirming the integrated CUDA Tile freq attention uses
  BF16 Tensor Core instructions.

End-to-end BF16 inference on the current A10G:

```text
CUDA Tile padded freq path:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1432.5 ms
  test.wav       infer time: 8542.1 ms
  test_clean.wav under nsys CUDA trace: 1447.9 ms

Fallback with CUDASEP_DISABLE_FREQ_ATTENTION60_CUTILE_PADDED=1:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1442.5 ms
```

Nsight Systems CUDA kernel summary for `test_clean.wav` confirms the integrated
runtime path:

```text
freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16>:
  instances=48 total=38.609946 ms avg=804.3739 us

split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel:
  instances=48 total=36.764422 ms avg=765.9255 us
```

This moves the frequency attention body to CUDA Tile, but also shows the next
bottom-level issue: the split/rotary/pad kernel now costs about the same as the
fused attention body. The next A10G-specific direction is to fuse qkv linear
output with split, rotary, and pad64 for the freq branch, so q/k/v padded tiles
are produced without a separate global-memory pass.

Output comparison against the fallback for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=0.00740920007 rms=0.000608842786 mean_abs=0.000345860309
Instrumental: max_abs=0.00673258305 rms=0.000790170511 mean_abs=0.000521421002
```

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Freq QKV Linear + Rotary + Pad64 Fusion

The next attempted source-level fusion was the fixed freq qkv path:

```text
x:      [1301, 60, 256] BF16
weight: [1536, 256] BF16
output: q/k/v [1301, 8, 64, 64] BF16
```

The CUDA Tile kernel computes 32 rotary pairs per block. For each head and q/k/v
group, it runs two `ct::mma` accumulators:

- even dims use a strided RHS view with output-feature stride `2*K`
- odd dims use the adjacent strided RHS view

This keeps the even/odd rotary pair in registers and writes rotated q/k or v
directly to the padded output layout. The path is correct, but it is a negative
performance result and is opt-in only:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_QKV_FREQ_ROTARY_CUTILE_FUSED=1
```

Measured source results:

```text
Default path, qkv fused disabled:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1433.6 ms
  test.wav       infer time: 8554.5 ms

Opt-in fused qkv, TM=32:
  static full kernel resource: REG=151, SHARED=65560
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1608.6 ms
  nsys avg per static full kernel: 6.223943 ms

Opt-in fused qkv, TM=16:
  static full kernel resource: REG=92, SHARED=49176
  SASS: HMMA.16816.F32.BF16
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1509.0 ms
  nsys avg per static full kernel: 4.239700 ms
```

For comparison, the unfused default source path is roughly:

```text
freq qkv linear static full kernel: ~1.95 ms per call
freq split/rotary/pad64 kernel:    ~0.766 ms per call
```

Interpretation:

- The fusion is numerically correct. Compared with the default path for
  `test_clean.wav`, output differences are only:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rms=9.68645947e-09 mean_abs=2.80792984e-09
Instrumental: max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rms=1.2856022e-08 mean_abs=4.84500229e-09
```

- The first fused shape, `TM=32`, is too heavy: two pair accumulators plus
  strided RHS views raise register pressure to `REG=151` and shared memory to
  `65560` bytes.
- Reducing to `TM=16` fixes much of the occupancy pressure (`REG=92`,
  `SHARED=49176`) and cuts the fused qkv kernel from `6.22 ms` to `4.24 ms`, but
  it is still slower than the original qkv GEMM plus split/rotary/pad.
- The bottom-level reason is not launch overhead or global-memory qkv traffic
  alone. For this shape, forcing rotary-pair locality inside the GEMM makes the
  RHS access strided and doubles the accumulator path, so Tensor Core efficiency
  drops more than the saved qkv write/read is worth.
- This fused-pair design should stay opt-in. The next direction should not be
  "one pair-fused qkv kernel at any cost"; it should test a layout-aware qkv
  weight/output format that preserves contiguous `64`-column Tensor Core tiles
  while making split/rotary cheaper, or fuse only the post-GEMM qkv scatter with
  a lower-register design.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Time Attention1301 q32 Source Path

The next positive CUDA Tile source integration is the fixed time attention path:

```text
q/k/v: [BH=480, N=1301, D=64] BF16
output: [BH=480, N=1301, D=64] BF16
QK + online softmax + AV in one CUDA Tile kernel
```

The source kernels are:

```text
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<32>
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64>
```

The q64 CUDA Tile path is now the default BF16 time attention path. The previous
raw-WMMA path remains available for comparison with:

```text
CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q64=1
```

The older q32 CUDA Tile path remains opt-in for regression experiments:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q32=1
```

Unlike the standalone padded bench, the source kernel directly consumes the
current unpadded `[BH,1301,64]` tensors and uses `partition_view.load_masked()`
for the final partial K/V and Q tiles. That avoids a separate global-memory pad
pass, while still using two `ct::mma` operations per K tile:

- `Q[32,64] x K^T[64,64] -> scores[32,64]`
- `P[32,64] x V[64,64] -> out[32,64]`

Source validation on the current A10G:

```text
Default path, raw-WMMA time attention:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1432.3 ms
  test.wav       infer time: 8556.9 ms

Opt-in CUDA Tile q32 time attention:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1358.2 ms
  test.wav       infer time: 8122.0 ms

Default CUDA Tile q64 time attention before default promotion:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1288.4 ms
  test.wav       infer time: 7696.3 ms

Default CUDA Tile q64 time attention after default promotion:
  test_clean.wav infer time: 1290.6 ms
  test.wav       infer time: 7659.7 ms
```

Nsight Systems for `test_clean.wav` with the opt-in path:

```text
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<32>:
  instances=48 total=336.909288 ms avg=7.0189435 ms
  real_math ~= 29.63 TF/s, about 42.3% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 ceiling

time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64>:
  instances=48 total=269.874207 ms avg=5.6223793 ms
  real_math ~= 36.99 TF/s, about 52.8% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 ceiling

Default trace after promotion:
  instances=48 total=270.545539 ms avg=5.6363654 ms
  real_math ~= 36.90 TF/s
```

Resource/SASS evidence:

```text
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<32>:
  REG=213 STACK=0 SHARED=45056 LOCAL=0

time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64>:
  REG=230 STACK=0 SHARED=32768 LOCAL=0

SASS contains HMMA.16816.F32.BF16 inside both functions.
```

Output drift versus the current default path:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.0114445388 rms=0.000901338924 mean_abs=0.000472182701 rel_rms=0.00488890921
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.0116160735 rms=0.00110768319  mean_abs=0.000681114028 rel_rms=0.00460647

test.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.022369504  rms=0.00080486004  mean_abs=0.000454981317 rel_rms=0.0042591306
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.0246918797 rms=0.000962342607 mean_abs=0.000591317152 rel_rms=0.00501941405

q64 versus default:

test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.00676959753 rms=0.000753294239 mean_abs=0.000420131251 rel_rms=0.00408590714
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.00894547999 rms=0.000910971772 mean_abs=0.000593693664 rel_rms=0.00378841546

test.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.0170776993 rms=0.000769514744 mean_abs=0.000443384357 rel_rms=0.00407209158
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.0194589868 rms=0.000926054231 mean_abs=0.000580667508 rel_rms=0.0048301401
```

The expanded `bench/bf16_time_attention_cutile.cu` masked/padded sweep gives the
A10G shape evidence:

```text
padded qrows=8  ktile=64 best=50.868 ms real_math=4.09 TF/s
padded qrows=16 ktile=64 best=15.199 ms real_math=13.68 TF/s
padded qrows=32 ktile=64 best=6.787 ms  real_math=30.65 TF/s
padded qrows=64 ktile=64 best=5.568 ms real_math=37.36 TF/s
padded qrows=128 ktile=64 best=9.071 ms real_math=22.93 TF/s

masked qrows=16 ktile=32 best=20.422 ms real_math=10.18 TF/s
masked qrows=16 ktile=64 best=15.193 ms real_math=13.69 TF/s
masked qrows=32 ktile=32 best=7.272 ms  real_math=28.60 TF/s
masked qrows=32 ktile=64 best=6.842 ms  real_math=30.40 TF/s
masked qrows=64 ktile=64 best=5.525 ms  real_math=37.65 TF/s

Follow-up A10G KTile/query-block probe after raw-WMMA source deletion:
  masked qrows=64  ktile=32 best=5.793 ms real_math=35.90 TF/s
  masked qrows=64  ktile=64 best=5.538 ms real_math=37.55 TF/s
  masked qrows=128 ktile=32 best=5.847 ms real_math=35.57 TF/s

CUDA Tile QK+AV lower-bound probe that keeps both `ct::mma` operations but
removes online-softmax max/sum/exp/denom:
  score_av_lb qrows=64 ktile=32 best=4.726 ms real_math=44.01 TF/s
  score_av_lb qrows=64 ktile=64 best=4.521 ms real_math=46.01 TF/s

Validation run with `--validate`:
  q64/k32 BH0 rows=3 max_abs=0 rms=0
```

Resource evidence from the bench:

```text
masked q16/k32: REG=181 SHARED=55296
masked q16/k64: REG=231 SHARED=90112
masked q32/k32: REG=175 SHARED=34944
masked q32/k64: REG=213 SHARED=45056
padded q64/k64: REG=230 SHARED=32768
masked q64/k32: REG=228 SHARED=24576
masked q64/k64: REG=230 SHARED=32768
score_av_lb q64/k32: REG=233 SHARED=40960
score_av_lb q64/k64: REG=209 SHARED=32768
padded q128/k64: REG=255 STACK=24 SHARED=57344
masked q128/k32: REG=254 SHARED=49152
```

Interpretation:

- This is the first source-level time attention CUDA Tile path that beats the
  previous raw-WMMA source path end to end. It is now the default BF16 time
  attention path.
- q64 is the new best measured A10G source shape. It increases register use
  versus q32 (`230` versus `213`) but cuts shared memory (`32768` versus
  `45056`) and doubles query-row reuse of each K/V tile, so it improves from
  about `29.6 TF/s` to about `37.0 TF/s` in the source path.
- The speedup is real but still not close to dense GEMM roofline. The bottom-level
  blockers are visible in the kernel profile: high register pressure, online
  softmax `exp`, row max/sum reductions, BF16 probability conversion, and masked
  partial tiles leave q64 around `37 TF/s` rather than near `70 TF/s`.
- The output drift is expected because this CUDA Tile path sums BF16-rounded
  probabilities for the denominator, while the current raw-WMMA path does not
  use exactly the same probability/denominator rounding sequence.
- `ct::tile<..., shape<48,64>>` is rejected by the CUDA Tile `tile_shape`
  concept, so q48 is not a legal CUDA Tile shape here. The viable sweep points
  are not arbitrary multiples of 16.
- A follow-up attempt to sweep intermediate source-style query tiles
  `QRows=80/96/112` was also rejected at compile time. The official CUDA Tile
  C++ API defines `tile_shape` around tile-compatible dimensions, including
  power-of-two extent requirements, so `ct::shape<80,64>`,
  `ct::shape<96,64>`, and `ct::shape<112,64>` are not direct single-tile
  shapes. Bottom-level conclusion: the A10G sweet spot search cannot simply try
  arbitrary middle QRows inside one CUDA Tile object. The viable next directions
  are legal power-of-two shapes, or a deliberately composite design such as
  `64+32` subtiles if the extra code and scheduling cost can be justified.
- The q64/k32 and q128/k32 follow-up is a negative result. q64/k32 cuts shared
  memory from `32768` to `24576` bytes and register count from `230` to `228`,
  but it doubles the online-softmax loop count and falls from `37.55 TF/s` to
  `35.90 TF/s`. q128/k32 increases register pressure to `254` and shared memory
  to `49152`, so fewer query blocks do not pay for the lower occupancy/resource
  pressure. Bottom-level conclusion: for this CUDA Tile source-style kernel,
  `KTile=64,QRows=64` remains the best measured A10G point.
- Padding time attention from `N=1301` to `NPad=1344` is also a negative result
  for the source-style CUDA Tile kernel. Padded q64 keeps the same `REG=230` and
  `SHARED=32768` resource shape as masked q64 but is slightly slower
  (`5.568 ms` versus `5.528 ms` in the same run). Padded q128 is much worse
  (`9.071 ms`) because it reaches `REG=255`, needs stack, and uses `57344` bytes
  of shared memory. Bottom-level conclusion: the current cost is not primarily
  the masked K/V loads; the expensive part remains the online softmax and large
  Q/out tile state.
- The QK+AV lower-bound probe is the clearest bottom-level split so far.
  Removing online-softmax work improves q64/k64 from `5.535 ms` / `37.58 TF/s`
  to `4.521 ms` / `46.01 TF/s`, and drops register pressure from `230` to
  `209`. That means softmax scalar work and row state cost roughly `1.0 ms` on
  this bench, but even the no-softmax lower bound is only `65.7%` of the
  accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 ceiling. The remaining gap is therefore not
  just `exp`; it is also the current CUDA Tile attention shape: large output
  accumulator tile, shared-memory backed tile state, masked tail handling, and
  the non-GEMM reductions/loads around the two Tensor Core MMAs.
- Next A10G directions are no longer KTile=32 or q128 expansion; the remaining
  gap needs a different approach. A pure source-style q64 CUDA Tile attention
  microkernel can likely gain about 1 ms by reducing softmax work, but getting
  closer to 70 TF/s requires changing the surrounding computation, most likely
  fusing QKV/rotary/attention/gate so the current attention kernel does less
  global-memory traffic and exposes larger Tensor Core-friendly work.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Fixed Time Split QKV + Rotary

The time-branch BF16 `split_qkv_heads_rotary()` dispatch now has a fixed-shape
CUDA Tile branch for the current MelBandRoformer time attention shape:

```text
N=1301, heads=8, dim_head=64, qkv dtype=BF16
kernel: split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_time1301_d64_kernel
disable: CUDASEP_DISABLE_SPLIT_QKV_TIME_CUTILE_FIXED=1
```

Validation on the current A10G:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
test_clean.wav --bf16: 1208.2 ms
test.wav       --bf16: 7209.0 ms
Nsight test_clean.wav --bf16: 1230.6 ms
```

Nsight Systems split/rotary kernel totals for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel:
  instances=48 total=36.763 ms avg=765.896 us

split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_time1301_d64_kernel:
  instances=48 total=35.821 ms avg=746.275 us

split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_threaded_kernel:
  instances=0
```

Interpretation:

- This is a CUDA Tile-first source-path replacement for the previous default
  threaded time split/rotary branch. It keeps end-to-end runtime close to the
  prior threaded default (`test_clean.wav` was about `1205 ms`; `test.wav` was
  about `7191 ms` in the previous checkpoint).
- This kernel is not a Tensor Core workload and should not be judged by BF16
  TFLOP/s. It performs BF16 loads/stores, FP32 rotary arithmetic, BF16 rounding
  when requested, and layout scattering from fused `[Q,K,V]` to `[B,H,N,D]`.
- The bottom-level limiter is not dense math. It is global-memory traffic plus
  per-element index math and rotary elementwise work. The fixed-shape Tile branch
  removes generic runtime shape math but does not remove the separate global
  write of q/k/v.
- The next meaningful fix is deeper fusion, not another split-only tile shape:
  fuse the preceding QKV projection output and/or the following attention so q/k
  do not have to be materialized in global memory before QK.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Fixed Gate Merge Token D64

The BF16 gate merge path now has a fixed-shape CUDA Tile token kernel for the
current attention output shape:

```text
heads=8, dim_head=64, merged token width=512
kernel: apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_cutile_kernel
disable: CUDASEP_DISABLE_GATE_MERGE_TOKEN_D64_CUTILE=1
```

Validation on the current A10G:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
test_clean.wav --bf16: 1206.2 ms
test.wav       --bf16: 7180.8 ms
Nsight test_clean.wav --bf16: 1225.5 ms
```

Nsight Systems gate-merge totals for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_cutile_kernel:
  instances=96 total=23.575 ms avg=245.575 us

apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_kernel:
  instances=0
```

Previous same-path reference from the ordinary CUDA token kernel:

```text
apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_kernel:
  instances=96 total=26.768 ms avg=278.832 us
```

Interpretation:

- This is a positive CUDA Tile-first replacement. The source default no longer
  uses the ordinary CUDA token gate-merge kernel, and the CUDA Tile kernel is
  about `3.2 ms` faster on the `test_clean.wav` profile.
- This is still not a Tensor Core workload. It is BF16 load, per-head gate
  broadcast, elementwise multiply, optional BF16 arithmetic rounding, and a
  contiguous 512-wide token write.
- The bottom-level reason it beats the ordinary token kernel is shape exposure:
  one tile block maps exactly to one merged token, so the compiler sees the full
  512-element power-of-two tile instead of a manual `threadIdx.x` loop over two
  256-thread passes.
- The remaining ceiling for this operation is memory and elementwise math, not
  BF16 Tensor Core throughput. Further improvement should come from fusing gate
  application into the preceding attention output write or into the following
  residual/linear path, not from more standalone gate-merge tile shapes.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Fixed D256 BF16 Strided Copy

The stable BF16 D256 layout-copy specializations now have CUDA Tile fixed-shape
variants:

```text
[B,T,F,256] -> [B,F,T,256]: strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_bft_d256_cutile_kernel
[B,F,T,256] -> [B,T,F,256]: strided_copy_bf16_bft_to_btf_d256_cutile_kernel
[B,T,F,256] -> [F,B,T,256]: strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_fbt_d256_cutile_kernel
disable: CUDASEP_DISABLE_STRIDED_COPY_D256_CUTILE=1
```

Validation on the current A10G:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
test_clean.wav --bf16: 1208.1 ms
test.wav       --bf16: 7178.4 ms
Nsight test_clean.wav --bf16: 1225.6 ms
```

Nsight Systems D256 copy totals for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_bft_d256_cutile_kernel:
  instances=96 total=13.055 ms avg=135.993 us

strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_fbt_d256_cutile_kernel:
  instances=8 total=1.156 ms avg=144.481 us

ordinary D256 copy kernels:
  instances=0
```

Previous ordinary CUDA `uint4` vector-copy reference:

```text
strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_bft_d256_kernel:
  instances=96 total=12.018 ms

strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_fbt_d256_kernel:
  instances=8 total=1.002 ms
```

Interpretation:

- This is a CUDA Tile-first coverage win but a small performance regression for
  the standalone copy kernels: about `+1.0 ms` for the 96-call BTF->BFT group and
  about `+0.15 ms` for the 8-call BTF->FBT group.
- The bottom-level reason is vector width. The ordinary CUDA kernel copies each
  row with `uint4` 16-byte vector elements. The CUDA Tile version must use scalar
  tile element types; the attempted `ct::tile<uint4, shape<8,32>>` does not
  compile because CUDA Tile requires `detail::scalar<E>`, and `uint4` is not a
  scalar tile element type. The working Tile version uses 64-bit words, which
  doubles the element count per row versus `uint4`.
- This operation is pure memory/layout movement, so BF16 Tensor Core TFLOP/s is
  not an appropriate metric. The next real fix is not another copy tile shape; it
  is to remove the layout copy by fusing producer/consumer layout expectations
  or by making adjacent fixed-shape kernels consume the existing layout directly.

## Source Cleanup: Removed Ordinary CUDA Fallbacks After Tile Replacement

After the fixed CUDA Tile replacements above were validated, the ordinary CUDA
fallback kernels were removed from source for the already-covered paths:

```text
removed:
  split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_threaded_kernel
  apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_threaded_kernel
  apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_kernel
  strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_bft_d256_kernel
  strided_copy_bf16_bft_to_btf_d256_kernel
  strided_copy_bf16_btf_to_fbt_d256_kernel

remaining ordinary CUDA kernels in src:
  stft2048_r2c_window_kernel
  istft2048_c2r_overlap_add_kernel
```

Validation on the current A10G after deletion:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
test_clean.wav --bf16: 1206.0 ms
test.wav       --bf16: 7182.1 ms
```

Interpretation:

- This moves the source tree closer to the CUDA Tile-first objective by deleting
  stale ordinary CUDA fallback code instead of keeping env-controlled alternate
  paths that are no longer part of the project direction.
- The remaining ordinary CUDA kernels are the fixed 2048-point STFT/iSTFT FFT
  kernels. They are not simple elementwise/copy kernels: they depend on shared
  memory and repeated `__syncthreads()` over butterfly stages. A direct CUDA Tile
  rewrite would likely become an O(N^2) DFT or require a new Tile-friendly FFT
  algorithm, so the next STFT step should be a deliberate FFT Tile redesign, not
  a mechanical port.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Hard GELU Inside FFN1 Linear

The FFN1 fixed-shape CUDA Tile linear kernel is a major Tensor Core path:

```text
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1024,256,true,true>
M=78048 N=1024 K=256, bias + GELU, 96 calls on test_clean.wav
```

The experiment adds an opt-in hard-GELU approximation:

```text
enable: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_HARD_GELU=1
hard_gelu(x) = x * clamp(0.5 + 0.2 * x, 0, 1)
```

Validation on the current A10G:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default, test.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav --output outputs/linear_hard_gelu_default_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 7366.3 ms

hard-GELU, test.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_HARD_GELU=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav --output outputs/linear_hard_gelu_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 7167.2 ms
```

Nsight Systems `test_clean.wav` FFN1 kernel totals:

```text
default erf-GELU reference:
  96 calls, 166.961 ms total, 1.739182 ms avg
  23.53 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

hard-GELU:
  96 calls, 164.916 ms total, 1.717871 ms avg
  23.82 TFLOP/s, 34.0% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Output drift versus the default path, read as IEEE float32 WAV:

```text
clean Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.184398080 rms_diff=0.232127259 rel=1.258837720 snr=-1.999 dB max_abs=0.994719943
clean Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.240457025 rms_diff=0.234441826 rel=0.974984309 snr=0.220 dB max_abs=0.993455889
test Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.188970806 rms_diff=0.146298322 rel=0.774184781 snr=2.223 dB max_abs=1.013671740
test Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.191726529 rms_diff=0.155763346 rel=0.812424583 snr=1.804 dB max_abs=1.010326530
```

Interpretation:

- This is a negative/marginal speed experiment. The FFN1 Tensor Core kernel only
  improves from about `23.53` to `23.82 TFLOP/s`, or `33.6%` to `34.0%` of the
  local dense BF16 reference. The end-to-end `test.wav` timing is also within the
  observed run-to-run range, so it is not strong evidence for a real large gain.
- The quality drift is very large. This is not the same class of risk as BF16
  rounding noise: the output RMS difference is comparable to the signal RMS.
- Bottom-level cause: replacing erf-GELU removes some scalar math but does not
  address the main limiter. The fused FFN1 kernel still carries GEMM
  accumulation, bias, activation, BF16 rounding, and output conversion in one
  tile kernel. The likely bottleneck is register pressure / post-op scheduling /
  fixed tile efficiency, not just the cost of `exp`.
- Keep hard-GELU as an explicit opt-in experiment only. The next useful FFN1
  experiment is either a split GEMM+bias and separate GELU comparison, or a
  deeper FFN1 activation -> FFN2 fusion that avoids writing the activation to
  global memory.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Split FFN1 GEMM+Bias and GELU

This diagnostic experiment checks whether FFN1 is slow because the fused
GEMM+bias+GELU Tile kernel carries too much post-op work in the same kernel.
It adds an opt-in split path:

```text
enable: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_GELU_SPLIT=1

step 1:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1024,256,true,false>
  writes BF16 after GEMM+bias

step 2:
  gelu_bf16_inplace_kernel
  reads BF16, applies GELU, writes BF16
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default, test_clean.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/linear_gelu_split_default_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1236.4 ms

split, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_GELU_SPLIT=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/linear_gelu_split_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1219.4 ms

split, test.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_GELU_SPLIT=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav --output outputs/linear_gelu_split_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 7269.6 ms
```

Nsight Systems `test_clean.wav` split profile:

```text
fused default FFN1 reference:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,1024,256,true,true>
  96 calls, 166.961 ms total, 1.739177 ms avg
  23.53 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

split GEMM+bias only:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,1024,256,true,false>
  96 calls, 129.842 ms total, 1.352520 ms avg
  30.25 TFLOP/s, 43.2% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

split GELU:
  gelu_bf16_inplace_kernel
  96 calls, 48.343 ms total, 0.503574 ms avg

split GEMM+bias+GELU effective:
  178.185 ms total, 1.856094 ms avg
  22.05 effective TFLOP/s, 31.5% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Output drift versus the default fused path, read as IEEE float32 WAV:

```text
clean Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.184398080 rms_diff=0.000869583 rel=0.004715794 snr=46.529 dB max_abs=0.009463578
clean Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.240457025 rms_diff=0.001022052 rel=0.004250457 snr=47.431 dB max_abs=0.008587122
test Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.188970806 rms_diff=0.000899257 rel=0.004758707 snr=46.450 dB max_abs=0.015812993
test Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.191726529 rms_diff=0.001057620 rel=0.005516297 snr=45.167 dB max_abs=0.017520323
```

Interpretation:

- This confirms that the fused FFN1 post-op is suppressing GEMM throughput:
  removing GELU from the main Tile GEMM raises the FFN1 GEMM body from `23.53`
  to `30.25 TFLOP/s`.
- The split path is still not a win. The separate BF16 GELU pass costs
  `48.343 ms` over 96 calls, so the combined FFN1 path becomes slower than the
  fused default.
- Bottom-level cause: standalone split writes the 78048x1024 activation to
  global memory as BF16 and then reads/writes it again for GELU. That memory
  traffic plus 96 extra launches costs more than the Tensor Core scheduling
  recovered by removing GELU from the GEMM kernel.
- The next useful direction is not a permanent split. It is a deeper FFN design:
  either reduce fused post-op register/scheduling pressure inside the Tile GEMM,
  or fuse FFN1 activation directly into FFN2 tiling so the activation does not
  round-trip through global memory.

## CUDA Tile Fix: Make Hard-GELU Selection Compile-Time

The hard-GELU experiment initially passed `hard_gelu` as a runtime bool into the
fixed-shape Tile linear kernels. That made the default erf-GELU FFN1 kernel carry
an unnecessary branch and extra Tile IR even when `CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_HARD_GELU`
was not set.

Before this fix, a default Nsight run after the runtime-bool change showed:

```text
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,78048,1024,256,true,true>
signature: (..., bool, bool)
96 calls, 198.841 ms total, 2.071260 ms avg
19.76 TFLOP/s, 28.2% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

The fix makes the GELU choice a template parameter:

```text
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,ApplyGelu,HardGelu>
linear_cutile_tail_bf16_kernel<...,ApplyGelu,HardGelu>
gelu_bf16_inplace_kernel<HardGelu>
```

Host launchers instantiate `HardGelu=false` for the default path and only select
`HardGelu=true` when `CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_HARD_GELU=1`.

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default, test_clean.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/hard_gelu_template_default_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1204.0 ms

default Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1231.6 ms

hard-GELU opt-in, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_HARD_GELU=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/hard_gelu_template_hard_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1177.8 ms
```

Nsight default FFN1 after the fix:

```text
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,78048,1024,256,true,true,false>
signature: (..., bool)
96 calls, 167.093 ms total, 1.740552 ms avg
23.51 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Interpretation:

- This recovers `31.748 ms` on the 96-call FFN1 group compared with the
  accidental runtime-bool default profile (`198.841 -> 167.093 ms`).
- The fixed default is back in line with the previous erf-GELU reference
  (`166.961 ms`), so hard-GELU remains an opt-in experiment without slowing the
  normal fused FFN1 path.
- Bottom-level cause: a runtime branch inside a CUDA Tile GEMM post-op can
  materially increase generated Tile IR/register pressure even if the branch is
  almost always false. For fixed-shape A10G kernels, experiment toggles that
  affect inner-tile math should be compile-time template choices, not runtime
  scalar parameters.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: FFN1 M16xN128xK32

This experiment checks whether the FFN1 fused linear path prefers a wider N tile
on A10G. The default FFN1 fused Tile shape is:

```text
default:
  TM=32 TN=64 TK=32
  M=78048 N=1024 K=256, bias + GELU
  accumulator elements per output tile: 2048
```

The opt-in candidate keeps the same accumulator element count but trades M rows
for wider N columns:

```text
enable: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN1_M16N128=1

candidate:
  TM=16 TN=128 TK=32
  M=78048 N=1024 K=256, bias + GELU
  accumulator elements per output tile: 2048
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default, test_clean.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/ffn1_m16n128_default_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1205.0 ms

M16xN128, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN1_M16N128=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/ffn1_m16n128_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1260.8 ms

M16xN128 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1281.7 ms
```

Nsight FFN1 fused kernel totals:

```text
default M32xN64xK32:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1024,256,true,true,false>
  96 calls, 167.093 ms total, 1.740552 ms avg
  23.51 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

candidate M16xN128xK32:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<16,128,32,78048,1024,256,true,true,false>
  96 calls, 222.968 ms total, 2.322583 ms avg
  17.62 TFLOP/s, 25.2% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Output drift versus default fused path:

```text
clean Vocals:
  rms_diff=9.6786417e-09 rel=5.24877574e-08 snr=145.599 dB max_abs=1.78813934e-07
clean Instrumental:
  rms_diff=1.27456919e-08 rel=5.30061117e-08 snr=145.513 dB max_abs=2.98023224e-07
```

Interpretation:

- This is a clear negative shape result on A10G. It regresses FFN1 by
  `55.875 ms` over 96 calls and drops from `23.51` to `17.62 TFLOP/s`.
- The output drift is near numerical noise, so the candidate is correct but slow.
- Bottom-level cause: widening N to 128 while shrinking M to 16 keeps the same
  accumulator element count, but the CUDA Tile backend schedules this layout much
  worse for the FFN1 fused post-op. The likely causes are poorer MMA tiling /
  memory-access grouping for the wide B tile and worse post-op scheduling over
  128 columns.
- Do not make this shape default. The next FFN1 shape candidates should move in
  the other direction, such as taller M / narrower N, or should focus on reducing
  the fused GELU post-op pressure rather than widening N.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: FFN1 M64xN32xK32

After `M16xN128xK32` regressed, the next candidate moved in the opposite
direction: taller M and narrower N.

Two compile-time shape constraints were hit before the final candidate:

```text
M64xN32 with StaticM=78048:
  compile fail: static_assert(M % TM == 0)
  reason: 78048 % 64 = 32

M48xN32 with StaticM=78048:
  compile fail: cuda::tiles::tile_shape concept rejected shape<48,32>
  reason: this CUDA Tile backend does not accept arbitrary 2D tile extents
```

The tested opt-in candidate uses a full-kernel prefix that is divisible by 64 and
leaves the remaining rows to the existing tail kernel:

```text
enable: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN1_M64N32=1

candidate:
  full kernel: TM=64 TN=32 TK=32, StaticM=78016
  tail: 44 rows
  M=78048 N=1024 K=256, bias + GELU
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

M64xN32, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN1_M64N32=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/ffn1_m64n32_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1266.4 ms

M64xN32 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1284.0 ms
```

Nsight FFN1 fused kernel totals:

```text
default M32xN64xK32:
  full kernel rows=78048
  96 calls, 167.093 ms total, 1.740552 ms avg
  23.51 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

candidate M64xN32xK32:
  full kernel rows=78016
  96 calls, 217.398 ms total, 2.264562 ms avg
  18.06 TFLOP/s, 25.8% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

candidate tail:
  linear_cutile_tail_bf16_kernel<1024,256,true,true,false>
  96 calls, 12.340 ms total, 0.128540 ms avg

candidate full + tail effective:
  229.738 ms total, 2.393104 ms avg
  17.10 effective TFLOP/s, 24.4% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Output drift versus the default fused path:

```text
clean Vocals:
  rms_diff=0.000543859 rel=0.002949376 snr=50.605 dB max_abs=0.006083898
clean Instrumental:
  rms_diff=0.000728426 rel=0.003029340 snr=50.373 dB max_abs=0.007025358
```

Interpretation:

- This is also a clear negative shape result. Even the full M64xN32 Tile GEMM
  body regresses by `50.305 ms` versus the default M32xN64 full kernel; including
  the larger tail makes the effective regression `62.645 ms`.
- The failure of `shape<48,32>` is an important CUDA Tile language constraint:
  not every arithmetically reasonable tile shape is accepted by the backend.
- Bottom-level cause: making M taller and N narrower gives worse scheduling for
  this FFN1 fused bias+GELU path. Combined with the non-divisible 78048-row
  prefix, this direction is worse than the default.
- Current best FFN1 fused shape remains `M32xN64xK32`. Both tested same-sized
  accumulator alternatives, `M16xN128` and `M64xN32`, are slower on A10G.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: QuickGELU FFN1 Post-Op

The FFN1 fused kernel is limited by post-op pressure. This experiment replaces
the default erf-GELU approximation with QuickGELU:

```text
enable: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_QUICK_GELU=1
quick_gelu(x) = x * sigmoid(1.702 * x)
```

The GELU choice is a compile-time template mode, not a runtime branch:

```text
GeluMode=0: erf-GELU default
GeluMode=1: hard-GELU
GeluMode=2: QuickGELU
GeluMode=3: tanh-GELU
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default, test_clean.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/quick_gelu_default_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1204.6 ms

QuickGELU, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_QUICK_GELU=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/quick_gelu_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1199.2 ms

QuickGELU Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1222.5 ms

QuickGELU, test.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_QUICK_GELU=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav --output outputs/quick_gelu_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 7126.3 ms
```

Nsight FFN1 fused kernel totals:

```text
default erf-GELU:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,78048,1024,256,true,true,0>
  96 calls, 167.093 ms total, 1.740552 ms avg
  23.51 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

hard-GELU reference:
  96 calls, 164.916 ms total, 1.717875 ms avg
  23.82 TFLOP/s, 34.0% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

QuickGELU:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,78048,1024,256,true,true,2>
  96 calls, 157.600 ms total, 1.641667 ms avg
  24.93 TFLOP/s, 35.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Output drift versus the default fused path, read as IEEE float32 WAV:

```text
clean Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.184398080 rms_diff=0.146520859 rel=0.794589936 snr=1.997 dB max_abs=1.094755460
clean Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.240457025 rms_diff=0.147154691 rel=0.611979173 snr=4.265 dB max_abs=1.109418320
test Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.188970806 rms_diff=0.031567126 rel=0.167047636 snr=15.543 dB max_abs=0.479512900
test Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.191726529 rms_diff=0.034121054 rel=0.177967306 snr=14.993 dB max_abs=0.489339754
```

Interpretation:

- This is speed-positive at the FFN1 kernel level: `167.093 -> 157.600 ms`,
  recovering `9.493 ms` over 96 calls and raising FFN1 from `23.51` to
  `24.93 TFLOP/s`.
- The quality drift is still large. It is smaller than hard-GELU on the long
  `test.wav`, but it is not BF16 noise; it changes the model output materially.
- Bottom-level cause: QuickGELU removes the erf polynomial and keeps only one
  sigmoid-like `exp`, reducing post-op pressure, but the activation function is
  still meaningfully different from the trained model's GELU.
- Keep QuickGELU as an explicit opt-in experiment. It is useful evidence that
  post-op simplification improves Tensor Core scheduling, but it is not a safe
  default path.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Tanh-GELU FFN1 Post-Op

This experiment tests the common tanh-GELU approximation:

```text
enable: CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_TANH_GELU=1
tanh_gelu(x) = 0.5 * x * (1 + tanh(sqrt(2/pi) * (x + 0.044715*x^3)))
```

Like QuickGELU and hard-GELU, this is selected with a compile-time `GeluMode`
template parameter. It does not add a runtime branch to the default path.

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

tanh-GELU, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_TANH_GELU=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/tanh_gelu_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1187.3 ms

tanh-GELU Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1206.3 ms

tanh-GELU, test.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_TANH_GELU=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav --output outputs/tanh_gelu_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 7056.0 ms
```

Nsight FFN1 fused kernel totals:

```text
default erf-GELU:
  96 calls, 167.093 ms total, 1.740552 ms avg
  23.51 TFLOP/s, 33.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

QuickGELU:
  96 calls, 157.600 ms total, 1.641667 ms avg
  24.93 TFLOP/s, 35.6% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference

tanh-GELU:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<...,78048,1024,256,true,true,3>
  96 calls, 145.876 ms total, 1.519542 ms avg
  26.93 TFLOP/s, 38.5% of the local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference
```

Output drift versus the default fused path, read as IEEE float32 WAV:

```text
clean Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.184398080 rms_diff=0.146242438 rel=0.793080046 snr=2.014 dB max_abs=1.097091490
clean Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.240457025 rms_diff=0.146902384 rel=0.610929891 snr=4.280 dB max_abs=1.112987530
test Vocals:
  rms_ref=0.188970806 rms_diff=0.031624176 rel=0.167349532 snr=15.528 dB max_abs=0.475112289
test Instrumental:
  rms_ref=0.191726529 rms_diff=0.034175804 rel=0.178252871 snr=14.979 dB max_abs=0.489999197
```

Interpretation:

- This is the fastest FFN1 activation experiment so far: `167.093 -> 145.876 ms`,
  a `21.217 ms` gain over 96 calls, and `26.93 TFLOP/s`.
- The quality drift is still large and almost identical to QuickGELU on the long
  `test.wav`. The standard tanh approximation is not numerically equivalent
  enough for this trained model under the current BF16 path.
- Bottom-level cause: replacing the erf approximation with tanh-GELU gives the
  CUDA Tile backend a cheaper/lower-pressure post-op schedule, but the model
  output is sensitive to the activation curve change.
- Keep tanh-GELU opt-in only. It is strong evidence that FFN1 is post-op-bound,
  but not a default-quality-preserving optimization.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Compile-Time FullBF16 Linear Kernels

The static CUDA Tile linear kernels used to carry `full_bf16` as a runtime bool
inside the tile code. Earlier hard-GELU testing showed that a runtime bool in the
fused FFN1 post-op can make the default kernel much slower. This experiment moves
the full-BF16 arithmetic choice into a `FullBF16` template parameter for the
fixed-shape linear kernels and GELU helpers.

Implementation:

```text
gelu_selected<GeluMode, FullBF16>(x)
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<..., GeluMode, FullBF16>
linear_cutile_tail_bf16_kernel<..., GeluMode, FullBF16>
linear_cutile_static_masked_m_bf16_kernel<..., GeluMode, FullBF16>
linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel<..., GeluMode, FullBF16>
```

The host launcher still chooses `FullBF16=true/false` from the environment, but
the tile kernel body sees a compile-time constant.

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default BF16, test_clean.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/fullbf16_template_default_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1194.8 ms

FullBF16 arithmetic experiment, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/fullbf16_template_fullarith_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1209.8 ms

default BF16, test.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav --output outputs/fullbf16_template_default_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 7150.9 ms

Nsight default BF16, test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1224.1 ms
  report: outputs/nsys_fullbf16_template_default_clean.nsys-rep
  sqlite: outputs/nsys_fullbf16_template_default_clean.sqlite
```

Nsight fixed-shape CUDA Tile GEMM-like kernels:

```text
QKV projection:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1536,256,false,false,0,false>
  96 calls, 188.339 ms total
  31.29 TFLOP/s, 44.7% of 70 TFLOP/s

FFN1 fused bias+erf-GELU:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1024,256,true,true,0,false>
  96 calls, 160.702 ms total
  24.45 TFLOP/s, 34.9% of 70 TFLOP/s

FFN2:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,256,1024,true,false,0,false>
  96 calls, 105.387 ms total
  37.28 TFLOP/s, 53.2% of 70 TFLOP/s

attention output projection:
  linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,256,512,false,false,0,false>
  96 calls, 59.151 ms total
  33.21 TFLOP/s, 47.4% of 70 TFLOP/s

small padded 1301x1024x1024:
  480 calls, 42.256 ms total
  30.99 TFLOP/s, 44.3% of 70 TFLOP/s

small padded 1301x1024x256:
  480 calls, 13.829 ms total
  23.68 TFLOP/s, 33.8% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Attention fused-kernel accounting:

```text
time attention QK+softmax+AV:
  time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64>
  48 calls, 271.806 ms total
  Useful QK+AV math assumes B=60 bands, H=8, N=1301, D=64:
    4 * (60*8) * 1301 * 1301 * 64 FLOPs per launch
  36.73 TFLOP/s, 52.5% of 70 TFLOP/s

freq attention QK+softmax+AV:
  freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16>
  48 calls, 38.956 ms total
  Useful QK+AV math assumes B=1301 time tokens, H=8, N=60, D=64:
    11.82 TFLOP/s, 16.9% of 70 TFLOP/s
  Padded 64x64 math:
    13.45 TFLOP/s, 19.2% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Interpretation:

- Compile-time `FullBF16=false` is a real default-path win for FFN1:
  `167.093 -> 160.702 ms` over 96 calls, raising FFN1 from `23.51` to
  `24.45 TFLOP/s`.
- The full-BF16 arithmetic experiment still runs, but it is slower on
  `test_clean.wav` (`1194.8 -> 1209.8 ms`). The extra BF16 round points reduce
  precision-conversion churn conceptually, but they also add tile operations and
  can increase generated instruction pressure.
- The bottom-level limiter for FFN1 is still the fused post-op, not the BF16
  toggle. Split GEMM-only FFN1 already reached `30.25 TFLOP/s`; erf-GELU fusion
  pulls it back to `24.45 TFLOP/s`.
- Time attention is closer to the A10G dense BF16 reference because each launch
  covers a large `BH=480` workload and keeps QK/softmax/AV in one tile kernel.
  It still remains below 70 TFLOP/s because online softmax, exponentials,
  reductions, masked 1301 tail handling, and BF16 probability recasts are not
  dense GEMM work.
- Freq attention is much lower because the per-head attention matrix is only
  60x60 (padded to 64x64). The useful work per tile block is small, so softmax,
  masks, launch overhead, and padding dominate. This path should be optimized as
  a small-N fused attention problem, not judged like a large dense GEMM.
- NVIDIA's CUDA Tile guide documents `ct::mma(a, b, acc)` with an explicit FP32
  accumulator. So "all BF16 arithmetic" inside Tile MMA currently means BF16
  inputs and BF16 stores with optional BF16-round simulations around FP32
  accumulators, not a native BF16-accumulate MMA path.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: Time Attention QRows16/32/64/128

The fused time-attention kernel is the current top CUDA Tile kernel:

```text
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<QRows>
N=1301, D=64, BH=60 bands * 8 heads = 480
K tile = 64
Useful QK+AV math per launch = 4 * BH * N * N * D
```

This experiment tests the query-row tile size. Q64 remains the default path;
Q16/Q32/Q128 are explicit opt-in experiment paths.

Implementation switches:

```text
Q16:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q16=1

Q32:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q32=1

Q64:
  default, unless CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q64=1

Q128:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q128=1
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

Q32 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q64=1 \
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q32=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_q32_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1288.8 ms

Q128 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q128=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_q128_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1384.5 ms

Q16 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_Q16=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_q16_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1687.6 ms
```

Results:

```text
QRows  q-blocks  kernel total  avg/call   useful TFLOP/s  % of 70  e2e
16     82        739.922 ms    15.415 ms  13.49           19.3%    1687.6 ms
32     41        336.997 ms     7.021 ms  29.62           42.3%    1288.8 ms
64     21        271.806 ms     5.663 ms  36.73           52.5%    1224.1 ms
128    11        436.484 ms     9.093 ms  22.87           32.7%    1384.5 ms
```

Interpretation:

- Q64 is the current A10G best point for this fused online-softmax attention
  kernel.
- Q16 and Q32 lose because the number of q-blocks rises to 82 and 41. Each
  q-block repeats the online softmax loop, reductions, exponentials, masked
  loads, and AV MMA loop. The smaller tile does not reduce enough pressure to
  compensate for the extra block work.
- Q128 loses even though it reduces q-blocks to 11. The `ScoreTile` and
  `OutTile` become large enough that register pressure, row reductions,
  exponentials, and softmax bookkeeping dominate. In other words, the kernel
  stops being Tensor Core MMA limited and becomes tile-state/post-op limited.
- Bottom-level cause for the remaining gap to 70 TFLOP/s: this kernel performs
  QK MMA and AV MMA, but each K tile also does online softmax max/sum updates,
  `exp`, BF16 probability recast, masked tail handling for N=1301, and output
  normalization. Those operations do not map to dense Tensor Core throughput.
- Next useful shapes are not more QRows powers of two. The evidence points to
  improving the internal K tile / softmax schedule, reducing BF16 recast and
  mask overhead, or splitting the 1301 tail into a specialized no-tail main
  kernel plus a small tail path.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: Time Attention KTile32/64/128

After QRows testing, Q64 was the best query-row tile. This experiment keeps
`QRows=64` and varies the key/value tile width used inside the online-softmax
loop:

```text
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<QRows=64, KTile>
N=1301, D=64, BH=60 bands * 8 heads = 480
Useful QK+AV math per launch = 4 * BH * N * N * D
```

Implementation switches:

```text
K32:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K32=1

K64:
  default

K128:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K128=1
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

K128 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K128=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_k128_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 2001.0 ms

K32 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K32=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_k32_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1231.0 ms
```

Results:

```text
Shape    k-tiles  kernel total  avg/call   useful TFLOP/s  % of 70  e2e
Q64K32   41       282.491 ms     5.885 ms  35.34           50.5%    1231.0 ms
Q64K64   21       271.806 ms     5.663 ms  36.73           52.5%    1224.1 ms
Q64K128  11      1060.612 ms    22.096 ms   9.41           13.4%    2001.0 ms
```

Interpretation:

- K64 remains the current A10G best point for the fused time-attention kernel.
- K32 nearly works but loses `10.685 ms` over 48 calls. The smaller score tile
  lowers per-iteration tile state, but doubles online-softmax loop iterations
  from 21 to 41, repeating max/sum/exp/recast/update work too often.
- K128 is a clear negative. It reduces loop iterations to 11, but the
  `64x128` score tile and `128x64` probability/V tile make the kernel heavily
  tile-state/post-op limited. Tensor Core work is no longer the pacing item.
- Bottom-level cause: the best current shape balances Tensor Core MMA tile size
  with the non-MMA work attached to every key tile. Moving either direction
  shifts the bottleneck to repeated softmax overhead (K32) or excessive tile
  state and reductions (K128).
- The next time-attention direction should not be another plain KTile sweep.
  More likely wins are a no-tail main path for the first 1280 tokens plus a
  specialized 21-token tail, or reducing the BF16 probability recast / online
  softmax bookkeeping inside each K tile.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Time Attention Split 1280 Main + 21 Tail

This experiment specializes the fixed `N=1301` time attention. The default
single kernel handles all 21 Q blocks and all 21 K tiles with masked loads and
validity checks:

```text
default:
  time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64,64>
  Q blocks: 21
  K tiles:  21
```

The split-tail path was first tested as an opt-in path, then default-enabled
after output comparison. It can still be disabled for A/B testing:

```text
disable:
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_SPLIT_TAIL=1

main kernel:
  time_attention1301_q64k64_main1280_cutile_kernel
  Q blocks: first 20 full blocks = 1280 rows
  K tiles: first 20 full tiles use unmasked load/store/update
           final 21-key K tail uses masked load/update

tail kernel:
  time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64,64,20>
  Q blocks: final 21 query rows only
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

default, current build, test_clean.wav:
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/timeattn_split_ref_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
  inference: 1201.8 ms

split-tail, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_SPLIT_TAIL=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_split_tail_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1162.3 ms

default, current build, test.wav:
  inference: 7169.6 ms

split-tail, test.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_SPLIT_TAIL=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 6794.6 ms

default-enabled split-tail, test_clean.wav:
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_timeattn_split_default_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1163.0 ms

default-enabled split-tail, test.wav:
  inference: 6813.4 ms
```

Nsight time-attention totals on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default Q64K64 single kernel:
  48 calls, 271.806 ms total, 5.662625 ms avg
  36.73 TFLOP/s, 52.5% of 70 TFLOP/s

split main1280:
  48 calls, 197.163 ms total, 4.107566 ms avg
  49.82 TFLOP/s, 71.2% of 70 TFLOP/s

split tail21:
  48 calls, 14.906 ms total, 0.310535 ms avg
  10.81 TFLOP/s, 15.4% of 70 TFLOP/s

split total:
  48 main launches + 48 tail launches
  212.069 ms total, 4.418104 ms effective avg
  47.08 TFLOP/s, 67.3% of 70 TFLOP/s

default-enabled split-tail:
  main1280: 48 calls, 197.172 ms total
  tail21:   48 calls,  14.907 ms total
```

Output drift versus the current default Q64K64 path:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.184398070 rms_diff=9.69512559e-09 rel=5.25771533e-08 snr=145.584 dB max_abs=1.78813934e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.240457028 rms_diff=1.27909550e-08 rel=5.31943485e-08 snr=145.483 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07

test.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.188970804 rms_diff=9.93007099e-09 rel=5.25481755e-08 snr=145.589 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.191726536 rms_diff=1.01977324e-08 rel=5.31889465e-08 snr=145.484 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- This is a strong positive CUDA Tile specialization. It reduces the
  time-attention kernel group from `271.806 ms` to `212.069 ms`, a `21.98%`
  kernel-level gain.
- The main1280 kernel alone reaches `49.82 TFLOP/s`, which is `71.2%` of the
  local 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense reference. This is the closest current
  time-attention path to the A10G dense-BF16 reference.
- Bottom-level cause: the previous single kernel paid row/key mask and
  `load_masked` overhead in every full tile. The split main kernel removes those
  checks for the 1280x1280 full region and only pays masking for the final
  21-key tail and the final 21-query tail kernel.
- The tail kernel is low-throughput (`10.81 TFLOP/s`) because it has only 21
  useful query rows, but it is small enough that the total path still wins.
- The extra tail launch is worthwhile here because it removes mask/tail overhead
  from almost all of the main attention work.
- This path is now default-enabled. Use
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_SPLIT_TAIL=1` only for comparison or
  rollback.

Bench follow-up for the fixed main1280 region, added to
`bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile`:

```text
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate

main1280 qrows=32 ktile=64:
  best=8.806 ms, real_math=23.24 TF/s, logical_tile_math=24.00 TF/s
  33.2% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_score_av_lb qrows=32 ktile=64:
  best=4.327 ms, real_math=47.29 TF/s, logical_tile_math=48.85 TF/s
  67.6% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=4.037 ms, real_math=50.69 TF/s, logical_tile_math=52.36 TF/s
  72.4% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_score_av_lb qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.616 ms, real_math=56.60 TF/s, logical_tile_math=58.47 TF/s
  80.9% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_score_av_lb_prescale_q qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.711 ms, real_math=55.14 TF/s, logical_tile_math=56.96 TF/s
  78.8% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_score_av_lb_split_d32 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.630 ms, real_math=56.37 TF/s, logical_tile_math=58.23 TF/s
  80.5% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280 qrows=128 ktile=64:
  best=5.177 ms, real_math=39.53 TF/s, logical_tile_math=40.83 TF/s
  56.5% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_score_av_lb qrows=128 ktile=64:
  best=4.356 ms, real_math=46.97 TF/s, logical_tile_math=48.53 TF/s
  67.1% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_qk_only qrows=32 ktile=64:
  best=3.969 ms, real_math=25.78 TF/s, logical_tile_math=26.63 TF/s
  36.8% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_qk_only qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=2.767 ms, real_math=36.97 TF/s, logical_tile_math=38.20 TF/s
  52.8% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_qk_only qrows=128 ktile=64:
  best=2.200 ms, real_math=46.51 TF/s, logical_tile_math=48.05 TF/s
  66.4% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_av_const qrows=32 ktile=64:
  best=3.164 ms, real_math=32.34 TF/s, logical_tile_math=33.41 TF/s
  46.2% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_av_const qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=2.056 ms, real_math=49.78 TF/s, logical_tile_math=51.42 TF/s
  71.1% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_av_const qrows=128 ktile=64:
  best=1.099 ms, real_math=93.08 TF/s, logical_tile_math=96.15 TF/s
  133.0% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference

main1280_split_global qrows=64 ktile=64:
  qk_store_p=28.322 ms, 3.61 TF/s
  av_load_p=9.047 ms, 11.31 TF/s
  pair=37.029 ms, pair_math=5.53 TF/s
  7.9% of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G dense BF16 reference
  P global write+read: 3.30 GB, 89.2 GB/s effective
```

Resource evidence:

```text
main1280_qk_only q32:     REG=255 STACK=8  SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_qk_only q64:     REG=255 STACK=24 SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_qk_only q128:    REG=255 STACK=24 SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_av_const q32:    REG=214 STACK=0  SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_av_const q64:    REG=255 STACK=32 SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_av_const q128:   REG=255 STACK=32 SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_qk_store_p q64:  REG=180 STACK=0  SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280_av_load_p q64:   REG=255 STACK=96 SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280 q32:             REG=255 STACK=56 SHARED=81920 LOCAL=0
main1280_score_av_lb q32: REG=243 STACK=0  SHARED=98304 LOCAL=0
main1280 q64:             REG=254 STACK=0  SHARED=49152 LOCAL=0
main1280_score_av_lb q64: REG=196 STACK=0  SHARED=49152 LOCAL=0
main1280_prescale_q q64:  REG=204 STACK=0  SHARED=49152 LOCAL=0
main1280_split_d32 q64:   REG=255 STACK=40 SHARED=81920 LOCAL=0
main1280 q128:            REG=255 STACK=0  SHARED=65536 LOCAL=0
main1280_score_av_lb q128: REG=255 STACK=40 SHARED=65536 LOCAL=0
SASS contains HMMA.16816.F32.BF16
```

Interpretation:

- The main1280 fixed-shape path is now reproducible in the standalone bench.
  q64 reaches about `50.7 TF/s`, close to the source Nsight value
  (`49.82 TF/s`).
- q32 is a negative result for the full softmax kernel. Even though it reduces
  query rows per tile, it doubles q-blocks and the generated resource shape gets
  worse (`REG=255 STACK=56 SHARED=81920`), so it falls to `23.24 TF/s`.
- q128 is also negative. It reduces q-blocks to 10, but the larger score/out
  state pushes resource pressure to `REG=255 SHARED=65536`, so it only reaches
  `39.53 TF/s`.
- Removing online softmax, row max/sum state, exponentials, denominator update,
  and output normalization improves q64 to `56.60 TF/s`. That accounts for
  about `0.42 ms` on this bench and drops q64 register pressure from `254` to
  `196`.
- Pre-scaling Q once by `scale=0.125` before QK, then removing the per-score
  multiply, is a negative result for the lower-bound path. It falls to
  `55.14 TF/s` and raises register pressure from `196` to `204`. Bottom
  conclusion: the per-score scale multiply is not the current limiter; the
  extra Q tile conversion/rounding path is worse than leaving `scores * scale`
  after QK.
- Splitting the AV/output dimension into two `D=32` halves is also a negative
  result. It looks like it should reduce each output accumulator tile, but the
  generated resource shape gets much worse (`REG=255 STACK=40 SHARED=81920`)
  and throughput slightly falls from `56.60 TF/s` to `56.37 TF/s`. Bottom
  conclusion: the CUDA Tile compiler already gives the single `64x64` AV output
  a better schedule than two explicit `64x32` halves for this shape.
- QK-only changes the shape preference: q128 is fastest for the isolated QK
  phase (`46.51 TF/s`) because fewer q-blocks matter more when there is no AV
  output accumulator and no softmax state. But q128 loses as soon as the AV
  phase is added (`46.97 TF/s` lower-bound versus q64's `56.59 TF/s`). Bottom
  conclusion: the problematic part is not QK alone; it is the score/probability
  handoff into AV plus the output accumulator/resource shape.
- AV-only with a constant BF16 probability tile is intentionally an idealized
  scheduling probe, not a valid full-attention throughput claim. It reaches
  `93.08 TF/s` for q128 because it removes the QK dependency and score/prob
  materialization, leaving a highly regular AV MMA stream. This confirms that
  the AV MMA itself is not the blocker; the blocker is feeding AV from freshly
  generated scores/probabilities inside one fused attention tile.
- Splitting QK and AV with a global BF16 P tensor is a strong negative result.
  It breaks the QK->AV dependency, but writes and reads about `3.30 GB` of P per
  launch pair and collapses to `5.53 TF/s`. The effective P traffic rate is only
  `89.2 GB/s`, and the AV-load-P kernel has a poor resource shape
  (`REG=255 STACK=96 SHARED=98304`). Bottom conclusion: global memory is the
  wrong place to solve the score/prob staging problem.
- Even the QK+AV lower bound is only `80.8%` of the A10G dense BF16 reference,
  so the remaining gap is not just softmax. The bottom-level limiter is the
  CUDA Tile attention schedule itself: two dependent MMA phases per K tile,
  BF16 score/probability materialization between phases, `49152` bytes of
  shared tile state, and much higher register pressure than a plain static GEMM.
- The next main1280 work should therefore attack tile state and inter-MMA
  staging, not another direct QRows sweep and not global P materialization.
  Direct q32/q64/q128 already shows q64 is the best fused generated schedule.
  Candidate experiments are reducing in-kernel score/probability
  materialization, changing the score-to-AV staging shape inside Tile state, or
  a layout/fusion change that keeps Q/K/V and the attention output closer to the
  producer/consumer kernels without round-tripping P through global memory.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Attention Softmax Sum from F32 Probs

This source experiment removes a small conversion loop inside the fused
time/frequency attention softmax. The old path computed probabilities in F32,
rounded them to BF16 for AV MMA, then converted that BF16 tile back to F32 for
the softmax denominator / online row sum:

```text
old: probs_f32 -> probs_bf16 -> sum(float(probs_bf16))
new: probs_f32 -> probs_bf16, sum(probs_f32)
```

The AV MMA still consumes BF16 probabilities. This only changes the softmax
normalization sum and removes the immediate BF16->F32 round-trip.

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

test_clean.wav:
  pre-change default: 1108.1 ms
  sum(probs_f32):     1104.5 ms

test.wav:
  pre-change default: 6565.2 ms
  sum(probs_f32):     6540.9 ms
```

Standalone `bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile` result:

```text
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate

main1280 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=4.037 ms, real_math=50.69 TF/s, roof70=72.4%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.947 ms, real_math=51.84 TF/s, roof70=74.1%

main1280_sum_f32_exp2 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.977 ms, real_math=51.46 TF/s, roof70=73.5%
```

Output difference against the pre-change default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals       max_abs=0.0104215443 rel_rms=0.00471707447
  Instrumental max_abs=0.0122372359 rel_rms=0.00423167287

test.wav:
  Vocals       max_abs=0.0287493467 rel_rms=0.00433615921
  Instrumental max_abs=0.0341453850 rel_rms=0.00511297756
```

Resource usage after the change:

```text
time_attention1301_q64k64_main1280_cutile_kernel: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<64,64,20>: REG=232 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
bench main1280 q64 old/new: REG=254 -> 255, SHARED=49152 -> 49152
bench main1280_sum_f32 vs exp2: REG=255 -> 255, SHARED=49152 -> 49152
```

Interpretation:

- This is a small speed-positive source change on both validation WAVs.
- Resource usage does not drop, so the likely gain is fewer conversion
  instructions / less tile expression work, not better occupancy.
- Replacing `ct::exp` with `ct::exp2(x * log2(e))` is a small negative result
  at the same resource shape, so the current `ct::exp` path remains the best
  valid q64 main1280 softmax variant in this bench.
- The output drift is around `0.42-0.51%` relative RMS, far above BF16 write
  noise. Keep this result in mind when comparing later attention experiments:
  it is a speed/quality tradeoff, not a bit-equivalent cleanup.

## CUDA Tile Layout Experiment: Time-Attention K Transposed

This bench-only experiment tests whether the QK phase is limited by reading K
through a `layout_left` transpose view. It pre-transposes the benchmark K tensor
from `[BH, N, D]` to `[BH, D, N]` with a CUDA Tile helper, then runs a QK-only
main1280 kernel using a normal row-major `[D, N]` K view. The measured QK-only
kernel time excludes the transpose cost.

```text
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate

main1280_qk_only qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=2.775 ms, real_math=36.87 TF/s, roof70=52.7%, checksum=10.937500

main1280_qk_only_kt qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=6.362 ms, real_math=16.08 TF/s, roof70=23.0%, checksum=10.937500
```

Resource usage:

```text
main1280_qk_only q64:    REG=255 STACK=24 SHARED=98304
main1280_qk_only_kt q64: REG=255 STACK=0  SHARED=98304
```

Interpretation:

- The transposed-K layout is a clear negative result even before counting the
  cost of producing the transposed K tensor.
- The checksum matches, so this is not a correctness failure.
- Resource usage is not worse, so the loss is likely from the generated K tile
  load / MMA operand schedule rather than occupancy. For this CUDA Tile QK
  shape, the current `layout_left` view is not the bottom-level limiter and
  should not be replaced by a source-side K-transposed layout.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: QKV Projection M32xN128xK32

After split-tail time attention, the next largest GEMM-like kernel is QKV
projection:

```text
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,78048,1536,256,false,false,0,false>
M=78048, N=1536, K=256
96 calls on test_clean.wav
```

This experiment widens only the output-column tile from `N64` to `N128`, keeping
`M32` and `K32` unchanged:

```text
old:
  M32xN64xK32

new:
  M32xN128xK32

rollback:
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N128=1
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

opt-in QKV N128 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N128=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_qkv_m32n128_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1153.6 ms

opt-in QKV N128, test.wav:
  inference: 6727.1 ms

default-enabled QKV N128 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_qkv_m32n128_default_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1154.7 ms

default-enabled QKV N128, test.wav:
  inference: 6727.4 ms
```

Nsight QKV totals:

```text
old default M32xN64xK32:
  96 calls, 187.975 ms total, 1.958073 ms avg
  31.35 TFLOP/s, 44.8% of 70 TFLOP/s

opt-in M32xN128xK32:
  96 calls, 176.215 ms total, 1.835573 ms avg
  33.44 TFLOP/s, 47.8% of 70 TFLOP/s

default-enabled M32xN128xK32:
  96 calls, 176.285 ms total, 1.836302 ms avg
  33.43 TFLOP/s, 47.8% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Output drift versus old M32xN64 default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.184398070 rms_diff=9.67267333e-09 rel=5.24553935e-08 snr=145.604 dB max_abs=1.78813934e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.240457028 rms_diff=1.27963924e-08 rel=5.32169614e-08 snr=145.479 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07

test.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.188970804 rms_diff=9.92039784e-09 rel=5.24969869e-08 snr=145.597 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.191726536 rms_diff=1.01953574e-08 rel=5.31765591e-08 snr=145.486 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- M32xN128 is a default-worthy positive result for QKV projection:
  `187.975 -> 176.285 ms`, a `6.2%` QKV kernel gain.
- Bottom-level cause: this QKV shape has a wide `N=1536` output and no fused
  post-op, so widening the N tile improves output-column reuse / scheduling
  without increasing activation or bias pressure. This differs from FFN1, where
  N128 lost because GELU post-op pressure dominated the GEMM.
- The kernel is still only `47.8%` of the dense BF16 reference. Remaining likely
  causes are small `K=256`, Tile backend scheduling overhead, and one CTA per
  tile without deeper pipeline/staging control exposed in the current code.
- This path is now default-enabled. Use
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N128=1` for rollback or A/B testing.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: QKV Projection M32xN256xK32

The previous QKV experiment showed that widening the output tile from N64 to
N128 helped. This follow-up tests whether the same pure-GEMM QKV path benefits
from an even wider N tile:

```text
old old:
  M32xN64xK32

previous default:
  M32xN128xK32

new:
  M32xN256xK32

rollback:
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N256=1
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

opt-in QKV N256 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N256=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_qkv_m32n256_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1125.9 ms

opt-in QKV N256, test.wav:
  inference: 6585.4 ms

default-enabled QKV N256 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_qkv_m32n256_default_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1125.5 ms

default-enabled QKV N256, test.wav:
  inference: 6577.0 ms
```

Nsight QKV totals:

```text
old M32xN64xK32:
  96 calls, 187.975 ms total, 1.958073 ms avg
  31.35 TFLOP/s, 44.8% of 70 TFLOP/s

previous M32xN128xK32:
  96 calls, 176.285 ms total, 1.836302 ms avg
  33.43 TFLOP/s, 47.8% of 70 TFLOP/s

opt-in M32xN256xK32:
  96 calls, 150.465 ms total, 1.567344 ms avg
  39.16 TFLOP/s, 55.9% of 70 TFLOP/s

default-enabled M32xN256xK32:
  96 calls, 150.639 ms total, 1.569155 ms avg
  39.12 TFLOP/s, 55.9% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Output drift versus previous N128 default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.184398070 rms_diff=9.70570291e-09 rel=5.26345146e-08 snr=145.575 dB max_abs=1.78813934e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.240457013 rms_diff=1.27431612e-08 rel=5.29955896e-08 snr=145.515 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07

test.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.188970804 rms_diff=9.92722526e-09 rel=5.25331165e-08 snr=145.591 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.191726536 rms_diff=1.01799982e-08 rel=5.30964488e-08 snr=145.499 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- M32xN256 is a stronger QKV result than N128:
  `176.285 -> 150.639 ms`, and `33.43 -> 39.12 TFLOP/s`.
- Compared with the original N64 shape, QKV improves from `187.975` to
  `150.639 ms`, a `37.336 ms` kernel-group gain over 96 calls.
- Bottom-level cause: QKV has a large `N=1536` and no post-op. Larger N tiles
  reduce N-grid fragmentation and raise useful MMA work per tile enough to
  outweigh the larger accumulator tile. The same logic does not carry to FFN1
  because FFN1 has GELU post-op pressure.
- M32xN256 is now default-enabled. Use
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N256=1` to fall back to N128, and also set
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N128=1` to fall all the way back to N64.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: FFN1 M32xN128xK32

This experiment checks the direct analogue of the QKV N-tile widening on FFN1.
Unlike the older FFN1 `M16xN128` experiment, this keeps the default M tile and
only widens N:

```text
default:
  M32xN64xK32

candidate:
  M32xN128xK32

enable:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN1_M32N128=1
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

M32xN128 Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN1_M32N128=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_ffn1_m32n128_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1124.8 ms
```

Nsight FFN1 totals:

```text
default M32xN64xK32:
  96 calls, 160.168 ms total, 1.668417 ms avg
  24.53 TFLOP/s, 35.0% of 70 TFLOP/s

candidate M32xN128xK32:
  96 calls, 161.057 ms total, 1.677677 ms avg
  24.39 TFLOP/s, 34.8% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Interpretation:

- M32xN128 is a slight negative result for FFN1: `+0.889 ms` over 96 calls.
- This completes the immediate FFN1 same-area shape checks:
  `M16xN128`, `M64xN32`, and now `M32xN128` are all worse than the default
  `M32xN64`.
- Bottom-level cause: FFN1 is not limited like pure QKV GEMM. The fused
  bias+erf-GELU post-op dominates enough that widening N increases tile/post-op
  pressure without improving Tensor Core scheduling.
- Keep FFN1 at `M32xN64xK32`. Future FFN1 work should target activation/post-op
  structure or FFN1->FFN2 fusion, not more simple same-kernel N widening.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: FFN2 M32xN128xK32

This experiment checks whether FFN2 benefits from the same N-tile widening that
helped QKV. FFN2 has `M=78060,N=256,K=1024` with fused bias and a 12-row scalar
tail after the static `M=78048` Tile GEMM:

```text
default:
  M32xN64xK32

candidate:
  M32xN128xK32

enable:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN2_M32N128=1
```

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8:
  pass, no work to do

default Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_ffn2_default_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1124.2 ms

candidate Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN2_M32N128=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_ffn2_m32n128_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1196.8 ms
```

Nsight FFN2 totals:

```text
default main Tile GEMM M32xN64xK32:
  96 calls, 105.118 ms total, 1.094982 ms avg
  37.37 TFLOP/s, 53.4% of 70 TFLOP/s

default full FFN2 path, including 12-row tail:
  96 calls, 123.447 ms total
  31.83 TFLOP/s, 45.5% of 70 TFLOP/s

candidate main Tile GEMM M32xN128xK32:
  96 calls, 190.630 ms total, 1.985726 ms avg
  20.61 TFLOP/s, 29.4% of 70 TFLOP/s

candidate full FFN2 path, including 12-row tail:
  96 calls, 208.589 ms total
  18.84 TFLOP/s, 26.9% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
M32xN64xK32:
  REG:152, SHARED:98304

M32xN128xK32:
  REG:226, SHARED:94208
```

Interpretation:

- M32xN128 is a strong negative result for FFN2: the main Tile GEMM regresses
  from `105.118` to `190.630 ms`.
- Bottom-level cause: FFN2 has only `N=256`. Widening the N tile from 64 to 128
  cuts the N-grid from 4 tiles to 2, but increases the per-block accumulator and
  post-bias pressure. Register usage rises from `152` to `226`, so A10G loses
  scheduling/occupancy headroom faster than it gains from fewer output tiles.
- Keep FFN2 at `M32xN64xK32`. The `M32xN128` variant remains opt-in only for
  future A/B testing with `CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_FFN2_M32N128=1`.

## CUDA Tile Shape Experiment: Attention Out M32xN128xK32

This experiment tests the post-attention output projection:
`M=78060,N=256,K=512`, no bias and no activation. Because adding new Tile
template instances to `mbr_cuda_tile.cu` triggers a slow full Tile backend
compile, the runtime candidate was first checked with the standalone CUDA Tile
GEMM bench:

```text
default:
  M32xN64xK32

candidate:
  M32xN128xK32

rollback:
  CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_ATTN_OUT_M32N128=1
```

Standalone bench:

```text
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --preset infer_linear --shape infer_attn_out \
  --variant t32x64x32s --warmup 4 --iters 20

M32xN64xK32:
  best=0.599 ms, median=0.601 ms
  34.13 TFLOP/s, 48.8% of 70 TFLOP/s

./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --preset infer_linear --shape infer_attn_out \
  --variant t32x128x32s --warmup 4 --iters 20

M32xN128xK32:
  best=0.503 ms, median=0.504 ms
  40.71 TFLOP/s, 58.2% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Main inference validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8:
  pass

opt-in Nsight, test_clean.wav:
  CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_ATTN_OUT_M32N128=1 \
  nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
    -o outputs/nsys_attn_out_m32n128_clean ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
  inference: 1114.7 ms
```

Nsight attention out totals:

```text
default M32xN64xK32:
  96 calls, 59.030 ms total, 0.614898 ms avg
  33.27 TFLOP/s, 47.5% of 70 TFLOP/s

candidate M32xN128xK32:
  96 calls, 49.487 ms total, 0.515495 ms avg
  39.69 TFLOP/s, 56.7% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
M32xN64xK32:
  REG:90, SHARED:98304

M32xN128xK32:
  REG:228, SHARED:98304
```

Output drift versus previous default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:
    rms_ref=0.184398080 rms_diff=9.74001142e-09 rel=5.28205684e-08 snr=145.544 dB max_abs=1.78813934e-07
  Instrumental:
    rms_ref=0.240457025 rms_diff=1.27890246e-08 rel=5.31863212e-08 snr=145.484 dB max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- M32xN128 is a positive result for attention out:
  `59.030 -> 49.487 ms`, a `9.543 ms` kernel-group gain over 96 calls.
