- Bottom-level cause: unlike FFN2, this projection has no bias/GELU post-op and
  only `K=512`, so cutting the N grid from 4 tiles to 2 improves useful MMA work
  per Tile block enough to offset the register jump from `90` to `228`.
- The kernel is still only `56.7%` of the 70 TFLOP/s dense BF16 reference. The
  likely remaining causes are small fixed `N=256`, one CTA per output tile, and
  high register pressure at N128 limiting occupancy. N256 was not promoted into
  the main path because it caused excessive Tile backend compile cost during
  main-binary experimentation; future wider-N testing should stay in a separate
  bench or isolated compilation unit first.
- M32xN128 is now default-enabled. Use
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_ATTN_OUT_M32N128=1` for rollback or A/B testing.

## Cleanup: Remove Raw WMMA/MMA Bench Code

The project direction is now CUDA Tile-first. The old exploratory raw
WMMA/MMA microbench files were removed from the build and deleted from
`bench/`:

```text
removed targets/files:
  bench_bf16_peak_mma              bench/bf16_peak_mma.cu
  bench_bf16_gemm_shapes           bench/bf16_gemm_shapes.cu
  bench_bf16_time_attention_block_fused
                                    bench/bf16_time_attention_block_fused.cu
  bench_bf16_freq_attention_fused  bench/bf16_freq_attention_fused.cu
  bench_wmma_fragment_map          bench/wmma_fragment_map.cu
```

Replacement policy:

- Use CUDA Tile benches for future shape searches:
  `bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm`, `bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile`,
  and `bench_bf16_freq_attention_cutile`.
- Do not add raw WMMA or inline MMA back into the project path.
- Keep historical notes in this README only as measurement history; they are no
  longer backed by build targets or source files.

## Inference Experiment: CUDA Tile Scratch FFT STFT/iSTFT

`src/stft_cuda_tile.cu` was converted from the previous in-tile FFT attempt to
a CUDA Tile global-scratch radix-2 implementation. The rejected idea was to keep
the 2048 FFT in a 1024-lane tile and use `ct::permute` for bit-reversal and
butterfly partners. CUDA Tile `permute` only accepts `ct::dimension_map` and
reorders tile dimensions, not arbitrary lane elements, so that approach is not a
valid CUDA Tile C++ implementation.

The current implementation is correctness-oriented:

- STFT writes windowed real input into bit-reversed `[B*T, 2048, 2]` scratch.
- Forward FFT runs 11 CUDA Tile `fft2048_stage_kernel` passes, ping-ponging two
  scratch tensors.
- R2C extraction writes the first 1025 bins to `[B, 1025, T, 2]`.
- iSTFT mirrors the R2C spectrum into full complex scratch, runs 11 inverse
  stage passes, then Hann-windowed overlap-adds into the output.
- The main binary still links no cuFFT/cuBLAS/cuDNN, and `src` has no raw
  `__global__`, `threadIdx`, `blockIdx`, raw WMMA, or inline MMA hits.

Validation:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/stft_cutile_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav --output outputs/stft_cutile_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Measured on A10G:

```text
test_clean.wav: 1118.2 ms
test.wav:       6633.2 ms
Nsight test_clean.wav: 1135.5 ms
```

Output difference against the previous default `attn_out_default` outputs:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.00814965367 rmse=0.000723106961 rel_rms=0.00392144539 snr=48.13 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0119421482  rmse=0.00102670118  rel_rms=0.0042697907  snr=47.39 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0179472566 rmse=0.000640895974 rel_rms=0.00339150789 snr=49.39 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.016004622  rmse=0.000797672721 rel_rms=0.00416047116 snr=47.62 dB
```

Nsight Systems `test_clean.wav` STFT/iSTFT-related totals:

```text
fft2048_stage_kernel:             88 calls, 17.628 ms total, 200.3 us avg
istft2048_prepare_bitrev_kernel:   4 calls,  3.275 ms total, 818.8 us avg
window_sum_kernel:                 4 calls,  2.984 ms total, 746.0 us avg
stft2048_window_bitrev_kernel:     4 calls,  1.338 ms total, 334.4 us avg
istft2048_overlap_add_kernel:      4 calls,  0.647 ms total, 161.9 us avg
stft2048_extract_r2c_kernel:       4 calls,  0.316 ms total,  79.1 us avg
normalize_by_window_kernel:        4 calls,  0.142 ms total,  35.4 us avg
```

Interpretation:

- This removes cuFFT from the inference path using valid CUDA Tile semantics.
- The FFT path is not Tensor Core work, so comparing it to the 70 TFLOP/s BF16
  dense reference is not meaningful. The bottom-level bottleneck is global
  memory traffic and launch count: each 2048 FFT direction uses 11 full scratch
  passes, and `test_clean.wav` has `4 chunks * 2 directions * 11 stages = 88`
  `fft2048_stage_kernel` launches.
- The stage kernel alone moves roughly the same data many times through global
  memory. For the validation shape, that is approximately bandwidth-bound rather
  than compute-bound.
- Output drift is materially larger than BF16 write noise. The likely causes are
  different FFT operation order plus per-stage dynamic fast-math `sin/cos`
  twiddles. This is acceptable as an experiment result, but it should not be
  treated as numerically equivalent to the previous path.
- Next fixes should be radix-4/radix-8 CUDA Tile stage fusion to reduce global
  scratch passes, then a twiddle-table or higher-accuracy twiddle experiment to
  quantify whether the 0.3-0.4% final-output drift comes from fast-math twiddles
  or FFT operation order.

### Follow-up: radix-4-style two-stage fusion

The FFT schedule now fuses adjacent radix-2 stages into one CUDA Tile
`fft2048_two_stage_kernel`, leaving only the final 2048 stage as the original
single-stage kernel. Each direction becomes:

```text
5 x two-stage kernels: len = 2, 8, 32, 128, 512
1 x single-stage kernel: len = 2048
```

This reduces FFT stage launches from 11 to 6 per direction, while staying within
valid CUDA Tile semantics. The two-stage kernel reads four complex inputs,
computes two butterfly levels in registers, and writes one complex output.

Validation:

```text
test_clean.wav: 1108.7 ms
test.wav:       6592.9 ms
Nsight test_clean.wav: 1131.4 ms
```

Output difference versus the previous scratch FFT implementation:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.45326342e-08 snr=145.27 dB
  Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.47508234e-08 snr=145.23 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.49585157e-08 snr=145.20 dB
  Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.47908148e-08 snr=145.23 dB
```

So the fusion is numerically equivalent to the previous CUDA Tile scratch FFT at
final-audio scale. Output difference versus the older `attn_out_default` baseline
is unchanged:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=0.00392144539 snr=48.13 dB
  Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=0.0042697907  snr=47.39 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=0.00339150789 snr=49.39 dB
  Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=0.00416047146 snr=47.62 dB
```

Nsight Systems `test_clean.wav` stage totals:

```text
previous scratch FFT:
  fft2048_stage_kernel:     88 calls, 17.628 ms total

two-stage fused FFT:
  fft2048_two_stage_kernel: 40 calls,  7.959 ms total
  fft2048_stage_kernel:      8 calls,  1.606 ms total
  combined stage total:     48 calls,  9.565 ms total
```

Bottom-level cause and fix status:

- Cause addressed: too many full global scratch passes and launches. Stage total
  dropped by `8.063 ms` on `test_clean.wav`, a `45.7%` reduction for the FFT
  stage group.
- Remaining cause: the FFT is still a global-memory ping-pong algorithm, not an
  in-register/shared-memory FFT. It is still not Tensor Core work and should not
  be measured against the 70 TFLOP/s BF16 dense GEMM reference.
- Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
fft2048_two_stage_kernel: REG:62, SHARED:0, LOCAL:0, STACK:32
fft2048_stage_kernel:     REG:36, SHARED:0, LOCAL:0, STACK:32
```

### Follow-up: radix-8-style three-stage fusion negative result

`fft2048_three_stage_kernel` fuses three radix-2 stages at a time. The schedule
is:

```text
3 x three-stage kernels: len = 2, 16, 128
1 x two-stage kernel:    len = 1024
```

It is available only for experiments:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_STFT_FFT_THREE_STAGE=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
```

Validation:

```text
three-stage opt-in:
  test_clean.wav: 1108.6 ms
  test.wav:       6606.1 ms
  Nsight test_clean.wav: 1134.7 ms

default two-stage after guarding three-stage:
  test_clean.wav: 1118.8 ms
  test.wav:       6584.2 ms
```

Output difference versus the two-stage default remains at run-noise scale:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.39299933e-08 snr=145.36 dB
  Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.42929748e-08 snr=145.31 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.42299334e-08 snr=145.32 dB
  Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.42970813e-08 snr=145.30 dB
```

Nsight Systems `test_clean.wav` stage totals:

```text
two-stage default:
  fft2048_two_stage_kernel:   40 calls,  7.959 ms
  fft2048_stage_kernel:        8 calls,  1.606 ms
  combined stage total:       48 calls,  9.565 ms

three-stage opt-in:
  fft2048_three_stage_kernel: 24 calls, 10.421 ms
  fft2048_two_stage_kernel:    8 calls,  1.585 ms
  combined stage total:       32 calls, 12.006 ms
```

Bottom-level cause:

- Launch count decreased, but per-kernel work became too heavy. The three-stage
  kernel reads eight complex inputs and evaluates three levels of twiddles per
  output element.
- Register usage confirms the loss of scheduling headroom:

```text
fft2048_two_stage_kernel:   REG:62,  SHARED:0, LOCAL:0, STACK:32
fft2048_three_stage_kernel: REG:114, SHARED:0, LOCAL:0, STACK:32
```

Conclusion: three-stage fusion is a negative result on the current A10G shape.
The default remains the two-stage schedule. Future FFT work should try a more
balanced split, such as stage-specialized radix-4 with cheaper twiddle handling,
rather than blindly fusing more radix-2 stages.

## CUDA Tile Experiment: Time-Attention Main1280 KTile=32

This experiment narrows the split-tail time-attention main1280 kernel from
`QRows=64,KTile=64` to `QRows=64,KTile=32`. The goal is to test whether the
large `64x64` score/probability tile is the remaining limiter after the
`sum(probs_f32)` change.

Bench-only command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate
```

Relevant result:

```text
main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.962 ms, real_math=51.65 TF/s, roof70=73.8%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=16:
  best=4.016 ms, real_math=50.96 TF/s, roof70=72.8%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=32:
  best=3.809 ms, real_math=53.73 TF/s, roof70=76.8%

main1280_sum_f32_score_bf16 qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=3.938 ms, real_math=51.96 TF/s, roof70=74.2%

main1280_sum_f32_score_bf16 qrows=64 ktile=32:
  best=4.059 ms, real_math=50.41 TF/s, roof70=72.0%
```

Bench resource usage:

```text
main1280_sum_f32 q64/k64:             REG=255 SHARED=49152
main1280_sum_f32 q64/k32:             REG=243 SHARED=49152
main1280_sum_f32 q64/k16:             REG=202 SHARED=32768
main1280_sum_f32_score_bf16 q64/k64:  REG=254 SHARED=49152
main1280_sum_f32_score_bf16 q64/k32:  REG=243 SHARED=45056
```

Interpretation:

- `KTile=32` is a real bench-level win for the full online softmax path:
  `51.65 -> 53.73 TF/s`, or `73.8% -> 76.8%` of the accepted 70 TF/s A10G
  dense BF16 reference.
- `KTile=16` lowers resources but loses speed, so the bottom cause is not just
  register count. More K-loop iterations and MMA/tile scheduling overhead
  outweigh the smaller score tile.
- Rounding scaled QK scores to BF16 before softmax does not help. It slightly
  helps q64/k64 but loses badly with q64/k32, so "more BF16 staging" is not a
  free fix for this path.

Source-side status:

- The split-tail source kernel is now templated so the same binary can test
  the main1280 `KTile=32` path with:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K32=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
```

- Default source behavior remains `KTile=64`, because the bench win did not
  transfer cleanly to end-to-end timing.

Source resource usage:

```text
time_attention1301_q64_main1280_cutile_kernel<KTile=64,FullKTiles=20>:
  REG=255 SHARED=49152

time_attention1301_q64_main1280_cutile_kernel<KTile=32,FullKTiles=40>:
  REG=249 SHARED=49152
```

End-to-end checks with the same binary:

```text
default K64:
  test_clean.wav: 1100.7 ms
  test.wav:       6540.3 ms

K32 opt-in:
  test_clean.wav: 1105.0 ms
  earlier same-source K32 runs: test_clean.wav 1110.3 ms / 1103.1 ms,
                                test.wav 6542.6 ms
```

Output difference for K32 opt-in versus default K64 on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.00882762671 rms=0.000750731181 rel_rms=0.00407358409 snr=47.800 dB
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.00820866972 rms=0.000919932433 rel_rms=0.00382481570 snr=48.348 dB
```

Conclusion: `KTile=32` proves that the `64x64` softmax staging tile is a real
bench-level limiter, but the source path sees only a small resource reduction
and no stable end-to-end gain. Keep it as an opt-in A10G experiment, not the
default. The next attention experiments should look for a staging/layout change
that keeps the q64/k32 bench advantage while reducing source overhead, rather
than simply quantizing scores to BF16.

### Follow-up: legal CUDA Tile QRows/KTile sweep

CUDA Tile shapes are compile-time tile shapes, so this follow-up stays within
power-of-two dimensions. It compares the remaining legal `main1280_sum_f32`
combinations that were not covered by the previous q64/k32 test.

Command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate
```

Result:

```text
main1280_sum_f32 qrows=32 ktile=64:
  best=8.395 ms, real_math=24.37 TF/s, roof70=34.8%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=32 ktile=32:
  best=7.049 ms, real_math=29.03 TF/s, roof70=41.5%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=32:
  best=3.816 ms, real_math=53.62 TF/s, roof70=76.6%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=128:
  best=12.770 ms, real_math=16.02 TF/s, roof70=22.9%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=128 ktile=64:
  best=5.171 ms, real_math=39.57 TF/s, roof70=56.5%

main1280_sum_f32 qrows=128 ktile=32:
  best=3.873 ms, real_math=52.83 TF/s, roof70=75.5%
```

Resource usage:

```text
q32/k32:   REG=248 STACK=0   SHARED=58496
q32/k64:   REG=255 STACK=64  SHARED=81920
q64/k32:   REG=243 STACK=0   SHARED=49152
q64/k128:  REG=255 STACK=416 SHARED=98304
q128/k32:  REG=255 STACK=0   SHARED=49152
q128/k64:  REG=255 STACK=32  SHARED=65536
```

Interpretation:

- `q64/k32` remains the best valid CUDA Tile shape measured for this full
  online-softmax main1280 path: `53.62 TF/s`, `76.6%` of the 70 TF/s A10G
  dense BF16 reference.
- `q128/k32` is close but still lower at `52.83 TF/s`. It reaches `REG=255`,
  so the larger Q tile loses the register headroom that made `q64/k32` useful.
- `q64/k128` is a clear negative result: `REG=255`, `STACK=416`,
  `SHARED=98304`, and only `16.02 TF/s`. Larger K tiles reduce loop count but
  make the score/prob tile too heavy for A10G.
- `q32/*` leaves too little work per tile and does not keep Tensor Core work
  fed well enough.

Bottom cause: the useful A10G point for this CUDA Tile expression is not the
largest legal tile. The best measured balance is still `QRows=64,KTile=32`,
where the score/prob tile is small enough to avoid register saturation but
large enough to amortize the online softmax loop.

### Follow-up: source-shaped split-tail pair timing

The previous main1280 bench measured only the main query rows. The source path
launches two kernels in split-tail mode:

```text
main: query rows 0..1279, QRows=64, main KTile variable
tail: query rows 1280..1300, QRows=64, KTile=64
```

This follow-up measures the same two-launch pair in a single CUDA event range.

```text
split_tail_pair_sum_f32 main_ktile=64 tail_ktile=64:
  best=4.288 ms, real_math=48.50 TF/s, roof70=69.3%

split_tail_pair_sum_f32 main_ktile=32 tail_ktile=64:
  best=4.133 ms, real_math=50.32 TF/s, roof70=71.9%
```

Resource usage:

```text
bench main q64/k64: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
bench main q64/k32: REG=243 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
bench tail q64/k64 offset20: REG=232 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
```

Interpretation:

- The tail launch does not erase the `q64/k32` gain. The source-shaped pair
  still improves by `0.155 ms` for this attention shape.
- Therefore the earlier weak end-to-end transfer is not caused by the extra
  split-tail launch itself.
- The likely remaining causes are source codegen differences and whole-inference
  timing noise/interaction, not a fundamental K32 pair cost.

Source codegen follow-up:

The source main1280 kernel was changed from a fixed-q64 template to a
`<QRows,KTile>` template, with `FullKTiles` derived from `KTile`, to test whether
the source/bench register gap came from template shape. It did not:

```text
source main q64/k64: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
source main q64/k32: REG=249 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
```

Default source inference still runs after the signature-only change:

```text
test_clean.wav: 1113.8 ms
```

Conclusion: template parameter shape is not the bottom cause of the source
resource gap. The next useful step is to inspect codegen/SASS differences or
make the source kernel body closer to the bench body in smaller controlled
changes, while keeping K32 opt-in rather than default.

SASS opcode comparison:

```text
bench q64/k32:
  instructions=2977 max_R_seen=240
  LOP3=135 IADD3=114 SHF.R=75 PRMT=80 HMMA=160 MUFU.EX2=90

source q64/k32:
  instructions=3000 max_R_seen=246
  LOP3=141 IADD3=121 SHF.R=80 PRMT=81 HMMA=160 MUFU.EX2=90
```

Interpretation:

- HMMA and SFU/exp work are identical between bench and source.
- The source gap is in integer/address-generation code: extra `LOP3`, `IADD3`,
  `SHF`, and one extra `PRMT`, matching the `REG=249` versus `REG=243` gap.
- Top-level CMake CUDA flags are shared between `cudasep_infer` and the bench
  target, so this is not caused by different optimization flags.

A small source address-codegen experiment merged the four batch-base pointer
expressions into one `batch_offset`. It did not change the source resource or
opcode counts:

```text
source q64/k32 after batch_offset merge:
  REG=249 SHARED=49152
  instructions=3000 max_R_seen=246
  LOP3=141 IADD3=121 SHF.R=80 PRMT=81 HMMA=160 MUFU.EX2=90
```

Default CLI validation after these source-shape/codegen probes:

```text
test_clean.wav: 1113.8 ms
test.wav:       6548.5 ms
```

Conclusion: the current source/bench register gap is specifically address
generation code emitted around the same CUDA Tile math expression. Simple C++
template reshaping and common batch-base CSE are not enough to fix it. The next
useful experiment should isolate the view/addressing expression itself, for
example by changing how `tensor_span`/`partition_view` is constructed or by
testing a source-side microbench in the same translation unit as
`mbr_cuda_tile.cu`.

### Follow-up: direct pointer addressing is a negative result

This bench-only experiment bypasses `partition_view` for the main1280 q64/k32
kernel and constructs Q/K/V tiles with direct pointer-plus-offset
`ct::load` / `ct::load_masked`. The purpose is to test whether the
`tensor_span` / `partition_view` / `layout_left` view layer is the source of the
extra source address-generation code.

Result:

```text
main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=32:
  best=3.813 ms, real_math=53.67 TF/s, roof70=76.7%

main1280_direct_ptr_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=32:
  best=10.067 ms, real_math=20.33 TF/s, roof70=29.0%
```

Resource usage:

```text
partition_view q64/k32: REG=243 SHARED=49152
direct_ptr q64/k32:     REG=188 SHARED=32768
```

SASS opcode comparison:

```text
partition_view q64/k32:
  HMMA=160 LDSM.M88=32 LDSM.MT88=32 LDG.E.128=8 STS=40
  MUFU.EX2=90 F2FP.BF16.PACK_AB=56

direct_ptr q64/k32:
  HMMA=64  LDSM.M88=0  LDSM.MT88=0  LDG.E.128=0 STS=0
  MUFU.EX2=36 F2FP.BF16.PACK_AB=32 LDG.E.U16=91 PRMT=240 LOP3=238
```

Interpretation:

- Direct pointer addressing reduces register and shared-memory usage, but it
  destroys the Tensor Core-friendly tile load/staging pattern.
- The compiler no longer emits the same `LDSM`/shared staging sequence and the
  effective HMMA count drops from `160` to `64`.
- The runtime loss is severe: `53.67 -> 20.33 TF/s`.

Conclusion: do not bypass CUDA Tile `partition_view` for this attention path.
The view layer is not just syntactic glue; it is carrying layout information the
compiler uses to generate the high-throughput Tensor Core path. The source/bench
gap must be solved within the view-based formulation, not by replacing views
with scalar-ish direct pointer offset tiles.

### Source default change: split-tail main K32

Nsight Systems was used to decide whether the q64/k32 source path should remain
opt-in or become the default. Unlike noisy end-to-end CLI timing, the kernel
summary isolates the actual source kernels.

Commands:

```bash
nsys profile --trace=cuda --stats=true --force-overwrite=true \
  -o /tmp/mbr_k64_profile ./build-min/cudasep_infer ... test_clean.wav

CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K32=1 \
nsys profile --trace=cuda --stats=true --force-overwrite=true \
  -o /tmp/mbr_k32_profile ./build-min/cudasep_infer ... test_clean.wav
```

Kernel result:

```text
K64 default before change:
  time_attention1301_main1280_cutile_kernel<64,64>
  instances=48 total=191.840564 ms avg=3.996678 ms

K32 opt-in before change:
  time_attention1301_main1280_cutile_kernel<64,32>
  instances=48 total=186.645184 ms avg=3.888441 ms

Tail kernel:
  K64 run total=14.765361 ms avg=0.307612 ms
  K32 run total=14.836835 ms avg=0.309101 ms
```

Interpretation:

- The source K32 main kernel is genuinely faster despite using `REG=249`
  instead of the bench kernel's `REG=243`.
- K32 saves about `5.20 ms` over 48 main time-attention calls on
  `test_clean.wav`; the tail kernel changes by only about `0.07 ms`.
- The earlier end-to-end ambiguity was caused by whole-inference noise and
  small effect size, not by K32 being slower in source.

Default behavior:

- Split-tail time attention now uses main `KTile=32` by default.
- To force the old main `KTile=64` path:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_K32=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
# or
CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_CUTILE_SPLIT_TAIL_K32=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
```

Resource usage:

```text
source main q64/k64: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
source main q64/k32: REG=249 STACK=0 SHARED=49152
```

Validation after changing the default:

```text
test_clean.wav: 1103.4 ms
test.wav:       6533.6 ms
```

Output difference against the K64 split-tail output on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.00882765651 rms=0.000750731194 rel_rms=0.00407358416 snr=47.800 dB
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.00820866972 rms=0.000919932442 rel_rms=0.00382481574 snr=48.348 dB
```

Conclusion: K32 is now the default for split-tail main time attention. This is
a measured source-kernel win with a known output drift around `0.38-0.41%`
relative RMS on `test_clean.wav`.

## CUDA Tile Fusion Probe: FFN1 GELU Into FFN2

This diagnostic bench checks whether the long-M FFN block should move beyond
same-kernel tile-shape tweaks. The current inference structure writes the
`M x 1024` FFN1 GELU activation to global memory, then FFN2 reads it back:

```text
FFN1: M=78048, N=1024, K=256, bias + erf-GELU
FFN2: M=78048, N=256,  K=1024, bias
activation write + read: about 0.298 GiB per FFN pair launch
```

`bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile` compares that two-launch path with a one-kernel
CUDA Tile fusion that computes a `32 x 64` hidden chunk, applies GELU, casts to
BF16, and immediately feeds it into the FFN2 MMA. The fused variants differ by
how much FFN2 output they cover per tile:

```text
fused64:  out_tile=64,  recomputes FFN1 4 times
fused128: out_tile=128, recomputes FFN1 2 times
fused256: out_tile=256, no FFN1 recompute
```

Command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --warmup 3 --iters 20
```

Result:

```text
separate out_tile= 64 launches=2 recompute_ffn1=1
  best=2.697 ms median=2.702 ms
  useful=30.34 TF/s, roof70=43.3%
  actual=30.34 TF/s, roof70=43.3%

fused64  out_tile= 64 launches=1 recompute_ffn1=4
  best=6.513 ms median=6.520 ms
  useful=12.56 TF/s, roof70=17.9%
  actual=31.41 TF/s, roof70=44.9%

fused128 out_tile=128 launches=1 recompute_ffn1=2
  best=3.630 ms median=3.635 ms
  useful=22.55 TF/s, roof70=32.2%
  actual=33.82 TF/s, roof70=48.3%

fused256 out_tile=256 launches=1 recompute_ffn1=1
  best=2.544 ms median=2.554 ms
  useful=32.16 TF/s, roof70=45.9%
  actual=32.16 TF/s, roof70=45.9%
```

Resource usage:

```text
ffn1_gelu_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,...>: REG=101 SHARED=61440
ffn2_bf16_kernel<32,64,64,...>:      REG=230 SHARED=98304
ffn12_fused_recompute<64>:           REG=230 SHARED=90112
ffn12_fused_recompute<128>:          REG=232 SHARED=94208
ffn12_fused_recompute<256>:          REG=254 SHARED=86016
```

Interpretation:

- The "avoid global memory" hypothesis is real only when the fused kernel covers
  the full `Nout=256`. `fused256` avoids the `M x 1024` activation write/read
  without recomputing FFN1, improving the standalone FFN pair from `2.697` to
  `2.544 ms`.
- `fused64` and `fused128` demonstrate the bottom-level failure mode: their
  actual Tensor Core throughput is around `31-34 TF/s`, but useful model
  throughput collapses because FFN1 is recomputed for each output tile group.
- `fused256` is now the next source-path candidate, but it is already at
  `REG=254`. The next implementation step must check real inference scheduling,
  occupancy, and whether the 12-row tail can be handled without erasing the
  gain.
- The remaining gap to 70 TF/s is mostly not global-memory traffic anymore in
  this fused probe. It is high register pressure from the `32 x 256` output
  accumulator plus GELU SFU/post-op work inside the same Tile kernel.

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8: pass
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
static purity search for cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw WMMA/asm: no matches
ldd cudasep_infer cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN search: no matches
git diff --check: pass
test_clean.wav BF16 CLI inference: 1106.3 ms
```

Conclusion: move FFN1->FFN2 fusion forward only with the full-width
`out_tile=256` shape. Narrower output tiles are useful as proof of the recompute
trap, but should not enter the main inference path.

## Source Experiment: Opt-In FFN12 Fused256 Path

The standalone FFN12 fusion probe was promoted into the real inference path as
an opt-in experiment:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer ...
```

Trigger constraints:

```text
BF16 mode enabled
input total rows = 78060
linear1: 256 -> 1024, BF16 weight
linear2: 1024 -> 256, BF16 weight
no B(K,N) FFN weight path
default erf-GELU path only
```

Implementation:

```text
full rows 0..78047:
  ffn12_fused256_cutile_kernel<FullBF16>
  one Tile kernel, out_tile=256, no FFN1 recompute

tail rows 78048..78059:
  ffn12_tail_ffn1_gelu_cutile_kernel<FullBF16>
  ffn12_tail_ffn2_cutile_kernel<FullBF16>
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
ffn12_fused256_cutile_kernel<false>:    REG=254 SHARED=86016
ffn12_tail_ffn1_gelu_kernel<false>:     REG=142 SHARED=61440
ffn12_tail_ffn2_kernel<false>:          REG=247 SHARED=81920

ffn12_fused256_cutile_kernel<true>:     REG=254 SHARED=86016
ffn12_tail_ffn1_gelu_kernel<true>:      REG=148 SHARED=61440
ffn12_tail_ffn2_kernel<true>:           REG=254 SHARED=81920
```

End-to-end validation on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default source path:
  inference: 1114.3 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1:
  inference: 1067.9 ms
  gain: 46.4 ms
```

Output difference versus same-binary default on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:
  max_abs=0.00461533666 rms=0.000529229788 rel_rms=0.00287005251 snr=50.842 dB

Instrumental.wav:
  max_abs=0.00567135960 rms=0.000705080315 rel_rms=0.00293215526 snr=50.656 dB
```

Nsight kernel-level comparison on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default FFN pair:
  FFN1 main: 160.027 ms / 96 calls
  FFN1 tail:   4.614 ms / 96 calls
  FFN2 main: 105.152 ms / 96 calls
  FFN2 tail:  17.857 ms / 96 calls
  total:     287.650 ms

fused FFN12:
  fused main: 247.808 ms / 96 calls
  tail FFN1:   0.714 ms / 96 calls
  tail FFN2:   3.304 ms / 96 calls
  total:     251.826 ms

kernel-level gain:
  35.824 ms
```

Long-input validation:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1 test.wav:
  inference: 6249.0 ms

recent default K32 reference:
  inference: 6533.6 ms

observed gain:
  284.6 ms
```

Full-BF16 arithmetic validation:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1 \
CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1069.5 ms

default fused arithmetic:
  inference: 1067.9 ms
```

Interpretation:

- The source-path result confirms the standalone bench: full-width `out_tile=256`
  FFN12 fusion is useful on A10G because it removes the `M x 1024` activation
  write/read and one full FFN launch without recomputing FFN1.
- The tail path is now much cheaper than the generic linear tails:
  `22.471 ms -> 4.018 ms` over 96 FFN pairs.
- The bottom-level limiter is still register pressure. The full fused kernel is
  already at `REG=254`, so further speedup cannot come from simply adding more
  work to this CTA. The next useful direction is reducing accumulator/register
  pressure or splitting the fused work with a better Tile pipeline while keeping
  FFN1 activation off global memory.
- Compile time for `mbr_cuda_tile.cu` increased noticeably because both
  `FullBF16=false` and `FullBF16=true` fused instances live in the main
  translation unit. If this path becomes default, move the FFN12 kernels to an
  isolated compilation unit or keep only the chosen arithmetic mode in the main
  binary.

Validation:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
test_clean.wav default: pass, 1114.3 ms
test_clean.wav fused:   pass, 1067.9 ms
test_clean.wav fused + CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1: pass, 1069.5 ms
test.wav fused:         pass, 6249.0 ms
static purity search for cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw WMMA/asm: no matches
```

### FFN12 Fused Shape Follow-Up: M16 And Hidden32

The first source fused path is register-limited (`REG=254`), so the next A10G
shape sweep tested whether smaller tiles could reduce pressure without
recomputing FFN1.

Bench command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256 --warmup 3 --iters 20
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_m16_256 --warmup 3 --iters 20
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_h32_256 --warmup 3 --iters 20
```

Bench results:

```text
fused256:
  tile=32x256, hidden_tile=64
  best=2.396 ms
  useful=34.16 TF/s, roof70=48.8%
  REG=254 SHARED=86016

fused_m16_256:
  tile=16x256, hidden_tile=64
  best=3.057 ms
  useful=26.77 TF/s, roof70=38.2%
  REG=250 SHARED=96256

fused_h32_256:
  tile=32x256, hidden_tile=32
  best=2.337 ms
  useful=35.02 TF/s, roof70=50.0%
  REG=226 SHARED=88064
```

Bench interpretation:

- Reducing `M` from 32 to 16 is a clear negative result. Register usage barely
  drops (`254 -> 250`) and shared memory increases (`86016 -> 96256`), while
  useful throughput falls to `26.77 TF/s`.
- Reducing only the hidden chunk from 64 to 32 looks positive in the standalone
  bench: `34.16 -> 35.02 TF/s`, with `REG=226`.

Source validation of hidden_tile=32:

```text
source hidden_tile=32 resource:
  ffn12_fused256<false>: REG=242 SHARED=88064
  ffn12_fused256<true>:  REG=230 SHARED=88064

test_clean.wav opt-in source hidden_tile=32:
  inference: 1065.8 ms

test_clean.wav opt-in source hidden_tile=32 + FullBF16:
  inference: 1064.2 ms

Nsight source hidden_tile=32:
  ffn12_fused256 main: 250.972 ms / 96 calls

Nsight source hidden_tile=64 reference:
  ffn12_fused256 main: 247.808 ms / 96 calls
```

Decision:

- Do not promote hidden_tile=32 into the source path yet. It is bench-positive
  but source-kernel-negative under Nsight (`250.972 ms` vs `247.808 ms`).
- Keep the source fused path at `hidden_tile=64`.
- Bottom-level cause: lowering hidden chunk reduces register pressure in bench,
  but source codegen does not preserve the same gain. The smaller hidden-K MMA
  loop doubles the number of hidden chunks and appears to lose enough Tile
  backend scheduling efficiency to outweigh the register drop.
- Next direction: inspect SASS/opcode differences between bench and source for
  the H32/H64 fused kernels before changing source shape again.

### FFN12 Fused GELU Mode Experiment

The H64 fused kernel remained limited to about half of the accepted A10G dense
BF16 roof. SASS showed that the FFN12 fused kernel keeps the same Tensor Core
work but also executes a large scalar erf-GELU block between FFN1 and FFN2.

This experiment parameterized the FFN12 fused CUDA Tile kernel by GELU mode.
Default behavior remains erf. The source path now also accepts isolated FFN12
flags:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_HARD_GELU=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_QUICK_GELU=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_TANH_GELU=1
```

Standalone bench commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile cudasep_infer -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_hard --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_quick --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_tanh --warmup 3 --iters 30
```

Standalone results:

```text
fused256 erf:
  best=2.395 ms
  useful=34.17 TF/s, roof70=48.8%

fused256_hard:
  best=2.184 ms
  useful=37.47 TF/s, roof70=53.5%

fused256_quick:
  best=2.345 ms
  useful=34.90 TF/s, roof70=49.9%

fused256_tanh:
  best=2.240 ms
  useful=36.53 TF/s, roof70=52.2%
```

Bench SASS summary:

```text
erf:
  inst=3528 HMMA=256 MUFU=66 FFMA=492 FADD=269 FMUL=230
  F2FP=102 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=251

hard:
  inst=2184 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=32 FADD=128 FMUL=64
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252

quick:
  inst=2944 HMMA=256 MUFU=66 FFMA=300 FADD=162 FMUL=96
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=251

tanh:
  inst=2696 HMMA=256 MUFU=64 FFMA=224 FADD=160 FMUL=224
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252
```

Source validation on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default:
  inference: 1110.9 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1:
  inference: 1059.9 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_HARD_GELU=1:
  inference: 1028.7 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_QUICK_GELU=1:
  inference: 1058.1 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_TANH_GELU=1:
  inference: 1050.6 ms
```

Nsight source kernel summary:

```text
erf fused main:
  total=247.830 ms / 96 calls
  useful=31.70 TF/s, roof70=45.3%

hard fused main:
  total=214.945 ms / 96 calls
  useful=36.55 TF/s, roof70=52.2%

hard tail FFN1:
  total=0.596 ms / 96 calls

hard tail FFN2:
  total=3.306 ms / 96 calls
```

Source SASS summary:

```text
erf main:
  inst=3568 HMMA=256 MUFU=66 FFMA=492 FADD=298 FMUL=224
  F2FP=100 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=251

hard main:
  inst=2176 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=32 FADD=128 FMUL=64
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252

erf tail FFN1:
  inst=1760 HMMA=64 MUFU=34 FFMA=252 FADD=118 FMUL=112 max_R=139

hard tail FFN1:
  inst=1040 HMMA=64 MUFU=0 FFMA=16 FADD=32 FMUL=32 max_R=145
```

Quality drift versus erf fused output:

```text
quick_vs_erf_fused:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=1.093810678 rms=0.146515071 rel_rms=0.794527840 snr=1.998 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=1.108750582 rms=0.147147045 rel_rms=0.611943610 snr=4.266 dB

tanh_vs_erf_fused:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=1.098173141 rms=0.146224216 rel_rms=0.792950576 snr=2.015 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=1.113588214 rms=0.146892458 rel_rms=0.610884856 snr=4.281 dB

hard_vs_erf_fused:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.994915307 rms=0.232009888 rel_rms=1.258152580 snr=-1.995 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.994631171 rms=0.234362796 rel_rms=0.974649649 snr=0.223 dB
```

Interpretation:

- The Tensor Core part did not change: HMMA, LDSM, LDG, STG, and STS counts are
  effectively unchanged between erf and hard.
- Hard GELU proves the bottom-level issue: the current erf-GELU scalar block is
  a real limiter. Removing `ct::exp` drops `MUFU=66 -> 0` and reduces the main
  source kernel from `247.830 ms` to `214.945 ms`.
- This only raises the source main kernel to `36.55 TF/s`, or `52.2%` of the
  accepted 70 TF/s roof. The remaining gap is not caused by global memory
  bandwidth; it is mainly one-CTA-per-SM pressure from `REG~252` and
  `SHARED=86016`, plus scalar activation/control work interrupting the MMA
  pipeline.
- Hard/quick/tanh cannot be promoted as correctness-preserving optimizations.
  They change the activation semantics enough to produce large audio drift.
- The useful next fix is not "use hard GELU"; it is to fit or derive a
  semantically compatible no-exp approximation for the current erf-GELU function
  over the observed FFN1 activation range, then repeat the same SASS/source
  timing/quality checks.

Decision:

- Keep the default FFN12 fused source path on erf.
- Keep the isolated FFN12 GELU flags as experimental switches.
- Continue optimization by replacing the expensive erf block with a close
  approximation to the current function, not by changing to a different GELU
  family.

### FFN12 Fused No-Exp Erf-GELU Approximation

The hard/quick/tanh experiment proved that `ct::exp`/`MUFU` in the current
erf-GELU block is a real limiter, but those modes changed the activation
semantics too much. This experiment keeps the current erf-GELU family and
approximates its `erf(x)` term with odd polynomials:

```text
erf(x) ~= sign(x) * clamp(abs(x) * P(abs(x)^2), 0, 1)
gelu(x) = 0.5 * x * (1 + erf(x))
```

The coefficients were least-squares fitted on `abs(x) in [0, 2.5]` against the
same erf approximation already used by the kernel.

Math-only error over `x in [-8, 8]`:

```text
poly5_l25:
  max_abs=0.004753677
  rms=0.000839799

poly7_l25:
  max_abs=0.000513003
  rms=0.000067214
```

Standalone bench commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_poly5 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_poly7 --warmup 3 --iters 30
```

Standalone results:

```text
fused256 erf:
  best=2.395 ms
  useful=34.16 TF/s, roof70=48.8%

fused256_poly5:
  best=2.206 ms
  useful=37.10 TF/s, roof70=53.0%

fused256_poly7:
  best=2.233 ms
  useful=36.65 TF/s, roof70=52.4%
```

Standalone SASS:

```text
erf:
  inst=3528 HMMA=256 MUFU=66 FFMA=492 FADD=269 FMUL=230
  F2FP=102 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=251

poly5:
  inst=2464 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=128 FADD=192 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252

poly7:
  inst=2528 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=192 FADD=192 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252
```

Source flags:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY5_GELU=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY7_GELU=1
```

Source `test_clean.wav` validation:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY5_GELU=1:
  inference: 1033.0 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY7_GELU=1:
  inference: 1033.5 ms
```

Long-input validation:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY7_GELU=1
test.wav:
  inference: 6106.4 ms

erf fused reference:
  inference: 6244.7 ms

observed gain:
  138.3 ms
```

Nsight source kernel summary:

```text
erf fused main:
  total=247.830 ms / 96 calls
  useful=31.70 TF/s, roof70=45.3%

poly5 fused main:
  total=221.513 ms / 96 calls
  useful=35.47 TF/s, roof70=50.7%

poly7 fused main:
  total=222.038 ms / 96 calls
  useful=35.38 TF/s, roof70=50.5%
```

Source SASS:

```text
erf:
  inst=3568 HMMA=256 MUFU=66 FFMA=492 FADD=298 FMUL=224
  F2FP=100 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=251

poly5:
  inst=2496 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=128 FADD=224 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252

poly7:
  inst=2584 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=192 FADD=224 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252
```

Quality drift versus erf fused output:

```text
poly5_vs_erf:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.065674782 rms=0.003419906 rel_rms=0.018545608 snr=34.635 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.070242941 rms=0.003558654 rel_rms=0.014799452 snr=36.595 dB

poly7_vs_erf:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.009322912 rms=0.000744643 rel_rms=0.004038082 snr=47.876 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.008458808 rms=0.000904299 rel_rms=0.003760727 snr=48.495 dB

poly7_vs_erf on test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.025418818 rms=0.000785465 rel_rms=0.004156382 snr=47.626 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.030415744 rms=0.000939779 rel_rms=0.004901769 snr=46.193 dB
```

Interpretation:

- Poly5/poly7 solve the same root cause as hard GELU (`MUFU=66 -> 0`) while
  staying much closer to the current erf-GELU semantics.
- Poly7 is the better source candidate: it keeps quality drift in the same
  rough range as other aggressive BF16 experiments while still lifting the
  source main kernel from `31.70 TF/s` to `35.38 TF/s`.
- The remaining gap to 70 TF/s is not the exp anymore. It is now mostly the
  fused kernel structure: `REG~252`, `SHARED=86016`, one resident CTA per SM,
  and scalar polynomial/control work between the FFN1 and FFN2 MMA segments.
- Poly5 is faster in the standalone bench but has noticeably larger audio drift.

Decision:

- Keep erf as default for now.
- Keep poly5/poly7 as isolated source flags.
- Next useful direction is to improve the polynomial under the real FFN1
  activation distribution, then reduce CTA resource pressure or split the fused
  work into a better CUDA Tile pipeline without writing FFN1 activation to
  global memory.

#### Poly9 Follow-Up

After poly7, a 9-term odd polynomial was fitted on `abs(x) in [0, 3.0]`.
It reduces math-only approximation error without reintroducing `exp`.

Math-only error over `x in [-8, 8]`:

```text
poly9_l30:
  max_abs=0.000080603
  rms=0.000012677
```

Standalone bench:

```text
fused256_poly7:
  best=2.234 ms
  useful=36.63 TF/s, roof70=52.3%

fused256_poly9:
  best=2.207 ms
  useful=37.09 TF/s, roof70=53.0%
```

Standalone SASS:

```text
poly7:
  inst=2528 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=192 FADD=192 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252

poly9:
  inst=2592 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=256 FADD=192 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252
```

Source validation:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1
test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1033.9 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1
test.wav:
  inference: 6088.8 ms

erf fused test.wav reference:
  inference: 6244.7 ms

observed long-input gain:
  155.9 ms
```

Nsight source kernel summary:

```text
poly9 fused main:
  total=221.729 ms / 96 calls
  useful=35.43 TF/s, roof70=50.6%

poly9 tail FFN1:
  total=0.636 ms / 96 calls

poly9 tail FFN2:
  total=3.304 ms / 96 calls
```

Source SASS:

```text
poly7:
  inst=2584 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=192 FADD=224 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252

poly9:
  inst=2656 HMMA=256 MUFU=0 FFMA=256 FADD=224 FMUL=160
  F2FP=96 LDSM=128 LDG=96 STG=8 STS=44 max_R=252
```

Quality drift versus erf fused output:

```text
poly9_vs_erf on test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.007306457 rms=0.000703915 rel_rms=0.003817217 snr=48.365 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.009483796 rms=0.000890899 rel_rms=0.003705002 snr=48.624 dB

poly9_vs_erf on test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.010033607 rms=0.000684009 rel_rms=0.003619517 snr=48.827 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.010342345 rms=0.000836987 rel_rms=0.004365620 snr=47.199 dB
```

Interpretation:

- Poly9 is currently a better isolated candidate than poly7: similar or better
  source speed, lower math error, and slightly lower measured audio drift.
- It does not solve the main roof gap. The source main kernel remains around
  `35.43 TF/s` (`50.6%` of 70 TF/s) because CTA resource pressure and scalar
  work between MMA phases still limit Tensor Core saturation.
- The compile cost of adding another source mode was again high because all
  FFN12 fused modes still live in `mbr_cuda_tile.cu`. Before broader FFN12
  shape/mode sweeps, move these kernels to an isolated translation unit.

Decision:

- Keep erf as default.
- Keep poly9 as the best current opt-in no-exp erf-GELU candidate:
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1`.
- Next source-facing work should either collect real FFN1 activation
  distribution for a better polynomial fit or split FFN12 fused into its own
  CUDA Tile `.cu` to make further A10G sweeps practical.

### FFN12 Source TU Split

The FFN12 fused kernels and launch wrapper were moved from the main
`mbr_cuda_tile.cu` translation unit into `src/mbr_ffn12_cutile.cu`.

Purpose:

- keep future FFN12 shape/mode sweeps from recompiling the very large main MBR
  CUDA Tile translation unit;
- keep the source path on CUDA Tile (`__tile_global__`, `ct::mma`,
  `ct::partition_view`, `ct::assume_aligned`);
- preserve the current opt-in behavior and all GELU mode flags.

Implementation:

```text
src/mbr_ffn12_cutile.cu:
  ffn12_fused256_cutile_kernel<GeluMode, FullBF16>
  ffn12_tail_ffn1_gelu_cutile_kernel<GeluMode, FullBF16>
  ffn12_tail_ffn2_cutile_kernel<FullBF16>
  launch_ffn12_fused256_cutile(...)

src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu:
  try_feedforward_fused(...) now selects an integer GELU mode and calls the
  external launch wrapper.
```

Incremental compile check:

```text
touch src/mbr_ffn12_cutile.cu
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

observed rebuild:
  [1/2] Building CUDA object CMakeFiles/cudasep_infer.dir/src/mbr_ffn12_cutile.cu.o
  [2/2] Linking CXX executable cudasep_infer
```

Validation:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1 test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1065.8 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1
test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1037.8 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1
test.wav:
  inference: 6111.3 ms

poly9 split-cleaned output vs pre-split poly9:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.000000238 rms=0.000000010 snr=145.345 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.000000238 rms=0.000000013 snr=145.263 dB
```

Interpretation:

- The split preserves numerical behavior; the observed output delta is at
  float WAV quantization/noise level.
- Future FFN12-only CUDA Tile experiments can now iterate by rebuilding the new
  FFN12 TU instead of the whole `mbr_cuda_tile.cu` file.
- Source line count increases because the FFN12 TU currently owns its own GELU
  helper copies. That is a deliberate tradeoff for fast experimentation; if the
  selected GELU mode stabilizes, delete unused modes or factor the tiny helper
  set into a shared internal header.

### FFN12 CUDA Tile Occupancy Hint Probe

After the TU split, the next FFN12 resource-pressure experiment tested whether
the CUDA Tile `occupancy` hint could force a better A10G codegen/resource shape
for the current `32 x 256` fused FFN12 kernel.

Implementation:

```text
bench/bf16_ffn12_cutile.cu:
  fused256_occ2 -> [[cutile::hint(860, occupancy=2)]]
  fused256_occ4 -> [[cutile::hint(860, occupancy=4)]]
```

Command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_occ2 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused256_occ4 --warmup 3 --iters 30
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile
```

Result:

```text
fused256:
  best=2.396 ms, useful=34.16 TF/s, roof70=48.8%
  REG=254, SHARED=86016

fused256_occ2:
  best=2.861 ms, useful=28.60 TF/s, roof70=40.9%
  REG=208, SHARED=32904

fused256_occ4:
  best=2.395 ms, useful=34.17 TF/s, roof70=48.8%
  REG=254, SHARED=86016
```

Interpretation:

- `occupancy=2` is a useful negative result: it does make the generated kernel
  much smaller (`REG=254 -> 208`, `SHARED=86016 -> 32904`), but throughput
  drops by about 16.3%.
- Therefore the current FFN12 limiter is not solved by simply forcing more
  resident CTAs. The hinted schedule likely loses more MMA/tile scheduling
  efficiency than it gains from occupancy.
- `occupancy=4` is effectively ignored or converges to the default resource
  shape on this kernel.
- The next FFN12 fix should target the accumulator/tile pipeline itself:
  reduce live output accumulator pressure or stage partial output differently
  without destroying Tensor Core scheduling, instead of relying on global
  occupancy hints.

### CUDA Tile Benchmark Target Cleanup

The default CMake benchmark target set and the benchmark source tree were
tightened to current CUDA Tile experiments only.

Removed from `CMakeLists.txt`:

```text
bench_bf16_split_qkv_rotary
bench_bf16_rms_norm
bench_bf16_time_attention_fused
bench_fft2048_custom
```

The corresponding historical raw CUDA `.cu` probes were also deleted:

```text
bench/bf16_split_qkv_rotary.cu
bench/bf16_rms_norm.cu
bench/bf16_time_attention_fused.cu
bench/fft2048_custom.cu
```

Kept:

```text
bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm
bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile
bench_bf16_freq_attention_cutile
bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile
```

Validation:

```text
cmake -S . -B build-min -G Ninja -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=86: pass
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile bench_bf16_freq_attention_cutile bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8: pass
rg old raw benchmark target names in CMakeLists.txt and build-min/build.ninja: no matches
src ordinary __global__ search: no matches
bench ordinary __global__ search: no matches
static cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm search: no matches
```

The retained `bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile` initializer was also converted
from a raw `__global__` helper to a CUDA Tile `__tile_global__` helper. Validation
after the conversion:

```text
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 1 --iters 1 --validate: pass
validate BH0 rows=3 max_abs=0 rms=0
main1280_sum_f32 qrows=64 ktile=32: 53.66 TF/s, roof70=76.7%
```

Interpretation:

- The active build graph and retained benchmark `.cu` files are now CUDA
  Tile-first.
- Historical results remain documented in this README, but the raw CUDA probe
  source files are no longer kept as current project code.

### Time Attention Exp2 Softmax Probe

The best current time-attention shape is the split-tail `q64/k32` main path.
The benchmark already had an `exp2` softmax variant for `k64`; this probe added
the missing `q64/k32 + SumF32 + exp2` variant:

```text
time_attention1301_main1280_kernel<QRows=64, KTile=32, SumF32=true, UseExp2=true>
```

Benchmark command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate
```

Filtered benchmark result:

```text
main1280_sum_f32      qrows=64 ktile=32: best=3.811 ms, 53.70 TF/s, roof70=76.7%
main1280_sum_f32_exp2 qrows=64 ktile=32: best=3.403 ms, 60.14 TF/s, roof70=85.9%
validate BH0 rows=3 max_abs=0 rms=0
```

Benchmark resource usage:

```text
q64/k32 exp:  REG=243, SHARED=49152
q64/k32 exp2: REG=237, SHARED=40960
```

This is a positive A10G result: `ct::exp2(x * log2e)` gives a lighter generated
Tile resource shape and a much faster fused-attention main path than `ct::exp(x)`
for the current `q64/k32` shape.

The result was first ported to the source time-attention split-tail path behind:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_EXP2=1
```

After source validation, it was promoted to the default BF16 time-attention
path. The fallback switch is:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_EXP2=1
```

Post-promotion smoke on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default BF16 exp2: 1091.0 ms
disabled exp2:     1106.8 ms
default gain:      15.8 ms, about 1.4% end-to-end

Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.008033842 rms=0.000661757 rel_rms=0.003588759 snr=48.901 dB
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.009507239 rms=0.000811496 rel_rms=0.003374699 snr=49.435 dB
```

Source validation on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default BF16: 1106.2 ms
exp2 opt-in: 1088.5 ms
gain: 17.7 ms, about 1.6% end-to-end

Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.008033842 rms=0.000661757 rel_rms=0.003588759 snr=48.901 dB
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.009507239 rms=0.000811496 rel_rms=0.003374699 snr=49.435 dB
```

Source validation on `test.wav`:

```text
default BF16: 6514.9 ms
exp2 opt-in: 6424.9 ms
gain: 90.0 ms, about 1.4% end-to-end

Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.007719191 rms=0.000578711 rel_rms=0.003062264 snr=50.279 dB
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.007963240 rms=0.000735360 rel_rms=0.003835548 snr=48.323 dB
```

Source resource usage:

```text
main1280 q64/k32 exp:  REG=249, SHARED=49152
main1280 q64/k32 exp2: REG=233, SHARED=40960
tail q64/k64 exp:      REG=232, SHARED=32768
tail q64/k64 exp2:     REG=234, SHARED=32768
```

Interpretation:

- The speedup comes from the main1280 body. The tail kernel is too small to
  matter and does not improve resource shape.
- The output drift is in the same rough range as other accepted BF16/softmax
  approximation experiments in this project.
- This is now the default source-backed time-attention math variant for BF16
  inference, with the disable switch kept for A/B and quality regression checks.

### Time Attention Source TU Split

The source time-attention CUDA Tile kernels were moved out of
`src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu` into `src/mbr_time_attention_cutile.cu`.

Purpose:

- keep A10G time-attention shape/math sweeps from recompiling the large main
  MBR CUDA Tile translation unit;
- keep the source path on CUDA Tile (`__tile_global__`, `ct::mma`,
  `ct::partition_view`, `ct::reduce_max`, `ct::sum`);
- preserve the default `exp2` time-attention behavior and the
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_EXP2=1` fallback.

Implementation:

```text
src/mbr_time_attention_cutile.cu:
  time_attention1301_main1280_cutile_kernel<QRows, KTile, UseExp2>
  time_attention1301_cutile_qk_softmax_av_kernel<QRows, KTile, QBlockOffset, UseExp2>
  launch_time_attention1301_split_tail_cutile(...)
  launch_time_attention1301_full_cutile(...)

src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu:
  scaled_dot_product_attention(...) now keeps only shape/flag selection and
  calls the external launch wrappers.
```

Incremental compile check:

```text
touch src/mbr_time_attention_cutile.cu
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

observed rebuild:
  [1/2] Building CUDA object CMakeFiles/cudasep_infer.dir/src/mbr_time_attention_cutile.cu.o
  [2/2] Linking CXX executable cudasep_infer
```

Validation:

```text
test_clean.wav after split:
  inference: 1093.0 ms

output diff vs pre-split exp2 default:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.000000238 rms=0.000000010 rel_rms=0.000000054 snr=145.321 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.000000179 rms=0.000000013 rel_rms=0.000000055 snr=145.270 dB
```

Interpretation:

- Runtime behavior is preserved; the output delta is at float WAV
  quantization/noise level.
- Future time-attention-only CUDA Tile experiments now rebuild the new TU plus
  link, instead of recompiling the full `mbr_cuda_tile.cu` file.

### FFN12 Fused CUDA Tile Default

The FFN12 fused CUDA Tile path is now the default for BF16 inference. The old
opt-in switch is no longer required; use
`CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1` for A/B checks or quality regression
debugging.

Current-tree validation before the default flip:

```text
test_clean.wav current BF16 default:
  inference: 1096.7 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_CUTILE=1 test_clean.wav:
  inference: 1045.5 ms

gain: 51.2 ms, about 4.7% end-to-end

output diff, fused candidate vs current default:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.005257845 rms=0.000547531 rel_rms=0.002969396 snr=50.547 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.005855441 rms=0.000714026 rel_rms=0.002969501 snr=50.546 dB

post-flip default validation:
  test_clean.wav: 1049.2 ms
  test.wav:       6231.8 ms
```

Interpretation:

- This promotes a CUDA Tile fused path into the normal BF16 source path instead
  of leaving it as an experiment-only opt-in.
- The default remains erf GELU. Faster no-exp GELU approximations such as
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1` stay isolated experiments.
- The remaining FFN12 bottleneck is still the fused kernel resource shape
  (`REG=254`, high shared memory pressure), not a lack of Tensor Core use.

### FFN12 H32 Source Default

After the fused FFN12 path became default, the next A10G shape probe targeted
the main fused kernel resource pressure. Official CUDA Tile docs were checked
again before changing the source path: the experiment keeps compile-time tile
shapes and uses CUDA Tile `ct::mma` with BF16 input tiles and FP32 accumulator
tiles.

Benchmark command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant all --warmup 3 --iters 20
```

Key results:

```text
separate          best=2.698 ms useful=30.33 TF/s roof=43.3%
fused256 H64      best=2.541 ms useful=32.21 TF/s roof=46.0%
fused_h32_256     best=2.340 ms useful=34.98 TF/s roof=50.0%
fused_m16_256     best=3.066 ms useful=26.69 TF/s roof=38.1%
fused_m16_h32_256 best=3.085 ms useful=26.52 TF/s roof=37.9%
fused_h16_256     best=3.465 ms useful=23.62 TF/s roof=33.7%
fused_m16_h16_256 best=12.512 ms useful=6.54 TF/s roof=9.3%
```

Resource evidence from the benchmark binary:

```text
H64 main fused:      REG=254 SHARED=86016
H32 main fused:      REG=226 SHARED=88064
H16 main fused:      REG=188 SHARED=97280
M16/H32 main fused:  REG=182 SHARED=76800
M16/H16 main fused:  REG=158 SHARED=49664
```

The source path was changed from `HiddenTile=64` to `HiddenTile=32` for the
main `ffn12_fused256_cutile_kernel`. Tail kernels keep their old shapes because
they handle only the final 12 rows and are not the bottleneck.

Source resource check after the flip:

```text
default erf, FullBF16=false main fused: REG=242 SHARED=88064
previous H64 source reference:          REG=254 SHARED=86016
```

Source validation:

```text
test_clean.wav H64 default: 1049.2 ms
test_clean.wav H32 default: 1043.9 ms
gain: 5.3 ms

test.wav H64 default:       6231.8 ms
test.wav H32 default:       6201.0 ms
gain: 30.8 ms

H32 output diff vs H64:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.000000179 rms=0.000000010 rel_rms=0.000000054 snr=145.319 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.000000238 rms=0.000000013 rel_rms=0.000000055 snr=145.271 dB
```

Interpretation:

- `H32` is a real resource-shape win: it reduces register pressure enough to
  improve useful FFN12 throughput from `32.21` to `34.98 TF/s` in the standalone
  benchmark, even though shared memory rises slightly.
- `H16` over-splits the hidden K dimension. The lower register count does not
  pay for the extra hidden-loop work and the shared memory footprint rises to
  about `97 KB`.
- `M16` halves per-CTA M work and doubles CTA count; on A10G this loses more in
  scheduling/tile overhead than it gains from smaller accumulator tiles.
- The next FFN12 problem is still the large `32 x 256` output accumulator and
  high shared-memory schedule. The next useful probe should reduce output
  accumulator pressure without recomputing FFN1 as much as `fused128/fused64`.

### FFN12 H32 Poly9 Default

The next FFN12 experiment checked whether the no-exp polynomial GELU still helps
after the source path moved to `HiddenTile=32`.

CUDA Tile shared-memory hidden-cache idea:

- Official CUDA Tile docs and the local `cuda_tile.h` header were checked first.
- The current API exposes tile load/store, tensor views, partition views, and
  `ct::mma`, but there is no obvious tile-level shared-memory allocation or
  tile barrier/sync API in the installed header.
- A raw `__shared__` hidden-cache experiment is therefore left for a separate
  smoke test before touching the source path.

Benchmark result:

```text
fused_h32_256   best=2.343 ms useful=34.93 TF/s roof=49.9%
fused_h32_poly9 best=2.350 ms useful=34.82 TF/s roof=49.7%
```

The standalone benchmark does not show a speedup from poly9 once `HiddenTile=32`
is used. The source path does:

```text
test_clean.wav H32 erf:       1043.9 ms
test_clean.wav H32 poly9 env: 1019.6 ms

test.wav H32 erf:             6201.0 ms
test.wav H32 poly9 env:       6026.0 ms
```

After flipping poly9 to default:

```text
test_clean.wav default poly9: 1023.7 ms
test_clean.wav disable poly9: 1049.5 ms

test.wav default poly9:       6025.3 ms

output diff, default poly9 vs disable-poly9:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.006877601 rms=0.000674432 rel_rms=0.003658560 snr=48.734 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.007104263 rms=0.000842047 rel_rms=0.003501702 snr=49.114 dB
```

Default behavior:

- FFN12 fused now uses H32 + poly9 GELU by default under BF16.
- Use `CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_FUSED_POLY9_GELU=1` to return to H32 erf.
- Explicit alternative GELU flags still override the default poly9 selection.

Interpretation:

- The benchmark/source mismatch means the standalone probe is still useful for
  Tensor Core/resource trends, but source-path timing is the authority for
  default policy.
- On the real MBR source path, removing `ct::exp` from FFN12 GELU is still an
  end-to-end win after H32, saving about `175 ms` on `test.wav`.
- The remaining FFN12 gap to 70 TF/s is not fixed by GELU approximation; the
  main structural bottleneck remains output accumulator/shared-memory pressure.

### Post-Poly9 Hotspot Profile And BKN Long Linear Default

After H32 + poly9 became default, a fresh `nsys` profile was collected on
`test_clean.wav`:

```bash
nsys profile --force-overwrite true --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none -t cuda \
  -o outputs/nsys_h32_poly9_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/nsys_h32_poly9_clean_audio \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys stats --report cuda_gpu_kern_sum outputs/nsys_h32_poly9_clean.nsys-rep
```

Top source-path GPU kernels:

```text
ffn12_fused256 H32/poly9:                221.157 ms, 96 calls, 22.1%
time_attention1301_main1280 q64/k32:     165.576 ms, 48 calls, 16.6%
linear static 32x256x32 long path:       149.148 ms, 96 calls, 14.9%
rms_norm_to_bf16:                         57.521 ms, 528 calls, 5.8%
linear static 32x128x32:                  49.009 ms, 96 calls, 4.9%
freq_attention60 padded:                  38.683 ms, 48 calls, 3.9%
freq qkv split/rotary pad64:              36.860 ms, 48 calls, 3.7%
time qkv split/rotary:                    35.943 ms, 48 calls, 3.6%
```

The profile moved the next source-path check to the long QKV linear layout. The
existing `weight_bkn` CUDA Tile path was still opt-in through
`CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1`.

Candidate results:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1 test_clean.wav:
  1011.9 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1 test.wav:
  6003.3 ms
```

After flipping BKN long linear to default:

```text
test_clean.wav default BKN:       1017.2 ms
test_clean.wav disable BKN:       1018.1 ms

test.wav no-BKN default reference: 6025.3 ms
test.wav default BKN:              6007.6 ms

output diff, default BKN vs no-BKN:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.000000238 rms=0.000000010 rel_rms=0.000000055 snr=145.223 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.000000298 rms=0.000000010 rel_rms=0.000000055 snr=145.241 dB
```

Default behavior:

- Long QKV BKN CUDA Tile layout is now enabled by default under BF16.
- Use `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1` to return to the non-BKN long-linear
  path.
- Short-input timing is noisy for this small change; `test.wav` is the better
  signal and shows a modest `17.7 ms` gain.

Interpretation:

- The long linear BKN layout is a small but nearly zero-risk win: output drift is
  at the same `~145 dB` numerical-noise level seen in other layout-only changes.
- The next largest unchanged hotspots are still FFN12, time attention, RMSNorm,
  and QKV split/rotary. Since time attention is already around 60 TF/s in its
  standalone path, the next useful low-level work should either reduce FFN12
  accumulator pressure or fuse/trim the QKV split+rotary kernels.

### Negative Probe: Freq QKV Projection + Rotary Fusion

The post-poly9 profile showed freq/time QKV split+rotary kernels at about
`72.8 ms` total on `test_clean.wav`. The existing fixed-shape freq QKV+rotary
CUDA Tile fusion path was therefore retested.

Important interaction:

- The current default long QKV path uses the BKN weight layout.
- The freq QKV+rotary fused path uses the ordinary weight layout and bypasses
  BKN.
- The candidate must therefore be measured as a net tradeoff, not as a pure
  split/rotary removal.

Candidate command:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1 \
CUDASEP_ENABLE_QKV_FREQ_ROTARY_CUTILE_FUSED=1 \
./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/goal_qkv_freq_fused_test_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Results:

```text
default BKN test_clean.wav:             1017.2 ms
freq fused TM16 candidate test_clean:   1111.1 ms
freq fused TM16 nsys-profiled run:      1139.3 ms

TM16 candidate profile:
  qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_cutile_static_full_kernel:
    202.512 ms, 48 calls, 4.219 ms/call, 18.5%

TM32 source-shape probe:
  qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_cutile_static_full_kernel:
    REG=151 SHARED=65560
  test_clean.wav:
    1201.9 ms
```

Interpretation:

- The existing fused freq QKV+rotary kernel is much slower than the separate
  long-linear + split/rotary path, especially after BKN became default.
- `TM32` reduces CTA count but makes end-to-end time worse, so the limiter is
  not simply too many CTAs.
- The bottom cause is the fused projection+rotary CUDA Tile schedule itself:
  it gives up the faster BKN long-linear layout and produces a heavy fused
  kernel (`~4.2 ms/call`) that overwhelms the split/rotary savings.
- This path remains opt-in only. The attempted `TM32` source edit was reverted;
  do not promote this fused path until a new kernel design beats BKN + separate
  split/rotary.

### CUDA Tile Experiment: Fixed D256 BF16 RMSNorm

The current profile after FFN12 H32/poly9 and BKN long linear still showed
`rms_norm_to_bf16` as a non-Tensor-Core hotspot:

```text
pre-change current profile, test_clean.wav:
  rms_norm_to_bf16: 57.549 ms / 528 calls
```

Most transformer RMSNorm calls have hidden dimension `D=256`, but the generic
kernel used a `1024` element tile with masking. This experiment adds a
fixed-shape CUDA Tile path for `D=256`:

```text
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<InT, FullBF16>
  tile:       256 elements
  input:      BF16 or FP32
  gamma:      FP32
  output:     BF16
  launch:     one CUDA Tile CTA per row
  fallback:   CUDASEP_DISABLE_RMS_NORM_D256_CUTILE_FIXED=1
```

The implementation keeps the source path on CUDA Tile and uses the documented
tile load/store/reduction style (`ct::load`, `ct::sum`, `ct::store`). The
official CUDA Tile docs and the installed `cuda_tile.h` API were checked before
changing the source path.

Resource evidence from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
generic rms_norm_to_bf16<float>:  REG=31 SHARED=16
generic rms_norm_to_bf16<bf16>:   REG=33 SHARED=16
D256 rms_norm_d256<float,false>:  REG=18 SHARED=16
D256 rms_norm_d256<float,true>:   REG=16 SHARED=16
D256 rms_norm_d256<bf16,false>:   REG=16 SHARED=16
D256 rms_norm_d256<bf16,true>:    REG=17 SHARED=16
```

Build note:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass

The edit recompiles the large mbr_cuda_tile.cu TU. The CUDA Tile backend
(`tileiras`/`ptxas`) took several minutes, which is another reason to split
future hot kernels into smaller translation units once they stabilize.
```

A/B runtime on the same binary:

```text
test_clean.wav default D256:                    998.4 ms
test_clean.wav disable D256 fixed RMSNorm:     1018.6 ms
gain:                                           20.2 ms

test.wav default D256:                         5882.9 ms
test.wav disable D256 fixed RMSNorm:           6002.7 ms
gain:                                          119.8 ms
```

Nsight Systems profile for the default D256 path:

```text
test_clean.wav profiled inference: 1003.4 ms

rms_norm_d256_to_bf16<bf16,false>: 35.772 ms / 288 calls
remaining generic rms_norm_to_bf16<bf16>:       1.153 ms / 240 calls

pre-change generic rms_norm_to_bf16 total:     57.549 ms / 528 calls
measured RMSNorm reduction:                    ~20.624 ms
```

Output difference versus the disabled-D256 path:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.009417531 rms_diff=0.000700625 rel_rms=0.003798789 snr=48.407 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.009516090 rms_diff=0.000840590 rel_rms=0.003495843 snr=49.129 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.007137626 rms_diff=0.000596327 rel_rms=0.003155471 snr=50.019 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.008053660 rms_diff=0.000754917 rel_rms=0.003937620 snr=48.095 dB
```

Interpretation:

- This is a positive A10G fixed-shape CUDA Tile result. It removes the obvious
  `1024`-tile waste for `D=256` RMSNorm and saves about `20 ms` on
  `test_clean.wav` and about `120 ms` on `test.wav`.
- The kernel is not a Tensor Core workload, so it should not be judged by the
  70 TF/s dense BF16 reference. It reduces non-Tensor-Core time that otherwise
  prevents the Tensor Core kernels from dominating end-to-end runtime.
- The output drift is not bit-noise. The likely bottom cause is a different
  CUDA Tile reduction tree: the `256`-element `ct::sum` path does not reduce in
  the same order as the old `1024`-element masked path with zeroed lanes. The
  change is therefore an aggressive math-order optimization, not a layout-only
  transformation.
- Given the current project goal of aggressive CUDA Tile/BF16 experimentation,
  this path is enabled by default with an explicit rollback flag.

Full-BF16 arithmetic smoke:

```text
CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 test_clean.wav: pass, 989.4 ms

diff vs default D256:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.030854024 rms_diff=0.002265548 rel_rms=0.012281612 snr=38.215 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.031525493 rms_diff=0.002570411 rel_rms=0.010691907 snr=39.419 dB
```

This confirms the new D256 kernel also works in the aggressive
`FullBF16=true` template path. The full-BF16 output drift is much larger than
the D256-only reduction-order drift, so it remains an explicit experiment
switch rather than a default behavior.

### Negative Probe: FFN12 H32 Smaller Output Tiles

After the D256 RMSNorm improvement, `ffn12_fused256_cutile_kernel` remained the
largest default-path hotspot. The previous positive FFN12 source shape is:

```text
M tile:      32
hidden tile: 32
out tile:    256
GELU:        poly9 by default in source
```

The next hypothesis was that reducing the `32 x 256` output accumulator could
reduce register/shared-memory pressure enough to improve A10G Tensor Core
utilization. The existing benchmark had tested `out_tile=128/64` with
`HiddenTile=64`, but not the crossed `HiddenTile=32` variants. This benchmark
edit adds:

```text
fused_h32_128
fused_h32_64
fused_h32_poly9_128
fused_h32_poly9_64
```

The implementation only extends `bench/bf16_ffn12_cutile.cu` and reuses the
existing CUDA Tile `launch_fused<TM,TNOut,THidden,GeluMode>` path. The default
source path was not changed. Official CUDA Tile matrix-multiply documentation
was checked before adding the variants; the probe continues to use `ct::mma`
with BF16 input tiles and FP32 accumulators.

Build:

```text
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8

First attempt failed with:
  LLVM ERROR: IO failure on output stream: No space left on device

The filesystem was full because historical generated WAV/profile outputs used
about 41 GB under outputs/. The generated outputs directory was cleaned,
leaving 41 GB free, then the benchmark target built successfully.
```

Benchmark command:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_h32_128 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_h32_64 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_128 --warmup 3 --iters 30
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_64 --warmup 3 --iters 30
```

Results:

```text
fused_h32_poly9:
  tile=32x256 recompute_ffn1=1 best=2.384 ms
  useful=34.33 TF/s, 49.0% of 70 TF/s
  actual=34.33 TF/s, 49.0% of 70 TF/s

fused_h32_128:
  tile=32x128 recompute_ffn1=2 best=4.106 ms
  useful=19.93 TF/s, 28.5% of 70 TF/s
  actual=29.90 TF/s, 42.7% of 70 TF/s

fused_h32_64:
  tile=32x64 recompute_ffn1=4 best=6.709 ms
  useful=12.20 TF/s, 17.4% of 70 TF/s
  actual=30.50 TF/s, 43.6% of 70 TF/s

fused_h32_poly9_128:
  tile=32x128 recompute_ffn1=2 best=3.288 ms
  useful=24.89 TF/s, 35.6% of 70 TF/s
  actual=37.33 TF/s, 53.3% of 70 TF/s

fused_h32_poly9_64:
  tile=32x64 recompute_ffn1=4 best=5.662 ms
  useful=14.45 TF/s, 20.6% of 70 TF/s
  actual=36.13 TF/s, 51.6% of 70 TF/s
```

Resource evidence:

```text
H32 erf  out_tile=256: REG=226 SHARED=88064
H32 erf  out_tile=128: REG=198 SHARED=71680
H32 erf  out_tile= 64: REG=171 SHARED=55296

H32 poly9 out_tile=256: REG=248 SHARED=88064
H32 poly9 out_tile=128: REG=212 SHARED=71680
H32 poly9 out_tile= 64: REG=214 SHARED=55296
```

Interpretation:

- Smaller output tiles do reduce register/shared-memory pressure, so the
  original pressure diagnosis is real.
- The fix is wrong for this FFN12 structure: `out_tile=128` recomputes FFN1
  twice and `out_tile=64` recomputes FFN1 four times. That extra FFN1 work costs
  far more than the accumulator-pressure reduction saves.
- Poly9 helps the recompute variants because it removes expensive GELU math
  from the duplicated FFN1 path, but it still does not make them competitive
  with `out_tile=256`.
- The bottom limiter is therefore not just "too many registers". The useful
  path needs to reduce or stage the output accumulator without recomputing
  FFN1. A future positive direction would need a true hidden-tile staging path
  or another CUDA Tile-supported way to share the `M x HiddenTile` activation
  across multiple output tiles.
- Do not promote these smaller-output FFN12 variants into the source path.

### Negative Smoke: CUDA Tile Raw Shared Memory Staging

The FFN12 smaller-output probe showed that the useful direction would be to
reuse `M x HiddenTile` activations across multiple output tiles without
recomputing FFN1. The most direct idea was a CUDA Tile kernel that writes the
hidden activation tile to CTA shared memory and reloads it for multiple FFN2
output tiles.

Official CUDA Tile docs and the installed `crt/cuda_tile.h` were checked first.
The public API exposes tile load/store, partition views, atomics, and thread
scope constants, but no obvious public tile-level shared-memory allocation or
barrier/sync API.

A minimal smoke target was temporarily added:

```text
__tile_global__ shared_bf16_roundtrip_kernel:
  __shared__ __nv_bfloat16 smem[256]
  ct::load(global)
  ct::store(smem)
  ct::load(smem)
  ct::store(global)
```

Build result:

```text
ninja -C build-min bench_cutile_shared_staging_smoke -j8

bench/cutile_shared_staging_smoke.cu(33): error:
  tile functions cannot access __shared__ variables
```

The failed smoke target/source was removed so the normal build remains valid.

Interpretation:

- Plain CUDA `__shared__` is not usable inside CUDA Tile functions in this CUDA
  13.3 toolchain.
- That blocks the most direct FFN12 hidden-staging design under the current
  "CUDA Tile first, no raw CUDA/WMMA" constraint.
- The previous FFN12 negative result is therefore stronger: smaller output
  accumulators require FFN1 recompute unless CUDA Tile exposes another public
  staging primitive. With current APIs, a source-path FFN12 improvement should
  look elsewhere: reduce live accumulator pressure without shared memory,
  change the math schedule, or move to another hotspot such as time attention,
  long linear, or split/rotary.

### Negative Probe: Fixed Split/Rotary Full-BF16 Template Dispatch

The fixed time/freq split+rotary kernels still take about `72 ms` total on
`test_clean.wav`, so the next small hypothesis was that the runtime
`full_bf16` boolean kept extra `ct::select(full_bf16, bf16_round(x), x)` work
alive in the default path. A temporary source edit changed only the fixed-shape
time and freq kernels to template dispatch:

```text
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_time1301_d64_kernel<FullBF16>
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel<FullBF16>
```

Official CUDA Tile docs were checked before the edit; this stayed within the
documented fixed tile-kernel model and did not add raw CUDA, WMMA, inline MMA,
cuBLAS, cuDNN, or cuFFT.

Resource evidence from the temporary binary:

```text
previous fixed time split/rotary:  REG=37
template time <false>:             REG=40
template time <true>:              REG=38

previous fixed freq split/rotary:  REG=40
template freq <false>:             REG=46
template freq <true>:              REG=40
```

Runtime/profile evidence:

```text
template-dispatch default test_clean.wav:        990.4 ms
template-dispatch nsys test_clean.wav:          1001.8 ms

template-dispatch nsys split totals:
  split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel<false>:
    36.795 ms / 48 calls
  split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_time1301_d64_kernel<false>:
    36.001 ms / 48 calls

previous fixed split totals:
  freq: 36.763 ms / 48 calls
  time: 35.821 ms / 48 calls
```

Interpretation:

- The runtime `full_bf16` boolean is not the meaningful bottom-level limiter
  for fixed split/rotary.
- Template specialization did not simplify the default CUDA Tile codegen in a
  useful way. It increased register allocation for the default `<false>` path
  and left split kernel time effectively unchanged/slightly worse.
- The temporary source edit was reverted. The next useful split/rotary work is
  not boolean specialization; it must reduce global q/k/v materialization,
  change the layout/scatter schedule, or fuse with neighboring QKV/attention
  work without giving up the faster BKN long-linear path.

### CUDA Tile Experiment: Time-Attention Split Tail Q32/K32

The default split-tail time attention already uses `QRows=64,KTile=32` for the
main `1280` query rows, but the final tail has only `21` useful query rows and
still used `QRows=64,KTile=64`. This experiment checks whether a fixed smaller
tail shape reduces wasted tile state.

The benchmark edit adds `tail_only` and tail-shape pair probes using the same
CUDA Tile `partition_view` / `load_masked` / `ct::mma` formulation as the
source path. Official CUDA Tile docs were checked before the edit; no raw
CUDA shared memory, WMMA, inline MMA, cuBLAS, cuDNN, or cuFFT path was added.

Benchmark command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --warmup 2 --iters 8 --validate
```

Relevant benchmark results:

```text
tail_only qrows=64 ktile=64:
  best=0.310 ms, real_math=10.84 TF/s, logical_tile_math=34.12 TF/s

tail_only qrows=32 ktile=64:
  best=0.274 ms, real_math=12.27 TF/s, logical_tile_math=19.31 TF/s

tail_only qrows=16 ktile=64:
  best=0.389 ms, real_math=8.64 TF/s, logical_tile_math=13.60 TF/s

tail_only qrows=32 ktile=32:
  best=0.271 ms, real_math=12.37 TF/s, logical_tile_math=19.00 TF/s
```
