full output. This cuts one live output accumulator from the block, but it
recomputes the FFN1 hidden projection for each output half. The bench marks it
as `recompute_ffn1=2` so `actual` TF/s includes the duplicated FFN1 work.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_halfout --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Benchmark result:

```text
baseline pair-H32/TK64:
  best=1.852 ms, median=1.857 ms, useful=44.19 TF/s, actual=44.19 TF/s

halfout pair-H32/TK64:
  best=3.389 ms, median=3.393 ms, useful=24.15 TF/s, actual=36.22 TF/s
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
baseline pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=2760, HMMA=256, F2F=96, LDG=208, LDSM=128, STS=124,
  FFMA=256, FADD=192, FMUL=160

halfout pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=234, STACK=0, SHARED=94208
  instr=2080, HMMA=192, F2F=64, LDG=156, LDSM=112, STS=96,
  FFMA=256, FADD=160, FMUL=160
```

Interpretation:

- Halfout is the first probe here that actually lowers register pressure versus
  the current default: `REG=242` to `REG=234`.
- It still loses badly because `grid.y=2` doubles the hidden projection work.
  Per block HMMA falls to 192, but per M tile the two output-half blocks imply
  384 static HMMA instructions versus baseline 256.
- The output-accumulator pressure is real but not expensive enough to justify
  recomputing FFN1 on A10G.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_halfout` as a candidates-only
  lower-bound probe.
- Do not split the source FFN12 output into independent half-output blocks.
- Future FFN12 work should try to reduce pressure inside the existing
  two-output-half block rather than trade it for hidden recomputation.

### FFN12 Single-H32/TK64 Versus Pair-H32/TK64

This candidates-only probe separates two effects that were combined in the
current source default: `TK64` input-K tiling and pair-H32 A-tile reuse. The new
`fused_h32_poly9_split2_tk64` variant keeps the single-H32 split2 schedule but
uses `TK64`. This tests whether the TK64 improvement alone explains the current
pair-H32/TK64 speedup.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant all --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Benchmark result:

```text
pair-H32/TK64:
  best=1.853 ms, median=1.856 ms, useful=44.17 TF/s, roof70=63.1%

single-H32/TK64:
  best=2.198 ms, median=2.201 ms, useful=37.23 TF/s, roof70=53.2%

all rerun:
  pair-H32/TK64 best=1.856 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s
  single-H32/TK64 best=2.186 ms, useful=37.44 TF/s
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=2760, HMMA=256, F2F=96, LDG=208, LDSM=128, STS=124,
  FFMA=256, FADD=192, FMUL=160

single-H32/TK64:
  REG=253, STACK=0, SHARED=96256
  instr=2224, HMMA=192, F2F=88, LDG=190, LDSM=120, STS=111,
  FFMA=192, FADD=160, FMUL=120
```

Interpretation:

- TK64 alone is not enough. The single-H32/TK64 schedule is much slower than
  pair-H32/TK64.
- The single-H32/TK64 variant has fewer static instructions and fewer static
  HMMA instructions, but worse runtime and higher register pressure
  (`REG=253` versus `REG=242`).
- Pair-H32 is not merely a load-reuse optimization; it drives CUDA Tile codegen
  into a better Tensor Core schedule shape on A10G.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_tk64` as a candidates-only diagnostic.
- Do not replace pair-H32/TK64 with single-H32/TK64.
- Future FFN12 experiments should preserve pair-H32 and perturb narrower
  schedule details around it, rather than falling back to single-H32.

### FFN12 Pair-H32/TK128 Upper-K Probe

After TK64 improved pair-H32 versus the original TK32 source shape, this
candidates-only probe tests whether increasing the input-K tile again to TK128
continues the trend. The hypothesis was that halving the W1 K loop could reduce
loop overhead and global-load instructions enough to improve throughput.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk128 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant all --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Benchmark result:

```text
pair-H32/TK64:
  best=1.853 ms, median=1.856 ms, useful=44.17 TF/s, roof70=63.1%

pair-H32/TK128:
  best=2.441 ms, median=2.449 ms, useful=33.53 TF/s, roof70=47.9%

all rerun:
  pair-H32/TK64 best=1.855 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s
  pair-H32/TK128 best=2.440 ms, useful=33.53 TF/s
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=2760, HMMA=256, F2F=96, LDG=208, LDSM=128, STS=124,
  FFMA=256, FADD=192, FMUL=160

pair-H32/TK128:
  REG=255, STACK=56, SHARED=94208
  instr=2536, HMMA=256, F2F=96, LDG=196, LDSM=128, STS=124,
  FFMA=256, FADD=192, FMUL=160
```

Interpretation:

- TK128 keeps the same static HMMA count and reduces static instructions and
  LDG versus TK64.
- It still loses badly because the larger input-K tile drives register usage to
  `REG=255` and introduces `STACK=56`.
- The bottleneck is not the remaining K-loop instruction count; it is the
  generated register allocation and stack pressure.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk128` as a candidates-only negative
  result.
- Do not promote TK128 into the source FFN12 path.
- Current A10G sweet spot for pair-H32 remains TK64.

### FFN12 Pair-H32/TK64 TM8 Lower-M Probe

The current exact-tile FFN12 kernels require `kM % TM == 0`. For the fixed
benchmark shape `kM=78048`, the largest power-of-two divisor is 32, so TM64
would leave a tail unless a separate masked-tail kernel is added. Since CUDA
Tile shapes are compile-time fixed and the current bench intentionally avoids
tail masking, this probe tests the legal lower side instead: TM8 with the
current pair-H32/TK64 schedule.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant fused_m8_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Benchmark result:

```text
TM32 pair-H32/TK64:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.13 TF/s, roof70=63.0%

TM8 pair-H32/TK64:
  best=77.015 ms, median=77.583 ms, useful=1.06 TF/s, roof70=1.5%
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
TM32 pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=2760, HMMA=256, F2F=96, LDG=208, LDSM=128, STS=124,
  STL=0, LDL=0, FFMA=256, FADD=192, FMUL=160

TM8 pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=255, STACK=1256, SHARED=91648
  instr=11392, HMMA=0, F2F=24, LDG=96, LDSM=0, STS=88,
  STL=351, LDL=367, FFMA=64, FADD=3200, FMUL=3112
```

Interpretation:

- TM8 drops completely out of the Tensor Core path: `HMMA=0` and `LDSM=0`.
- The compiler emits a huge scalar/local-memory schedule instead, with
  `STACK=1256` and hundreds of `LDL/STL` instructions.
- This is not a mild occupancy tradeoff. It is a different, unusable codegen
  path for this FFN12 shape on A10G.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_m8_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64` as a candidates-only negative
  result.
- Do not use TM8 for FFN12 pair-H32/TK64.
- Do not test TM64 in this exact-tile bench until a masked-tail design exists;
  TM32 is currently the only valid power-of-two M tile above the known-negative
  TM16/TM8 side for this fixed `kM`.

### Current Profile After FFN12 Shape Boundary Sweep

After the FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 negative boundary sweep, the current default BF16
path was rerun to refresh the end-to-end and hotspot ranking before starting
another local FFN12 experiment. This is not a dense GEMM roofline rerun; it is a
source-path profile of the current no-library CUDA Tile inference binary.

Commands:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/goal_current_test_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys profile --force-overwrite=true --stats=true \
  -o outputs/nsys_goal_current_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/nsys_goal_current_clean_out \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Run result:

```text
plain test_clean.wav:
  infer=949.9 ms, RTF=41.68x

nsys test_clean.wav:
  infer=959.1 ms, RTF=41.28x
```

Top CUDA kernels from `cuda_gpu_kern_sum`:

```text
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<poly9,false>:
  181.914 ms, 96 calls, 19.6%, avg=1.895 ms

time_attention1301_main1280_cutile_kernel<64,32,true>:
  165.915 ms, 48 calls, 17.9%, avg=3.457 ms

linear_cutile_static_full_bkn_bf16_kernel<32,256,...>:
  135.669 ms, 96 calls, 14.6%, avg=1.413 ms

linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,128,32,...>:
  49.125 ms, 96 calls, 5.3%, avg=0.512 ms

linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel<32,64,32,...>:
  41.847 ms, 480 calls, 4.5%, avg=0.087 ms

freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16,false>:
  38.758 ms, 48 calls, 4.2%, avg=0.807 ms

split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel:
  36.859 ms, 48 calls, 4.0%, avg=0.768 ms

split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_time1301_d64_kernel:
  35.960 ms, 48 calls, 3.9%, avg=0.749 ms

rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel:
  35.804 ms, 288 calls, 3.9%, avg=0.124 ms

binary_inplace_same_shape_kernel<bf16>:
  35.288 ms, 192 calls, 3.8%, avg=0.184 ms
```

Interpretation:

- FFN12 remains the largest single kernel group, but the recent shape-boundary
  probes found no better pair-H32/TK64 variant: TK128, TM8, split4 output,
  half-output recompute, no-bias, and single-H32/TK64 are all negative.
- Time attention is still second, but the existing standalone time-attention
  main path already sits near `60 TF/s`, so simple shape sweeps are less
  attractive than lower-throughput Tensor Core groups.
- The strongest next non-FFN Tensor Core target is the long QKV BKN linear group:
  `135.669 ms / 96 calls`, with existing standalone results around the low-to-mid
  40 TF/s range. That leaves more headroom than time attention.
- The QKV split/rotary pair still costs about `72.8 ms` combined, but previous
  direct QKV+rotary fusion attempts were negative; any revisit should focus on
  load/scatter layout, not naive direct indexing.

Decision:

- Keep FFN12 source default at pair-H32/TK64.
- Move the next Tensor Core shape work to QKV BKN long-linear, unless a new
  FFN12 schedule idea specifically preserves pair-H32/TK64 and reduces
  register pressure without falling off HMMA.
- Keep RMSNorm D256 and current split-QKV fixed kernels enabled; they are still
  meaningful non-Tensor-Core/default-path wins, but not the next largest
  TFLOP/s headroom target.

### Negative Probe: QKV BKN TK8

The refreshed profile moved the next Tensor Core shape work to the long QKV BKN
linear group. Existing evidence showed `TK16` beating `TK32`, so this probe
tests whether shrinking the input-K tile further to `TK8` continues that trend.
The shape remains the source-relevant QKV BKN macro shape:
`M32 x N256 x Ktile`, with B stored as `[K,N]`.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x8bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Benchmark result:

```text
qkv_t32x256x8bkn:
  best=3.161 ms, median=3.164 ms, useful=19.42 TF/s, roof70=27.7%

qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  best=1.381 ms, median=1.404 ms, useful=44.46 TF/s, roof70=63.5%

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled:
  best=1.462 ms, median=1.475 ms, useful=42.00 TF/s, roof70=60.0%
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x8bkn:
  REG=255, STACK=0, SHARED=97792
  instr=1328, HMMA=512, F2F=32, LDG=160, LDSM=96, STS=104

qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88

qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=832, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80
```

Interpretation:

- TK8 does not lose Tensor Core codegen; it emits HMMA.
- It still fails because the generated schedule doubles static HMMA count to
  512, increases LDG/STS, and pushes registers to `REG=255`.
- TK16 remains the QKV BKN K-tile sweet spot: it avoids the TK8 HMMA/instruction
  explosion while using fewer registers than TK32.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t32x256x8bkn` as a benchmark-only negative result.
- Keep source QKV BKN on TK16 by default.
- The next QKV BKN search should not shrink K further; it should perturb N/M
  schedule or fusion boundaries around the established `M32 x N256 x K16`
  macro shape.

### QKV BKN N-width TK16 Sweep

After TK8 failed, this probe keeps the known-good `M32 x K16` structure and
only changes the output-N tile width. The goal is to test whether fewer CTAs
(`N512`) or lower register pressure (`N128`) can beat the current `N256`
source-relevant shape.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x128x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x512x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt | rg -A8 "qkv_bkn_static_tiled_kernel<32, (128|256|512), 16, 78048, 1536, 256>"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
qkv_t32x128x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,12), best=1.772 ms, median=1.788 ms
  useful=34.64 TF/s, roof70=49.5%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.380 ms, median=1.416 ms
  useful=44.46 TF/s, roof70=63.5%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,3), best=1.931 ms, median=1.939 ms
  useful=31.78 TF/s, roof70=45.4%, checksum=92.0000
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x128x16bkn:
  REG=92, STACK=0, SHARED=90112
  instr=736, HMMA=128, F2F=16, LDG=80, LDSM=64, STS=52

qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88

qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  REG=255, STACK=8, SHARED=81920
  instr=1504, HMMA=512, F2F=64, LDG=176, LDSM=160, STS=160
```

Interpretation:

- All three N-width variants stay on the Tensor Core path; the issue is not
  missing HMMA.
- `N128` lowers registers substantially, but doubles grid.y versus `N256` and
  loses throughput. For this QKV BKN shape, smaller per-CTA work does not
  offset the extra CTA scheduling and lower work density.
- `N512` cuts grid.y to 3 but maxes registers, introduces stack usage, doubles
  static HMMA versus `N256`, and increases LDG/LDSM/STS. The wider tile is too
  large for this CUDA Tile codegen shape on A10G.
- `N256/TK16` remains the current sweet spot because it balances CTA count,
  HMMA density, register pressure, and shared-memory staging.

Decision:

- Keep the source QKV BKN macro shape at `M32 x N256 x K16`.
- Keep `qkv_t32x128x16bkn` and `qkv_t32x512x16bkn` as benchmark-only evidence.
- The next QKV BKN attempt should preserve `N256/TK16` and look for a lower
  register or memory-traffic schedule, or evaluate fusion boundaries around
  the QKV consumer path.

### QKV BKN M-width TK16 Sweep

This probe keeps the current `N256/TK16` sweet spot and changes only the M tile
width. `M16` tests whether a smaller accumulator tile lowers register pressure
enough to win. `M64` tests whether fewer CTAs improve work density. The `M64`
case uses `fullM=78016` because the exact-tile helper only runs the largest
multiple of `TM` below the real `M=78060`.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t16x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t64x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt | rg -A8 "qkv_bkn_static_tiled_kernel<(16, 256, 16, 78048|32, 256, 16, 78048|64, 256, 16, 78016), 1536, 256>"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
qkv_t16x256x16bkn:
  grid=(4878,6), fullM=78048, best=2.397 ms, median=2.420 ms
  useful=25.60 TF/s, roof70=36.6%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=1.382 ms, median=1.417 ms
  useful=44.42 TF/s, roof70=63.5%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t64x256x16bkn:
  grid=(1219,6), fullM=78016, best=2.215 ms, median=2.224 ms
  useful=27.70 TF/s, roof70=39.6%, checksum=92.0000
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t16x256x16bkn:
  REG=150, STACK=0, SHARED=97792
  instr=752, HMMA=128, F2F=16, LDG=112, LDSM=80, STS=88

qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88

qkv_t64x256x16bkn:
  REG=218, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=1336, HMMA=512, F2F=64, LDG=112, LDSM=128, STS=96
```

Interpretation:

- All three M-width variants emit HMMA, so the slowdown is not caused by losing
  the Tensor Core path.
- `M16` lowers registers from 170 to 150, but doubles grid.x and keeps the same
  static LDG/STS count per CTA as `M32`. The smaller M tile therefore increases
  total CTA scheduling and memory-instruction pressure.
- `M64` halves grid.x, but raises registers to 218 and doubles static HMMA/F2F
  versus `M32`; the wider accumulator tile is too heavy for this CUDA Tile
  codegen shape on A10G.
- `M32/N256/TK16` remains the best balance found so far.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t16x256x16bkn` and `qkv_t64x256x16bkn` as benchmark-only negative
  evidence.
- Do not port the M-width variants into the source QKV BKN path.
- The next useful QKV experiment should keep `M32/N256/TK16` and target
  instruction/memory traffic within that macro shape, or move to a consumer
  fusion experiment where global-memory traffic can actually be removed.

### Neutral Probe: QKV BKN TK16 Pair-K Loop Grouping

The older `TK32` pair-K probe compiled to the same SASS as the baseline. After
`TK16` became the current QKV sweet spot, this probe repeats the same idea for
`M32 x N256 x TK16`: keep `ct::irange`, but process two adjacent `K16` tiles
inside each loop body.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk --warmup 10 --iters 200
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt | rg -A8 "qkv_bkn_static_tiled(_pairk)?_kernel<32, 256, 16, 78048, 1536, 256>"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=1.381 ms, median=1.393 ms
  useful=44.44 TF/s, roof70=63.5%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=1.380 ms, median=1.404 ms
  useful=44.49 TF/s, roof70=63.6%, checksum=92.0000
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88
```

Interpretation:

- Pair-K16 is effectively neutral. The best time differs by only `0.001 ms`,
  and the median is slightly worse.
- Resource usage and SASS instruction counts are exactly identical to the
  baseline. The CUDA Tile compiler already emits the same generated shape for
  the plain structured `ct::irange` loop.
- The current QKV gap is therefore not caused by loop-control overhead that can
  be removed with mild pair grouping.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk` as benchmark-only diagnostic evidence.
- Do not port pair-K16 into the source QKV BKN path.
- The next QKV work should target data movement: either reduce B/A staging
  instructions inside `M32/N256/TK16`, or fuse the QKV output consumer so that
  the BF16 QKV buffer does not round-trip through global memory unchanged.

### Negative Probe: QKV BKN Scatter-Time Output Lower Bound

Direct QKV+rotary fusion was previously negative because it gave up the faster
BKN weight layout. This lower-bound keeps the current best BKN projection shape
(`M32 x N256 x TK16`) and changes only the final store: instead of writing a
contiguous `[M,1536]` BF16 QKV buffer, it scatters the accumulator tile directly
to the time-attention `[batch, heads, 1301, 64]` q/k/v layout. This version does
not apply rotary and does not read cos/sin, so it measures the raw cost of the
consumer-facing scatter store.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time --warmup 10 --iters 200
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt | rg -A8 "qkv_bkn_static_tiled(_scatter_time)?_kernel<32, 256, 16, 78048, 1536, 256>"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=1.380 ms, median=1.393 ms
  useful=44.47 TF/s, roof70=63.5%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=5.956 ms, median=5.983 ms
  useful=10.30 TF/s, roof70=14.7%, checksum=92.0000
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88
  STG=8, STG128=8, STG16=1
  IMAD=72, IADD3=75, SHF=61, LOP3=77

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  REG=172, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=1736, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88
  STG=192, STG128=3, STG16=192
  IMAD=83, IADD3=91, SHF=70, LOP3=149
```

Interpretation:

- The scatter lower-bound still emits the same HMMA count and keeps register
  pressure nearly unchanged, so the failure is not Tensor Core loss or spill.
- The final store changes from eight coalesced global stores in the contiguous
  baseline to 192 small U16-style global stores. The extra q/k/v offset math
  also roughly doubles total static instruction count and nearly doubles LOP3.
- Because this version has no rotary math and no cos/sin loads, adding the real
  consumer work would only make it slower. Direct per-element q/k/v scatter from
  the GEMM accumulator tile is therefore not a viable fusion boundary.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time` as benchmark-only negative evidence.
- Do not port accumulator-tile q/k/v scatter into the source QKV path.
- Any future QKV consumer fusion must preserve vectorized/coalesced stores or
  change the downstream attention layout so that the BKN projection can store
  tile-contiguous output. Removing the global QKV buffer is still desirable,
  but not through per-element scatter at the GEMM tail.

### Positive Lower Bound: QKV BKN Split-Contiguous Output

The scatter-time lower-bound proved that writing directly to the current
`[batch, heads, N, D]` time-attention layout destroys store coalescing. This
probe keeps the same `M32 x N256 x TK16` BKN projection but writes to three
separate contiguous `[M,512]` Q/K/V buffers using `partition_view.store()`. It
does not apply rotary and does not change the consumer yet; it only tests
whether Q/K/V separation can remain tile-contiguous.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time --warmup 10 --iters 200
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt | rg -A8 "qkv_bkn_static_tiled(_split_contig|_scatter_time)?_kernel<32, 256, 16, 78048, 1536, 256>"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=1.380 ms, median=1.393 ms
  useful=44.48 TF/s, roof70=63.5%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=1.411 ms, median=1.436 ms
  useful=43.49 TF/s, roof70=62.1%, checksum=92.0000

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  grid=(2439,6), fullM=78048, best=5.969 ms, median=5.989 ms
  useful=10.28 TF/s, roof70=14.7%, checksum=92.0000
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=936, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88
  STG=8, STG128=8, STG16=1
  IMAD=72, IADD3=75, SHF=61, LOP3=77

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig:
  REG=174, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=1088, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88
  STG=24, STG128=24, STG16=0
  IMAD=143, IADD3=82, SHF=63, LOP3=79

qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  REG=172, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=1736, HMMA=256, F2F=32, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88
  STG=192, STG128=3, STG16=192
  IMAD=83, IADD3=91, SHF=70, LOP3=149
```

Interpretation:

- Splitting Q/K/V is not inherently expensive if each output remains
  tile-contiguous. `split_contig` keeps HMMA/LDG/LDSM/STS identical to the
  baseline and still uses vectorized `STG.128` stores.
- The extra cost is modest: 24 coalesced global stores instead of 8, plus more
  IMAD for selecting the q/k/v output view. Throughput drops only from
  `44.48` to `43.49 TF/s`.
- This directly explains the scatter failure: the bad part is not Q/K/V
  separation, it is the current attention layout's per-element scatter address
  pattern.

Decision:

- Keep `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig` as a positive layout lower-bound.
- Do not port it directly yet, because current attention kernels consume
  `[B,H,N,D]`, not `[M,512]`.
- The next viable fusion/layout direction is to prototype a time-attention or
  split/rotary consumer that accepts `[M,512]` Q/K/V-contiguous input, preserving
  coalesced projection stores and moving layout conversion closer to the
  attention tile where data reuse can pay for it.

### Negative Recheck: Time-Attention Packed-QKV Direct Rotary Consumer

The QKV split-contiguous lower-bound suggests that Q/K/V separation can be cheap
if the projection store remains tile-contiguous. The existing time-attention
bench already has a stronger consumer-side probe:
`main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary`. It reads packed QKV plus rotary data
directly inside the main time-attention kernel instead of consuming pre-split
compact `[BH,N,D]` Q/K/V buffers.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2 --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary --warmup 3 --iters 10
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile | c++filt | rg -A8 "time_attention1301_main1280_(qkv_direct_rotary_kernel<64, 32, true>|kernel<64, 32, true, true, false, false>)"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2:
  best=3.404 ms, median=3.406 ms
  real_math=60.11 TF/s, logical_tile_math=62.09 TF/s, roof70=85.9%

main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary:
  best=19.114 ms, median=19.214 ms
  real_math=10.71 TF/s, logical_tile_math=10.80 TF/s, roof70=15.3%
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2:
  REG=237, STACK=0, SHARED=40960
  instr=2720, HMMA=160, F2F=56, LDG=32, LDSM=64, STS=60, STG=4
  IMAD=63, IADD3=128, SHF=121, LOP3=153, MUFU_EX2=90

main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary:
  REG=224, STACK=0, SHARED=32768
  instr=3440, HMMA=64, F2F=63, LDG=268, LDSM=0, STS=20, STG=32
  IMAD=515, IADD3=430, SHF=92, LOP3=293, MUFU_EX2=36
```

Interpretation:

- The direct packed-QKV consumer is not register-limited; it even uses fewer
  registers than the baseline.
- It destroys the efficient attention Tensor Core/staging pattern: HMMA drops
  from 160 to 64, LDG jumps from 32 to 268, and integer address arithmetic
  explodes. This matches the QKV scatter-store lesson from the producer side.
- Moving rotary/layout work into attention is only useful if Q/K/V tiles remain
  compact and tile-contiguous at the point where attention loads them. Direct
  packed-QKV indexing inside the attention loop is not that design.

Decision:

- Keep `main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary` as a negative consumer-side
  layout probe.
- Do not pursue packed-QKV direct indexing inside the current attention kernel.
- The viable direction is narrower: keep projection output coalesced
  (`[M,512]` split-contig or equivalent), then design a consumer that stages
  compact per-head Q/K/V tiles before the QK/AV loop rather than doing arbitrary
  packed-QKV loads throughout attention.

### Positive Probe: Time-Attention Split-Contiguous Input

After the packed-QKV direct rotary consumer failed, this probe tests the
narrower layout idea from the QKV producer side: keep Q/K/V as three separate
split-contiguous `[B,N,H,D]` buffers, and let the time-attention main kernel
consume one head through `layout_strided` views. This matches the
`qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig` producer lower-bound: projection stores remain
coalesced, and attention does not index packed `[Q,K,V]` inside the hot loop.

The split-contiguous input mapping for each fixed `(b,h)` is:

```text
Q/V view: shape [N,D], strides [H*D, 1]
K^T view: shape [D,N], strides [1, H*D]
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2 --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary --warmup 3 --iters 10
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input --warmup 3 --iters 10 --compare-baseline
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile | c++filt | rg -A8 "time_attention1301_main1280_(split_contig_input_kernel<64, 32, true>|qkv_direct_rotary_kernel<64, 32, true>|kernel<64, 32, true, true, false, false>)"
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile | c++filt
```

Benchmark result:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2:
  best=3.404 ms, median=3.405 ms
  real_math=60.12 TF/s, logical_tile_math=62.10 TF/s, roof70=85.9%

main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input:
  best=3.394 ms, median=3.396 ms
  real_math=60.29 TF/s, logical_tile_math=62.28 TF/s, roof70=86.1%

main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary:
  best=19.087 ms, median=19.178 ms
  real_math=10.72 TF/s, logical_tile_math=10.81 TF/s, roof70=15.3%
```

Correctness against the compact baseline:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input:
  elems=39966720, max_abs=0, rms=0, ref_rms=0.2478777, rel_rms=0
```

Resource and SASS evidence:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2:
  REG=237, STACK=0, SHARED=40960
  instr=2720, HMMA=160, F2F=56, LDG=32, LDSM=64, STS=60, STG=4
  IMAD=63, IADD3=128, SHF=121, LOP3=153, MUFU_EX2=90

main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input:
  REG=233, STACK=0, SHARED=40960
  instr=2736, HMMA=160, F2F=56, LDG=32, LDSM=64, STS=60, STG=4
  IMAD=62, IADD3=138, SHF=123, LOP3=141, MUFU_EX2=90

main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary:
  REG=224, STACK=0, SHARED=32768
  instr=3440, HMMA=64, F2F=63, LDG=268, LDSM=0, STS=20, STG=32
  IMAD=515, IADD3=430, SHF=92, LOP3=293, MUFU_EX2=36
```

Interpretation:

- Split-contiguous Q/K/V input keeps the exact efficient Tensor Core and memory
  staging shape of the compact `[BH,N,D]` baseline: `HMMA=160`, `LDG=32`,
  `LDSM=64`, `STG=4`.
- The `layout_strided` view does not become arbitrary per-element indexing for
  this fixed shape. The generated kernel is effectively the same as the compact
  baseline, and the synthetic output is bit-identical at BF16 output precision.
- This is the missing positive consumer-side evidence for the QKV
  split-contiguous producer result. The bad path is specifically packed-QKV
  direct rotary/indexing, not split-contiguous `[B,N,H,D]` itself.

Decision:

- Keep `main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input` as a positive layout probe.
- The next source-level QKV/time-attention direction should be an opt-in
  end-to-end path: BKN projection writes split-contiguous Q/K/V buffers, then
  time attention consumes those buffers through the strided views validated
  here.
- Rotary still needs a layout-aware plan. The source path should avoid doing
  packed-QKV direct rotary inside attention; instead, either apply rotary while
  preserving split-contiguous stores, or add a separate split-contiguous rotary
  pass and measure whether removing the old `[B,H,N,D]` materialization wins.

### Source Scaffold: Time-Attention Split-Contiguous Launcher

The positive split-contiguous consumer probe has now been moved into the source
binary as a callable, guarded building block. It is not connected to the default
inference path yet. The new source launcher expects:

```text
q/k/v: [B,1301,8,64] BF16 split-contiguous
out:   [B*8,1301,64] BF16 compact attention output
```

Changed source files:

```text
src/mbr_time_attention_cutile.cu:
  time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel<64,32,UseExp2>
  launch_time_attention1301_split_contig_main_cutile(...)

src/mbr_cuda_tile.h:
  launcher declaration
```

Build/resource evidence:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

source time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel<64,32,true>:
  REG=237, STACK=0, SHARED=40960
  instr=2792, HMMA=160, F2F=56, LDG=32, LDSM=64, STS=60, STG=4
  IMAD=69, IADD3=184, SHF=125, LOP3=157, MUFU_EX2=90
```

Validation:

```text
git diff --check: pass
cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm audit: no matches
source LOC: 11860 total
test_clean.wav default BF16 path: 951.1 ms, RTF=41.62x
```

Interpretation:

- The source kernel preserves the important bench properties: `HMMA=160`,
  `LDG=32`, `LDSM=64`, and `STG=4`.
- This is only a scaffold. It does not yet remove the old QKV buffer or old
  split/rotary path, because the source QKV producer still writes the current
  layout and rotary is still handled before attention.
- The next source experiment should add an opt-in producer/rotary path that
  creates `[B,1301,8,64]` split-contiguous Q/K/V for time attention, then call
  this launcher for the main1280 path and keep the existing tail path as the
  initial fallback.

### Source Scaffold: Time-QKV BKN Split-Contiguous Producer

The positive QKV BKN split-contiguous producer probe has now been moved into the
source binary as a callable scaffold. It is not connected to the default
inference path yet and does not apply rotary. The new source launcher expects:

```text
x:          [B,1301,256] BF16
weight:     [1536,256] BF16
weight_bkn: [256,1536] BF16
q/k/v out:  [B,1301,8,64] BF16 split-contiguous
```

Changed source files:

```text
src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu:
  qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256>
  qkv_bkn_split_contig_tail_kernel<1536,256>
  linear_qkv_bkn_split_contig_time(...)

src/mbr_cuda_tile.h:
  producer declaration
```

Build/resource/SASS evidence:

```text
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile cudasep_infer -j8

source qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256>:
  REG=174, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=2176, HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=24
  STG128=24, STG16=0, F2F=32, MUFU=0

source qkv_bkn_split_contig_tail_kernel<1536,256>:
  REG=36, STACK=0, SHARED=0
  instr=288, HMMA=0, LDG=4, LDSM=0, STS=0, STG=6
  F2F=1, MUFU=0
```

Validation:

```text
git diff --check: pass
cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm audit: no matches
source LOC: 12038 total
test_clean.wav default BF16 path: 950.6 ms, RTF=41.65x
test.wav default BF16 path: 5583.2 ms, RTF=55.81x
```

Interpretation:

- The source producer preserves the bench lower-bound shape for the full tiles:
  `HMMA=256`, `LDG=112`, `LDSM=96`, `STS=88`, and only 24 coalesced 128-bit
  global stores.
- This confirms the good path is split-contiguous tile stores, not arbitrary
  per-element scatter. The bad bench scatter variant had many more stores and
  collapsed to roughly 10 TF/s.
- The current source cost is still positive code growth: LOC increased from the
  previous recorded 11860 total to 12038 total. It only becomes a net win after
  the old packed QKV output plus split/rotary materialization is bypassed or
  removed.
- Next required step: add split-contiguous rotary for `[B,1301,8,64]`, then wire
  an opt-in time-attention path that calls this producer and
  `launch_time_attention1301_split_contig_main_cutile(...)`.

### Source Scaffold: Time-QKV Split-Contiguous Rotary

The missing rotary building block for the split-contiguous time-QKV path has now
been moved into the source binary as an in-place CUDA Tile scaffold. It is not
connected to the default inference path yet. It expects the producer layout:

```text
q/k inout: [B,1301,8,64] BF16 split-contiguous
cos/sin:   [1301,32] float
```

Changed source files:

```text
src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu:
  rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel(...)
  apply_rotary_time_split_contig_inplace(...)

src/mbr_cuda_tile.h:
  rotary declaration
```

Build/resource/SASS evidence:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile cudasep_infer -j8

source rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel:
  REG=29, STACK=0, SHARED=0
  instr=448, HMMA=0, LDG=12, LDSM=0, STS=0, STG=8
  F2F=14, MUFU=0
```

Validation:

```text
git diff --check: pass
cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm audit: no matches
source LOC: 12144 total
test_clean.wav default BF16 path: 950.2 ms, RTF=41.66x
test.wav default BF16 path: 5610.4 ms, RTF=55.54x
```

Interpretation:

- The kernel is a light CUDA Tile elementwise pass and has no Tensor Core work,
  as expected for rotary.
- It is useful as a source-level scaffold because it completes the producer ->
  rotary -> split-contig attention chain at the API level.
- It is not the final performance target. SASS shows it adds a separate global
  memory read/write pass (`LDG=12`, `STG=8`), so the extreme A10G path should
  later fuse rotary into the QKV producer stores or into the attention load path.
- Next required step: add an opt-in time-attention path that runs
  `linear_qkv_bkn_split_contig_time(...)`,
  `apply_rotary_time_split_contig_inplace(...)`, and
  `launch_time_attention1301_split_contig_main_cutile(...)` for the main1280
  time path, with the existing tail path used only as an initial bridge.

### Opt-In Source Path: Time-QKV Split-Contiguous Attention

The time-QKV split-contiguous producer, rotary, and attention consumer are now
connected behind an opt-in environment variable:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_SPLIT_CONTIG_PATH=1
```

The opt-in path runs:

```text
linear_qkv_bkn_split_contig_time(...)
apply_rotary_time_split_contig_inplace(...)
launch_time_attention1301_split_contig_main_cutile(...)
launch_time_attention1301_split_contig_tail_cutile(...)
```

Shape flow:

```text
normed: [60,1301,256] BF16
q/k/v:  [60,1301,8,64] BF16 split-contiguous
attn:   [60,8,1301,64] BF16 after reshape from [480,1301,64]
```

Changed source files:

```text
src/mbr_time_attention_cutile.cu:
  time_attention1301_split_contig_tail_kernel<...>
  launch_time_attention1301_split_contig_tail_cutile(...)

src/mbr_cuda_tile.cu:
  time_qkv_split_contig_path_enabled(...)
  scaled_dot_product_attention_time_split_contig(...)

src/model_mel_band_roformer.cu:
  opt-in time attention branch
```

Build/resource/SASS evidence:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile cudasep_infer -j8

source time_attention1301_split_contig_tail_kernel<32,32,40,true>:
  REG=194, STACK=0, SHARED=26752
  instr=4656, HMMA=64, LDG=26, LDSM=24, STS=58, STG=16
  F2F=24, MUFU_EX2=48
```

Validation and timing:

```text
git diff --check: pass
cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm audit: no matches
source LOC: 12404 total

test_clean.wav default BF16 path:
  952.2 ms, RTF=41.58x
test_clean.wav opt-in split-contig path:
  935.8 ms, RTF=42.31x

test.wav default BF16 path:
  5577.3 ms, RTF=55.87x
test.wav opt-in split-contig path:
  5501.4 ms, RTF=56.64x
```

Output difference, default vs opt-in:

```text
test_clean.wav Vocals:
  max_abs=1.7881393e-07, rms=9.9055992e-09, rel_rms=5.3703405e-08
test_clean.wav Instrumental:
  max_abs=2.3841858e-07, rms=1.3084316e-08, rel_rms=5.4417685e-08

test.wav Vocals:
  max_abs=2.3841858e-07, rms=1.0219652e-08, rel_rms=5.4081055e-08
test.wav Instrumental:
  max_abs=2.3841858e-07, rms=1.0419442e-08, rel_rms=5.4345606e-08
```

Interpretation:

- The connected source path is now runnable and numerically matches the default
  BF16 path to tiny float-WAV differences.
- End-to-end timing improves slightly in both smoke runs, but this is not yet
  the final A10G shape. The path still has a separate rotary global read/write
  pass and keeps the old path in the binary.
- The new tail kernel keeps the split-contiguous layout and uses Tensor Core
  work (`HMMA=64`) for the final 21 rows, avoiding the earlier full-layout
  bridge idea.
- Next required step: profile the opt-in path kernel sequence, then either fuse
  rotary into the QKV producer or into attention load. After that, make the path
  default and remove the old packed QKV + split/rotary time path if the evidence
  still holds.

### Negative Source Experiment: Fused Rotary Inside Time-QKV Producer

Attempted to remove the separate split-contiguous rotary kernel by fusing time
rotary into the BKN QKV producer. The experiment is guarded by:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_SPLIT_CONTIG_PATH=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY_PRODUCER=1
```

Implementation shape:

```text
full producer:
  qkv_bkn_rotary_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256>
  reshapes acc [32,256] -> [32,4,32,2], extract even/odd, rotate, cat back

tail producer:
  qkv_bkn_rotary_split_contig_tail_kernel<1536,256>
  scalar double-accumulate for q/k pair columns
```

Resource/SASS evidence:

```text
qkv_bkn_rotary_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256>:
  REG=178, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=4160, HMMA=256, LDG=176, LDSM=96, STS=136, STG=24
  STG128=24, F2F=160, MUFU=0

qkv_bkn_rotary_split_contig_tail_kernel<1536,256>:
  REG=60, STACK=0, SHARED=0
  instr=592, HMMA=0, LDG=10, LDSM=0, STS=0, STG=6
  F2F=6, MUFU=0
```

Timing and quality:

```text
test_clean.wav separate-rotary opt-in:
  934.7 ms, RTF=42.36x
test_clean.wav fused-rotary producer opt-in:
  990.0 ms, RTF=39.99x

fused vs default Vocals:
  max_abs=0.010340005, rms=0.00079386819, rel_rms=0.0043039723
fused vs default Instrumental:
  max_abs=0.011306554, rms=0.0011220141, rel_rms=0.0046664577
```

Decision:

- Do not use this fused producer as the normal opt-in path.
- The normal opt-in path remains the previously validated producer + separate
  split-contiguous rotary, which still gives `rel_rms ~= 5.4e-8` vs default on
  `test_clean.wav`.
- The current `reshape/extract/cat` approach either pairs dimensions in a way
  that is not equivalent to the standalone rotary path or otherwise changes the
  numerical path too much. It is also slower: `LDG`, `STS`, and `F2F` rise enough
  to outweigh removing one light rotary kernel.
- Next fusion attempt should use a correctness-first microbench before touching
  inference again. Candidate directions: pair-tile even/odd projection kernels,
  or applying rotary in the attention load path where q/k are already being
  consumed.

Follow-up round-fix:

The first fused attempt rotated from FP32 accumulators, while the separate path
does `QKV acc -> BF16 store -> rotary`. The fused producer was changed to
BF16-round q/k even and odd values before rotary.

```text
qkv_bkn_rotary_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256>:
  REG=174, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  instr=3984, HMMA=256, LDG=176, LDSM=96, STS=136, STG=24
  STG128=24, F2F=160, MUFU=0

test_clean.wav fused-rotary producer opt-in after round-fix:
  991.4 ms, RTF=39.93x

fused after round-fix vs default Vocals:
  max_abs=0.0041534603, rms=0.00038288531, rel_rms=0.0020758204
fused after round-fix vs default Instrumental:
  max_abs=0.0059004426, rms=0.00051721773, rel_rms=0.0021511091
```

Decision after round-fix:

- The precision mismatch was real but not the only problem.
- Quality improved from roughly `rel_rms ~= 0.0043-0.0047` to
  `rel_rms ~= 0.0021`, but this is still far worse than the separate-rotary
  opt-in path (`rel_rms ~= 5.4e-8`).
- The fused producer also remains slower than separate rotary. Keep it behind
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY_PRODUCER=1`; do not promote it.
- Next step is a focused CUDA Tile layout probe for `[32,256] -> [32,4,32,2]`
  `reshape/extract/cat`, or switch to pair-tile even/odd projection where the
  pairing is explicit.

Follow-up layout probe:

A focused CUDA Tile microbench was added to check whether the fused producer's
`[32,256] -> [32,4,32,2]` reshape/extract/cat assumption is wrong.

```text
ninja -C build-min bench_cutile_tile_layout_probe -j8
./build-min/bench_cutile_tile_layout_probe

identity max_abs=0
rot90 max_abs=0
```

Probe semantics:

- identity path:
  `reshape -> extract even/odd -> cat(even, odd) -> reshape` reproduces the
  source tile exactly.
- rot90 path:
  `cat(-odd, even)` matches the expected per-head even/odd rotation for
  `cos=0, sin=1`.

Decision after layout probe:

- The fused producer quality problem is not explained by a basic CUDA Tile
  `reshape/extract/cat` layout-order mismatch.
- Keep the fused producer as an explicit negative experiment for now.
- Next debugging target should be producer math/head mapping/rounding relative
  to the standalone path, or move the next rotary fusion attempt into the
  attention load path where the stored q/k layout is already validated.

Follow-up tensor compare and tail fix:

A debug-only compare path was added behind:

```text
CUDASEP_DEBUG_COMPARE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY=1
CUDASEP_DEBUG_COMPARE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY_LIMIT=<N>
```

It runs both producer paths for the first `N` time-attention calls and compares
the BF16 q/k/v tensors after rotary. The first check showed that the full
producer tiles were exact, but the scalar fused tail rows had small ULP-level
differences:

```text
fused actual path before tail fix, first 12 time-attention calls:
  call=2 q max_abs=0.00390625 rel_rms=5.28006e-07 at=[b59,n1300,h3,d26]
  call=8 k max_abs=0.000488281 rel_rms=7.35829e-08 at=[b59,n1297,h0,d9]
  most other q/k/v compares were exactly zero

test_clean.wav fused before tail fix vs safe split-contig:
  Vocals       max_abs=0.00415347517 rel_rms=0.00207582034
  Instrumental max_abs=0.00590044260 rel_rms=0.00215110908
```

The fused producer tail was changed to use the already validated non-rotary
QKV tail kernel followed by a new tail-only split-contiguous rotary kernel.
This keeps the full rows fused, but makes the last 12 rows follow the same
`QKV acc -> BF16 store -> rotary` path as the standalone implementation.

```text
fused actual path after tail fix, first 12 time-attention calls:
  all q/k/v compares max_abs=0, rms=0, rel_rms=0

test_clean.wav safe split-contig:
  939.0 ms, RTF=42.16x
test_clean.wav fused full + exact tail:
  996.5 ms, RTF=39.73x

test_clean.wav fused full + exact tail vs safe split-contig:
  Vocals       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rel_rms=5.41805326e-08
  Instrumental max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rel_rms=5.41080358e-08

test.wav fused full + exact tail vs safe split-contig:
  Vocals       max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rel_rms=5.48226971e-08
  Instrumental max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rel_rms=5.47553802e-08
```

Decision after tail fix:

- The previous fused producer quality regression was caused by non-identical
  scalar tail math, not by the full CUDA Tile producer layout.
- The exact-tail fix restores output quality to the normal float-WAV noise
  floor on both `test_clean.wav` and `test.wav`.
- The fused producer remains slower than separate rotary on `test_clean.wav`
  (`996.5 ms` vs `939.0 ms`), so it should stay behind
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY_PRODUCER=1`.
- Next performance direction is not more scalar tail work; it is reducing the
  full fused producer's extra `LDG/STS/F2F` cost or moving rotary fusion into
  the attention load path.

### Negative Source Experiment: Time-QKV Pair Rotary Producer

After the exact-tail fix, the next hypothesis was that the full fused producer
was slow because it rotates a full `[32,256]` accumulator tile via
`reshape/extract/cat`. A pair-tile producer was added behind:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_SPLIT_CONTIG_PATH=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_PAIR_ROTARY_PRODUCER=1
```

Implementation shape:

```text
qkv_time_rotary_pair_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,32,16,78048,256>
  grid: [78048/32, 3*8]
  per block: one (part, head), two [32,32] MMA accumulators for even/odd dims

tail:
  qkv_bkn_split_contig_tail_kernel<1536,256>
  rotary_time_split_contig_tail_inplace_kernel
```

Correctness:

```text
CUDASEP_DEBUG_COMPARE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY=1
CUDASEP_DEBUG_COMPARE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY_LIMIT=12

first 12 time-attention calls:
  all q/k/v compares max_abs=0, rms=0, rel_rms=0

test_clean.wav pair producer vs safe split-contig:
  Vocals       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rel_rms=5.42215024e-08
  Instrumental max_abs=2.98023224e-07 rel_rms=5.43744192e-08
```

Timing:

```text
test_clean.wav safe split-contig:
  939.0 ms, RTF=42.16x
test_clean.wav fused full + exact tail:
  996.5 ms, RTF=39.73x
test_clean.wav pair producer:
  1144.1 ms, RTF=34.60x
```

Resource/SASS evidence:

```text
pair producer full kernel:
  REG=110, STACK=0, SHARED=65560
  HMMA=64, LDG=115, LDSM=52, STS=78, STG=32, F2F=40

full fused producer full kernel:
  REG=174, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
  HMMA=256, LDG=176, LDSM=96, STS=136, STG=24, F2F=160
```

Interpretation:

- Pair producer is numerically exact, so the pair-tile mapping is valid.
- It is slower because it changes the full producer from 6 blocks per
  `tile_m` to 24 blocks per `tile_m`. Total HMMA work is roughly the same, but
  aggregate LDG/STS and block scheduling increase substantially.
- This is a useful A10G shape result: narrower pair tiles reduce per-block
  resources, but they are not a better shape for time QKV because global memory
  traffic and launch block count dominate.
- Do not promote `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_PAIR_ROTARY_PRODUCER`. The next useful
  direction is either optimizing the existing wide full fused producer, or
  prototyping attention-load rotary carefully enough to keep K in `[D,KTile]`
  Tensor Core layout.

### Time-QKV Producer Timing Split

A profile-only timing path was added behind:

```text
CUDASEP_PROFILE_TIME_QKV_PRODUCER=1
```

It uses CUDA events around the actual model calls and prints cumulative producer
timing at process exit. This separates the safe split producer, standalone
rotary, full fused producer, and pair fused producer.

Existing QKV GEMM microbench, `infer_qkv M=78060 N=1536 K=256`:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  best=1.379 ms, median=1.383 ms, 44.51 TF/s, roof70=63.6%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig:
  best=1.399 ms, median=1.411 ms, 43.89 TF/s, roof70=62.7%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk:
  best=1.380 ms, median=1.383 ms, 44.48 TF/s, roof70=63.5%
qkv_t64x256x16bkn:
  best=2.223 ms, 27.60 TF/s, roof70=39.4%
qkv_t32x128x16bkn:
  best=1.773 ms, 34.63 TF/s, roof70=49.5%
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  best=1.937 ms, 31.69 TF/s, roof70=45.3%
qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled:
  best=1.464 ms, 41.93 TF/s, roof70=59.9%
qkv_t32x256x64bkn:
  best=1.774 ms, 34.59 TF/s, roof70=49.4%
qkv_t32x256x128bkn:
  best=1.709 ms, 35.92 TF/s, roof70=51.3%
```

Actual `test_clean.wav` model-call profile, 48 time-attention calls:

```text
safe split-contig:
  split_producer calls=48 total_ms=71.2573 avg_ms=1.48453
  split_rotary   calls=48 total_ms=24.1986 avg_ms=0.504137
  combined producer+rotary avg ~= 1.98867 ms
  end-to-end 939.1 ms, RTF=42.16x

full fused producer + exact tail:
  bkn_fused_producer calls=48 total_ms=155.595 avg_ms=3.24156
  end-to-end 1001.7 ms, RTF=39.52x

pair fused producer:
  pair_fused_producer calls=48 total_ms=301.113 avg_ms=6.27318
  end-to-end 1151.7 ms, RTF=34.38x
```

Interpretation:

- The standalone rotary pass is not the dominant cost: about `0.50 ms` per
  time-attention call.
- The current wide fused producer is slower than safe producer+rotary combined
  (`3.24 ms` vs `1.99 ms` per call), so its extra `LDG/STS/F2F` and resource
  pressure outweigh removing one light global pass.
- The best tested QKV producer shape remains `32x256x16` on A10G, around
  `44.5 TF/s` or `63-64%` of the local 70 TF/s dense BF16 reference.
- Next step should not be more producer-side rotary fusion unless the wide
  producer's full-tile rotate can be made close to the unfused `32x256x16bkn`
  cost. Attention-load rotary is worth prototyping only if it keeps K in the
  `[D,KTile]` Tensor Core view and avoids recomputing K rotation per Q block.

### Negative Attention Experiment: QKV Direct Rotary Load

The existing time-attention bench includes a direct-rotary variant that reads
packed QKV, rotates q/k inside the attention kernel, then feeds the resulting
tiles to `ct::mma`:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input
main1280_q64k32_exp2_qkv_direct_rotary
```

Timing on A10G:

```text
split-contig input:
  best=3.397 ms, median=3.423 ms
  real_math=60.24 TF/s, logical_tile_math=62.23 TF/s, roof70=86.1%
  compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2: max_abs=0, rms=0, rel_rms=0

qkv direct rotary:
  best=18.963 ms, median=19.147 ms
  real_math=10.79 TF/s, logical_tile_math=10.88 TF/s, roof70=15.4%
  compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2: max_abs=0, rms=0, rel_rms=0
```

Resource/SASS evidence:

```text
split-contig input kernel<64,32,true>:
  REG=233, STACK=0, SHARED=40960
  HMMA=160, LDG=32, LDSM=64, STS=60, STG=4, F2F=56, MUFU=124

qkv direct rotary kernel<64,32,true>:
  REG=224, STACK=0, SHARED=32768
  HMMA=64, LDG=268, STS=20, STG=32, F2F=63, MUFU=70
```

Interpretation:

- Direct rotary is numerically exact, but it is not a viable performance path.
- It does not preserve the efficient split-contiguous K load pattern. Instead,
  it computes per-element packed-QKV addresses and rotates K inside each K tile.
- The result is far more global loads (`LDG=268` vs `32`) and much less useful
  Tensor Core structure (`HMMA=64` vs `160` in the SASS slice), dropping from
  `86.1%` to `15.4%` of the 70 TF/s reference.
- Attention-load rotary is only worth revisiting if K can still be loaded as a
  `[D,KTile]` tile through a strided view, with rotation applied without
  per-element gathered loads or per-Q-block K recomputation.

### Full Infer-QKV CUDA Tile Shape Sweep

Command:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --preset infer_linear --shape infer_qkv --variant all --warmup 3 --iters 10
```

Shape:

```text
infer_qkv M=78060 N=1536 K=256
```

Results on A10G:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  best=1.393 ms, median=1.395 ms, 44.07 TF/s, roof70=63.0%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk:
  best=1.392 ms, median=1.394 ms, 44.09 TF/s, roof70=63.0%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig:
  best=1.410 ms, median=1.413 ms, 43.52 TF/s, roof70=62.2%
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  best=1.468 ms, median=1.476 ms, 41.80 TF/s, roof70=59.7%
qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled:
  best=1.465 ms, median=1.471 ms, 41.91 TF/s, roof70=59.9%
qkv_t32x256x32bkn_pairk:
  best=1.467 ms, median=1.471 ms, 41.83 TF/s, roof70=59.8%
t32x256x32s:
  best=1.538 ms, median=1.544 ms, 39.92 TF/s, roof70=57.0%

Negative shapes:
qkv_t32x256x8bkn:
  best=3.193 ms, median=3.195 ms, 19.22 TF/s, roof70=27.5%
qkv_t64x256x16bkn:
  best=2.227 ms, median=2.229 ms, 27.55 TF/s, roof70=39.4%
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  best=1.939 ms, median=1.944 ms, 31.65 TF/s, roof70=45.2%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  best=5.995 ms, median=6.006 ms, 10.24 TF/s, roof70=14.6%
```

Interpretation:

- `32x256x16 BKN` remains the best tested QKV projection shape on A10G.
- Split-contiguous output storage is a small positive-path cost: about `0.019 ms`
  or `0.6 TF/s` versus plain BKN, much cheaper than scatter-time layout.
- `KTile=16` is consistently better than `KTile=32/64/128` for this shape. The
  larger K tiles likely raise shared-memory/register pressure enough to lose more
  than they save in loop overhead.
- `MTile=64` and `NTile=512` are both negative despite reducing some block-count
  overhead. For this fixed `M=78060,N=1536,K=256` workload, A10G prefers the
  smaller M tile and `N=256` output tile.
- The remaining problem is not choosing between the already-tested shapes; it is
  why the best CUDA Tile QKV producer stalls at about `44 TF/s` while the
  split-contiguous time-attention path reaches about `60 TF/s`. Next evidence
  should come from resource usage and SASS counts for the best QKV kernels.

Resource usage:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig:
  REG=174, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  REG=172, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  REG=206, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  REG=255, STACK=8, SHARED=81920
qkv_t64x256x16bkn:
  REG=218, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x8bkn:
  REG=255, STACK=0, SHARED=97792
```

Selected SASS mnemonic counts:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=24, F2F=32
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_scatter_time:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=192, F2F=32
qkv_t32x256x32bkn:
  HMMA=256, LDG=88, LDSM=96, STS=80, STG=8, F2F=32
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  HMMA=512, LDG=176, LDSM=160, STS=160, STG=16, F2F=64
qkv_t64x256x16bkn:
  HMMA=512, LDG=112, LDSM=128, STS=96, STG=16, F2F=64
qkv_t32x256x8bkn:
  HMMA=512, LDG=160, LDSM=96, STS=104, STG=8, F2F=32
```

SASS interpretation:

- `pairk` is instruction-identical to the best `32x256x16` in this binary, so it
  is not a path to a new bottleneck regime.
- Split-contiguous output only adds stores (`STG=24` vs `8`) and a few registers,
  matching the small timing loss. This is acceptable for the source path because
  it enables the much faster split-contiguous attention consumer.
- Scatter-time output keeps the same Tensor Core body but explodes stores
  (`STG=192`), which explains the `10 TF/s` result and rules it out.
- `KTile=32` reduces LDG/STS versus `KTile=16`, but raises registers
  (`REG=206` vs `170`) and remains slower. The likely limiter is now occupancy
  and shared/register pressure around the CUDA Tile generated staging, not raw
  global-load count alone.
- `MTile=64`, `NTile=512`, and `KTile=8` all double the HMMA instruction count in
  the static SASS slice or push register usage to `218-255`, which matches their
  poor throughput. The next useful experiment should reduce per-block resource
  pressure for the `32x256x16` mapping without changing the output layout.

### Infer-QKV CUDA Tile Hint Sweep

The next A10G-specific test was to keep the best `32x256x16 BKN` mapping and
change only CUDA Tile codegen hints. This follows the CUDA Tile optimization
hint model from the official CUDA Tile documentation: hints can guide codegen
without changing program semantics.

Command:

```bash
for v in qkv_t32x256x16bkn qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ8 qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ12 qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ16 qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ24 qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat4 qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat7 qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat10; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 5 --iters 30
done
```

Timing:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  best=1.381 ms, median=1.405 ms, 44.46 TF/s, roof70=63.5%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ8:
  best=1.390 ms, median=1.393 ms, 44.15 TF/s, roof70=63.1%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ12:
  best=1.380 ms, median=1.393 ms, 44.49 TF/s, roof70=63.6%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ16:
  best=1.391 ms, median=1.397 ms, 44.12 TF/s, roof70=63.0%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ24:
  best=1.389 ms, median=1.393 ms, 44.18 TF/s, roof70=63.1%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat4:
  best=1.421 ms, median=1.426 ms, 43.20 TF/s, roof70=61.7%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat7:
  best=1.545 ms, median=1.551 ms, 39.72 TF/s, roof70=56.7%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat10:
  best=1.545 ms, median=1.550 ms, 39.72 TF/s, roof70=56.7%
```

Resource usage:

```text
occ8/occ12/occ16/occ24:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
lat4:
  REG=174, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
lat7/lat10:
  REG=186, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
```

SASS counts:

```text
base and occ8/occ12/occ16/occ24:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=72
lat4:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=72
lat7/lat10:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=80
```

Interpretation:

- Occupancy hints do not move this kernel. Resource usage and selected SASS counts
  are identical to baseline, and timing stays within noise.
- Latency hints are negative. `lat4` slightly raises register usage; `lat7/lat10`
  raise register usage to `186` and add integer address/control work, dropping to
  about `39.7 TF/s`.
- The public hint surface is not enough to fix the QKV gap. The next useful QKV
  experiment should change the tile decomposition or the way CUDA Tile stages
  A/B tiles, not only add hints around the same load/mma/store body.

### Infer-QKV Unsupported Intermediate N Tile Probe

After the hint sweep, the next direct idea was to fill the gap between
`N=128`, `N=256`, and `N=512` by testing `32x192x16` and `32x384x16` BKN
single-tile kernels. That does not compile with CUDA Tile because these shapes
are not accepted by the `tile_shape` constraints.

Attempted variants:

```text
qkv_t32x192x16bkn
qkv_t32x384x16bkn
```

Compiler evidence:

```text
ct::tile<float, ct::shape<32, 192>>:
  template constraint not satisfied
  concept tile_shape is false

ct::partition_view with ct::shape<16, 192> / ct::shape<32, 192>:
  no instance of constructor matches the argument list

ct::partition_view with ct::shape<16, 384> / ct::shape<32, 384>:
  no instance of constructor matches the argument list
```

Interpretation:

- CUDA Tile shape sweep is not arbitrary. For this GEMM family, the compatible
  N tile sizes observed so far are the power-of-two style shapes already tested:
  `32/64/128/256/512`.
- The `32x192` and `32x384` middle points cannot be represented as one
  `ct::tile` accumulator. Future `N=384`-like experiments would need a compound
  CTA such as `256+128` output tiles, which is a different kernel structure with
  multiple accumulator tiles, not a simple shape parameter.
- This narrows the next QKV optimization path: either improve the existing
  `32x256x16` staging, or explicitly prototype a compound-output CTA to reduce
  block count while keeping CUDA Tile-compatible subtiles.

### Infer-QKV Compound 256+128 Output CTA

Because `32x384` is not a compatible single CUDA Tile accumulator shape, the next
prototype keeps CUDA Tile-compatible subtiles and computes `256+128` output
columns inside one CTA. The kernel uses `layout_strided` views so the B and C
submatrices keep the original row stride `N=1536`; it does not treat the
submatrix as compact `Kx384`.

Variant:

```text
qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn
```

Command:

```bash
for v in qkv_t32x256x16bkn qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn qkv_t32x512x16bkn; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 5 --iters 30
done
```

Timing:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.379 ms, median=1.384 ms, 44.52 TF/s, roof70=63.6%
qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,4), best=2.346 ms, median=2.351 ms, 26.17 TF/s, roof70=37.4%
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  grid=(2439,3), best=1.933 ms, median=1.940 ms, 31.76 TF/s, roof70=45.4%
```

Resource usage:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn:
  REG=254, STACK=0, SHARED=96256
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  REG=255, STACK=8, SHARED=81920
```

SASS counts:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=72
qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn:
  HMMA=384, LDG=160, LDSM=128, STS=124, STG=12, F2F=48, IMAD=94
qkv_t32x512x16bkn:
  HMMA=512, LDG=176, LDSM=160, STS=160, STG=16, F2F=64, IMAD=107
```

Interpretation:

- The compound CTA is numerically sane at the checksum level, but it is a clear
  performance negative.
- Reducing the N-direction grid from `6` to `4` blocks does not reduce total
  Tensor Core work; the per-CTA static HMMA count rises from `256` to `384`, so
  aggregate HMMA work is effectively unchanged.
- The real loss is resource pressure: `REG=254`, close to the `N=512` kernel and
  much higher than the `REG=170` baseline. With roughly one large-shared-memory
  CTA per SM, this leaves too little latency hiding and drops to `26 TF/s`.
- Reusing the A tile across `256+128` output subtiles does not compensate for two
  accumulator tiles and strided subviews. Do not promote this compound-output CTA
  to the source path.
- The QKV path should focus on making the existing `32x256x16` CTA lighter or
  on a more radical split-K/pipelined design; simply packing more output columns
  into one CTA is not an A10G win.

### Infer-QKV Load-Temporary and Matmul API Probe

This probe keeps the best `32x256x16 BKN` shape and changes only the inner-loop
expression form:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp:
  load A/B tiles into named temporaries, then call ct::mma
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_matmul:
  use acc = acc + ct::matmul(load(A), load(B))
```

Command:

```bash
for v in qkv_t32x256x16bkn qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp qkv_t32x256x16bkn_matmul; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 5 --iters 30
done
```

Timing:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  best=1.380 ms, median=1.404 ms, 44.47 TF/s, roof70=63.5%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp:
  best=1.379 ms, median=1.390 ms, 44.52 TF/s, roof70=63.6%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_matmul:
  best=2.535 ms, median=2.538 ms, 24.21 TF/s, roof70=34.6%
```

Resource usage:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_matmul:
  REG=255, STACK=8, SHARED=98304
```

SASS counts:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=72
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=72
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_matmul:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=9
```

Interpretation:

- The nested call form `ct::mma(a_view.load(...), b_view.load(...), acc)` is not
  the QKV bottleneck. The explicit-load variant is resource- and SASS-identical
  to baseline and has the same timing.
- `ct::matmul` still emits the same HMMA count, but it drives register usage to
  `255` and spills/uses stack, dropping throughput to `24 TF/s`. For this QKV
  shape, `ct::mma` is the correct low-level API.
- This narrows the remaining QKV gap to CUDA Tile's generated staging/resource
  footprint for the `32x256x16` CTA, not to expression nesting or choosing
  `matmul` instead of `mma`.

### Infer-QKV K=128 Prefix Probe for Split-K

Before implementing a full split-K QKV path with partial outputs and reduction,
this probe tests whether a half-K CTA is intrinsically more efficient. It keeps
the best `32x256x16 BKN` output tile, but computes only the first `K=128`
columns. A uses a strided view because the real activation row stride remains
`KFull=256`; reported TFLOPS use `KRun=128`, not the full `K=256`.

Variant:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_k128
```

Command:

```bash
for v in qkv_t32x256x16bkn qkv_t32x256x16bkn_k128; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 5 --iters 30
done
```

Timing:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  best=1.381 ms, median=1.385 ms, 44.45 TF/s, roof70=63.5%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_k128:
  best=0.947 ms, median=0.977 ms, 32.40 TF/s, roof70=46.3%
```

Resource usage:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  REG=170, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_k128:
  REG=133, STACK=0, SHARED=90112
```

SASS counts:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn:
  HMMA=256, LDG=112, LDSM=96, STS=88, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=72
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_k128:
  HMMA=128, LDG=56, LDSM=48, STS=48, STG=8, F2F=32, IMAD=64
```

Interpretation:

- K-prefix resource pressure does drop (`REG=170 -> 133`,
  `SHARED=98304 -> 90112`), so the split-K idea does reduce some per-CTA
  resource pressure.
- Throughput gets worse because useful HMMA work is halved while output stores,
  BF16 conversion, control/address overhead, and launch scheduling do not fall
  proportionally. `STG` and `F2F` are unchanged, and `IMAD` barely drops.
- A full split-K implementation would need two half-K partial producers plus at
  least one reduction/writeback pass, so this precheck strongly predicts it will
  be slower than the current full-K `32x256x16` QKV producer.
- Do not prioritize split-K for this QKV projection unless a future CUDA Tile
  feature exposes lower-overhead pipelined accumulation without extra global
  partial writes.

### FFN12 Pair-H32/TK64 W1 Load Latency Hint

After the QKV detour, the candidates-only FFN12 bench was rerun to refresh the
current best schedule and add one narrow candidate around the established
pair-H32/TK64 shape. The new candidate changes only W1-side loads:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat8
```

It keeps `TM=32`, pair-H32, `TK=64`, split2 output, poly9 GELU, and normal bias
handling. It adds `cutile::hint(0, latency=8)` around the A tile and two W1 tile
loads in the FFN1 inner loop. The existing W2 latency hint is included as a
direct comparison.

Focused command:

```bash
for v in fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat8 fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2lat8; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile --variant "$v" --warmup 10 --iters 200
done
```

Focused timing:

```text
pair-H32/TK64 baseline:
  best=1.856 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s, roof70=63.0%
W1 latency=8:
  best=3.321 ms, median=3.323 ms, useful=24.64 TF/s, roof70=35.2%
W2 latency=8:
  best=3.363 ms, median=3.365 ms, useful=24.34 TF/s, roof70=34.8%
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
W1 latency=8:
  REG=253, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
W2 latency=8:
  REG=255, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
```

SASS counts:

```text
baseline:
  HMMA=256, LDG=208, LDSM=128, STS=124, STG=8, F2F=96,
  FFMA=256, FADD=192, FMUL=160, IMAD=352
W1 latency=8:
  HMMA=128, LDG=136, LDSM=64, STS=74, STG=8, F2F=80,
  FFMA=128, FADD=128, FMUL=80, IMAD=342
W2 latency=8:
  HMMA=128, LDG=136, LDSM=64, STS=74, STG=8, F2F=80,
  FFMA=128, FADD=128, FMUL=80, IMAD=341
```

Candidates-only `all` rerun after adding W1 latency:

```text
pair-H32/TK64 baseline:      best=1.857 ms, useful=44.07 TF/s
single-H32/TK64:             best=2.187 ms, useful=37.43 TF/s
TM8 pair-H32/TK64:           best=77.290 ms, useful=1.06 TF/s
pair-H32/TK128:              best=2.451 ms, useful=33.39 TF/s
halfout recompute:           best=3.406 ms, useful=24.03 TF/s, actual=36.04 TF/s
no output bias:              best=2.050 ms, useful=39.92 TF/s
no hidden/output bias:       best=1.996 ms, useful=41.00 TF/s
W1 latency=8:                best=3.533 ms, useful=23.16 TF/s
split4 output:               best=2.106 ms, useful=38.86 TF/s
accumulator grouped order:   best=1.886 ms, useful=43.39 TF/s
W2 latency=8:                best=3.603 ms, useful=22.72 TF/s
```

Interpretation:

- W1 load latency hint is a clear negative result, just like the previous W2 load
  latency hint.
- Both hints push register usage toward the upper limit (`253/255`) and change
  CUDA Tile codegen into a different, slower schedule. Static HMMA count is lower
  in the SASS slice, but runtime is much worse and checksum remains consistent,
  so the issue is schedule/resource quality rather than correctness.
- Public latency hints are not a useful lever for the current FFN12 pair-H32/TK64
  kernel. The current best remains pair-H32/TK64 with no latency hint.
- Future FFN12 work should look for structural changes that preserve the baseline
  HMMA=256 schedule while reducing `REG=242`/`SHARED=98304`, rather than hinting
  individual W1/W2 loads.

### BF16 Binary Inplace Same-Shape: Op Specialization and Tile-Size Probe

The source `binary_inplace_same_shape_kernel<bf16>` hotspot is now compiled as a
`BinaryOp` template instead of carrying a runtime op selector inside every tile.
This removes the unused add/sub/mul/div select path from the hot `add_` case.

Resource evidence from `build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
previous binary_inplace_same_shape_kernel<bf16>: REG=27 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
new add/sub/mul 256-tile BF16 instances:         REG=12 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
new div 256-tile BF16 instance:                 REG=23 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
```

The BF16 inplace same-shape path also has a source-profile experiment switch:

```bash
CUDASEP_BINARY_INPLACE_BF16_TILE=128|512|1024
```

Default remains `256`. Focused Nsight Systems profiles on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default 256 tile:
  infer=965.8 ms, binary=35.293 ms / 192 calls, avg=183.817 us
128 tile:
  infer=965.6 ms, binary=35.300 ms / 192 calls, avg=183.853 us
512 tile:
  infer=964.1 ms, binary=35.374 ms / 192 calls, avg=184.238 us
1024 tile:
  infer=959.8 ms, binary=35.209 ms / 192 calls, avg=183.381 us
```

Resource usage for the BF16 add/mul tile-size variants:

```text
128 tile:  REG=10 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
256 tile:  REG=12 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
512 tile:  REG=16 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
1024 tile: REG=24 SHARED=0 LOCAL=0
```

Interpretation:

- Runtime-op specialization is codegen-positive but not source-profile positive:
  the 192-call binary group stays around `35.2-35.4 ms`.
- Larger tiles reduce CTA count but raise register usage; the best observed
  1024-tile run is only about `0.08 ms` faster than default 256, within normal
  profiler noise for the whole run.
- This hotspot is not limited by the extra enum/select arithmetic or by a
  slightly wrong one-dimensional tile shape. It is dominated by repeated global
  memory read/read/write traffic plus 192 separate launches.
- The next useful move is to eliminate these calls by fusing the adjacent
  residual/add sites into their producer or consumer kernels, not by further
  tuning this standalone binary kernel.

### Negative Probe: FFN12 Output + Residual Add Fusion

The FFN residual add site in `apply_transformer()` was tested by adding an
opt-in residual output path to the source FFN12 fused CUDA Tile kernel. The path
is intentionally disabled by default:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_RESIDUAL=1
```

The opt-in path is restricted to the current default FFN12 shape:

```text
GELU=poly9, FullBF16=false, split2 output, pair-H32, InputTileK=64
```

Implementation shape:

- Full rows: `ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,true>`
- Tail rows: `ffn12_tail_ffn2_cutile_kernel<false,true>`
- The FFN output is first rounded/stored as BF16 value in-tile, then added to
  the BF16 residual in the same CUDA Tile kernel to mimic the old standalone
  BF16 `out.add_(ff_out)` behavior.

Resource evidence:

```text
default FFN12 pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
residual-fused FFN12 pair-H32/TK64:
  REG=254 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
residual-fused tail FFN2:
  REG=255 STACK=24 SHARED=65664
```

Runtime evidence on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default-off plain run:
  infer=961.2 ms
opt-in residual-fused plain run:
  infer=1162.5 ms

default-off Nsight:
  infer=980.6 ms
  ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32<6,false,64,false>: 183.503 ms / 96 calls
  binary_inplace_same_shape add<bf16>:              35.296 ms / 192 calls
  tail_ffn2<false,false>:                            5.333 ms / 96 calls

opt-in residual-fused Nsight:
  infer=1197.3 ms
  ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32<6,false,64,true>:  418.797 ms / 96 calls
  binary_inplace_same_shape add<bf16>:              17.612 ms / 96 calls
  tail_ffn2<false,true>:                             5.830 ms / 96 calls
```

Output difference, opt-in residual-fused versus default-off on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rms=1.00157234e-08 rel_rms=5.43004452e-08
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rms=1.31252343e-08 rel_rms=5.45878629e-08
```

Interpretation:

- The fusion does remove half of the standalone BF16 residual adds:
  `binary_inplace_same_shape` drops from `192` calls to `96` calls, saving about
  `17.7 ms`.
- That saving is overwhelmed by the FFN12 main kernel becoming much slower:
  about `183.5 ms -> 418.8 ms`. The direct cause is extra residual load/add
  pressure on an already high-pressure Tensor Core tile kernel, pushing the
  default pair-H32/TK64 path from `REG=242` to `REG=254`.
- This is a concrete example where "deeper fusion" is counterproductive: the
  removed global-memory pass is small, while the producer kernel is already at
  the register cliff.
- Future residual work should not fuse into the current FFN12 accumulator kernel.
  Better candidates are lower-pressure output-projection kernels or a separate
  residual epilogue design that does not disturb the FFN12 HMMA schedule.

### Positive Probe: Attention Output Projection + Residual Epilogue

The attention residual add site in `apply_transformer()` was tested by adding an
initially opt-in residual epilogue to the CUDA Tile static output projection:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_ATTENTION_OUT_RESIDUAL=1
```

After the positive result below and the follow-up `TN=64` negative sweep, the
`TN=128` residual epilogue was promoted to the default BF16 path. Rollback:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_ATTENTION_OUT_RESIDUAL=1
```

It is restricted to the current MelBandRoformer attention output projection shape:

```text
total_batch=78060, K=512, N=256, StaticM=78048, TM=32, TN=128, TK=32
```

Implementation shape:

- Full rows:
  `linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,128,32,78048,256,512,false,false,0,false,true>`
- Tail rows:
  `linear_cutile_tail_bf16_kernel<256,512,false,false,0,false,true>`
- The projection accumulator is first rounded to BF16, then the BF16 residual is
  added inside the CUDA Tile kernel. This intentionally mimics the old behavior:
  output projection writes BF16, then the standalone BF16 inplace add consumes
  that value.

Resource evidence:

```text
default attention out static full:
  REG=228 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
residual-epilogue attention out static full:
  REG=243 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
default attention out tail:
  REG=32 STACK=0 SHARED=0
residual-epilogue attention out tail:
  REG=27 STACK=0 SHARED=0
```

Runtime evidence on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
plain default:
  infer=967.3 ms
```
