```text
plain opt-in:
  infer=952.3 ms

Nsight default:
  infer=979.5 ms
  total CUDA kernel time: 939.500 ms
  attention out static full:       49.038 ms / 96 calls
  attention out tail:               8.863 ms / 96 calls
  binary_inplace_same_shape add:   35.287 ms / 192 calls

Nsight opt-in:
  infer=963.3 ms
  total CUDA kernel time: 925.556 ms
  attention out static full:       50.724 ms / 96 calls
  attention out tail:               8.907 ms / 96 calls
  binary_inplace_same_shape add:   17.711 ms / 96 calls
```

Runtime evidence on `test.wav`:

```text
plain default:
  infer=5675.7 ms
plain opt-in:
  infer=5584.6 ms
```

Output difference, opt-in versus default on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.43537710e-08 diff_rms=1.00255593e-08 max_abs=1.78813934e-07
Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.46992895e-08 diff_rms=1.31520260e-08 max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Output difference, opt-in versus default on `test.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.42285888e-08 diff_rms=1.02475315e-08 max_abs=2.38418579e-07
Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.43589378e-08 diff_rms=1.04219977e-08 max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- This fusion removes the attention residual add but leaves the FFN residual add
  as a standalone kernel. The standalone BF16 add drops from `192` calls to
  `96` calls, saving about `17.6 ms` on `test_clean.wav`.
- The projection kernel pays for the extra residual read/add:
  `49.038 ms -> 50.724 ms` for the full rows, plus a negligible tail increase.
  The net Nsight kernel-time saving is about `13.9 ms`.
- Unlike FFN12 residual fusion, this kernel had enough register headroom for the
  epilogue to be worthwhile: `REG=228 -> 243`, instead of the FFN path hitting
  the `254` register cliff and slowing the producer kernel by more than `2x`.
- The result supports a narrow rule for deeper fusion on A10G CUDA Tile: fuse
  cheap residual epilogues into lower-pressure projection kernels first; avoid
  adding work to already register-limited Tensor Core kernels.

#### A10G Shape Sweep: Attention Out Residual `TN=64` vs `TN=128`

The residual epilogue path supports a narrow A/B selector:

```bash
CUDASEP_ATTENTION_OUT_RESIDUAL_TILE_N=64   # or 128; default is 128
```

Both shapes use the same fixed projection:

```text
total_batch=78060, K=512, N=256, StaticM=78048, TM=32, TK=32
```

Resource evidence:

```text
TN=128 residual full:
  REG=243 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
TN=64 residual full:
  REG=91 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
tail residual:
  REG=27 STACK=0 SHARED=0
```

Plain `test_clean.wav` runtime:

```text
default, no residual epilogue:
  infer=961.0 ms
TN=128 residual epilogue:
  infer=947.5 ms
TN=64 residual epilogue:
  infer=963.2 ms
```

Nsight `test_clean.wav` runtime:

```text
TN=128:
  infer=966.6 ms
  total CUDA kernel time: 925.439 ms
  attention out static full:       50.724 ms / 96 calls
  attention out tail:               8.904 ms / 96 calls
  binary_inplace_same_shape add:   17.714 ms / 96 calls

TN=64:
  infer=977.2 ms
  total CUDA kernel time: 942.905 ms
  attention out static full:       66.628 ms / 96 calls
  attention out tail:               8.899 ms / 96 calls
  binary_inplace_same_shape add:   17.714 ms / 96 calls
```

Estimated static-full projection throughput, counting only the `M=78048,N=256,K=512`
work across 96 calls:

```text
TN=128: ~38.7 TFLOP/s
TN=64:  ~29.5 TFLOP/s
```

Long input check:

```text
test.wav TN=64 residual epilogue:
  infer=5676.2 ms
```

Output difference versus default on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
TN=128 Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.40530438e-08 diff_rms=9.97009014e-09 max_abs=1.78813934e-07
TN=128 Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.41885751e-08 diff_rms=1.30292286e-08 max_abs=2.98023224e-07
TN=64 Vocals.wav:        rel_rms=5.42023268e-08 diff_rms=9.99762541e-09 max_abs=1.78813934e-07
TN=64 Instrumental.wav:  rel_rms=5.40524950e-08 diff_rms=1.29965092e-08 max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- `TN=64` dramatically lowers register pressure (`243 -> 91`), but it is still
  slower because `N=256` requires twice as many N tiles/CTAs: `4` N tiles
  instead of `2`.
- The extra tile scheduling and repeated operand movement cost more than the
  lower register pressure saves on A10G for this projection shape.
- Keep `TN=128` as the residual epilogue default. `TN=64` remains available as a
  negative A/B probe, but should not be promoted.

#### Promotion: Attention Out Residual Epilogue Default-On

After the positive `TN=128` result and the negative `TN=64` sweep, the
attention output residual epilogue was promoted to the default BF16 path.

Default path:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/attn_out_residual_promoted_default_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Rollback path:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_ATTENTION_OUT_RESIDUAL=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/attn_out_residual_promoted_rollback_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Runtime evidence:

```text
test_clean.wav default promoted:
  infer=945.8 ms
test_clean.wav rollback:
  infer=962.4 ms
test.wav default promoted:
  infer=5573.5 ms
```

Nsight default promoted on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
infer=960.6 ms
total CUDA kernel time: 924.558 ms
attention out static full:       50.657 ms / 96 calls
attention out tail:               8.894 ms / 96 calls
binary_inplace_same_shape add:   17.715 ms / 96 calls
```

Output difference, default promoted versus rollback on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.41628601e-08 diff_rms=9.99034578e-09 max_abs=2.38418579e-07
Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.43402743e-08 diff_rms=1.30657035e-08 max_abs=2.38418579e-07
```

Interpretation:

- The default path now includes the proven `TN=128` attention output residual
  epilogue and leaves the rollback environment variable for A/B testing.
- Nsight confirms the default path has only `96` BF16 residual add calls; before
  the epilogue promotion it had `192`.

### Negative Probe: Fuse Gate Merge Into Attention Output Projection

The attention path currently does:

```text
attention out [B,H,N,64]
  -> apply_gates_and_merge_heads -> [B,N,512]
  -> to_out projection 512 -> 256
  -> residual epilogue
```

A temporary opt-in CUDA Tile probe was tested to skip the global-memory
materialization of `[B,N,512]` and compute the projection directly:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_GATE_MERGE_OUT_PROJ_FUSED=1
```

After the negative result, the slow probe code was removed from the source tree.
This section keeps the evidence so the same approach is not repeated blindly.

For each output tile, the kernel computes:

```text
C[m, n] = sum_h gate[m, h] * dot(attn[m, h, :], W[n, h, :]) + residual[m, n]
```

The fused full kernel still uses `ct::mma`. It constructs the BF16 A tile from
`attn * gate` in registers before calling MMA, to mimic the old path where gate
merge stores BF16 before the output projection consumes it.

Resource evidence:

```text
gated_attn_out_residual_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,128,32,78048>:
  REG=234 STACK=0 SHARED=43008
gated_attn_out_residual_tail_bf16_kernel:
  REG=46 STACK=0 SHARED=0
```

Runtime evidence on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default promoted path:
  infer=946.4 ms
opt-in gate+projection fused path:
  infer=1016.4 ms
```

Nsight opt-in on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
infer=1028.1 ms
total CUDA kernel time: 992.180 ms
gated_attn_out_residual_static_full: 144.111 ms / 96 calls
gated_attn_out_residual_tail:          9.224 ms / 96 calls
apply_gates_and_merge_heads:           removed
attention out projection full/tail:    removed
binary_inplace_same_shape add:        17.713 ms / 96 calls
```

Output difference, opt-in versus default promoted on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=5.43426101e-08 diff_rms=1.00235007e-08 max_abs=1.78813934e-07
Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=5.41751583e-08 diff_rms=1.30260027e-08 max_abs=2.98023224e-07
```

Interpretation:

- The fusion correctly removes both the standalone gate merge kernel and the old
  attention output projection kernels.
- It is slower because the old projection consumed a contiguous `[M,512]` BF16 A
  matrix. The fused kernel must dynamically gather `attn[b,h,n,d]`, load and
  broadcast `gate[m,h]`, multiply, round to BF16, and then feed the computed A
  tile into MMA.
- On A10G, the saved global-memory write/read of `[M,512]` is not enough to
  compensate for worse A tile construction and less efficient operand movement:
  old `gate merge + projection full + tail` was about `83 ms`, while the fused
  full+tail path is about `153 ms`.
- The probe was removed from source after recording the result. Future attempts
  should not fuse gate merge into the output projection this way unless the A
  tile can be made contiguous or staged more efficiently before MMA.

### Promotion: Time QKV Split-Contiguous Path Default-On

The safe split-contiguous time-QKV path was promoted from opt-in to the BF16
default. The rollback is now:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_QKV_SPLIT_CONTIG_PATH=1
```

The still-negative fused-rotary producers remain explicit experiments:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_FUSED_ROTARY_PRODUCER=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_QKV_PAIR_ROTARY_PRODUCER=1
```

Current same-binary A/B on `test.wav`:

```text
default split-contiguous path: 5522.6 ms
rollback old path:            5600.8 ms
```

`test_clean.wav` remains too short/noisy for this small promotion. Two recent
same-binary short-input checks moved in opposite directions:

```text
previous check: default 934.5 ms, rollback 948.8 ms
latest check:   default 951.9 ms, rollback 947.9 ms
```

Nsight Systems on the promoted default path for `test_clean.wav`:

```text
infer=953.2 ms
total CUDA kernel time: 914.676 ms
qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel:               68.943 ms / 48 calls
rotary_time_split_contig...:                           24.069 ms / 48 calls
time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel: 163.580 ms / 48 calls
time_attention1301_split_contig_tail_kernel:            12.909 ms / 48 calls
old time split-rotary kernels:                          absent
old time attention main kernels:                        absent
```

Output difference, promoted default versus rollback:

```text
test_clean.wav
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rms_diff=9.97730204e-09 rel_rms=5.40921432e-08 snr=145.337 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=2.98023224e-07 rms_diff=1.30651180e-08 rel_rms=5.43378389e-08 snr=145.298 dB

test.wav
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rms_diff=1.02376506e-08 rel_rms=5.41763007e-08 snr=145.324 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rms_diff=1.04159843e-08 rel_rms=5.43275735e-08 snr=145.300 dB
```

Interpretation:

- The long-input timing is the more reliable end-to-end signal for this change
  and shows a `78.2 ms` win on `test.wav`.
- Nsight confirms the promoted path is not accidentally falling back to the old
  time-attention layout: the split-contiguous QKV, rotary, and attention kernels
  are the active path.
- The numerical delta is at the same BF16 write-noise scale as earlier safe
  layout promotions.

### Current CUDA Tile Path Audit: Hotspots and Effective BF16 Throughput

Current profile command:

```bash
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none \
  --force-overwrite=true -o bench/nsys_current_cutile_audit_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/nsys_current_cutile_audit_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys stats --report cuda_gpu_kern_sum --format csv --force-overwrite=true \
  bench/nsys_current_cutile_audit_clean.nsys-rep
```

Runtime:

```text
test_clean.wav under Nsight: infer=943.2 ms
```

Top CUDA Tile kernels:

```text
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false>:
  184.624 ms / 96 calls

time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel<64,32,true>:
  163.023 ms / 48 calls

qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256>:
  68.725 ms / 48 calls

linear_cutile_static_full_bkn_bf16_kernel<32,256,16,78048,1536,256,...>:
  68.289 ms / 48 calls

linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel<32,128,32,78048,256,512,...,residual>:
  50.781 ms / 96 calls

rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,false>:
  47.609 ms / 384 calls

linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel<32,64,64,1312,1301,1024,1024,...>:
  41.866 ms / 480 calls

freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel<16,false>:
  38.825 ms / 48 calls

split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel:
  36.886 ms / 48 calls

rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel:
  24.061 ms / 48 calls

apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_cutile_kernel:
  23.567 ms / 96 calls

binary_inplace_same_shape_kernel<bf16 add>:
  17.707 ms / 96 calls
```

Rough effective BF16 Tensor Core throughput for the GEMM-like parts, computed
from fixed model shapes and Nsight kernel time. This uses the existing local
`70 TFLOP/s` dense BF16 reference only as a denominator; it does not rerun a
dense roofline test.

```text
time attention main1280:
  work ~= 48 * 4 * (60*8) * 1280 * 1301 * 64
  time = 163.023 ms
  effective ~= 60.25 TFLOP/s, about 86.1% of 70 TFLOP/s

ffn12 fused main:
  work ~= 96 * 4 * 78048 * 256 * 1024
  time = 184.624 ms
  effective ~= 42.55 TFLOP/s, about 60.8% of 70 TFLOP/s

time QKV split-contiguous full:
  work ~= 48 * 2 * 78048 * 256 * 1536
  time = 68.725 ms
  effective ~= 42.87 TFLOP/s, about 61.2% of 70 TFLOP/s

large BKN linear/QKV full:
  work ~= 48 * 2 * 78048 * 256 * 1536
  time = 68.289 ms
  effective ~= 43.14 TFLOP/s, about 61.6% of 70 TFLOP/s

attention output projection with residual epilogue:
  work ~= 96 * 2 * 78048 * 512 * 256
  time = 50.781 ms
  effective ~= 38.68 TFLOP/s, about 55.3% of 70 TFLOP/s

freq attention padded:
  work ~= 48 * 4 * (1301*8) * 60 * 64 * 64
  time = 38.825 ms
  effective ~= 12.65 TFLOP/s, about 18.1% of 70 TFLOP/s

time attention tail q21:
  work ~= 48 * 4 * (60*8) * 21 * 1301 * 64
  time = 12.889 ms
  effective ~= 12.50 TFLOP/s, about 17.9% of 70 TFLOP/s
```

Interpretation:

- The time-attention main kernel is already the closest CUDA Tile path to the
  A10G dense BF16 reference. It still contains online softmax, FP32 row max/sum,
  exponentiation, BF16 probability rounding, and BF16 output normalization, so
  `~60 TFLOP/s` for the counted QK+AV math is a good current result.
- The large FFN/QKV/linear kernels sit around `39-43 TFLOP/s`. These are the
  next main Tensor Core utilization problem: the code uses BF16 inputs and
  `ct::mma`, but kernel-specific epilogues, smaller N tiles, BKN layout choices,
  register pressure, and tail handling keep them near `55-62%` of the dense
  reference.
- Freq attention and time-attention tail are not dense-GEMM-like enough to
  approach `70 TFLOP/s`: they have very small sequence lengths or row tails,
  softmax/reduce work, padding/masking, and much less reuse per CTA.
- RMSNorm, gate merge, rotary, `binary_inplace` residual adds, STFT/iSTFT, and
  chunk merge are CUDA Tile kernels but are not Tensor Core BF16 math kernels.
  They are memory/reduction/scalar-transcendental stages, so the right metric is
  kernel time and bytes moved rather than dense TFLOP/s.

Next experiment priority from this profile:

1. Revisit large linear/QKV/attention-output tile shapes and layouts first,
   because they still have large Tensor Core work and only reach about
   `39-43 TFLOP/s`.
2. Treat FFN12 as the next high-impact but high-register-pressure target. Prior
   residual fusion was negative, so new probes should focus on layout/tile shape
   and reducing scalar GELU/register pressure rather than simply doing more work
   in the same kernel.
3. Keep freq attention optimization focused on small-shape overhead, padding,
   and fusion decisions, not on expecting dense-GEMM utilization.

### Attention-Out Residual Epilogue TM/TN Sweep

The current default attention-output residual epilogue uses:

```text
M=78060, fullM=78048, N=256, K=512, TM=32, TN=128, TK=32
```

Because this exact fused epilogue is a current hotspot, a standalone
`bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm` probe was added for the full-row projection plus
BF16 residual add:

```text
out = bf16(mma(A, W)) + residual_bf16
```

The probe keeps the CUDA Tile structure used in the source path: `partition_view`
loads, `ct::mma`, FP32 accumulator, BF16 cast, BF16 residual load/add, BF16
store. It does not include the separate tail kernel.

Command:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
for v in attnres_t16x128x32 attnres_t32x64x32 attnres_t32x128x32 attnres_t32x256x32 attnres_t64x128x32; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
    --preset infer_linear --shape infer_attn_out \
    --variant "$v" --warmup 5 --iters 80
done
```

Timing:

```text
attnres_t16x128x32:
  tile=16x128x32, grid=(4878,2), fullM=78048
  best=0.741 ms, median=0.745 ms, 27.62 TF/s, roof70=39.5%

attnres_t32x64x32:
  tile=32x64x32, grid=(2439,4), fullM=78048
  best=0.680 ms, median=0.692 ms, 30.08 TF/s, roof70=43.0%

attnres_t32x128x32:
  tile=32x128x32, grid=(2439,2), fullM=78048
  best=0.522 ms, median=0.524 ms, 39.21 TF/s, roof70=56.0%

attnres_t32x256x32:
  tile=32x256x32, grid=(2439,1), fullM=78048
  best=0.664 ms, median=0.673 ms, 30.81 TF/s, roof70=44.0%

attnres_t64x128x32:
  tile=64x128x32, grid=(1219,2), fullM=78016
  best=0.825 ms, median=0.831 ms, 24.78 TF/s, roof70=35.4%
```

Resource usage:

```text
attnres_t16x128x32: REG=139 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
attnres_t32x64x32:  REG=91  STACK=0 SHARED=98304
attnres_t32x128x32: REG=243 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
attnres_t32x256x32: REG=182 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
attnres_t64x128x32: REG=184 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Selected SASS counts:

```text
attnres_t16x128x32:
  instr=1728, HMMA=128, LDG=114, LDSM=96,  STS=88,  STG=2, F2F=8

attnres_t32x64x32:
  instr=1648, HMMA=128, LDG=82,  LDSM=96,  STS=56,  STG=2, F2F=8

attnres_t32x128x32:
  instr=2080, HMMA=256, LDG=116, LDSM=128, STS=88,  STG=4, F2F=16

attnres_t32x256x32:
  instr=2976, HMMA=512, LDG=184, LDSM=192, STS=160, STG=8, F2F=32

attnres_t64x128x32:
  instr=2944, HMMA=512, LDG=136, LDSM=192, STS=112, STG=8, F2F=32
```

Interpretation:

- `TM32/TN128/TK32` remains the best attention-output residual epilogue shape
  tested for A10G. The standalone bench result (`0.522 ms`) matches the current
  source Nsight per-call range (`~0.529 ms`).
- `TN64` has much lower register pressure but doubles grid.y and loses work
  density; it matches the earlier source-level negative result.
- `TN256` and `TM64` reduce one grid dimension, but the CUDA Tile generated SASS
  doubles HMMA count to `512` and raises LDSM/STS/LDG traffic. The wider tile is
  therefore slower even though register count is below the `TN128` kernel.
- `TM16` lowers per-CTA work too far and doubles grid.x, so the lower resource
  footprint does not translate into throughput.
- The attention-output residual hotspot is not waiting for a simple wider or
  narrower tile. Further improvement needs a different staging/fusion boundary,
  or a way to reduce generated staging instructions for the existing
  `TM32/TN128/TK32` shape.

Decision:

- Keep the source default attention-output residual epilogue at `TM32/TN128/TK32`.
- Keep the new `attnres_*` variants as benchmark-only evidence.
- Do not add source selectors for `TM16`, `TN256`, or `TM64`.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 no explicit accumulator round diagnostic

Purpose:

- Test whether the explicit `bf16_round()` calls inside the current FFN12
  `pair-H32/TK64` CUDA Tile kernel are the main reason the kernel remains around
  `42-44 TF/s` on A10G.
- This is a benchmark-only diagnostic. It changes the internal numeric schedule
  and is not promoted to the source inference path.

Change:

- Added candidate variant:
  `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_noround`.
- The variant uses the same `TM32/THidden32/TK64` pair-H32 structure as the
  current best FFN12 candidate, but skips the explicit accumulator
  `bf16_round()` before hidden GELU and before final output bias.

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
```

Timing command:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_noround \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s, roof70=63.0%

fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_noround:
  best=3.591 ms, median=3.597 ms, useful=22.79 TF/s, roof70=32.6%
```

Resource usage:

```text
round:   REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
noround: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Selected SASS counts:

```text
metric   instr HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG F2F FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD
round    2760  256  208 128  124 8   96  256  192  160  352
noround  1728  128  136 64   74  8   40  128  128  80   252
```

Interpretation:

- Removing explicit accumulator round does reduce static `F2F` instructions
  (`96 -> 40`), but it also pushes register use to the architectural limit
  (`242 -> 255`) and changes code generation/unrolling enough that throughput
  collapses (`44.12 -> 22.79 TF/s`).
- Therefore the explicit `bf16_round()` conversion count is not the dominant
  FFN12 bottleneck by itself. For this A10G shape, the current round version
  gives the compiler a better schedule/register allocation than the no-round
  variant.
- Future FFN12 work should not chase "fewer F2F instructions" in isolation.
  The next useful experiments should preserve the current pair-H32/TK64
  schedule and target staging, register lifetime, or fusion boundary changes
  that keep register pressure below the `255` cliff.

Decision:

- Keep source FFN12 unchanged.
- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_noround` as a bench-only negative
  result.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 BF16 accumulator feasibility probe

Purpose:

- Test whether FFN12 can move closer to an "all BF16" Tensor Core path by using
  `__nv_bfloat16` CUDA Tile accumulator tiles in `ct::mma`, instead of the
  current `float` accumulator tiles.
- This specifically targets the current FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 shape:
  - FFN1: `BF16[32,64] x BF16[64,32] -> Acc[32,32]`
  - FFN2: `BF16[32,32] x BF16[32,128] -> Acc[32,128]`

Attempted variant:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_bf16acc
```

Build result:

```text
error: no instance of function template "cuda::tiles::__1::mma"
       matches the argument list

argument types are:
  tile<__nv_bfloat16, shape<32,64>>,
  tile<__nv_bfloat16, shape<64,32>>,
  tile<__nv_bfloat16, shape<32,32>>

note: concept "cuda::tiles::__1::mma_compatible" not satisfied
```

The same constraint failure also appears for the FFN2 accumulator shape:

```text
tile<__nv_bfloat16, shape<32,32>>,
tile<__nv_bfloat16, shape<32,128>>,
tile<__nv_bfloat16, shape<32,128>>
```

Interpretation:

- CUDA Tile 13.3 on SM86 does not expose the BF16-input Tensor Core path with a
  BF16 accumulator tile for these GEMM shapes. The legal CUDA Tile `ct::mma`
  formulation for BF16 input remains the current FP32 accumulator tile path.
- Therefore, for this project's "no raw WMMA / no inline PTX / CUDA Tile-first"
  constraint, FFN12 cannot be made truly BF16-accumulating through the public
  CUDA Tile API.
- The remaining "all BF16" experiments should focus on BF16 storage, BF16
  epilogues, fewer global-memory round trips, and fewer FP32/BF16 conversion
  boundaries around `ct::mma`, while accepting FP32 Tensor Core accumulation
  where CUDA Tile requires it.

Decision:

- Do not keep a `bf16acc` variant in the build because it is not
  `mma_compatible` and would leave the bench target broken.
- Keep FFN12 source and candidates on BF16 inputs with FP32 `ct::mma`
  accumulators.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 int32 index tile diagnostic

Purpose:

- Test whether the current FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 kernel is paying avoidable
  overhead from using `long long` index tiles for hidden-bias and output-bias
  gathers.
- The candidate keeps the same CUDA Tile `partition_view` math path, same
  pair-H32/TK64 shape, same FP32 accumulator tiles, and same output shape. Only
  the local index tile type changes from `long long` to `int`.

Change:

- Added candidates-only variant:
  `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32`.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.852 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.18 TF/s, roof70=63.1%

fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32:
  best=1.872 ms, median=1.877 ms, useful=43.72 TF/s, roof70=62.5%
```

Resource usage:

```text
i64 index:   REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
idx32 index: REG=230 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Selected SASS counts:

```text
variant instr HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG F2F FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT
i64     2760  256  208 128  124 8   96  256  192  160  352  177   175  188 115
idx32   2784  256  208 128  124 8   96  256  192  160  368  173   184  197 119
```

Interpretation:

- `idx32` does reduce register allocation (`242 -> 230`) without changing the
  Tensor Core/staging shape: HMMA, LDG, LDSM, STS, F2F, FFMA, FADD, and FMUL
  are unchanged.
- Runtime still regresses slightly (`44.18 -> 43.72 TF/s`) because the generated
  integer/address code grows: total instructions, IMAD, LOP3, SHF, and PRMT are
  all higher or effectively unchanged.
- The local index element width is therefore not the current FFN12 bottleneck.
  Lower register count by itself is not enough when the instruction stream gets
  heavier and shared-memory residency remains at 98 KiB.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32` as a candidates-only negative
  result.
- Do not promote int32 index tiles into the source FFN12 path.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 output-only no-round diagnostic

Purpose:

- The earlier `noround` probe removed explicit accumulator round before both
  hidden GELU and final output bias. It reduced F2F but changed code generation
  enough to collapse throughput.
- This narrower candidate keeps the hidden path unchanged and removes only the
  final output-accumulator `bf16_round()` before output bias. The goal is to test
  whether the final output round is an isolated bottleneck.

Change:

- Split the pair-H32/TK64 body into separate template switches for hidden
  accumulator round and output accumulator round.
- Added candidates-only variant:
  `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround`.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 20 --iters 600

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround \
  --warmup 20 --iters 600
```

Timing:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.853 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.15 TF/s, roof70=63.1%

fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround:
  best=1.858 ms, median=1.861 ms, useful=44.05 TF/s, roof70=62.9%
```

Resource usage:

```text
round:      REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
outnoround: REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Selected SASS counts:

```text
variant    instr HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG F2F FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT
round      2760  256  208 128  124 8   96  256  192  160  352  177   175  188 115
outnoround 2616  256  208 128  124 8   64  256  192  160  280  172   175  180 55
```

Interpretation:

- Output-only no-round is much less disruptive than full no-round: HMMA, LDG,
  LDSM, STS, FFMA, FADD, FMUL, registers, and shared memory all match the
  baseline.
- It does remove a meaningful amount of scalar/address/conversion code:
  instructions `2760 -> 2616`, F2F `96 -> 64`, IMAD `352 -> 280`, and PRMT
  `115 -> 55`.
- Runtime still does not improve. The stable long-iteration rerun is slightly
  slower, so the final output accumulator round is not on the FFN12 critical
  path for this A10G shape. The bottleneck remains the heavy shared-memory
  staging / Tensor Core schedule residency around the main pair-H32/TK64 body.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround` as a candidates-only
  diagnostic.
- Do not promote output-only no-round into the source FFN12 path unless a future
  larger fusion boundary makes the removed scalar work visible in end-to-end
  timing.

### 2026-06-04 - Source Opt-In: FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 output-only no-round

The bench-only `outnoround` probe reduced scalar/conversion instructions but did
not improve the isolated FFN12 benchmark. This source opt-in checks whether the
same codegen change matters in full inference.

Environment flag:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_OUT_NO_ROUND=1
```

Default behavior is unchanged. The flag only affects the source default
pair-H32/TK64 full-row FFN12 path with `FullBF16=false` and no residual fusion.

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
```

Source resource usage:

```text
round:
  ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,true>
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

outnoround:
  ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,false>
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Selected source SASS counts:

```text
variant    instr HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG F2F FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT
round      2768  256  208 128  124 8   96  256  192  160  356  172   175  189 116
outnoround 2624  256  208 128  124 8   64  256  192  160  284  171   175  182 55
```

End-to-end same-binary A/B:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  default:    938.2 ms
  outnoround: 927.4 ms

test.wav:
  default:    5511.8 ms
  outnoround: 5482.0 ms
```

Output diff, outnoround versus default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0150873065 rel_rms=0.00516692231 snr=45.735 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0146494508 rel_rms=0.00454670471 snr=46.846 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0231194794 rel_rms=0.0044874161  snr=46.960 dB
  Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0200647973 rel_rms=0.0052762705  snr=45.553 dB
```

Nsight Systems source kernel isolation on `test_clean.wav`:

```bash
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none --stats=false \
  --force-overwrite=true -o bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_default \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/nsys_ffn12_out_round_default \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_OUT_NO_ROUND=1 \
nsys profile --trace=cuda --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none --stats=false \
  --force-overwrite=true -o bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_opt \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/nsys_ffn12_out_round_opt \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Profile artifacts:

```text
bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_default.nsys-rep
bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_default.sqlite
bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_opt.nsys-rep
bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_opt.sqlite
```

Profile runtime:

```text
default:    infer=948.0 ms
outnoround: infer=939.5 ms
```

CUDA kernel total:

```text
default:    911.865 ms / 6465 kernel launches
outnoround: 908.123 ms / 6465 kernel launches
delta:       -3.742 ms
```

FFN12 source kernel group:

```text
default:
  ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,true>
  184.487 ms / 96 calls, avg=1921.743 us, REG=242, SHARED=98304

outnoround:
  ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,false>
  181.794 ms / 96 calls, avg=1893.692 us, REG=242, SHARED=98304

delta:
  -2.693 ms total, -28.051 us/call
```

Interpretation:

- The source binary reproduces the bench codegen change: same Tensor Core/staging
  shape and same resources, but fewer F2F, IMAD, SHF, and PRMT instructions.
- Full inference and Nsight source-kernel isolation both show a small positive
  timing delta. Most of the CUDA-kernel improvement comes from the FFN12 group,
  but the isolated bench did not speed up, so keep this as an opt-in experiment
  rather than a default path.
- The output drift is around `0.45%-0.53%` relative RMS, much larger than the
  prior near-bit-identical BF16 scheduling changes. This is acceptable as a
  risk experiment but too large for a default path without more quality review.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_OUT_NO_ROUND=1` as an opt-in all-BF16/conversion
  boundary experiment.
- Do not enable it by default yet.

### Negative Source Experiment: Time Rotary Row Tile

The split-contiguous time-QKV path still pays a standalone rotary pass after
the QKV producer:

```text
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel: ~24 ms / 48 calls on test_clean.wav
```

Producer-side rotary fusion and packed-QKV direct rotary attention were already
negative. A narrower standalone experiment was added behind:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ROTARY_ROW_TILE=1
```

Hypothesis:

- Keep the existing split-contiguous q/k/v layout, so the main attention kernel
  still sees the efficient `[D,KTile]` K view.
- Map one CUDA Tile block to one `[heads=8, pair_dim=32]` token row.
- Remove the per-element global index decomposition from the default 1D pair
  kernel and broadcast the `[1,32]` cos/sin tile over heads.

Same-binary `test_clean.wav` timing with `CUDASEP_PROFILE_TIME_QKV_PRODUCER=1`:

```text
default:
  infer=932.9 ms
  split_producer calls=48 total_ms=71.0647 avg_ms=1.48051
  split_rotary   calls=48 total_ms=24.2919 avg_ms=0.506081

CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ROTARY_ROW_TILE=1:
  infer=936.6 ms
  split_producer calls=48 total_ms=71.3244 avg_ms=1.48592
  split_rotary   calls=48 total_ms=24.6017 avg_ms=0.512535
```

Output diff, row-tile opt-in vs default:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=1.78813934e-07 rel_rms=5.41418792e-08
Instrumental: max_abs=2.98023224e-07 rel_rms=5.42877249e-08
```

Resource usage:

```text
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel:
  REG=29 STACK=0 SHARED=0

rotary_time_split_contig_row_tile_inplace_kernel:
  REG=32 STACK=0 SHARED=0
```

SASS rough static instruction count:

```text
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel:          instr=205, REG=29
rotary_time_split_contig_row_tile_inplace_kernel: instr=156, REG=32
```

Interpretation:

- The row-tile mapping is numerically valid, but it is slower on A10G.
- The CUDA Tile broadcast/2D tile form reduces the static instruction count,
  but increases register pressure and does not reduce the memory-bound rotary
  pass enough to pay for the generated load/store shape.
- The standalone rotary pass is already a streaming global-memory kernel close
  to the practical bandwidth boundary for this shape. Removing it likely needs
  attention-load fusion that preserves the split-contiguous K tile view, not a
  different standalone rotary tile shape.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ROTARY_ROW_TILE=1` as an opt-in negative experiment.
- Do not promote it to the default path.

### Negative Source Experiment: Time Q-Rotary-In-Attention

After the standalone row-tile rotary experiment was negative, the next
hypothesis was to remove only the Q half of the standalone rotary pass:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION=1
```

Implementation boundary:

- The QKV producer still writes split-contiguous unrotated `q/k/v`.
- A new K-only standalone rotary pass rotates only `k`.
- The split-contiguous time attention kernel rotates the loaded Q tile once,
  casts it back to BF16, then feeds the existing QK Tensor Core path.
- K is not rotated inside attention, so it still uses the efficient
  split-contiguous `[D,KTile]` Tensor Core view and avoids per-Q-block K
  recomputation.

Same-binary `test_clean.wav` timing with `CUDASEP_PROFILE_TIME_QKV_PRODUCER=1`:

```text
default:
  infer=942.1 ms
  split_producer calls=48 total_ms=71.0512 avg_ms=1.48023
  split_rotary   calls=48 total_ms=24.2402 avg_ms=0.505003

CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION=1:
  infer=978.5 ms
  split_producer calls=48 total_ms=70.8460 avg_ms=1.47596
  split_rotary   calls=48 total_ms=12.1463 avg_ms=0.253047
```

Output diff, q-rotary-in-attention opt-in vs default:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=2.38418579e-07 rel_rms=5.38483479e-08
Instrumental: max_abs=2.98023224e-07 rel_rms=5.45790137e-08
```

Nsight Systems source-kernel isolation on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
default:
  infer=944.6 ms
  CUDA kernel total=912.365 ms / 6465 launches
  time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel<64,32,true>:
    163.074 ms / 48 calls, avg=3397.373 us, REG=237, SMEM=40960
  rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel:
     24.073 ms / 48 calls, avg=501.517 us, REG=29, SMEM=0
  time_attention1301_split_contig_tail_kernel<32,32,40,true>:
     12.898 ms / 48 calls, avg=268.709 us, REG=194, SMEM=26752

CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION=1:
  infer=992.0 ms
  CUDA kernel total=956.490 ms / 6465 launches
  time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_qrot_input_kernel<64,32,true>:
    224.578 ms / 48 calls, avg=4678.702 us, REG=225, SMEM=65536
  rotary_time_split_contig_k_only_inplace_kernel:
     11.949 ms / 48 calls, avg=248.940 us, REG=22, SMEM=0
  time_attention1301_split_contig_qrot_tail_kernel<32,32,40,true>:
     13.018 ms / 48 calls, avg=271.210 us, REG=194, SMEM=36992
```

Interpretation:

- The K-only pass behaves as expected and roughly halves standalone rotary cost
  (`~24.1 ms -> ~11.9-12.1 ms`).
- Moving Q rotary into the attention load is numerically valid, but it makes the
  main attention kernel much heavier (`~163.1 ms -> ~224.6 ms`).
- The main qrot attention kernel lowers registers (`237 -> 225`) but increases
  static shared memory (`40960 -> 65536`). The extra tile reshaping, cos/sin
  loads, BF16 recast, and larger staging footprint destroy more A10G occupancy
  and scheduling efficiency than the standalone Q rotary pass costs.
- This rules out Q-load rotary in the current monolithic attention kernel. A
  useful future variant would need a different staging design that keeps the
  40KB-class attention footprint, not just inserting rotary before QK MMA.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION=1` as an opt-in negative
  experiment.
- Do not promote it to the default path.

Follow-up QRows32 staging probe:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION_Q32=1
```

Resource usage:

```text
default time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel<64,32,true>:
  REG=237 SMEM=40960

qrot64 time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_qrot_input_kernel<64,32,true>:
  REG=225 SMEM=65536

qrot32 time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_qrot_input_kernel<32,32,true>:
  REG=170 SMEM=52352
```

Same-binary `test_clean.wav` timing with `CUDASEP_PROFILE_TIME_QKV_PRODUCER=1`:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION=1
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION_Q32=1:
  infer=1284.4 ms
  split_producer calls=48 total_ms=70.4443 avg_ms=1.46759
  split_rotary   calls=48 total_ms=12.6567 avg_ms=0.263681
```

Output diff, qrot32 opt-in vs default:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=0.00631919503 rel_rms=0.00334392658
Instrumental: max_abs=0.00636743009 rel_rms=0.00336122342
```

Interpretation:

- QRows32 reduces qrot main shared memory versus qrot64 (`65536 -> 52352`), but
  still misses the default 40KB-class footprint.
- It doubles the number of main Q blocks (`20 -> 40` per batch/head) and falls
  back into the historically negative QRows32 attention regime on A10G: too
  little Tensor Core work per CTA, worse scheduling, and visible output drift.
- This confirms that simply shrinking QRows is not the fix for qrot attention.
  A viable Q-load rotary design would need a different staging layout that
  keeps the default Q64/K32 attention footprint while adding rotary with little
  or no extra shared-memory allocation.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_Q_ROTARY_IN_ATTENTION_Q32=1` as a nested opt-in
  negative experiment.
- Do not use QRows32 for the qrot attention path.

### 2026-06-04 - Freq QKV+rotary+pad64 BKN fused producer probe

Goal:

- Revisit the previous negative freq QKV+rotary fused producer with the missing
  BKN ingredient. The older opt-in fused producer used ordinary
  `[out_features,in_features]` weights and lost the fast default BKN long-linear
  layout. This experiment keeps `weight_bkn [256,1536]`, emits padded
  `[B,8,64,64]` q/k/v directly, and rotates q/k inside the CUDA Tile GEMM
  epilogue.

Source switch:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_QKV_FREQ_ROTARY_BKN_CUTILE_FUSED=1
CUDASEP_QKV_FREQ_ROTARY_BKN_CUTILE_TILE_N=64|128|256
```

Default remains unchanged.

Implemented source path:

```text
qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_bkn_cutile_static_full_kernel<TM=32,TN,TK=16>
qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_bkn_cutile_tail_kernel<256,1536>
try_linear_qkv_freq_rotary_bkn_cutile_fused(...)
```

The kernel still uses CUDA Tile `ct::mma`; no cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm
path was introduced.

Build:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
TN=256: REG=240 SMEM=98320
TN=128: REG=212 SMEM=99344
TN=64:  REG=118 SMEM=65552
tail:   REG=40  SMEM=0
```

Same-binary `test_clean.wav` timings:

```text
default: 941.6 ms
TN=256:  1147.3 ms
TN=128:  1222.1 ms
TN=64:   1103.6 ms
```

Nsight Systems, `TN=256`:

```text
qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_bkn_cutile_static_full_kernel<32,256,16>:
  321.917 ms / 48 calls, avg=6706.611 us, REG=240, SMEM=98320
qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_bkn_cutile_tail_kernel:
    4.562 ms / 48 calls, avg=95.043 us, REG=40, SMEM=0
qkv_freq60_pad_rows_zero_kernel:
    1.193 ms / 48 calls, avg=24.846 us, REG=17, SMEM=0
```

Nsight Systems, `TN=64`:

```text
qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_bkn_cutile_static_full_kernel<32,64,16>:
  278.008 ms / 48 calls, avg=5791.826 us, REG=118, SMEM=65552
qkv_freq60_rotary_pad64_bkn_cutile_tail_kernel:
    4.593 ms / 48 calls, avg=95.685 us, REG=40, SMEM=0
qkv_freq60_pad_rows_zero_kernel:
    1.103 ms / 48 calls, avg=22.976 us, REG=17, SMEM=0
```

Output diff versus default:

```text
TN=64:
  Vocals:       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.461380665e-08
  Instrumental: max_abs=2.980232239e-07 rel_rms=5.472849875e-08

TN=128:
  Vocals:       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.463189952e-08
  Instrumental: max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.480246115e-08

TN=256:
  Vocals:       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.470878172e-08
  Instrumental: max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.475386099e-08
```

Default validation after the source edit:

```text
test_clean.wav default: 941.6 ms
test.wav       default: 5512.6 ms
```

Interpretation:

- The fused BKN producer is numerically correct at BF16 output precision, but
  it is a clear A10G performance negative.
- Preserving BKN fixed the old experiment's weight-layout weakness, but adding
  rotary/pad stores into the GEMM epilogue explodes the full-tile resource
  shape. `TN=256` reaches `REG=240` and a 98KB shared-memory footprint, similar
  to the worst FFN12 resource regime.
- `TN=64` proves that reducing per-CTA output width helps resources
  (`REG=118`, `SMEM=65552`) and improves the fused producer
  (`321.9 ms -> 278.0 ms`), but the extra CTA count and strided q/k/v stores
  still leave it far slower than the default separate freq BKN linear plus
  split/rotary/pad path.
- The bottom cause is not the separate pad-zero launch or correctness guards.
  It is the producer epilogue/staging shape: converting a dense BKN GEMM store
  into per-head padded q/k/v stores plus rotary reshapes prevents the CUDA Tile
  compiler from keeping the efficient long-linear schedule.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_QKV_FREQ_ROTARY_BKN_CUTILE_FUSED=1` as an opt-in
  negative experiment.
- Do not promote producer-side freq QKV+rotary+pad64 fusion in this form.
- The next viable freq direction should not fuse rotary into the GEMM epilogue
  this way. Better candidates are consumer-side attention variants that read a
  denser QKV layout, or a two-stage producer epilogue that preserves the fast
  dense BKN GEMM store shape while eliminating only the cheapest global-memory
  pass.

### 2026-06-04 - D256 RMSNorm rows-per-CTA probe

Goal:

- Test whether the fixed D256 RMSNorm kernel is limited by launching one CTA per
  row. The default D256 kernel uses a single `ct::tile<float, shape<256>>` per
  row. This probe adds a 2D tile variant that processes 2 or 4 rows per CTA and
  reduces over the D dimension with CUDA Tile reduction.

Source switch:

```text
CUDASEP_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=2
CUDASEP_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=4
```

Default remains `ROWS_PER_CTA=1`.

Implemented source path:

```text
rms_norm_d256_rows_to_bf16_kernel<InT, FullBF16, RowsPerCta>
```

Build:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
default D256 bf16,false rows=1: REG=16 SMEM=16
default D256 bf16,true  rows=1: REG=17 SMEM=16

rows=2 bf16,false: REG=32 SMEM=32
rows=2 bf16,true:  REG=29 SMEM=32
rows=4 bf16,false: REG=44 SMEM=64
rows=4 bf16,true:  REG=48 SMEM=64
```

Plain `test_clean.wav` timings:

```text
default rows=1 previous same build family: 941.6 ms
rows=2:                              931.2 ms
rows=4:                              935.4 ms
```

Nsight Systems, `rows=2`:

```text
infer=944.8 ms
rms_norm_d256_rows_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,false,2>:
  48.327 ms / 384 calls, avg=125.850 us, REG=32, SMEM=32
remaining generic rms_norm_to_bf16<bf16>:
   1.160 ms / 240 calls, avg=4.834 us, REG=33, SMEM=16
```

Previous default rows=1 Nsight baseline from the current profile:

```text
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,false>:
  47.609 ms / 384 calls, avg=123.983 us, REG=16, SMEM=16
```

Output diff versus default:

```text
rows=2:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.008009106 rel_rms=0.003994876
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.008673847 rel_rms=0.004137013

rows=4:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.007896155 rel_rms=0.004156797
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.010122657 rel_rms=0.003891101
```

Interpretation:

- Rows-per-CTA reduces CTA count, but it increases the tile resource footprint.
  For the common BF16 non-full path, rows=2 doubles registers (`16 -> 32`) and
  shared memory (`16 -> 32`); rows=4 is much heavier.
- Kernel-level timing is negative for rows=2 (`47.609 ms -> 48.327 ms`), so the
  apparent short-input end-to-end win is noise.
- The output drift is not bit noise. Like the earlier generic-1024-to-D256
  promotion, changing the CUDA Tile reduction shape changes the reduction tree.
- The bottom cause is that the existing rows=1 D256 kernel is already cheap
  enough per CTA that packing more rows into one tile loses more in register
  pressure and reduction shape than it gains in CTA scheduling.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=2|4` as opt-in negative experiments.
- Do not promote rows-per-CTA RMSNorm. Further RMSNorm work should target
  cross-op fusion, not wider per-CTA row packing.

### 2026-06-04 - RMSNorm gamma BF16 weight probe

Goal:

- Move RMSNorm gamma weights from FP32 storage to BF16 storage under an explicit
  experiment flag. This reduces one remaining FP32/BF16 boundary in the MBR
  inference path and tests whether gamma precision matters for output quality or
  A10G kernel time.

Source switch:

```text
CUDASEP_NORM_GAMMA_BF16=1
```

The switch is intentionally strict opt-in. It requires global BF16 inference and
does not participate in the broader default BF16 experiment bundle.

Implemented source path:

```text
maybe_bf16_norm_gamma(...)
rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel<InT, GammaT>
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<InT, GammaT, FullBF16>
rms_norm_d256_rows_to_bf16_kernel<InT, GammaT, FullBF16, RowsPerCta>
```

Build:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
```

Strict same-binary `test_clean.wav` timings:

```text
default FP32 gamma:      932.9 ms
BF16 gamma opt-in:      939.4 ms
default Nsight run:     944.8 ms
BF16 gamma Nsight run:  948.3 ms
```

Nsight Systems RMSNorm kernel comparison:

```text
default:
  rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,float,false>:
    47.611 ms / 384 calls, avg=123.987 us, REG=16, SMEM=16
  rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,float>:
     1.160 ms / 240 calls, avg=4.832 us, REG=33, SMEM=16

BF16 gamma:
  rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,bf16,false>:
    47.348 ms / 384 calls, avg=123.301 us, REG=16, SMEM=16
  rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,bf16>:
     1.184 ms / 240 calls, avg=4.933 us, REG=33, SMEM=16
```

Strict output diff versus default:

```text
Vocals:       max_abs=0.028814238 rel_rms=0.012021928
Instrumental: max_abs=0.035470217 rel_rms=0.012277572
```

Interpretation:

- This is a real BF16 purity improvement, but not a useful A10G speed win.
  The dominant D256 RMSNorm kernel is slightly faster, while the small generic
  RMSNorm kernel is slightly slower; the end-to-end result is noise-to-negative.
- The strict output diff is about `1.2% rel_rms`, so previous bit-level
  comparisons were not valid evidence for this experiment. They came from an
  earlier gating mistake that allowed the default and opt-in paths to collapse
  to the same gamma dtype.
- Gamma is read once per normalized element and immediately converted to FP32
  inside the RMSNorm math. Changing storage to BF16 saves bandwidth for the
  gamma vector, but the kernel remains dominated by reading activations,
  reduction, reciprocal square root, and writing BF16 output.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_NORM_GAMMA_BF16=1` as an opt-in BF16-purity experiment.
- Do not promote it to default because it does not improve end-to-end time and
  causes measurable output drift.
- Further RMSNorm work should focus on eliminating the standalone RMSNorm global
  memory boundary via residual/RMSNorm fusion, not on gamma storage alone.

Default validation after the source edit:

```text
test_clean.wav default: 937.3 ms
test.wav       default: 5502.0 ms
git diff --check: pass
forbidden library/raw WMMA/asm audit: no matches
src LOC: 14862 total
```

### 2026-06-04 - Promotion: FFN residual add + following D256 RMSNorm fusion

Goal:

- Remove the remaining standalone FFN residual add without adding work to the
  already register-limited FFN12 Tensor Core kernel. Instead of fusing residual
  into the FFN producer, this experiment fuses the consumer side:

```text
old:
  out.add_(ff_out)
  normed = rms_norm(out, next_or_final_gamma)

new:
  residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16(out, ff_out, gamma)
    -> residual_out
    -> normed
```

The fused CUDA Tile kernel stores the updated BF16 residual state and also emits
the next normalized BF16 tensor. This keeps the residual state available for the
next attention residual while eliminating one global read/write pass and one
kernel launch per FFN residual site.

Default path:

```text
enabled by default under --bf16
```

Rollback:

```text
CUDASEP_DISABLE_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_FUSED=1
```

Implemented source path:

```text
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<GammaT, FullBF16>
try_residual_add_rms_norm(...)
apply_attention_normed(...)
```

The implementation uses CUDA Tile `__tile_global__`, `ct::iota`, masked-free
fixed D256 tile loads/stores, and `ct::sum` for the RMSNorm reduction. No
cuBLAS/cuFFT/cuDNN/raw-WMMA/asm path was introduced.

Build:

```text
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8: pass
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<float,false>: REG=18 SMEM=16
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<float,true>:  REG=20 SMEM=16
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,false>:  REG=19 SMEM=16
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,true>:   REG=19 SMEM=16
```

Plain `test_clean.wav` timings before promotion:

```text
default-off: 936.6 ms
opt-in:      927.1 ms
```

Nsight Systems on `test_clean.wav` before promotion:

```text
default-off infer: 945.0 ms
default-off total CUDA kernel time: 911.994 ms
  rms_norm_d256_to_bf16<bf16,float,false>:
    47.610 ms / 384 calls, avg=123.984 us, REG=16, SMEM=16
  binary_inplace_same_shape add<bf16>:
    17.705 ms / 96 calls, avg=184.426 us, REG=16, SMEM=0

opt-in infer: 940.2 ms
opt-in total CUDA kernel time: 906.898 ms
  rms_norm_d256_to_bf16<bf16,float,false>:
    35.665 ms / 288 calls, avg=123.836 us, REG=16, SMEM=16
  residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16<float,false>:
    23.820 ms / 96 calls, avg=248.123 us, REG=18, SMEM=16
```

Target-segment kernel time:

```text
old add + RMSNorm segment: 47.610 + 17.705 = 65.315 ms
new fused segment:         35.665 + 23.820 = 59.485 ms
net kernel saving:         5.830 ms
```

Long-input timing before promotion:

```text
test.wav default-off: 5504.0 ms
test.wav opt-in:      5467.2 ms
```

Output diff versus default-off:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=1.788139343e-07 rel_rms=5.479820460e-08
  Instrumental: max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.484021198e-08

test.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.474416192e-08
  Instrumental: max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.477581911e-08
```

Promotion validation:

```text
test_clean.wav promoted default:  926.1 ms
test_clean.wav rollback:          936.3 ms
test.wav promoted default:       5486.3 ms

promoted default vs rollback on test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=2.384185791e-07 rel_rms=5.481902588e-08
  Instrumental: max_abs=2.980232239e-07 rel_rms=5.462117141e-08
```

Post-promotion audit:

```text
git diff --check: pass
forbidden library/raw WMMA/asm audit: no matches
src LOC: 15014 total
```

Interpretation:

- This is the consumer-side fusion that the earlier FFN12 producer residual
  negative result pointed toward. It removes the standalone FFN residual add
  without perturbing the high-pressure FFN12 HMMA schedule.
- The fused kernel costs more than one standalone D256 RMSNorm call
  (`248 us` vs `124 us`) because it also reads the residual/update vectors and
  writes both residual and normed outputs. It still wins because it replaces
  `add + RMSNorm` (`~308 us`) with one low-pressure kernel (`~248 us`).
- The resource footprint stays small (`REG=18`, `SMEM=16`) instead of touching
  FFN12's `REG=242` Tensor Core kernel, so it avoids the A10G register cliff.

Decision:

- Promote residual add + following D256 RMSNorm fusion to the default BF16 path.
- Keep `CUDASEP_DISABLE_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_FUSED=1` as a rollback and A/B switch.
- This result strengthens the current A10G fusion rule: fuse memory-bound
  consumer reductions when they can replace global-memory boundaries; avoid
  adding epilogue work to already register-limited Tensor Core producers.

### 2026-06-04 - Promotion: FFN12 output-only no-round default

Goal:

- Re-evaluate the existing source opt-in `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_OUT_NO_ROUND=1`
  after the residual+RMSNorm consumer fusion became default. This flag removes
  the final FFN12 output-accumulator BF16 round before output bias in the
  current `pair-H32/TK64/poly9` source kernel. It keeps the same Tensor Core
  tile schedule and resource shape, but removes epilogue F2F/integer work.

Default path:

```text
enabled by default under --bf16
```

Rollback:

```text
CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_OUT_NO_ROUND=1
```

Source kernel shape:

```text
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,false>
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer`:

```text
round:      ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,true>
            REG=242 SMEM=98304
outnoround: ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,false>
            REG=242 SMEM=98304
```

Latest same-binary A/B before promotion, after residual+RMSNorm fusion:

```text
test_clean.wav current default: 926.1 ms
test_clean.wav outnoround:     923.6 ms

test.wav current default:     5486.3 ms
test.wav outnoround:          5464.4 ms
```

Output diff, outnoround versus current default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.015087306 rel_rms=0.005166922 snr=45.735 dB
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.014649391 rel_rms=0.004546705 snr=46.846 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.023119479 rel_rms=0.004487416 snr=46.960 dB
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.020064794 rel_rms=0.005276271 snr=45.553 dB
```

Promotion validation:

```text
test_clean.wav promoted default: 924.6 ms
test_clean.wav rollback:         927.2 ms
test.wav promoted default:      5455.1 ms

promoted default vs rollback on test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.015087306 rel_rms=0.005166922 snr=45.735 dB
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.014649391 rel_rms=0.004546705 snr=46.846 dB
```

Post-promotion audit:

```text
git diff --check: pass
forbidden library/raw WMMA/asm audit: no matches
src LOC: 15014 total
```

Nsight Systems on promoted default:

```text
bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_promoted_default_clean.nsys-rep
bench/nsys_ffn12_outnoround_promoted_default_clean.sqlite

promoted default infer: 939.7 ms
promoted default total CUDA kernel time: 906.705 ms

ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,false>:
  183.038 ms / 96 calls, avg=1906.651 us, REG=242, SMEM=98304

residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<float,false>:
   23.816 ms / 96 calls, avg=248.079 us, REG=18, SMEM=16

rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,float,false>:
   35.797 ms / 288 calls, avg=124.295 us, REG=16, SMEM=16
```

Rollback reference from the residual+RMSNorm promotion profile:

```text
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,true>:
  184.797 ms / 96 calls, avg=1924.969 us, REG=242, SMEM=98304
```

Interpretation:

- This is a small but repeatable FFN12 source-path improvement. The kernel group
  improves by about `1.76 ms` in the promoted-default Nsight comparison, and the
  plain long-input run improves by about `22 ms` versus the prior current
  default run.
- The resource footprint does not change. The improvement comes from the
  epilogue codegen change already measured earlier: fewer F2F, IMAD, SHF, and
  PRMT instructions while preserving HMMA/staging shape.
- The output drift is real (`~0.45%-0.53% rel_rms`) and much larger than
  bit-level schedule changes. It is promoted because the project goal is an
  aggressive CUDA Tile/A10G experimental path, not a conservative production
  quality baseline. The rollback switch remains available for quality A/B.

Decision:

- Promote FFN12 output-only no-round to the default BF16 inference path.
- Keep `CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_OUT_NO_ROUND=1` as a rollback and quality A/B
  switch.
- Future FFN12 work should still target the main `REG=242/SMEM=98304` residency
  and staging problem; this promotion only trims epilogue overhead.

### 2026-06-04 - Linear padded-M bias no-round probe

Goal:

- Test whether the FFN12 epilogue no-round idea also helps ordinary static
  padded-M CUDA Tile linears with bias and no GELU. These kernels are visible
  but smaller hotspots after the FFN12 promotion:
  `linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel<32,64,64,1312,1301,1024,1024,true,false,...>`
  and the matching `K=256,N=1024` variant.

Opt-in:

```text
CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_PADDED_BIAS_NO_ROUND=1
```

Source behavior:

```text
default: RoundBeforeBias=true
opt-in:  RoundBeforeBias=false, only for AddBias && !ApplyGelu && !FullBF16
```

Plain same-binary A/B:

```text
test_clean.wav default: 925.5 ms
test_clean.wav opt-in:  923.9 ms

test.wav opt-in:       5467.8 ms
test.wav default rerun:5469.3 ms
```

Output diff, opt-in versus default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.008527681 rel_rms=0.003288939 snr=49.659 dB
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.008390032 rel_rms=0.003244758 snr=49.776 dB

test.wav:
  Vocals:       max_abs=0.010027498 rel_rms=0.003160901 snr=50.004 dB
  Instrumental: max_abs=0.012091577 rel_rms=0.003953519 snr=48.060 dB
```

Nsight Systems A/B:

```text
bench/nsys_linear_padded_bias_noround_default_clean.nsys-rep
bench/nsys_linear_padded_bias_noround_default_clean.sqlite
bench/nsys_linear_padded_bias_noround_opt_clean.nsys-rep
bench/nsys_linear_padded_bias_noround_opt_clean.sqlite

default infer: 940.1 ms
opt-in infer:  935.6 ms

default total CUDA kernel time: 905.438 ms
opt-in total CUDA kernel time:  904.135 ms

K=1024,N=1024 padded group:
  default: 41.969 ms / 480 calls, avg=87.436 us, REG=116, SMEM=32768
  opt-in:  41.880 ms / 480 calls, avg=87.249 us, REG=116, SMEM=32768

K=256,N=1024 padded group:
  default: 13.761 ms / 480 calls, avg=28.669 us, REG=116, SMEM=32768
  opt-in:  13.690 ms / 480 calls, avg=28.521 us, REG=116, SMEM=32768
```

Interpretation:

- The targeted groups improve by only about `0.16 ms` combined in Nsight
  (`0.089 ms + 0.071 ms`). The end-to-end delta is within normal run noise.
- Resource usage is unchanged. Unlike FFN12, these ordinary padded-M linears are
  not dominated by the same epilogue codegen cliff, so removing the pre-bias
  round does not materially change the A10G schedule.
- Output drift is measurable (`~0.32%-0.40% rel_rms`) while the speed evidence
  is weak.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_PADDED_BIAS_NO_ROUND=1` as an opt-in experiment.
- Do not promote it to the default BF16 path.
- Revisit only if a later fused padded-M producer/consumer path changes this
  kernel's epilogue pressure or makes this no-round variant part of a larger
  successful fusion.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 staged hidden epilogue probe

Goal:

- Test whether the current FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 kernel loses performance because
  the two hidden epilogue tiles overlap too long in the same loop body.
- The candidate keeps the same CUDA Tile `ct::mma` macro shape:
  `TM=32`, pair-H32, `TK=64`, split2 output, poly9 GELU, BF16 inputs, FP32 MMA
  accumulators.
- It changes only the hidden epilogue order: process hidden0
  bias/GELU/BF16-cast plus W2 MMA before processing hidden1, instead of
  materializing both hidden values before the four W2 MMAs.

Candidate:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_stagedhidden
```

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
```

Timing commands:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_stagedhidden \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.853 ms, median=1.857 ms, useful=44.17 TF/s, roof70=63.1%

fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_stagedhidden:
  best=3.292 ms, median=3.298 ms, useful=24.86 TF/s, roof70=35.5%
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
baseline:
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

stagedhidden:
  REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=96256
```

Interpretation:

- The hypothesis partially works on shared memory only: staged hidden epilogue
  reduces static shared memory by `2048 B`.
- It fails on the actual A10G limiter: register allocation jumps from `242` to
  the `255` cliff, and throughput collapses from `44.17` to `24.86 TF/s`.
- The likely bottom cause is that the baseline source order lets CUDA Tile's
  compiler schedule the two hidden epilogues and four W2 MMAs as one coherent
  high-pressure block. Manually serializing hidden0/hidden1 narrows one apparent
  lifetime but produces a worse global register allocation.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_stagedhidden` as a candidates-only
  negative result.
- Do not move staged hidden epilogue into source.
- Future FFN12 experiments should not assume shorter source-level tile lifetime
  reduces actual registers. Verify `REG/SMEM` first, then timing.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H64/TK64 int32 index probe

Goal:

- Revisit the H64 direction because the earlier pair-H64/TK64 candidate kept
  the desirable `HMMA=256` work shape and reduced static shared/load pressure,
  but lost to the current H32/TK64 baseline by hitting `REG=255`.
- The H32/TK64 `idx32` probe previously reduced registers
  (`242 -> 230`) without changing the Tensor Core shape, even though it did not
  improve runtime. This experiment tests whether the same index-width change can
  pull H64 below the register cliff.

Candidates:

```text
fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64
fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64_idx32
```

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
```

Timing commands:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 200

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64 \
  --warmup 20 --iters 600

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64_idx32 \
  --warmup 20 --iters 600
```

Timing:

```text
H32/TK64 baseline:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s, roof70=63.0%

H64/TK64:
  best=2.528 ms, median=2.533 ms, useful=32.38 TF/s, roof70=46.3%

H64/TK64 idx32:
  best=2.459 ms, median=2.466 ms, useful=33.27 TF/s, roof70=47.5%
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
H32/TK64 baseline:
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

H64/TK64:
  REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=94208

H64/TK64 idx32:
  REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=94208
```

Interpretation:

- `idx32` gives H64 a small, repeatable runtime improvement
  (`2.528 -> 2.459 ms`, about `2.7%`) but does not change the resource shape.
- The original H64 failure mode remains: it is still at `REG=255`. Lower shared
  memory (`94208 B` vs H32's `98304 B`) is not enough when register allocation
  stays at the architectural cliff.
- The H32 `idx32` trick was register-positive because the `32x32` hidden-index
  tile had enough integer/address pressure to remove. For H64, the dominant
  pressure is the larger hidden accumulator/epilogue tile and W2 staging, not
  the index element width.

Decision:

- Keep H64/TK64 and H64/TK64 idx32 as candidates-only negative results.
- Do not promote H64 into source.
- Future H64 work needs a different mechanism that actually lowers `REG=255`;
  index-width changes alone are insufficient.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H64/TK64 output no-round probe

Goal:

- Test whether H64's register-cliff slowdown is sensitive to the final output
  accumulator round, after the H32 output-only no-round source path showed a
  small end-to-end win.
- The candidates keep the H64/TK64 pair schedule, hidden bias, output bias, BF16
  inputs, and FP32 MMA accumulators. They change only `RoundOutAcc=false`, with
  and without `idx32`.

Candidates:

```text
fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64_outnoround
fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64_idx32_outnoround
```

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
```

Timing commands:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64_outnoround \
  --warmup 20 --iters 600

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64_idx32_outnoround \
  --warmup 20 --iters 600
```

Timing, compared with the previous H64 runs from the same candidates target:

```text
H64/TK64:
  best=2.528 ms, median=2.533 ms, useful=32.38 TF/s, roof70=46.3%

H64/TK64 idx32:
  best=2.459 ms, median=2.466 ms, useful=33.27 TF/s, roof70=47.5%

H64/TK64 outnoround:
  best=2.578 ms, median=2.587 ms, useful=31.74 TF/s, roof70=45.3%

H64/TK64 idx32 outnoround:
  best=2.521 ms, median=2.525 ms, useful=32.46 TF/s, roof70=46.4%
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
all four H64 variants:
  REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=94208
```

Interpretation:

- Output no-round does not reduce H64's resource shape. All H64 variants remain
  pinned at `REG=255`.
- Runtime gets worse, both without idx32 (`32.38 -> 31.74 TF/s`) and with idx32
  (`33.27 -> 32.46 TF/s`).
- Therefore H64 is not blocked by the final output accumulator round. The
  current limiter is earlier in the larger hidden accumulator/epilogue and W2
  staging schedule.

Decision:

- Keep both H64 output no-round variants as candidates-only negative results.
- Do not use output no-round as a path to rescue H64.
- Future H64 work should target the hidden/W2 body directly or stop spending
  time on H64 unless a new CUDA Tile scheduling mechanism becomes available.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention split-contiguous no key-tail lower bound

Goal:

- Quantify the cost of the final masked key-tail tile in the fixed-shape time
  attention main path.
- The baseline processes `N=1301` keys for `Q=0..1279` by running forty full
  `KTile=32` blocks plus one masked tail block for keys `1280..1300`.
- The candidate skips that last 21-key tail block. This is deliberately
  approximate and not correctness-preserving; it is a lower-bound experiment
  for resource pressure and launch runtime.

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
```

Timing commands:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input_no_keytail \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Timing:

```text
main1280_split_contig_input_exp2:
  best=3.395 ms, median=3.423 ms, real_math=60.27 TF/s,
  logical_tile_math=62.27 TF/s, roof70=86.1%

main1280_split_contig_input_exp2_no_keytail:
  best=3.293 ms, median=3.327 ms, real_math=61.15 TF/s,
  logical_tile_math=61.15 TF/s, roof70=87.4%
```

Output comparison against the baseline main1280 Q64/K32 exp2 kernel:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input_no_keytail \
  --warmup 5 --iters 30 --compare-baseline
```

```text
compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2 elems=39966720 max_abs=0.001953125
rms=0.000505273408 ref_rms=0.2478777 rel_rms=0.002038398
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
split-contiguous include key-tail:
  REG=233 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

split-contiguous no key-tail:
  REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
```

Interpretation:

- The final masked 21-key tile is not free. Removing it improves the synthetic
  main attention launch from `3.395 -> 3.293 ms`, about `3.0%`.
- The bigger signal is resource shape: `REG 233 -> 217` and
  `SMEM 40960 -> 32768`. The tail path increases compiler-visible pressure even
  though the useful math is small.
- The candidate reaches `61.15 TF/s`, about `87.4%` of the local `70 TF/s`
  dense BF16 reference, using the actual reduced math count rather than padded
  logical work.
- The output drift is measurable but small in the synthetic attention output
  (`rel_rms=0.2038%`). Full source-level audio drift still needs separate
  opt-in validation.

Decision:

- Keep this as a lower-bound CUDA Tile/A10G shape experiment.
- Do not promote it by default.
- A reasonable next experiment is a source opt-in path such as
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1`, followed by end-to-end timing
  and output-diff checks on `test_clean.wav` and `test.wav`.

### 2026-06-04 - Source opt-in time attention skip key-tail

Goal:

- Add the bench no-keytail candidate to the source path as an explicit
  aggressive experiment.
- Keep default inference unchanged.
- Use a compile-time `IncludeKeyTail` template parameter so the kernel hot path
  has no runtime branch when the experiment is enabled.

Environment:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1
```

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage` on `cudasep_infer`:

```text
split-contiguous main include key-tail:
  REG=237 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

split-contiguous main no key-tail:
  REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=32768

split-contiguous q-rot main include key-tail:
  REG=225 STACK=0 SHARED=65536

split-contiguous q-rot main no key-tail:
  REG=223 STACK=0 SHARED=57344
```

End-to-end commands:

```bash
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/time_skip_default_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/time_skip_optin_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav \
  --output outputs/time_skip_default_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav \
  --output outputs/time_skip_optin_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Timing:

```text
test_clean.wav default:
  922.5 ms, 925.0 ms

test_clean.wav skip-keytail:
  927.1 ms, 920.8 ms

test.wav default:
  5458.9 ms

test.wav skip-keytail:
  5435.8 ms
```

Output drift, comparing source opt-in against default float32 WAV output:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals       max_abs=0.14217028  rms=0.00629013673  rel_rms=0.0340649374
  Instrumental max_abs=0.187878575 rms=0.00672691631  rel_rms=0.0279847933

test.wav:
  Vocals       max_abs=0.107451975 rms=0.00243691515  rel_rms=0.0128938834
  Instrumental max_abs=0.118347377 rms=0.00244882563  rel_rms=0.0127733102
```

Interpretation:

- The source resource shape confirms the bench result for the ordinary
  split-contiguous path: no-keytail materially reduces register and shared
  memory pressure.
- The q-rotary-in-attention source path benefits much less in registers, though
  it still saves shared memory. This matters if that experimental producer path
  is enabled.
- End-to-end speed is at best a small signal. `test_clean.wav` is noise-level
  neutral, while `test.wav` shows one `23.1 ms` win over `5458.9 ms` (`0.42%`).
- Output drift is much larger than the synthetic attention-output comparison:
  about `2.8-3.4% rel_rms` on `test_clean.wav` and `1.28-1.29% rel_rms` on
  `test.wav`.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1` as an aggressive opt-in
  experiment only.
- Do not promote to default.
- The useful finding is architectural: the masked tail tile is a real resource
  pressure source, but removing it changes model behavior too much for the tiny
  end-to-end speed signal. Future work should look for a correctness-preserving
  way to reduce tail pressure, such as a narrower dedicated tail kernel or a
  different fixed-shape partition, instead of dropping keys.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention exact Q/K tile shape sweep

Goal:

- Find whether a correctness-preserving Q/K tile shape can reduce the same
  source of pressure observed in the no-keytail lower-bound experiment.
- All candidates process the full `N=1301` key range for `Q=0..1279`; no keys
  are dropped.

Commands:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q32k32_exp2 --warmup 10 --iters 100

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k16_exp2 --warmup 10 --iters 100

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2 --warmup 10 --iters 100

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k64_exp2 --warmup 10 --iters 100

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q128k32_exp2 --warmup 10 --iters 100

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Timing:

```text
Q32/K32:
  best=6.381 ms, median=6.390 ms, real_math=32.07 TF/s, roof70=45.8%

Q64/K16:
  best=3.774 ms, median=3.812 ms, real_math=54.22 TF/s, roof70=77.5%

Q64/K32:
  best=3.398 ms, median=3.404 ms, real_math=60.22 TF/s, roof70=86.0%

Q64/K64:
  best=3.986 ms, median=3.989 ms, real_math=51.34 TF/s, roof70=73.3%

Q128/K32:
  best=3.623 ms, median=3.631 ms, real_math=56.48 TF/s, roof70=80.7%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous:
  best=3.399 ms, median=3.418 ms, real_math=60.21 TF/s, roof70=86.0%
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
Q32/K32:
  REG=168 STACK=0 SHARED=42112

Q64/K16:
  REG=214 STACK=0 SHARED=32768

Q64/K32:
  REG=237 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

Q64/K64:
  REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=49152

Q128/K32:
  REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=49152

Q64/K32 split-contiguous:
  REG=233 STACK=0 SHARED=40960
```

Output comparison against Q64/K32 baseline:

```text
Q64/K16:
  max_abs=0.001953125 rms=0.000505273408 rel_rms=0.002038398

Q64/K64:
  max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

Q32/K32:
  max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0

Q128/K32:
  max_abs=0 rms=0 rel_rms=0
```

Interpretation:

- `Q64/K32` remains the exact-shape sweet spot on A10G for this CUDA Tile
  streaming attention kernel.
- `K16` lowers resource pressure (`REG=214`, `SHARED=32768`) but doubles the
  number of K-loop iterations, dropping from `60.22 -> 54.22 TF/s`.
- `K64` and `Q128/K32` both hit the `REG=255` cliff and lose throughput despite
  doing full exact work.
- `Q32/K32` has the lowest register count, but it under-utilizes the useful
  work per tile/block and falls to only `32.07 TF/s`.
- Split-contiguous Q64/K32 matches the generic exact benchmark speed while using
  slightly fewer registers (`233` vs `237`), so the current source layout is not
  the bottleneck.

Decision:

- Do not switch source time attention away from Q64/K32.
- The next correctness-preserving path should not be a simple Q/K tile shape
  change. It needs to target the masked 21-key tail implementation directly or
  reduce register lifetime inside the Q64/K32 body.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention exact TailKeys=21 compile-time negative

Goal:

- Test whether the final 21-key tail can be expressed as a narrower exact CUDA
  Tile MMA instead of a masked `KTile=32` operation.
- Candidate shape:
  - QK tail: `QTile<64,64> x KTail<64,21> -> ScoreTail<64,21>`
  - AV tail: `ProbTail<64,21> x VTail<21,64> -> Out<64,64>`
- This would have preserved all 1301 keys and removed the padded/masked 11
  inactive columns from the final tail tile.

Result:

- The candidate does not compile under CUDA Tile 13.3.
- The compiler rejects `ct::tile` shapes with a `21` extent before reaching
  `ct::mma`.

Representative error:

```text
template constraint not satisfied
using KTailTile = ct::tile<__nv_bfloat16, ct::shape<kD, TailKeys>>;

concept is false for arguments
<cuda::tiles::__1::extents<cuda::tiles::__1::uint32_t, 64UL, 21UL>>

atomic constraint evaluates to false
tile_shape = shape_like<T> &&
detail::is_tile_compatible_shape_v<detail::remove_cv_t<T>>
```

The same failure occurs for:

```text
ct::shape<64,21>
ct::shape<21,64>
```

Interpretation:

- A direct `64x21` / `21x64` exact Tensor Core tail is not expressible with the
  current CUDA Tile `ct::tile` shape rules.
- This explains why the source and bench currently use a masked `KTile=32` tail:
  `21` useful keys must be represented inside a tile-compatible shape.
- The no-keytail lower-bound showed the tail path costs resources, but this
  compile failure rules out the most direct correctness-preserving fix.

Decision:

- Do not keep the failed `TailKeys=21` code in source or bench.
- Keep the buildable masked `KTile=32` tail as the exact baseline.
- Next correctness-preserving options must stay tile-compatible, for example:
  a padded `KTile=32` tail with lower register lifetime, a staged partial-state
  design, or a different tile-compatible partition. Arbitrary `21`-wide CUDA
  Tile MMA is not available here.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention padded tail ordinary-load probe

Goal:

- Test whether the exact masked 21-key tail is expensive mainly because of
