  `load_masked()`.
- Keep a tile-compatible `KTile=32` tail, but pack split-contiguous Q/K/V into
  `[B,NPad=1344,H,D]` so the final tail tile can use ordinary `load()` for K/V.
- Preserve exact math by keeping the score mask for columns `1301..1311`, so
  padded keys receive `-inf` before softmax.

Candidate:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_padded_tail_load
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_padded_tail_load \
  --warmup 5 --iters 30 --compare-baseline

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_padded_tail_load \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Timing:

```text
Q64/K32 split-contiguous masked tail baseline:
  best=3.399 ms, median=3.418 ms, real_math=60.21 TF/s, roof70=86.0%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous padded tail ordinary-load:
  best=3.416 ms, median=3.445 ms, real_math=59.91 TF/s, roof70=85.6%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous no-keytail lower bound:
  best=3.293 ms, median=3.327 ms, real_math=61.15 TF/s, roof70=87.4%
```

Output comparison against Q64/K32 baseline:

```text
compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2 elems=39966720 max_abs=0 rms=0
ref_rms=0.2478777 rel_rms=0
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
masked tail baseline:
  REG=233 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

padded tail ordinary-load:
  REG=235 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

no-keytail lower bound:
  REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
```

Interpretation:

- Padding K/V and replacing tail `load_masked()` with ordinary `load()` is
  correctness-preserving and bit-identical to baseline.
- It does not reduce resource pressure. Registers get slightly worse
  (`233 -> 235`) and shared memory is unchanged.
- Runtime is slightly worse (`3.399 -> 3.416 ms`) before counting any source
  producer overhead for writing padded split-contiguous buffers.
- Therefore `load_masked()` is not the main limiter. The resource reduction in
  the no-keytail lower bound comes from removing the whole tail online-softmax
  and AV update path, not just from avoiding masked K/V loads.

Decision:

- Keep this as a bench-only negative candidate.
- Do not move padded split-contiguous K/V into source.
- Future exact tail work should target register lifetime across the tail
  softmax/MMA update or staged state, not ordinary-load padding.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention two-pass FP32 partial-state probe

Goal:

- Test whether the exact tail resource pressure can be solved by splitting the
  main attention path into two CUDA Tile kernels:
  - pass 1 computes keys `0..1279` and writes FP32 `out_acc`, `row_m`, `row_l`
    to global memory.
  - pass 2 reads that partial state, processes the exact final masked 21-key
    tail, normalizes, and writes BF16 output.
- This preserves all keys and keeps the state in FP32, so it tests the cost of
  falling back to global memory rather than changing math.

Candidate:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_two_pass_state
```

State footprint per synthetic main attention launch:

```text
out_acc: 480 * 1280 * 64 * sizeof(float) = 157286400 bytes
row_m:   480 * 1280 * sizeof(float)      =   2457600 bytes
row_l:   480 * 1280 * sizeof(float)      =   2457600 bytes
total:                                      162201600 bytes (~154.7 MiB)
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_two_pass_state \
  --warmup 5 --iters 30 --compare-baseline

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_two_pass_state \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Timing:

```text
Q64/K32 split-contiguous single-kernel exact:
  best=3.399 ms, median=3.418 ms, real_math=60.21 TF/s, roof70=86.0%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous two-pass FP32 state:
  best=3.807 ms, median=3.835 ms, real_math=53.75 TF/s, roof70=76.8%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous no-keytail lower bound:
  best=3.293 ms, median=3.327 ms, real_math=61.15 TF/s, roof70=87.4%
```

Output comparison against Q64/K32 baseline:

```text
compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2 elems=39966720 max_abs=0 rms=0
ref_rms=0.2478777 rel_rms=0
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
single-kernel exact:
  REG=233 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

no-keytail lower bound:
  REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=32768

two-pass state producer:
  REG=215 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

two-pass tail finalize:
  REG=124 STACK=0 SHARED=42240
```

Interpretation:

- Splitting the state is bit-identical and does reduce per-kernel register
  pressure. The state producer is even slightly below the no-keytail normalized
  output kernel (`REG=215` vs `217`) because it avoids final division/output
  rounding in that pass.
- The tail finalize kernel is light in registers (`REG=124`), confirming that
  the high register pressure in the single kernel comes from keeping the main
  accumulator state live across the tail update.
- The global-memory state round trip is too expensive: one write plus one read
  of about `154.7 MiB` of FP32 state, plus a second launch, drops throughput
  from `60.21 -> 53.75 TF/s`.
- This validates the working rule for this attention path: do not fall to
  global memory for `out_acc/row_m/row_l` unless a later fusion removes an even
  larger cost.

Decision:

- Keep this as a bench-only negative/diagnostic candidate.
- Do not move two-pass FP32 state into source.
- Future exact work should stay inside one kernel and reduce register lifetime
  locally, or find a tile-compatible way to make the tail update cheaper without
  materializing full FP32 state globally.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention tail-first single-kernel probe

Goal:

- Test whether moving the exact 21-key tail update before the 40 full KTile
  loop can shorten tail temporary lifetimes inside one kernel.
- Keep all 1301 keys, do not materialize global state, and keep the same
  `Q64/K32` tile-compatible shape.

Candidate:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_tail_first
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_tail_first \
  --warmup 5 --iters 30 --compare-baseline

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_tail_first \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Timing:

```text
Q64/K32 split-contiguous single-kernel exact:
  best=3.399 ms, median=3.418 ms, real_math=60.21 TF/s, roof70=86.0%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous tail-first:
  best=3.473 ms, median=3.502 ms, real_math=58.91 TF/s, roof70=84.2%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous no-keytail lower bound:
  best=3.293 ms, median=3.327 ms, real_math=61.15 TF/s, roof70=87.4%
```

Output comparison against Q64/K32 baseline:

```text
compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2 elems=39966720 max_abs=0 rms=0
ref_rms=0.2478777 rel_rms=0
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
single-kernel exact:
  REG=233 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

tail-first:
  REG=221 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

no-keytail lower bound:
  REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
```

Interpretation:

- Tail-first is bit-identical to the baseline in this synthetic benchmark.
- It reduces register pressure substantially (`233 -> 221`), proving source
  order can influence CUDA Tile register allocation for this kernel.
- Despite lower registers, runtime gets worse (`3.399 -> 3.473 ms`). The likely
  cause is less favorable scheduling around the hot 40 full-KTile loop: lower
  register count alone is not the objective if it disrupts the dominant steady
  Tensor Core work.
- This is a useful diagnostic because it separates "resource shape" from
  "throughput shape": closer to the no-keytail register count is not sufficient.

Decision:

- Keep tail-first as a bench-only diagnostic candidate.
- Do not move it into source.
- Future single-kernel work should preserve the full-KTile loop schedule and
  target local tail epilogue pressure without moving the tail before the loop.

### 2026-06-04 - Time attention tail idx32 probe

Goal:

- Test whether the exact tail epilogue is carrying unnecessary 64-bit integer
  pressure.
- Keep the `Q64/K32` split-contiguous single-kernel order unchanged.
- Change only the tail mask/index tile from `long long` to `int`.

Candidate:

```text
main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_tail_idx32
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_tail_idx32 \
  --warmup 5 --iters 30 --compare-baseline

./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_tail_idx32 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 100
```

Timing:

```text
Q64/K32 split-contiguous single-kernel exact:
  best=3.399 ms, median=3.418 ms, real_math=60.21 TF/s, roof70=86.0%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous tail idx32:
  best=3.457 ms, median=3.463 ms, real_math=59.18 TF/s, roof70=84.5%

Q64/K32 split-contiguous no-keytail lower bound:
  best=3.293 ms, median=3.327 ms, real_math=61.15 TF/s, roof70=87.4%
```

Output comparison against Q64/K32 baseline:

```text
compare_vs_main1280_q64k32_exp2 elems=39966720 max_abs=0 rms=0
ref_rms=0.2478777 rel_rms=0
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
single-kernel exact:
  REG=233 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

tail idx32:
  REG=232 STACK=0 SHARED=40960

no-keytail lower bound:
  REG=217 STACK=0 SHARED=32768
```

Interpretation:

- Tail idx32 is bit-identical, but it only removes one register
  (`233 -> 232`).
- Runtime gets worse (`3.399 -> 3.457 ms`), so integer index width is not the
  important pressure source in this kernel.
- The gap to the no-keytail lower bound remains almost entirely unexplained by
  tail index width.

Decision:

- Keep this as a bench-only negative candidate.
- Do not move tail idx32 into source.
- Future exact work should target the FP32 accumulator/softmax state lifetime,
  not the width of the tail mask index.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 H32/TK64 idx32 + output no-round combination

Goal:

- Test whether two previously isolated FFN12 observations combine:
  - `idx32` lowers H32/TK64 register allocation but was slower.
  - output-only no-round is the current source-default risk-tolerant path.
- Candidate keeps the pair-H32/TK64 schedule and changes only:
  - `IndexElement=int`
  - `RoundHiddenAcc=true`
  - `RoundOutAcc=false`

Candidate:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32_outnoround
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32_outnoround \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
H32/TK64 baseline:
  best=1.854 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.13 TF/s, roof70=63.0%

H32/TK64 output no-round:
  best=1.858 ms, median=1.861 ms, useful=44.05 TF/s, roof70=62.9%

H32/TK64 idx32:
  best=1.872 ms, median=1.878 ms, useful=43.72 TF/s, roof70=62.5%

H32/TK64 idx32 + output no-round:
  best=1.869 ms, median=1.874 ms, useful=43.79 TF/s, roof70=62.6%
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
H32/TK64 baseline:
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

H32/TK64 output no-round:
  REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

H32/TK64 idx32:
  REG=230 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

H32/TK64 idx32 + output no-round:
  REG=230 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Interpretation:

- The combination keeps the idx32 register reduction (`242 -> 230`).
- It still loses runtime (`1.854 -> 1.869 ms`), similar to idx32 alone.
- Output no-round does not make idx32 become throughput-positive in this kernel.
- Therefore the H32/TK64 limiter is not just register allocation count. The
  int32 index version likely changes instruction mix/scheduling enough to lose
  Tensor Core steady-loop throughput despite lower `REG`.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_idx32_outnoround` as a bench-only
  negative candidate.
- Do not move idx32 into the source FFN12 default, even with output no-round.
- Future FFN12 work should target the W1/W2 MMA schedule or hidden epilogue
  organization, not index width.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 H32/TK64 profiler fallback and SASS structure

Goal:

- Explain why the FFN12 H32/TK64 family stays near `44 TF/s` even when register
  allocation is reduced.
- First attempt Nsight Compute hardware counters; fall back to static SASS
  structure because counters are not available in this environment.

Attempted Nsight Compute command:

```bash
ncu --target-processes all --launch-skip 1 --launch-count 1 \
  --section SpeedOfLight --section Occupancy --section WarpStateStats \
  --section MemoryWorkloadAnalysis --kernel-name-base demangled \
  --log-file bench/ncu_ffn12_h32_tk64_baseline.txt \
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 1 --iters 1
```

Result:

```text
ERR_NVGPUCTRPERM - The user does not have permission to access NVIDIA GPU
Performance Counters on the target device 0.
```

Fallback SASS count command:

```bash
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile | c++filt
```

Static instruction counts for the four H32/TK64 variants:

```text
variant             total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
baseline            2760  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
outnoround          2616  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  278  168   175  180 55   35
idx32               2784  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  366  171   184  197 119  35
idx32_outnoround    2632  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  287  176   184  189 58   35
```

Timing/resource context:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.854 ms, useful=44.13 TF/s, REG=242

outnoround:
  best=1.858 ms, useful=44.05 TF/s, REG=242

idx32:
  best=1.872 ms, useful=43.72 TF/s, REG=230

idx32_outnoround:
  best=1.869 ms, useful=43.79 TF/s, REG=230
```

Interpretation:

- All four variants have the same static Tensor Core and memory-core structure:
  `256 HMMA`, `96 LDG`, `128 LDSM`, `44 STS`, and `8 STG`.
- Therefore these variants do not change the amount of Tensor Core work or the
  high-level memory staging shape.
- `outnoround` mostly removes scalar/index/conversion-adjacent work:
  `IMAD 350 -> 278`, `PRMT 115 -> 55`, and a small `SHF` reduction.
- `idx32` lowers register allocation (`242 -> 230`) but increases or preserves
  scalar address/control pressure: `IMAD 366`, `LOP3 184`, `SHF 197`,
  `PRMT 119`.
- The combination keeps the lower register allocation but does not change HMMA
  count or memory staging, and it still carries more scalar/control work than
  pure outnoround.
- This explains the timing: register count reduction alone is not enough when
  the steady Tensor Core/memory instruction structure is unchanged and scalar
  scheduling pressure worsens.

Decision:

- Use SASS/resource evidence as the local fallback when Nsight Compute counters
  are unavailable.
- For FFN12, prioritize changes that alter the W1/W2 MMA scheduling or reduce
  hidden epilogue scalar work without increasing address/control instruction
  pressure. Index-width-only changes are exhausted.

### 2026-06-04 - Source opt-in FFN12 H32/TK64 tinyblend GELU probe

Goal:

- Promote the bench-only `fused_h32_poly9_tinyblend_split2_pairh32_tk64`
  candidate into the source as an explicit opt-in experiment.
- Check whether its standalone speed signal survives in end-to-end inference.
- Determine whether the bottom cause is a real Tensor Core/resource improvement
  or just a source/SASS scheduling perturbation.

The new source switch is:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_TINYBLEND_GELU=1
```

The path stays opt-in. Default BF16 inference remains the existing H32/TK64
poly9 FFN12 path.

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile cudasep_infer -j8
```

Standalone candidate timings:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s, roof=63.0%

fused_h32_poly9_tinyblend_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.825 ms, median=1.829 ms, useful=44.85 TF/s, roof=64.1%

fused_h32_identity_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=2.144 ms, median=2.198 ms, useful=38.18 TF/s, roof=54.5%
```

Bench resource usage:

```text
poly9:      REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
tinyblend:  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
identity:   REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
```

Bench SASS counts:

```text
variant    total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
poly9      2760  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
tinyblend  2800  256  96  128  44  8   320  192  128  338  167   175  198 116  35
identity   2488  256  96  128  44  8   0    96   0    695  164   182  149 121  33
```

Source resource usage:

```text
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<6,false,64,false,false>:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304

ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<7,false,64,false,false>:
  REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304

ffn12_tail_ffn1_gelu_cutile_kernel<6,false>:
  REG=146, STACK=0, SHARED=61440

ffn12_tail_ffn1_gelu_cutile_kernel<7,false>:
  REG=148, STACK=0, SHARED=61440
```

Source SASS counts:

```text
variant    total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
poly9      2624  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  282  165   175  182 55   35
tinyblend  2640  256  96  128  44  8   320  192  128  255  170   175  188 55   35
```

End-to-end BF16 inference:

```text
test_clean.wav default:
  infer=926.8 ms

test_clean.wav CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_TINYBLEND_GELU=1:
  infer=924.0 ms

test.wav default:
  infer=5464.8 ms

test.wav CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_TINYBLEND_GELU=1:
  infer=5454.7 ms
```

Output drift versus the same-run default:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  Vocals:       rel_rms=2.265148232, max_abs=2.904817998
  Instrumental: rel_rms=3.261049922, max_abs=3.859327191

test.wav:
  Vocals:       rel_rms=2.253343708, max_abs=3.148460686
  Instrumental: rel_rms=3.484092459, max_abs=4.815154538
```

Interpretation:

- `tinyblend` is not a small GELU approximation. It is effectively
  `x + 1/1024 * (gelu - x)`, so it is close to identity and heavily changes
  model semantics.
- The standalone bench speed signal is real but small: `1.855 -> 1.825 ms`.
  The end-to-end signal is much smaller: `926.8 -> 924.0 ms` on
  `test_clean.wav`, and `5464.8 -> 5454.7 ms` on `test.wav`.
- The bottom cause is not a Tensor Core utilization improvement. HMMA, LDG,
  LDSM, STS, STG, register count, and shared-memory usage are unchanged for the
  main FFN12 kernel.
- The only source-level structural change is scalar instruction mix:
  `FFMA +64`, `FMUL -32`, `IMAD -27`, with the same 256 HMMA instructions.
  This can perturb scheduling on A10G, but it does not solve the FFN12
  residency/staging ceiling.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_TINYBLEND_GELU=1` as an explicit source
  experiment only.
- Do not promote tinyblend to the default path because output drift is extreme
  while end-to-end speed gain is only about `0.18%` on `test.wav`.
- The next FFN12 work should keep the H32/TK64 pair-H32 schedule, but target
  W1/W2 MMA staging, shared-memory residency, or a different hidden epilogue
  structure that changes more than the scalar instruction mix.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 H32/TK64 explicit W2 temporary load probe

Goal:

- Isolate the previous `w2lat8` negative result.
- The old candidate changed two things at once: it explicitly stored W2 tiles in
  local tile variables and also added `cutile::hint(0, latency=8)`.
- This probe adds `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2temp`, which keeps the
  explicit W2 tile variables but removes the latency hint.

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
```

Timing commands:

```bash
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2lat8 --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2temp --warmup 10 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.854 ms, median=1.857 ms, useful=44.15 TF/s, roof70=63.1%

w2lat8:
  best=3.363 ms, median=3.365 ms, useful=24.34 TF/s, roof70=34.8%

w2temp:
  best=1.988 ms, median=2.017 ms, useful=41.17 TF/s, roof70=58.8%
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline: REG=242, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
w2temp:   REG=252, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
w2lat8:   REG=255, STACK=0, SHARED=98304
```

SASS counts:

```text
variant  total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
baseline 2760  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
w2lat8   1896  128  80  64   34  8   128  128  80   341  109   111  143 49   35
w2temp   2616  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  274  152   140  174 119  47
```

Interpretation:

- Explicit W2 tile temporaries are already negative without latency hints:
  `REG=242 -> 252`, while the main HMMA/staging count stays unchanged.
- The latency hint is worse than the temporary variable alone. It pushes the
  kernel to the `REG=255` cliff and changes the generated Tensor Core/staging
  structure: static `HMMA 256 -> 128`, `LDSM 128 -> 64`, and `LDG 96 -> 80`.
- Therefore `cutile::hint(0, latency=8)` is not a harmless scheduling hint for
  this FFN12 W2 path under the current CUDA Tile compiler. It changes codegen
  enough to lose the current efficient 256-HMMA structure.
- `w2temp` also proves that lower scalar instruction count is not sufficient:
  it reduces `IMAD/LOP3/SHF`, but the register jump to 252 and extra predicate
  pressure (`ISETP 35 -> 47`) still slow the kernel by about 7%.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2temp` as a bench-only negative
  diagnostic.
- Do not promote explicit W2 tile temporaries or W2 latency hints into source.
- The next FFN12 work should preserve the baseline inline `w2_view.load(...)`
  form for W2 MMA and look for a different way to reduce `REG=242/SMEM=98304`
  without changing the compiler's 256-HMMA staging structure.

### 2026-06-04 - Promotion: Time split-contiguous rotary tile128 default

Goal:

- Tune the standalone split-contiguous time rotary pass after QKV split-contig
  became default.
- Prior negative results ruled out producer-side rotary fusion, Q-rotary inside
  attention, and a row-tile standalone rotary shape.
- This probe keeps the same 1D streaming rotary kernel and sweeps only the CUDA
  Tile vector length:

```text
CUDASEP_TIME_ROTARY_TILE=128|256|512|1024
```

Default is now `128`. Use `CUDASEP_TIME_ROTARY_TILE=256` to reproduce the old
default.

Build:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
```

`test_clean.wav` same-binary profile:

```text
tile256 old default:
  infer=929.7 ms
  split_producer=71.1355 ms / 48 calls
  split_rotary=24.5562 ms / 48 calls

tile128:
  infer=930.5 ms
  split_producer=71.2310 ms / 48 calls
  split_rotary=24.1873 ms / 48 calls

tile512:
  infer=931.0 ms
  split_producer=71.2656 ms / 48 calls
  split_rotary=24.8700 ms / 48 calls

tile1024:
  infer=927.4 ms
  split_producer=71.1484 ms / 48 calls
  split_rotary=24.6073 ms / 48 calls

tile128 promoted default:
  infer=931.1 ms
  split_producer=71.1252 ms / 48 calls
  split_rotary=24.1794 ms / 48 calls
```

`test.wav` validation:

```text
tile256 old default:
  infer=5486.1 ms
  split_producer=427.253 ms / 288 calls
  split_rotary=147.411 ms / 288 calls

tile128 opt-in before promotion:
  infer=5454.1 ms
  split_producer=425.182 ms / 288 calls
  split_rotary=144.242 ms / 288 calls

tile128 promoted default:
  infer=5465.1 ms
  split_producer=425.578 ms / 288 calls
  split_rotary=144.956 ms / 288 calls
```

Output drift versus tile256:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  tile128 Vocals:       rel_rms=5.417733484e-08 max_abs=2.384185791e-07
  tile128 Instrumental: rel_rms=5.463259197e-08 max_abs=2.384185791e-07
  tile512 Vocals:       rel_rms=5.412845218e-08 max_abs=1.788139343e-07
  tile512 Instrumental: rel_rms=5.420811286e-08 max_abs=2.384185791e-07
  tile1024 Vocals:      rel_rms=5.394554575e-08 max_abs=1.788139343e-07
  tile1024 Instrumental: rel_rms=5.451715468e-08 max_abs=2.384185791e-07

test.wav tile128:
  Vocals:       rel_rms=5.420417882e-08 max_abs=2.384185791e-07
  Instrumental: rel_rms=5.444901403e-08 max_abs=2.980232239e-07
```

Resource usage:

```text
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel<128>:  REG=18,  STACK=0, SHARED=0
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel<256>:  REG=29,  STACK=0, SHARED=0
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel<512>:  REG=48,  STACK=0, SHARED=0
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel<1024>: REG=105, STACK=0, SHARED=0
```

SASS counts:

```text
tile total LDG STG FFMA FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
128  120   6   4   4    4    33   9     5    8   4    3
256  224   12  8   8    8    69   20    11   13  29   5
512  376   24  16  16   16   120  35    19   16  50   9
1024 832   48  32  32   32   270  89    39   31  120  33
```

Interpretation:

- The rotary pass is memory/elementwise work, not Tensor Core work. The useful
  metric is global load/store count, integer address math, and registers.
- `TileSize=128` lowers the generated static instruction count by almost half
  versus 256 and reduces registers from `29` to `18`.
- Larger tiles are clearly worse for this CUDA Tile compiler shape:
  `REG=48` at 512 and `REG=105` at 1024, with proportional LDG/STG and address
  instruction growth.
- The targeted kernel improvement is repeatable in the profile counters:
  `24.56 -> 24.18 ms` on `test_clean.wav`, and `147.41 -> 144.24/144.96 ms`
  on `test.wav`.
- End-to-end inference is still noisy at this scale, but the local kernel is
  smaller, lower-register, and not measurably different numerically.

Decision:

- Promote 1D time split-contiguous rotary tile size from 256 to 128.
- Keep `CUDASEP_TIME_ROTARY_TILE=256|512|1024` for A/B and rollback.
- Future rotary work should not use larger standalone vector tiles. Any larger
  win must remove the standalone memory pass entirely without increasing the
  attention kernel's shared-memory footprint.

### 2026-06-04 - Current Profile After Time Rotary Tile128 Default

Command:

```bash
nsys profile --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
  -o bench/nsys_current_rotary128_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/nsys_current_rotary128_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
nsys export --type sqlite --force-overwrite=true \
  -o bench/nsys_current_rotary128_clean.sqlite \
  bench/nsys_current_rotary128_clean.nsys-rep
```

Aggregate:

```text
kernel calls: 6369
total CUDA kernel time: 902.856 ms
```

Top kernels:

```text
kernel group                                             calls  total_ms  avg_us    regs  smem
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel                 96  182.127  1897.153  242   98304
time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel       48  162.829  3392.272  237   40960
qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel                     48   68.611  1429.401  174   98304
linear_cutile_static_full_bkn_bf16_kernel                   48   68.143  1419.651  170   98304
linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel                  964   55.569    57.645  116   32768
linear_cutile_static_full_bf16_kernel                       96   50.713   528.260  243   98304
freq_attention60_cutile_padded_out60_kernel                 48   38.736   806.994   92   22528
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel         48   36.866   768.051   40       0
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel                               288   35.656   123.805   16      16
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel                   96   23.814   248.062   18      16
rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel<128>                48   23.743   494.651   18       0
apply_gates_and_merge_heads_bf16_token_d64_cutile_kernel    96   23.563   245.451   24    2048
```

Interpretation:

- The current bottleneck is not a missing global BF16 roofline number. The top
  CUDA Tile Tensor Core kernels are far below the local dense BF16 reference
  because they carry very high register and shared-memory pressure on A10G.
- `ffn12` is the largest remaining single group. Its current schedule uses
  `REG=242` and `96KB` shared memory, so a single CTA can monopolize a large
  fraction of the per-SM resources even before considering latency hiding.
- `time_attention` is the second largest group and also high-register
  (`REG=237`). Its main kernel is already closer to useful Tensor Core work than
  the smaller elementwise kernels, so future experiments should target schedule
  pressure and global-memory round trips rather than scalar math tweaks.
- Standalone rotary tile128 is now a smaller issue than FFN12, attention, QKV,
  and ordinary linears. Further rotary improvement should come from removing a
  memory pass, not from making the standalone vector tile larger.

Next target:

- Use this profile as the new post-rotary128 baseline.
- Prioritize a CUDA Tile/A10G experiment that reduces FFN12 or time-attention
  register/shared-memory pressure while preserving Tensor Core `ct::mma`
  generation. Do not repeat the already-negative W2 temp/hint and tinyblend GELU
  probes.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 Resource-Pressure Shape Probe

Goal:

- Explain why the top FFN12 CUDA Tile Tensor Core kernel is still around
  `44 TF/s`, well below the local dense BF16 reference.
- Test whether reducing apparent per-CTA resource pressure helps A10G, while
  preserving `ct::mma`/HMMA generation.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 3 --iters 12
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_occ2 --warmup 3 --iters 12
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_halfout --warmup 3 --iters 12
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split4_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 3 --iters 12
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile \
  --variant fused_h16_poly9_split2_pairh16_tk64 --warmup 3 --iters 12
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile \
  --variant fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64 --warmup 3 --iters 12
```

Timing:

```text
variant                                      best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof%
h32 split2 pairh32 tk64 baseline              1.856       44.09    63.0
h32 split2 pairh32 tk64 occ2                  2.881       28.40    40.6
h32 split2 pairh32 tk64 halfout               3.391       24.13    34.5
h32 split4 pairh32 tk64                       2.120       38.61    55.2
h16 split2 pairh16 tk64                       2.607       31.40    44.9
h64 split2 pairh64 tk64                       2.522       32.45    46.4
```

Resource and SASS:

```text
variant                    REG  SHARED  STACK  HMMA  LDSM  LDG  STS  STG  total_inst
h32 tk64 baseline          242   98304      0   256   128  208  124    8        5520
h32 tk64 occ2              186   32896      0   128    64  152  106    8        4096
h32 tk64 halfout           234   94208      0   192   112  156   96    4        4160
h32 split4 tk64            255   98304      8   256   128  216  148    8        5744
h16 tk64                   197  100352      0    --    --   --   --   --          --
h64 tk64                   255   94208      0    --    --   --   --   --          --
```

Interpretation:

- The `occ2` hint reduces reported `REG` and shared memory, but it also changes
  CUDA Tile codegen enough to cut HMMA count from `256` to `128`. The slowdown is
  therefore not benchmark noise; the Tensor Core schedule itself is weaker.
- `halfout` reduces per-output store width and instruction count, but it needs
  two output tiles and recomputes FFN1 work. Useful throughput falls despite
  less work per CTA.
- `split4` preserves `HMMA=256`, but raises register pressure to `255`, adds a
  stack frame, and increases shared-memory stores. It is slower even though the
  Tensor Core count remains intact.
- Smaller/larger hidden pair widths are both worse on A10G for this schedule:
  `h16` lowers registers but shared memory rises above the baseline, while
  `h64` hits `REG=255`.

Decision:

- Keep source default at `h32 split2 pairh32 tk64`.
- Do not promote `occ2`, `halfout`, `split4`, `h16`, or `h64`.
- The next FFN12 attempt should not rely on occupancy hints or output splitting
  that changes HMMA shape. A useful direction is a new schedule that preserves
  the baseline `HMMA=256` shape while reducing shared-memory staging or
  long-lived accumulator pressure without recomputing W1.

### 2026-06-04 - Time Attention Main1280 A10G Shape and BF16 Probe

Goal:

- Re-check the second largest current hotspot after rotary128:
  `time_attention1301_main1280_split_contig_input_kernel`.
- Explain why this CUDA Tile Tensor Core kernel reaches about `60 TF/s` but does
  not approach the dense BF16 reference more closely.
- Test whether more aggressive BF16 arithmetic, Q/K tile changes, or tail
  removal should be promoted.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input --warmup 3 --iters 8 \
  --compare-baseline
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_contig_input_no_keytail --warmup 3 \
  --iters 8 --compare-baseline
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q32k32_exp2 --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k16_exp2 --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k64_exp2 --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q128k32_exp2 --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_prescale_q --warmup 3 --iters 8 \
  --compare-baseline
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_split_d32 --warmup 3 --iters 8 \
  --compare-baseline
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_sum_bf16 --warmup 3 --iters 8 \
  --compare-baseline
./build-min/bench_bf16_time_attention_cutile \
  --variant main1280_q64k32_exp2_score_bf16 --warmup 3 --iters 8 \
  --compare-baseline
```

Timing:

```text
variant                                  best_ms  real_TF/s  roof%  rel_rms_vs_q64k32
q64k32 split-contig exp2                   3.394      60.29   86.1  0
q64k32 split-contig exp2 no-keytail        3.299      61.02   87.2  0.002038398
q64k32 generic exp2                        3.405      60.10   85.9  --
q32k32 exp2                                6.387      32.04   45.8  --
q64k16 exp2                                3.781      54.13   77.3  --
q64k64 exp2                                3.988      51.31   73.3  --
q128k32 exp2                               3.629      56.38   80.5  --
q64k32 exp2 prescale_q                     3.504      58.39   83.4  0
q64k32 exp2 split_d32                      3.711      55.15   78.8  0
q64k32 exp2 sum_bf16                       3.517      58.18   83.1  0.001107929
q64k32 exp2 score_bf16                     4.537      45.10   64.4  0.001326187
```

Resource and SASS:

```text
variant                     REG  SHARED  STACK  HMMA  LDG  LDSM  STS  STG  FFMA  MUFU  EX2  total_inst
q64k32 generic exp2         237   40960      0   160   32    64   60    4   358   124   90        5440
q64k32 split-contig exp2    233   40960      0   160   32    64   60    4   358   124   90        5472
q64k32 no-keytail           217   32768      0   128   28    64   48    4   308   106   72        4288
q64k32 sum_bf16             223   40960      0   160   32    64   60    4   358   124   90        5872
q64k32 score_bf16           207   32768      0    64   14    16   48    4   256    70   36        3680
q64k32 prescale_q           255   45056     64   192   38    80   64    4   392   142  108        6448
q64k32 split_d32            255   40960    104   192   43    80   72    4   392   142  108        6528
q128k32                     255   49152      0   128   18    16   48    8   490   134   68        5744
```

End-to-end lower-bound check:

```bash
CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/time_attn_skip_keytail_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Result versus default `outputs/ffn12_resource_probe_clean`:

```text
default test_clean.wav infer:      924.8 ms
skip-keytail test_clean.wav infer: 921.7 ms
Vocals.wav:       rel_rms=0.0340649392 max_abs=0.14217034
Instrumental.wav: rel_rms=0.0279847917 max_abs=0.187878579
```

Interpretation:

- The current stable `q64/k32` split-contig kernel is already the best tested
  A10G shape for main1280. It reaches about `60 TF/s`, or `86%` of the local
  dense BF16 reference.
- The remaining gap is not a missing BF16 GEMM trick. Each K tile performs two
  Tensor Core MMA groups plus online softmax work. The SASS contains many
  `MUFU/EX2`, reductions, and accumulator rescale operations that dense GEMM
  does not have.
- `KTile=16` increases loop/softmax overhead. `KTile=64` increases score tile
  pressure. `QRows=32` creates too many CTAs, while `QRows=128` hits `REG=255`
  and changes the HMMA/LDSM shape.
- More aggressive BF16 arithmetic is not automatically faster here. `sum_bf16`
  preserves `HMMA=160` but adds instructions and drift. `score_bf16` lowers
  reported resources but collapses to `HMMA=64`, so it is much slower.
- `prescale_q` and `split_d32` both increase HMMA count to `192`, but also
  raise registers to `255`, add stack frames, and increase MUFU/EX2 and scalar
  work. They are not useful A10G shapes.
- `skip_keytail` is a lower-bound only: it removes the tail tile and is slightly
  faster in isolation, but the end-to-end output drift is too large for default
  use.

Decision:

- Keep source default at `q64/k32 split-contig exp2 with keytail`.
- Keep `CUDASEP_ENABLE_TIME_ATTENTION_SKIP_KEYTAIL=1` as a risky lower-bound
  experiment only.
- Do not promote `q32`, `q128`, `k16`, `k64`, `sum_bf16`, `score_bf16`,
  `prescale_q`, or `split_d32`.
- Further gains require reducing the non-Tensor Core online softmax overhead or
  changing the algorithmic schedule, not just making Q/K/V or score buffers more
  BF16.

### 2026-06-04 - Infer-QKV BKN Split-Contig A10G Recheck

Goal:

- Re-check the source-relevant QKV producer after the current rotary128/time
  attention profile.
- Explain why the CUDA Tile QKV producer remains around `44 TF/s` instead of
  approaching the local dense BF16 reference more closely.
- Test whether K/N/M shape changes, occupancy hints, latency hints, or
  split-contiguous output should be promoted.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x8bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x64bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t16x256x16bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t64x256x16bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x128x16bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x512x16bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ8 \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ16 \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat7 \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp \
  --warmup 3 --iters 8
```

Timing:

```text
variant                                 best_ms  real_TF/s  roof%
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig            1.399      43.88   62.7
qkv_t32x256x16bkn                         1.381      44.46   63.5
qkv_t32x256x8bkn                          3.167      19.38   27.7
qkv_t32x256x32bkn_tiled                   1.470      41.74   59.6
qkv_t32x256x64bkn                         1.778      34.53   49.3
qkv_t16x256x16bkn                         2.413      25.43   36.3
qkv_t64x256x16bkn                         2.222      27.62   39.5
qkv_t32x128x16bkn                         1.771      34.66   49.5
qkv_t32x512x16bkn                         1.938      31.68   45.3
qkv_t32x256p128x16bkn                     2.346      26.16   37.4
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ8                    1.380      44.47   63.5
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_occ16                   1.381      44.44   63.5
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat7                    1.549      39.62   56.6
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_pairk                   1.381      44.46   63.5
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_loadtmp                 1.381      44.45   63.5
```

Resource and SASS:

```text
variant                         REG  SHARED  STACK  HMMA  LDG  LDSM  STS  STG  FFMA  total_inst
split-contig M32 N256 TK16      174   98304      0   256  112    96   88   24     0        2176
plain BKN M32 N256 TK16         170   98304      0   256  112    96   88    8     0        1872
plain BKN M32 N256 TK8          255   97792      0   512  160    96  104    8     0        2656
plain BKN M32 N256 TK32         206   98304      0   256   88    96   80    8     0        1664
plain BKN M32 N128 TK16          92   90112      0   128   80    64   52    4     0        1472
plain BKN M16 N256 TK16         150   97792      0   128  112    80   88    4     0        1504
plain BKN M32 N512 TK16         255   81920     16   512  176   160  160   16     0        3008
latency hint M32 N256 TK16      170   98304      0   256  112    96   88    8     0        1904
```

Interpretation:

- The source-relevant split-contiguous producer preserves the same Tensor Core
  shape as plain BKN: both emit `HMMA=256`. The split-contiguous output costs
  extra stores and instructions (`STG=24`, `total_inst=2176`) versus plain BKN
  (`STG=8`, `total_inst=1872`), but the timing penalty is only about `1.3%`.
- `M32 x N256 x TK16` remains the best tested A10G macro shape for this QKV
  projection. Shrinking `K` to `8` doubles HMMA count and increases staging
  traffic; widening `K` to `32/64` reduces useful throughput. Changing `M` or
  `N` also loses enough Tensor Core work or adds enough resource pressure to be
  negative.
- Occupancy hints and pair-K grouping do not move the result. The tested latency
  hint is worse. Explicit temporary-load spelling is SASS-identical to the
  default path, so the gap is not caused by a simple source-level load spelling.
- The bottom-level limiter is resource and staging pressure from the CUDA Tile
  schedule: the best producer still uses about `96KB` shared memory per CTA and
  around `170+` registers per thread, so A10G cannot hide latency like a dense
  library GEMM kernel. This is not a Tensor Core fallback issue: the best
  variants all keep HMMA generation and contain no FFMA fallback.

Decision:

- Keep source QKV producer at `M32 x N256 x TK16`.
- Keep the split-contiguous QKV producer path as the source-relevant layout for
  the current time attention path; its store overhead is small compared with the
  downstream layout benefit.
- Do not promote `TK8`, `TK32`, `TK64`, `M16`, `M64`, `N128`, `N512`,
  `N256+128`, latency hint, pair-K, or explicit-load variants.
- The next QKV attempt should not keep sweeping legal macro shapes. It should
  target the generated staging/resource pressure directly, or fuse the producer
  with a consumer only if the fused store/load pattern stays coalesced and does
  not recreate the previous scattered-store failure.

### 2026-06-04 - Negative Probe: Residual+RMSNorm D256 Rows Per CTA

Goal:

- Avoid perturbing the high-pressure FFN12 Tensor Core kernel after the previous
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_FUSED_RESIDUAL=1` negative result.
- Test a lower-pressure epilogue idea instead: keep `FFN12 -> residual+RMSNorm`
  as a separate CUDA Tile kernel, but process 2 or 4 D256 rows per CTA.
- Explain whether `residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16` is limited by too many
  tiny CTAs or by per-row memory/reduction work.

Implementation:

```bash
CUDASEP_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=2
CUDASEP_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=4
```

Default behavior is unchanged. The new opt-in kernels are:

```text
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_rows_to_bf16_kernel<GammaT, FullBF16, 2>
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_rows_to_bf16_kernel<GammaT, FullBF16, 4>
```

They use CUDA Tile `shape<RowsPerCta,256>` tiles, masked load/store, and
`ct::sum<1>` for the per-row RMS reduction.

Build and timing commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/residual_rms_rows_default_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
CUDASEP_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=2 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/residual_rms_rows_rows2_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
CUDASEP_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=4 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/residual_rms_rows_rows4_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Plain end-to-end timing:

```text
default: 933.2 ms
rows2:   927.3 ms
rows4:   930.5 ms
```

The plain timing had a small rows2-positive signal, so rows2 was profiled with
Nsight Systems.

Nsight timing:

```text
default profile infer: 938.4 ms
rows2 profile infer:   950.3 ms
```

Focused kernel group:

```text
variant  kernel                                      calls  total_ms  avg_us   REG  SMEM
default  residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16          96     23.816    248.084  18   16
rows2    residual_add_rms_norm_d256_rows_to_bf16<2>  96     23.822    248.148  37   32
```

Resource usage from `cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage`:

```text
default float,false: REG=18 STACK=0 SHARED=16
rows2   float,false: REG=37 STACK=0 SHARED=32
rows4   float,false: REG=64 STACK=0 SHARED=64
default bf16,false:  REG=19 STACK=0 SHARED=16
rows2   bf16,false:  REG=37 STACK=0 SHARED=32
rows4   bf16,false:  REG=61 STACK=0 SHARED=64
```

SASS count, float gamma / `FullBF16=false`:

```text
variant  total_inst  LDG  STG  F2F  FFMA  FADD  FMUL  MUFU  RSQ  IMAD
default         176    3    2    3     1    11    10     1    1     6
rows2           448   12    8    6     1    16    18     1    1    24
rows4           784   24   16   12     1    23    34     1    1    44
```

Output difference, rows2 versus default on `test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0120671540 rms=0.000786823046 rel_rms=0.00426112816
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0113271475 rms=0.000870347372 rel_rms=0.00362075156
```

Interpretation:

- Rows-per-CTA does not reduce the number of kernel launches; it only changes
  the amount of tile work inside each CTA.
- The expected CTA-count reduction is cancelled by larger per-CTA tile state:
  rows2 doubles reported shared memory and roughly doubles registers, while
  rows4 reaches `REG=64`.
- The SASS shows why this does not speed up A10G: LDG/STG/IMAD and total static
  instruction count scale up with rows per CTA. This kernel is dominated by
  per-row load/add/store/reduction work, not by one-row CTA launch overhead.
- The nonzero output drift is another reason not to promote the path. It is not
  a crash or gross correctness failure, but it gives no speed benefit to justify
  changing the default arithmetic schedule.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_RESIDUAL_RMS_NORM_D256_ROWS_PER_CTA=2|4` as an opt-in negative
  source experiment only.
- Do not make rows2 or rows4 default.
- Future residual/RMS work should look for dataflow changes that remove a
  memory pass or reuse a producer's coalesced output, not for packing more rows
  into the standalone D256 reduction CTA.

### 2026-06-04 - Attention Output Residual Shape Recheck

Goal:

- Re-check the current attention output residual projection after the latest
  CUDA Tile changes.
- Confirm whether a lower-register shape can beat the promoted `TM32/TN128/TK32`
  default on A10G.
- Explain why the kernel stays around `39 TF/s` instead of approaching the dense
  BF16 reference more closely.

Shape:

```text
infer_attn_out: M=78060, N=256, K=512
full-tile work: M=78048, N=256, K=512
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8
for v in \
  attnres_t16x128x32 \
  attnres_t32x64x32 \
  attnres_t32x128x32 \
  attnres_t32x256x32 \
  attnres_t64x128x32; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
    --shape infer_attn_out --variant "$v" --warmup 10 --iters 120
done
```

Timing:

```text
variant             tile        grid       best_ms  median_ms  TF/s   roof%
attnres_t16x128x32  16x128x32   4878x2       0.740     0.754   27.65   39.5
attnres_t32x64x32   32x64x32    2439x4       0.684     0.692   29.90   42.7
attnres_t32x128x32  32x128x32   2439x2       0.522     0.528   39.18   56.0
attnres_t32x256x32  32x256x32   2439x1       0.665     0.675   30.78   44.0
attnres_t64x128x32  64x128x32   1219x2       0.827     0.831   24.73   35.3
```

Resource usage:

```text
variant             REG  STACK  SHARED
attnres_t16x128x32  139      0   98304
attnres_t32x64x32    91      0   98304
attnres_t32x128x32  243      0   98304
attnres_t32x256x32  182      0   98304
attnres_t64x128x32  184      0   98304
```

SASS count:

```text
variant   total_inst  HMMA  LDG  LDSM  STS  STG  F2F  FFMA  IMAD
t64x128         2944   512  136   192  112    8   32     0    47
t32x256         2976   512  184   192  160    8   32     0   101
t32x128         2080   256  116   128   88    4   16     0    72
t32x64          1648   128   82    96   56    2    8     0    40
```

Interpretation:

- `TM32/TN128/TK32` remains the best tested A10G shape even though it reports
  `REG=243`. The higher register count is worth it because it keeps enough
  Tensor Core work per CTA while avoiding the doubled instruction and staging
  footprint of larger tiles.
- `TN64` lowers registers to `91`, but halves HMMA per CTA and doubles the N
  tile count. The lower pressure does not compensate for extra scheduling and
  operand movement.
- `TN256` and `TM64/TN128` both emit `HMMA=512`, but their static instruction
  count is about `1.4x` the default and they carry more LDG/LDSM/STS work.
  Wider tiles are therefore not a free way to increase Tensor Core occupancy on
  this CUDA Tile schedule.
- The remaining gap to dense BF16 is not a missing Tensor Core path: all tested
  variants keep HMMA and no FFMA fallback. The limiter is the generated staging
  and shared-memory footprint around a residual epilogue projection, plus the
  fact that `N=256` gives only a small number of output tiles per row block.

Decision:

- Keep the source default at attention output residual `TM32/TN128/TK32`.
- Do not promote `TN64`, `TN256`, `TM16`, or `TM64` variants.
- Future attention-output work should not keep macro-shape sweeping. It needs a
  schedule that reduces CUDA Tile staging instructions or changes the upstream
  layout so the projection consumes a more efficient A tile without rebuilding
  it inside the MMA kernel.

### 2026-06-04 - Promotion: RMSNorm Gamma BF16 Default-On

Goal:

- Move one remaining neural-network FP32 storage/load boundary to BF16 by
  default.
- Prioritize the current project goal of full BF16 inference purity over the
  earlier conservative default, while keeping a rollback switch.

Source behavior:

```bash
# default under --bf16
norm gamma weights are converted to BF16 by maybe_bf16_norm_gamma(...)

# rollback
CUDASEP_DISABLE_NORM_GAMMA_BF16=1
```

This promotes the earlier opt-in:

```bash
CUDASEP_NORM_GAMMA_BF16=1
```

The old enable flag is no longer required for the default BF16 path.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/norm_gamma_default_bf16_gamma_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
CUDASEP_DISABLE_NORM_GAMMA_BF16=1 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/norm_gamma_rollback_fp32_gamma_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
nsys profile -t cuda,nvtx --stats=false --force-overwrite=true \
  -o bench/nsys_norm_gamma_default_bf16_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/nsys_norm_gamma_default_bf16_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Timing:

```text
test_clean.wav default BF16 gamma:  926.4 ms
test_clean.wav rollback FP32 gamma: 927.2 ms
Nsight default BF16 gamma:          939.5 ms
test.wav default BF16 gamma:       5451.2 ms
```

Nsight Systems confirms the default path now uses BF16 gamma storage:

```text
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,bf16,false>:
  288 calls, 35.487 ms, REG=16, SMEM=16

residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,false>:
  96 calls, 23.772 ms, REG=19, SMEM=16

rms_norm_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,bf16>:
  240 calls, 1.183 ms, REG=33, SMEM=16
```

Output difference, default BF16 gamma versus rollback FP32 gamma on
`test_clean.wav`:

```text
Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0404371917 rms=0.00243498199 rel_rms=0.0131869171
Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0358597934 rms=0.00300075673 rel_rms=0.0124835144
```

Interpretation:

- This is not a speed win; timing is effectively noise-level. It is a BF16
  purity improvement.
- The default path no longer loads FP32 gamma in RMSNorm kernels. D256 RMSNorm,
  residual+RMSNorm, and generic RMSNorm all instantiate `GammaT=__nv_bfloat16`.
- The output drift is measurable, around `1.25%-1.32% rel_rms` versus rollback.
  This is the expected tradeoff for making norm gamma storage BF16 by default.

Decision:

- Promote BF16 norm gamma storage to the default `--bf16` inference path.
- Keep `CUDASEP_DISABLE_NORM_GAMMA_BF16=1` as the rollback/A-B switch.
- Continue treating STFT/ISTFT, chunk accumulation, and final mask scatter as
  FP32 audio/DSP boundaries; do not confuse those with neural-network BF16
  purity work.

## 2026-06-04 - Rotary freqs BF16 storage promoted

Goal: remove another FP32 neural-network boundary by storing rotary
`cos_freqs/sin_freqs` as BF16 in the default `--bf16` path and dispatching the
current CUDA Tile hot kernels with BF16 trig pointers instead of converting
freqs back to FP32.

Source changes:

- `MelBandRoformer::compute_rotary_cos_sin()` now converts the rotary cache to
  BF16 when `mbr_tile::rotary_freqs_bf16_enabled()` is true.
- `rotary_freqs_bf16_enabled()` is default-on for `--bf16`; rollback is
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_ROTARY_FREQS_BF16=1`.
- The default time split-contig rotary kernel and freq60 padded QKV
  split+rotary kernel are templated on trig dtype and now instantiate BF16 trig
  versions on the default path.
- Row-tile rotary and older fused producer experiments still keep their F32
  freqs fallback; they are not the default hot path for this run.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/rotary_freqs_default_bf16_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav \
  --output outputs/rotary_freqs_default_bf16_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_DISABLE_ROTARY_FREQS_BF16=1 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/rotary_freqs_rollback_f32_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys profile --trace=cuda,nvtx --force-overwrite=true \
  -o bench/nsys_rotary_freqs_default_bf16_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/rotary_freqs_default_bf16_nsys_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

git diff --check
rg -n "cuBLAS|cublas|cuFFT|cufft|cuDNN|cudnn|wmma::|nvcuda::wmma|mma\\.sync|asm\\s*\\(" \
  CMakeLists.txt src bench \
  --glob '!bench/README.md' --glob '!bench/*.sqlite' --glob '!bench/*.nsys-rep'
```

Timing:

```text
test_clean.wav default BF16 rotary freqs: 923.8 ms
test.wav       default BF16 rotary freqs: 5471.4 ms
test_clean.wav rollback FP32 rotary freqs: 928.5 ms
Nsight default BF16 rotary freqs: 942.6 ms
```

Output difference, default BF16 rotary freqs versus rollback FP32 rotary freqs:

```text
clean Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.00805532932 rms=0.000861156884 rel_rms=0.00468251043
clean Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0104619116  rms=0.00100107164  rel_rms=0.00415937763

test Vocals.wav:        max_abs=0.0403460711  rms=0.0009561868   rel_rms=0.00505845194
test Instrumental.wav:  max_abs=0.0473088771  rms=0.00113045758  rel_rms=0.00589629754
```

Nsight Systems default-path evidence:

```text
split_qkv_heads_rotary_qkv_bf16_freq60_pad64_kernel<__nv_bfloat16>:
  48 calls, 36.858 ms, REG=40, SMEM=0

rotary_time_split_contig_inplace_kernel<128,__nv_bfloat16>:
  48 calls, 23.670 ms, REG=20, SMEM=0

f32_to_bf16_kernel:
  1168 calls, 3.728 ms
```

The rotary/conversion query did not show `bf16_to_f32_kernel`, so the default
hot rotary paths are no longer BF16 freqs -> FP32 freqs -> kernel. The remaining
`f32_to_bf16_kernel` calls are from other conversion boundaries and should be
audited separately.

Decision:

- Promote BF16 rotary frequency storage to default `--bf16` inference.
- Keep `CUDASEP_DISABLE_ROTARY_FREQS_BF16=1` as rollback/A-B switch.
- Quality drift is measurable but in the same experimental range as other
  accepted BF16-purity moves: about `0.42%-0.59% rel_rms`.
- No meaningful speed win; this is a BF16-purity and conversion-boundary
  removal step, not a Tensor Core throughput improvement.

Current source LOC:

```text
15382 total
```

## 2026-06-04 - Runtime conversion audit after BF16 rotary freqs

Goal: determine whether the remaining `f32_to_bf16_kernel` calls in the default
Nsight profile are an actual per-chunk runtime boundary or just setup/model
preparation noise.

Profile source:

```text
bench/nsys_rotary_freqs_default_bf16_clean.sqlite
```

Query summary:

```text
f32_to_bf16_kernel:
  calls=1168
  total=3.728 ms
  first=0.000 ms from first GPU kernel
  last=72.076 ms from first GPU kernel
```

All pure `f32_to_bf16_kernel` launches are inside the first `~72 ms` of the GPU
timeline. The full clean profile spans roughly `~1 s` of GPU kernel time, and
the repeated chunk work continues far after that. Therefore these pure
conversion kernels are not a repeated inference-loop bottleneck in the current
default path. They mostly belong to startup, weight/bias/gamma preparation, or
rotary cache setup.

Runtime kernels containing BF16 conversion/output semantics after the first
100 ms:

```text
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,bf16,false>:
  275 calls, 33.885 ms

residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,false>:
  92 calls, 22.782 ms

tanh_bf16_to_bf16_kernel:
  960 calls, 6.318 ms

glu_last_dim_bf16_to_bf16_kernel:
  480 calls, 1.421 ms

gather_freqs_fold_complex_to_bf16_kernel:
  4 calls, 0.467 ms
```

Interpretation:

- The remaining hot-path work is not a standalone FP32->BF16 conversion kernel.
  It is fused compute that writes BF16, plus the expected STFT/audio boundary.
- The next meaningful A10G optimization target is therefore compute throughput
  and fusion depth, not eliminating `f32_to_bf16_kernel`.
- Current top runtime group remains FFN12 fused CUDA Tile.

FFN12 default recheck:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 120
```

Result:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64:
  best=1.855 ms
  median=1.858 ms
  useful=44.11 TF/s
  roof=63.0%
  hidden_rw=0.298 GiB
```

This matches the default clean Nsight profile, where
`ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<poly9,false,64,false,false>` ran
96 calls in `182.544 ms`, about `1.901 ms` per call.

Decision:

- Do not spend effort reducing startup-only `f32_to_bf16_kernel` calls right
  now.
- Continue treating FFN12 H32/TK64 as the main remaining fixed-shape Tensor Core
  throughput target. The existing evidence says the gap is not Tensor Core
  fallback: it is the current CUDA Tile schedule's 98KB SMEM footprint, high
  register pressure, and serial W2 accumulation structure.

## 2026-06-04 - Current FULL_BF16 arithmetic combination recheck

Goal: re-run the broad `CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1` path after
rotary freqs became BF16 by default, and determine whether the current all-BF16
arithmetic risk combination improves A10G throughput or only changes arithmetic
purity/quality.

Commands:

```bash
CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test_clean.wav \
    --output outputs/full_bf16_arith_current_clean \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
    --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
    --input test.wav \
    --output outputs/full_bf16_arith_current_test \
    --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1 \
  nsys profile --trace=cuda,nvtx --force-overwrite=true \
    -o bench/nsys_full_bf16_arith_current_clean \
    ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
      --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
      --input test_clean.wav \
      --output outputs/full_bf16_arith_current_nsys_clean \
      --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Timing versus current default BF16 rotary-freqs path:

```text
test_clean.wav default:       923.8 ms
test_clean.wav FULL_BF16:     936.1 ms

test.wav default:            5471.4 ms
test.wav FULL_BF16:          5526.3 ms

Nsight FULL_BF16 clean:       954.9 ms
```

Output difference, FULL_BF16 arithmetic versus current default BF16:

```text
clean Vocals.wav:       max_abs=0.0335207283 rms=0.00176954391 rel_rms=0.00962481595
clean Instrumental.wav: max_abs=0.0289040208 rms=0.00193271240 rel_rms=0.00802888400

test Vocals.wav:        max_abs=0.0811538100 rms=0.00198283582 rel_rms=0.01048934430
test Instrumental.wav:  max_abs=0.0956397280 rms=0.00229076094 rel_rms=0.01194884720
```

Nsight evidence:

```text
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<poly9,true,64,false,true>:
  91 calls, 185.960 ms, avg=2.0435 ms, REG=234, SMEM=98304

Default profile comparison:
ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel<poly9,false,64,false,false>:
  96 calls, 182.544 ms, avg=1.9015 ms, REG=242, SMEM=98304
```

The FULL_BF16 path also instantiates:

```text
rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,bf16,true>
residual_add_rms_norm_d256_to_bf16_kernel<bf16,true>
linear_cutile_static_padded_m_bf16_kernel<...,FullBF16=true,...>
ffn12_tail_ffn2_cutile_kernel<true,false>
```

Interpretation:

- The current broad FULL_BF16 arithmetic combination is runnable on both test
  WAVs.
- It is not a performance win: clean is about `+12.3 ms`, test is about
  `+54.9 ms` slower than the current default.
- Quality drift is measurable: about `0.80%-1.19% rel_rms` versus the current
  default BF16 path.
- FFN12 shows the central lesson: `FullBF16=true` lowers register allocation
  (`242 -> 234`) but increases runtime (`1.90 -> 2.04 ms` per main FFN12 call).
  The added BF16 rounding/select work does not improve Tensor Core throughput.
- Therefore the FFN12 gap is still structural scheduling/residency work, not a
  missing "turn everything FullBF16" switch.

Decision:

- Keep `CUDASEP_FULL_BF16_ARITH_EXPERIMENTS=1` as the broad all-BF16 arithmetic
  risk experiment.
- Do not promote it as the default performance path.
- Future FFN12 work should target the H32/TK64 W1/W2 schedule and 98KB SMEM
  residency, while preserving the current fast `FullBF16=false` source style
  unless a new structure changes the Tensor Core/memory staging balance.

## 2026-06-04 - FFN12 H32/TK64 epilogue and bias negative sweep

Goal: test whether the current FFN12 H32/TK64 pair-H32 bottleneck is dominated
by scalar epilogue/bias work. These candidates keep the same main fused shape
and perturb only local epilogue/bias/accumulator grouping choices.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

for v in \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_nooutbias \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_nobias \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_noround \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_accgroup; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
    --variant "$v" --warmup 10 --iters 300
done
```

Timing:

```text
variant          best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof70
baseline           1.853        44.17   63.1%
nooutbias          2.049        39.93   57.0%
nobias             2.046        40.01   57.2%
noround            3.589        22.80   32.6%
outnoround         1.856        44.10   63.0%
accgroup           1.883        43.46   62.1%
```

Resource usage:

```text
variant       REG  STACK  SHARED
baseline      242      0   98304
nooutbias     248      0   98304
nobias        254      0   94208
noround       255      0   98304
outnoround    242      0   98304
accgroup      242      0   98304
```

Static SASS counts:

```text
variant     total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
baseline    2718  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
nooutbias   2274  256  32  128  28  8   256  128  160  230  124   105  126 110  34
nobias      2022  256  0   128  12  8   256  96   160  181  113   91   118 80   39
noround     1679  128  80  64   34  8   128  128  80   252  119   131  123 0    35
outnoround  2576  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  278  168   175  180 55   35
accgroup    2759  256  96  128  44  8   256  192  160  370  166   175  189 116  35
```

Interpretation:

- Removing output bias or both biases drastically reduces static scalar work and
  LDG count, but runtime gets much worse. The reason is not instruction count;
  CUDA Tile codegen shifts register allocation upward:
  - `nooutbias`: `REG 242 -> 248`
  - `nobias`: `REG 242 -> 254`
- `noround` is a compiler/codegen cliff. It halves static HMMA/LDSM counts and
  becomes extremely slow. This confirms that the BF16 hidden rounding point is
  not optional in the current source-style schedule.
- `outnoround` keeps the same HMMA/staging structure and same register count,
  so it remains effectively tied with baseline. This matches the current source
  default behavior.
- `accgroup` keeps the same high-level structure but increases scalar address
  work (`IMAD 350 -> 370`) and loses about `1.6%`.
- Therefore the FFN12 gap is not dominated by output bias, hidden bias, or a
  removable scalar epilogue. Seemingly smaller SASS can still be slower if the
  generated register/resource shape moves toward the A10G occupancy cliff.

Decision:

- Do not promote no-bias/no-output-bias/accgroup/noround variants.
- Keep source default on baseline/out-no-round style.
- Continue FFN12 work at the W1/W2 tile schedule and residency level, not by
  removing bias/rounding epilogue operations.

## 2026-06-04 - FFN12 Pair-H32/TK64 W1 rank-3 MMA negative probe

Goal: test whether CUDA Tile rank-3 batched `ct::mma` can express the two
pair-H32 W1 MMA operations more profitably than two explicit rank-2 MMA calls.
The new candidates-only variant keeps the same useful math, shape, output split,
TK64, bias, rounding, and GELU path:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1batched2
```

It reshapes the shared `A[32,64]` tile to `[1,32,64]`, concatenates the two
`W1[64,32]` tiles into `[2,64,32]`, runs one rank-3 `ct::mma`, then extracts the
two `[32,32]` hidden accumulators before the unchanged GELU and W2 work.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1batched2 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
variant        best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof70  checksum
baseline         1.855        44.12   63.0%  4080.0000
w1batched2       4.132        19.81   28.3%  4080.0000
```

Resource usage:

```text
variant      REG  STACK  SHARED
baseline     242      0   98304
w1batched2   255     64   98304
```

Static SASS counts:

```text
variant     total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
baseline    2761  256  96  128  44  1   256  160  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
w1batched2  2337  128  80  16   84  1   128  112  80   351  120   170  84  377  68
```

Interpretation:

- CUDA Tile rank-3 `ct::mma` is accepted by the CUDA 13.3 compiler here, but it
  is a severe codegen cliff for this FFN12 W1 pair-H32 shape.
- The useful math is unchanged and the checksum matches, but generated Tensor
  Core structure is not preserved: static HMMA drops from `256` to `128`, LDSM
  drops from `128` to `16`, and runtime falls to only `28.3%` of the 70 TF/s
  A10G dense BF16 reference.
- The compiler also pushes the kernel to `REG=255` and introduces `STACK=64`.
  That is worse than the already tight baseline `REG=242, STACK=0`.
- The huge `PRMT` and `ISETP` increase is consistent with the extra
  reshape/cat/extract bookkeeping not being folded into the same efficient
  staging pattern as the explicit rank-2 W1 MMA sequence.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1batched2` as a candidates-only
  negative result.
- Do not replace explicit pair-H32 W1 MMA with rank-3 batched `ct::mma` in the
  source FFN12 path.
- The bottom-cause evidence is now narrower: the FFN12 gap is not solved by
  expressing the existing pair-H32 W1 work as batched tile syntax. The next
  structural probes should target a different W2 accumulation/residency
  organization or an entirely different CTA decomposition, while preserving the
  baseline's 256-HMMA rank-2 lowering.

## 2026-06-04 - FFN12 Pair-H32/TK64 W2 rank-3 MMA negative probe

Goal: test the other obvious rank-3 CUDA Tile expression: keep W1 as the
current explicit pair-H32 rank-2 MMA sequence, but combine the two W2 output
halves into a batched rank-3 MMA accumulator. The candidates-only variant is:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2batched2
```

For each hidden tile it reshapes `hidden[32,32]` to `[1,32,32]`, concatenates
the two W2 half tiles into `[2,32,128]`, accumulates into `[2,32,128]`, then
extracts the two output halves before the unchanged bias/store epilogue.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2batched2 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
variant        best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof70  checksum
baseline         1.853        44.16   63.1%  4080.0000
w2batched2       2.298        35.61   50.9%  4080.0000
```

Resource usage:

```text
variant      REG  STACK  SHARED
baseline     242      0   98304
w2batched2   250      0   90112
```

Static SASS counts:

```text
variant     total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
baseline    2761  256  96  128  44  1   256  160  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
w2batched2  3409  256  104 96   100 1   256  160  160  600  167   207  163 348  55
```

Interpretation:

- Unlike the W1 batched probe, W2 batched preserves the static `256` HMMA
  structure. It is not a Tensor Core count failure.
- It still loses about `24%` runtime versus baseline. The resource trade is bad
  for A10G: shared memory drops by `8192` bytes, but registers rise
  `242 -> 250`, moving closer to the occupancy cliff.
- Static instruction shape explains the slowdown: `IMAD 350 -> 600`,
  `PRMT 115 -> 348`, `ISETP 35 -> 55`, and `STS 44 -> 100`. The rank-3
  accumulator and reshape/cat/extract path introduce substantially more address
  and permutation work than the two explicit rank-2 output-half MMAs.
- Therefore the problem is not that the two W2 halves need to be expressed as a
  batched tile. CUDA Tile can keep the HMMA count, but the generated non-HMMA
  schedule gets worse.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2batched2` as a candidates-only
  negative result.
- Do not replace the source FFN12 W2 half-output rank-2 MMA sequence with
  rank-3 batched `ct::mma`.
- The next useful FFN12 work should avoid `cat/reshape/extract` around the
  accumulator hot loop and instead change CTA decomposition or W2 residency
  while preserving the explicit rank-2 HMMA lowering.

## 2026-06-04 - FFN12 hidden-split BF16 partial-output negative probe

Goal: test a real CTA-decomposition alternative instead of another rank-3
expression rewrite. The candidates split the hidden dimension across multiple
CTA groups, compute W1+GELU+W2 partial outputs for each hidden group, write BF16
partials to global memory, then reduce the partials and add output bias in a
second CUDA Tile kernel.

Variants:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_hsplit2_partial
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_hsplit4_partial
```

These do not recompute FFN1 hidden work: the hidden groups partition the 1024
columns. They do, however, add global partial output traffic and one extra
kernel launch.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_hsplit2_partial \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_hsplit4_partial \
  --warmup 10 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
variant          launches  best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof70  checksum
baseline                1    1.858        44.06   62.9%  4080.0000
hsplit2_partial         2    2.300        35.58   50.8%  4096.0000
hsplit4_partial         2    2.608        31.38   44.8%  4096.0000
```

Resource usage:

```text
kernel                 REG  STACK  SHARED
baseline               242      0   98304
hsplit2 partial        254      0   98304
hsplit2 reduce         150      0   49152
hsplit4 partial        254      0   98304
hsplit4 reduce         163      0   49152
```

Static SASS counts:

```text
kernel           total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
baseline          2761  256  96  128  44  1   256  160  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
hsplit2 partial   2337  256  32  128  28  1   256  96   160  262  131   115  128 103  51
hsplit2 reduce    1305  0    70  0    64  1   0    144  0    267  75    80   83  100  39
hsplit4 partial   2337  256  32  128  28  1   256  96   160  262  131   115  128 103  51
hsplit4 reduce    1265  0    68  0    64  1   0    192  0    301  72    74   85  136  40
```

Interpretation:

- Hidden splitting does not achieve the intended resource relief. The partial
  compute kernel is worse than baseline on the limiting resource:
  `REG 242 -> 254`, while shared memory stays at `98304`.
- The extra reduce kernel is not free: it adds a second launch, global BF16
  partial reads/writes, `49152` bytes shared memory, and substantial scalar
  address work.
- Splitting more finely makes runtime worse: hsplit4 is slower than hsplit2
  because the reduce/global-partial overhead grows and the partial kernel still
  does not become more resident.
- The checksum shift (`4080 -> 4096`) is expected for this risky BF16 partial
  experiment because partial sums are rounded to BF16 before the reduce.

Decision:

- Keep hsplit2/hsplit4 BF16 partial-output as candidates-only negative results.
- Do not move the source FFN12 path to hidden-split global partials.
- This rules out a simple CTA hidden-partition strategy for A10G. The next FFN12
  work should keep the full hidden loop in one CTA and look for ways to lower
  register pressure without global partial output traffic, or move to a
  fundamentally different rank-2 tile shape that still compiles to 256-HMMA
  style lowering.

## 2026-06-04 - FFN12 current rank-2 shape refresh

Goal: refresh the existing rank-2 CUDA Tile shape candidates on the current
source tree after the recent FFN12 negative probes. This sweep does not add new
code. It checks whether any already-existing rank-2 shape beats the current
M32/H32/TK64 pair-H32 baseline under the same CUDA 13.3/A10G conditions.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile -j8

for v in \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  fused_m16_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  fused_h16_poly9_split2_pairh16_tk64 \
  fused_h64_poly9_split2_pairh64_tk64 \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk16; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_cutile --variant "$v" --warmup 10 --iters 300
done
```

Timing:

```text
variant          best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof70
M32 H32 TK64       1.852        44.19   63.1%
M16 H32 TK64       2.899        28.23   40.3%
M32 H16 TK64       2.606        31.40   44.9%
M32 H64 TK64       2.525        32.41   46.3%
M32 H32 TK16       3.134        26.11   37.3%
```

Resource usage:

```text
variant       REG  STACK  SHARED
M32 H32 TK64  242      0   98304
M16 H32 TK64  244      0   96256
M32 H16 TK64  197      0  100352
M32 H64 TK64  255      0   94208
M32 H32 TK16  252      0   96256
```

Static SASS counts:

```text
variant       total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
```
