```text
M32 H32 TK64  2761  256  96  128  44  1   256  160  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
M16 H32 TK64  2385  192  56  144  46  1   192  104  120  296  178   172  125 142  34
M32 H16 TK64  2113  128  80  80   44  1   128  112  80   314  160   180  142 120  31
M32 H64 TK64  2457  256  96  96   44  1   256  160  160  209  119   95   163 137  57
M32 H32 TK16  2497  128  80  64   34  1   128  112  80   564  223   127  186 49   71
```

Interpretation:

- The current M32/H32/TK64 baseline remains the only tested rank-2 shape that
  combines acceptable register pressure with the full 256-HMMA lowering.
- H16 is the tempting low-register shape (`REG=197`), but it halves static HMMA
  to `128`, raises shared memory to `100352`, and loses about `29%` runtime.
- H64 preserves `256` HMMA, but it hits `REG=255`, so the lower shared memory
  does not translate into throughput.
- M16 and TK16 both disturb the Tensor Core/staging structure enough to lose
  badly. TK16 also explodes scalar address work (`IMAD=564`, `ISETP=71`).

Decision:

- Keep source default on M32/H32/TK64 pair-H32.
- Do not promote existing M16, H16, H64, or TK16 rank-2 shapes.
- The next FFN12 experiment should not only shrink one dimension. A viable new
  shape must preserve 256-HMMA lowering while reducing register pressure below
  the M32/H32/TK64 baseline, otherwise A10G throughput falls off immediately.

## 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32 large-TK negative probe

Goal: test whether a larger W1 input-K tile can reduce loop/address overhead
without changing the rank-2 CUDA Tile structure. The existing TK128 candidate
was refreshed, and a new candidates-only TK256 instantiation was added:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk256
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

for v in \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk128 \
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk256; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
    --variant "$v" --warmup 10 --iters 300
done
```

Timing:

```text
variant  best_ms  useful_TF/s  roof70
TK64       1.856        44.11   63.0%
TK128      2.443        33.50   47.9%
TK256      2.931        27.92   39.9%
```

Resource usage:

```text
variant  REG  STACK  SHARED
TK64     242      0   98304
TK128    255     56   94208
TK256    255      0   94208
```

Static SASS counts:

```text
variant total HMMA LDG LDSM STS STG FFMA FADD FMUL IMAD IADD3 LOP3 SHF PRMT ISETP
TK64    2761  256  96  128  44  1   256  160  160  350  171   175  188 115  35
TK128   2537  256  96  128  44  1   256  160  160  226  120   106  186 88   35
TK256   1777  128  80  64   34  1   128  112  80   289  79    86   138 53   33
```

Interpretation:

- TK128 does reduce scalar address instructions and keeps `256` HMMA, but it
  pushes the kernel to `REG=255` plus `STACK=56`; the lower shared memory does
  not compensate for the A10G occupancy/codegen cliff.
- TK256 is worse: it reaches `REG=255` and loses the full Tensor Core lowering
  (`HMMA 256 -> 128`).
- Therefore larger W1 input-K tiles are not a path to the missing FFN12
  throughput. The current TK64 staging is still the best tested compromise.

Decision:

- Keep source default on TK64.
- Keep TK128 and TK256 as candidates-only negative results.
- Future FFN12 shape work should preserve the TK64 W1 staging unless a new
  structure can prove it keeps `REG < 242` and `HMMA=256`.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 `-maxrregcount` compiler-cap diagnostic

Goal:

- Test whether the current FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 CUDA Tile bottleneck can be
  improved by forcing a lower register cap at compile time.
- This is a compiler-level diagnostic only. It does not change source math,
  CUDA Tile expressions, or the inference path.

Builds:

```bash
cmake -S . -B build-rreg224 -G Ninja \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=86 \
  -DCMAKE_CUDA_FLAGS=-maxrregcount=224
ninja -C build-rreg224 bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

cmake -S . -B build-rreg160 -G Ninja \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=86 \
  -DCMAKE_CUDA_FLAGS=-maxrregcount=160
ninja -C build-rreg160 bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
```

The generated compile commands confirmed the flags were present:

```text
build-rreg224: -maxrregcount=224 ... --enable-tile
build-rreg160: -maxrregcount=160 ... --enable-tile
```

Timing:

```text
build-min baseline:
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 best=1.853 ms, useful=44.17 TF/s

build-rreg224:
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 best=1.855 ms, useful=44.11 TF/s

build-rreg160:
  fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 best=1.855 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s
```

Resource usage for the target instantiated kernel:

```text
build-min baseline: REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
build-rreg224:      REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
build-rreg160:      REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Interpretation:

- Traditional `-maxrregcount` does not change this CUDA Tile kernel's generated
  resource shape, even with an aggressive cap of `160`.
- The unchanged timing and unchanged `REG/STACK/SHARED` show this is not a
  hidden register-spill tradeoff; the cap is effectively not a useful control
  lever for this `--enable-tile` FFN12 kernel.
- This closes the "force occupancy with compiler register cap" path for the
  current FFN12 hotspot. Prior `cutile::hint(occupancy=2)` was also negative,
  so the remaining path must change CUDA Tile dataflow/source structure, not
  rely on compiler resource caps.

Decision:

- Do not add `-maxrregcount` to the project build.
- Do not create source selectors for this diagnostic.
- Continue FFN12 work by targeting the pair-H32/TK64 W1/W2 staging and shared
  memory footprint directly while preserving `HMMA=256`.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 BKN weight-layout diagnostic

Goal:

- Test whether FFN12 fused pair-H32/TK64 is losing performance because W1/W2
  still use `layout_left` tensor spans while the QKV/large-linear path benefits
  from BKN row-major weights.
- Keep this bench-only and logically equivalent: initialize separate BKN buffers
  with a CUDA Tile conversion kernel from the existing `layout_left` weights.

Implementation:

- Added `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_bkn` to
  `bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile`.
- The candidate keeps the same macro dataflow as the current baseline:
  `TM=32`, `THidden=32`, `TK=64`, split2 pair-H32, one launch, `HMMA=256`.
- Only the W1/W2 tensor_span layout changes from:
  `ct::tensor_span{w*_nt, ct::shape<K,N>{}, ct::layout_left{}}`
  to default row-major `ct::tensor_span{w*_bkn, ct::shape<K,N>{}}`.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_bkn --warmup 10 --iters 200
cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile | c++filt
cuobjdump --dump-sass build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile | c++filt
```

Timing:

```text
layout_left baseline:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s, checksum=4080.0000

BKN row-major:
  best=2.024 ms, median=2.029 ms, useful=40.44 TF/s, checksum=4080.0000
```

Resource usage:

```text
layout_left baseline: REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
BKN row-major:        REG=224 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

SASS count for the two target kernels:

```text
layout_left baseline:
  HMMA=256, LDSM=128, LDGSTS=80, LDG=96, STS=44, STG=8
  IMAD=350, IADD3=171, PRMT=115, SHF=188, LOP3=175, BAR=27

BKN row-major:
  HMMA=256, LDSM=128, LDGSTS=80, LDG=96, STS=44, STG=8
  IMAD=325, IADD3=178, PRMT=101, SHF=195, LOP3=183, BAR=30, R2UR=17
```

Interpretation:

- BKN is slower despite reducing registers from `242` to `224`.
- This proves the current FFN12 bottleneck is not fixed by register reduction
  alone. `SHARED=98304` remains unchanged, so lower registers do not buy more
  CTA residency for this kernel.
- The Tensor Core work is unchanged (`HMMA=256`) and the broad load/store counts
  are unchanged, so the slowdown is not from doing less math or issuing fewer
  tensor operations.
- The BKN layout introduces a different CUDA Tile codegen shape (`BAR +3`,
  `R2UR +17`, more `SHF/LOP3`) and likely gives the compiler a worse operand
  staging/synchronization path for this pair-H32 fused FFN than `layout_left`.
  Inference: for this kernel, the current column-major logical B operand better
  matches the generated MMA staging path even though BKN helps separate QKV.

Decision:

- Do not promote BKN row-major W1/W2 into the FFN12 fused production path.
- Keep the candidate as a recorded negative diagnostic.
- Next FFN12 work should target the real limiter: `SHARED=98304` and the pair-H32
  staging structure, not W1/W2 row-major conversion.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 output-half sequential diagnostic

Goal:

- Test a narrower shared-memory hypothesis after the BKN result: reduce the
  number of simultaneously live W2/output-half staging objects by computing
  output half 0 and output half 1 sequentially inside the same CTA.
- Unlike the existing `halfout` candidate, this keeps one CTA per M tile and
  writes both output halves in one kernel invocation. It still recomputes the
  W1/GELU hidden path for the second output half, so it is a diagnostic rather
  than an expected production path.

Implementation:

- Added `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outseq` to
  `bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile`.
- It preserves `TM=32`, `THidden=32`, `TK=64`, pair-H32, and `layout_left`
  W1/W2. The only structural change is a loop over the two output halves around
  the hidden-pair loop.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_halfout --warmup 10 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outseq --warmup 10 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.856 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s, actual=44.10 TF/s

halfout:
  best=3.389 ms, median=3.392 ms, useful=24.15 TF/s, actual=36.22 TF/s

outseq:
  best=3.185 ms, median=3.191 ms, useful=25.69 TF/s, actual=38.54 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline: REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
halfout:  REG=234 STACK=0 SHARED=94208
outseq:   REG=238 STACK=0 SHARED=94208
```

SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  HMMA=256, LDSM=128, LDGSTS=80, LDG=96, STS=44, STG=8
  FFMA=256, BAR=27

halfout, per CTA:
  HMMA=192, LDSM=112, LDGSTS=64, LDG=64, STS=32, STG=4
  FFMA=256, BAR=21

outseq:
  HMMA=384, LDSM=224, LDGSTS=128, LDG=128, STS=64, STG=8
  FFMA=512, BAR=43
```

Interpretation:

- `outseq` is better than two-CTA `halfout` (`3.185 ms` vs `3.389 ms`), so
  avoiding the doubled CTA grid helps slightly.
- It is still far worse than the baseline because the resource reduction is too
  small: static shared memory drops by only `4096 B` while the Tensor Core work
  grows from `HMMA=256` to `HMMA=384`.
- The actual-throughput view is also negative: `38.54 TF/s` actual for `outseq`
  versus `44.10 TF/s` for the baseline. Even after accounting for extra math,
  sequential output-half scheduling produces a worse CUDA Tile schedule.

Decision:

- Do not promote output-half sequentialization.
- Do not repeat output-half splitting as the main FFN12 strategy unless a future
  variant avoids W1/GELU recompute and reduces shared memory by far more than
  `4 KiB`.
- The remaining FFN12 path needs a different W2 residency/dataflow change that
  preserves the baseline `HMMA=256` work count.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 W2 split-span diagnostic

Goal:

- Test whether the FFN12 W2 half-output loads are affected by expressing the two
  output halves as separate CUDA Tile tensor spans.
- Unlike `halfout`/`outseq`, this preserves the baseline work count:
  one CTA per M tile, one W1/GELU pass, and `HMMA=256`.

Implementation:

- Added `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2splitspan` to
  `bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile`.
- The candidate replaces one W2 view over `[hidden, 256]` with two views over
  `[hidden, 128]`:
  - `w2_view0` starts at W2 output column 0.
  - `w2_view1` starts at W2 output column 128.
- W1, hidden epilogue, output accumulators, output stores, and math are
  otherwise unchanged.

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.11 TF/s, checksum=4080.0000

w2splitspan:
  best=1.856 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s, checksum=4080.0000
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline:    REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
w2splitspan: REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  lines=5524, HMMA=256, LDSM=128, LDGSTS=80, LDG=96, STS=44, STG=8
  IMAD=350, IADD3=171, PRMT=115, SHF=188, LOP3=175, FFMA=256, BAR=27

w2splitspan:
  lines=5524, HMMA=256, LDSM=128, LDGSTS=80, LDG=96, STS=44, STG=8
  IMAD=350, IADD3=171, PRMT=115, SHF=188, LOP3=175, FFMA=256, BAR=27
```

Interpretation:

- The split-span source expression is compiled into the same resource shape and
  same broad SASS instruction mix as the baseline.
- Therefore the FFN12 W2 bottleneck is not caused by the single `[hidden, 256]`
  tensor_span abstraction or by the `tile_n=0/1` partition index.
- Future W2 work must change the actual MMA/staging schedule, not just split the
  view metadata.

Decision:

- Keep `w2splitspan` as a candidates-only equivalence diagnostic.
- Do not promote it into source; it is effectively the same codegen as baseline.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 W2 manual-offset load diagnostic

Goal:

- Test whether W2 `partition_view.load()` is hiding overhead or forcing a poor
  staging schedule in the current FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 kernel.
- Preserve the baseline work count and math while replacing only W2 tile loads
  with manual `ct::load(w2 + offset_tile)` expressions.

Implementation:

- Added `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2manual` to
  `bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile`.
- W1 still uses the baseline `partition_view`.
- W2 offsets are generated explicitly from a `[THidden, OutHalf]` index tile:
  `hidden_base + row + (out_half + col) * kHidden`.

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s, checksum=4080.0000

w2manual:
  best=3.724 ms, median=3.731 ms, useful=21.98 TF/s, checksum=4080.0000
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline: REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
w2manual: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  lines=5524, HMMA=256, LDSM=128, LDGSTS=80, LDG=96, STS=44, STG=8
  IMAD=350, IADD3=171, PRMT=115, SHF=188, LOP3=175, FFMA=256, BAR=27

w2manual:
  lines=4932, HMMA=128, LDSM=48, LDGSTS=24, LDG=208, STS=66, STG=8
  IMAD=555, IADD3=245, PRMT=175, SHF=101, LOP3=201, FFMA=128, BAR=26
```

Interpretation:

- Manual W2 offsets are much worse: runtime roughly doubles and registers hit
  the `255` cliff while static shared memory stays at `98304`.
- The instruction mix shows why: explicit offset tiles replace the compiler's
  structured W2 staging with far more scalar global loads and address math
  (`LDG 96 -> 208`, `IMAD 350 -> 555`), while reducing the optimized
  `LDGSTS/LDSM/HMMA` structure.
- Therefore W2 `partition_view.load()` is not the source of overhead here. It is
  helping CUDA Tile generate the better staging path.

Decision:

- Keep `w2manual` as a candidates-only negative diagnostic.
- Do not replace W2 `partition_view.load()` with manual offset-tile loads in
  source.
- Future FFN12 work should preserve structured W2 loads unless it changes the
  actual MMA decomposition with evidence that `HMMA` quality and register
  pressure are maintained.

### 2026-06-04 - Current FP32 island audit after FFN12 W2 diagnostics

Goal:

- Re-check where FP32 remains in the CUDA Tile inference path after the BF16
  storage/epilogue experiments.
- Avoid repeating already-closed "make `ct::mma` accumulator BF16" work.

Evidence from CUDA Tile 13.3 header:

```text
/usr/local/cuda/include/crt/cuda_tile.h

medium_precision_mma_v:
  (tf32 || bfloat16 || float32 input) && float32 accumulator

matmul_element_result<__nv_bfloat16>:
  type = float
```

This matches the earlier FFN12 BF16-accumulator compile failure. Under the
project constraint "CUDA Tile first, no raw WMMA, no inline PTX", BF16 input MMA
with BF16 accumulator is not exposed by the public CUDA Tile API on this stack.

Current FP32 island classes in `src`:

1. **Hard CUDA Tile MMA accumulator constraint**

   - FFN12/linear/QKV/attention Tensor Core kernels use BF16 input tiles and
     `ct::tile<float,...>` accumulators.
   - This is required by CUDA Tile `mma_compatible`, not just a conservative
     project choice.
   - These should not be counted as avoidable BF16/FP32 conversion kernels.

2. **Attention softmax and online row state**

   - Time/freq attention still has FP32 score tiles, row max/sum state, and
     output accumulation tiles.
   - Prior BF16-score/BF16-rounding probes showed quality/runtime tradeoffs, but
     the exact softmax path still needs FP32 row state unless a deliberately
     approximate attention path is selected.

3. **Rotary trig tables**

   - Default `--bf16` path stores rotary `cos_freqs/sin_freqs` as BF16 when
     `rotary_freqs_bf16_enabled()` is active.
   - The default standalone time split-contig rotary path dispatches BF16 trig
     kernels when the tensors are BF16.
   - Some opt-in/negative paths still force F32 trig, including:
     - time QKV fused-rotary producer
     - time QKV pair-rotary producer
     - time q-rotary-in-attention path
   - These are not current default-path conversion gaps; they are experimental
     paths that were kept opt-in or negative.

4. **STFT/ISTFT/audio overlap-add**

   - `src/stft_cuda_tile.cu`, `src/chunk_cuda_tile.cu`, and WAV IO operate on
     Float32 audio/spectrogram tensors.
   - This is outside the BF16 Tensor Core throughput target. It is a separate
     audio-quality and signal-processing experiment, not a path to the 70 TF/s
     dense BF16 objective.

5. **Utility conversion kernels and generic Tensor fallbacks**

   - `tensor_cuda_tile.cu` / `tensor_cuda_glue.cu` still expose Float32 generic
     ops and conversion kernels because tensors, WAV IO, STFT, and fallback
     utility paths still need them.
   - These are not evidence that the hot neural path is defaulting to FP32.

Decision:

- Do not repeat BF16 accumulator experiments for `ct::mma`; the API/header and
  prior compile failure are sufficient evidence.
- Do not prioritize BF16 trig support for opt-in qrot/fused-rotary paths until
  one of those paths becomes performance-relevant again.
- Continue performance work on hot default CUDA Tile kernels by changing
  staging/fusion/dataflow while accepting FP32 Tensor Core accumulation where
  CUDA Tile requires it.

### 2026-06-04 - Large linear/BKN default-path audit

Goal:

- Re-check whether any already-tested large linear/QKV/FFN CUDA Tile path has
  positive evidence but is still hidden behind an opt-in switch.
- Avoid spending the next A10G work cycle on macro-shape or BKN layout variants
  that are already explained by source and benchmark evidence.

Current source behavior:

```text
linear_bkn_long_path_enabled:
  default-on under --bf16, rollback CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_BKN_LONG=1

linear_bkn_qkv_tk16_enabled:
  default-on under --bf16, rollback CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_BKN_QKV_TK16=1

time_qkv_split_contig_path_enabled:
  default-on under --bf16, rollback CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_QKV_SPLIT_CONTIG_PATH=1

linear_qkv_m32n128_enabled / linear_qkv_m32n256_enabled:
  default-on under --bf16, rollback CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N128=1
  and CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_QKV_M32N256=1

attention_out_residual_enabled:
  default-on under --bf16, rollback CUDASEP_DISABLE_ATTENTION_OUT_RESIDUAL=1

linear_bkn_ffn_long_path_enabled:
  opt-in only, CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_BKN_FFN_LONG=1

linear_padded_bias_no_round_enabled:
  opt-in only, CUDASEP_ENABLE_LINEAR_PADDED_BIAS_NO_ROUND=1
```

Source audit:

- `linear_bkn_long_path_enabled()` is already the default BF16 path, so the
  long QKV B(K,N) weight layout is not a missed promotion.
- `linear_bkn_qkv_tk16_enabled()` is already default-on, so the best tested
  QKV BKN macro shape remains `M32 x N256 x K16`.
- `linear_bkn_ffn_long_path_enabled()` intentionally stays opt-in. When enabled,
  the model loader creates `linear1_w_bkn/linear2_w_bkn`; `apply_feedforward()`
  then skips the current FFN12 fused kernel because it only calls
  `try_feedforward_fused()` when both BKN FFN weights are empty. This is not a
  pure layout swap: it gives up the stronger fused FFN dataflow.
- `linear_padded_bias_no_round_enabled()` intentionally stays opt-in. The prior
  A/B showed only about `0.16 ms` combined Nsight kernel-group gain with
  measurable output drift, so it is a BF16-purity probe rather than a proven
  performance default.

Evidence summary:

- Long QKV BKN default was already promoted after a small positive long-input
  result: `test.wav` improved from `6025.3 ms` to `6007.6 ms`, with output drift
  around `145 dB` SNR.
- QKV BKN recheck keeps `M32 x N256 x TK16` as the best tested A10G producer
  shape, around `44 TF/s`; TK8/TK32/TK64, M16/M64, N128/N512, compound N,
  high latency hints, pair-K, and explicit-load spelling are all neutral or
  negative.
- FFN BKN is negative in both source structure and direct bench evidence. The
  FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 BKN diagnostic slowed from `1.855 ms` to `2.024 ms`
  despite lowering registers from `242` to `224`; shared memory stayed at
  `98304`, and SASS showed extra barriers/register-move style overhead.
- FFN1/FFN2 N-widening remains opt-in/negative: FFN1 `M32xN128` slightly
  regressed, while FFN2 `M32xN128` raised registers from `152` to `226` and
  strongly regressed.
- Attention output residual epilogue was already promoted because it removes a
  standalone add without crossing the same register cliff as FFN12. Its `TN=64`
  alternative remains negative; `TN=128` stays default.

Decision:

- No source promotion is warranted from this audit.
- Do not keep sweeping legal QKV/FFN BKN macro shapes as the next default-path
  task. The repeated limiter is the CUDA Tile generated staging/resource shape:
  large QKV and FFN12 best variants still consume about `96KB` shared memory per
  CTA, with high register pressure, so A10G cannot hide latency like a dense
  library GEMM.
- The next useful A10G work should target lower-level staging/dataflow changes:
  reduce the `96KB` shared-memory footprint, preserve coalesced QKV stores while
  removing a consumer round trip, or split/fuse only where the producer does not
  hit the FFN12-style register cliff.

### 2026-06-04 - QKV BKN low-latency staging hint promotion

Goal:

- Move beyond legal macro-shape sweeps for QKV BKN and test a lower-level CUDA
  Tile staging hypothesis: the default generated schedule may be using too much
  prefetch/register headroom for this `M32 x N256 x TK16` A10G shape.
- Use the official CUDA Tile `cutile::hint(... latency=...)` call-site hint on
  the load/store sites, while keeping the same BF16 input, `ct::mma`, output
  layout, and macro tile shape.

Bench implementation:

- Added `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat1` and `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat2` to
  `bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm`.
- Added `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2` for the source-relevant default
  time-QKV producer layout.
- The variants keep `M32 x N256 x TK16`, `HMMA=256`, `LDSM=96`, `LDGSTS=80`,
  `STS=88`, and the same BF16 checksum as baseline.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn --warmup 10 --iters 50
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_lat2 --warmup 10 --iters 50
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig --warmup 10 --iters 50
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 --warmup 10 --iters 50
```

Bench timing:

```text
plain BKN baseline:
  best=1.382 ms, median=1.405 ms, 44.42 TF/s, roof70=63.5%

plain BKN latency=2:
  best=1.237 ms, median=1.299 ms, 49.62 TF/s, roof70=70.9%

split-contig baseline:
  best=1.399 ms, median=1.407 ms, 43.87 TF/s, roof70=62.7%

split-contig latency=2:
  best=1.246 ms, median=1.290 ms, 49.26 TF/s, roof70=70.4%
```

Resource evidence:

```text
plain BKN baseline:       REG=170 SHARED=98304
plain BKN latency=2:      REG=163 SHARED=98304
split-contig baseline:    REG=174 SHARED=98304
split-contig latency=2:   REG=165 SHARED=98304
```

SASS evidence for plain BKN:

```text
baseline:
  HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=8 IMAD=72 IADD3=75
  PRMT=2 SHF=61 LOP3=77 F2F=32

latency=2:
  HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=8 IMAD=72 IADD3=75
  PRMT=2 SHF=61 LOP3=77 F2F=32
```

Source promotion:

- `linear_cutile_static_full_bkn_bf16_kernel` now supports a narrow
  `LoadLatency` template parameter. The default remains `0`; only the
  no-bias/no-GELU QKV BKN `M32 x N256 x TK16` launch passes `2`.
- `qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel` now supports the same
  `LoadLatency` template parameter, and the default time-QKV split-contiguous
  producer passes `2`.
- Rollbacks still use the existing switches:
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_LINEAR_BKN_QKV_TK16=1` for the generic long-QKV BKN TK16
  source path, and `CUDASEP_DISABLE_TIME_QKV_SPLIT_CONTIG_PATH=1` for the
  split-contiguous time-QKV path.

Validation:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/qkv_split_contig_lat2_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav --output outputs/qkv_split_contig_lat2_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys profile --force-overwrite=true --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none -t cuda \
  -o bench/nsys_qkv_split_contig_lat2_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/nsys_qkv_split_contig_lat2_clean_audio \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys stats --report cuda_gpu_kern_sum bench/nsys_qkv_split_contig_lat2_clean.nsys-rep
```

Validation timing:

```text
test_clean.wav:
  inference=916.3 ms

test.wav:
  inference=5390.6 ms

Nsight test_clean.wav:
  inference=933.3 ms
```

Nsight kernel evidence:

```text
qkv_bkn_split_contig_static_full_kernel:
  before latency=2 source promotion: 68.997 ms / 48 calls, avg=1.437 ms
  after  latency=2 source promotion: 61.282 ms / 48 calls, avg=1.277 ms

linear_cutile_static_full_bkn_bf16_kernel generic QKV group:
  after latency=2 source promotion: 64.906 ms / 48 calls, avg=1.352 ms
```

Interpretation:

- The bottleneck was not Tensor Core fallback: HMMA count and the broad
  load/store instruction mix are unchanged.
- The positive change is lower register pressure and a better generated CUDA
  Tile schedule for this high-shared-memory CTA. `SHARED=98304` still caps CTA
  residency, but reducing registers from `174 -> 165` for the source-relevant
  split-contig producer improves the A10G schedule enough to lift the producer
  from about `44 TF/s` to about `49 TF/s` in the standalone bench.
- Prior "latency hint is negative" evidence was specifically for higher values
  such as `4/7/10`. Low latency hints are a distinct positive regime for this
  QKV shape.

Decision:

- Keep `LoadLatency=2` as the default for both QKV BKN TK16 producer forms.
- Do not generalize this to FFN, attention output, or biased linears without
  separate bench evidence. The benefit is shape- and codegen-specific.
- The remaining QKV gap to the local dense BF16 reference is now smaller but
  still real: the kernel still uses about `96KB` shared memory per CTA. Next QKV
  work should target reducing that shared-memory footprint or fusing a consumer
  without losing coalesced split-contiguous stores.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 low-latency staging hint probe

Goal:

- Test whether the low `cutile::hint(... latency=2)` staging result that helped
  QKV also helps the largest remaining Tensor Core hotspot:
  `ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel`.
- Keep the experiment narrow: same `M32`, pair-H32, `TK64`, poly9 GELU, output
  no-round default, one launch, and the same BF16 checksum.

Bench variants:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2lat2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 20 --iters 300
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat2 --warmup 10 --iters 100
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w2lat2 --warmup 10 --iters 100
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.859 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s

W1 latency=2:
  best=3.320 ms, median=3.323 ms, useful=24.65 TF/s

W2 latency=2:
  best=2.159 ms, median=2.207 ms, useful=37.90 TF/s

W1+W2 latency=2:
  best=1.810 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.22 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline:        REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1 latency=2:    REG=253 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W2 latency=2:    REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1+W2 latency=2: REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
```

SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDGSTS=80 LDG=96 STS=124 STG=8
  IMAD=352 IADD3=177 PRMT=115 SHF=188 LOP3=175 BAR=54 R2UR=0 F2F=96 FFMA=256

W1 latency=2:
  HMMA=128 LDSM=64 LDGSTS=40 LDG=80 STS=74 STG=8
  IMAD=342 IADD3=112 PRMT=48 SHF=144 LOP3=118 BAR=38 R2UR=0 F2F=80 FFMA=128

W2 latency=2:
  HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDGSTS=80 LDG=96 STS=124 STG=8
  IMAD=329 IADD3=156 PRMT=120 SHF=191 LOP3=148 BAR=63 R2UR=0 F2F=96 FFMA=256

W1+W2 latency=2:
  HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDGSTS=80 LDG=96 STS=124 STG=8
  IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 BAR=64 R2UR=18 F2F=96 FFMA=256
```

Interpretation:

- FFN12 does not behave like QKV. QKV latency=2 improved by lowering register
  pressure; FFN12 W1+W2 latency=2 is faster despite worse resources
  (`REG=255`, `STACK=8`).
- W1-only latency=2 is invalid as an optimization path because codegen loses
  half the Tensor Core work in the counted SASS slice (`HMMA=256 -> 128`).
- W2-only latency=2 keeps HMMA but is slower. The useful regime is specifically
  W1+W2 together, where integer/indexing instructions drop enough to offset the
  extra barriers/R2UR/register pressure.
- This is therefore a narrow codegen scheduling result, not a generic
  "lower latency hint always helps" rule.

Source promotion:

- `ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel` now has a `MemoryLatency`
  template parameter. Default remains `0`.
- The default non-residual `pairH32/TK64` FFN12 source path now uses
  `MemoryLatency=2`.
- Rollback:

```bash
CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_LAT2=1
```

Validation:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/ffn12_lat2_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_LAT2=1 ./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/ffn12_lat2_rollback_clean \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test.wav --output outputs/ffn12_lat2_test \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys profile --force-overwrite=true --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none -t cuda \
  -o bench/nsys_ffn12_lat2_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/nsys_ffn12_lat2_clean_audio \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_LAT2=1 \
nsys profile --force-overwrite=true --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none -t cuda \
  -o bench/nsys_ffn12_lat2_rollback_clean \
  ./build-min/cudasep_infer --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav --output outputs/nsys_ffn12_lat2_rollback_clean_audio \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1
```

Runtime:

```text
test_clean.wav default latency=2:
  inference=908.6 ms

test_clean.wav rollback:
  inference=915.7 ms

test.wav default latency=2:
  inference=5463.6 ms

Nsight default latency=2:
  inference=926.8 ms

Nsight rollback:
  inference=932.0 ms
```

Nsight kernel evidence:

```text
FFN12 main kernel, default latency=2:
  177.650 ms / 96 calls, avg=1.851 ms

FFN12 main kernel, rollback:
  182.742 ms / 96 calls, avg=1.904 ms
```

Decision:

- Keep FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 `MemoryLatency=2` default-on for now, with
  `CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_LAT2=1` rollback.
- Do not apply this to FFN12 residual, tail kernels, or other GEMM-like kernels
  without separate bench evidence.
- The remaining FFN12 limiter is still `SHARED=98304` and very high register
  pressure. The next FFN12 work should try to reduce the shared-memory footprint
  or change staging structure, not just add more hint values.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 quarter-output shared-memory probe

Goal:

- Test a direct shared-memory-pressure hypothesis for the FFN12 pair-H32/TK64
  kernel: shrink each output CTA from `32x128` or two `32x128` halves to one
  `32x64` quarter output tile.
- Keep this bench-only. The variant intentionally recomputes W1/GELU per output
  quarter, so it is a diagnostic for whether lower per-CTA resources can offset
  extra Tensor Core work and CTA scheduling.

Bench variants:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_halfout
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_quarterout
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_quarterout_w1w2lat2
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_halfout --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_quarterout --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_quarterout_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.856 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s, actual=44.10 TF/s

current latency=2 best:
  best=1.809 ms, useful=45.23 TF/s, actual=45.23 TF/s

halfout:
  best=3.390 ms, useful=24.14 TF/s, actual=36.21 TF/s

quarterout:
  best=5.396 ms, useful=15.17 TF/s, actual=37.91 TF/s

quarterout W1+W2 latency=2:
  best=5.535 ms, useful=14.79 TF/s, actual=36.97 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline:          REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
current lat2 best: REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
halfout:           REG=234 STACK=0 SHARED=94208
quarterout:        REG=214 STACK=0 SHARED=81920
quarterout lat2:   REG=232 STACK=0 SHARED=81920
```

Static SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  total=2760 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=86 IMAD=352 IADD3=177 PRMT=115 SHF=188 LOP3=175

halfout:
  total=2080 HMMA=192 LDSM=112 LDG=64 LDGSTS=64 STS=96 STG=4
  F2F=64 FFMA=256 BAR=77 IMAD=149 IADD3=109 PRMT=88 SHF=148 LOP3=104

quarterout:
  total=1976 HMMA=160 LDSM=104 LDG=48 LDGSTS=56 STS=88 STG=2
  F2F=48 FFMA=256 BAR=66 IMAD=155 IADD3=157 PRMT=58 SHF=128 LOP3=105

quarterout W1+W2 latency=2:
  total=1856 HMMA=160 LDSM=104 LDG=48 LDGSTS=56 STS=88 STG=2
  F2F=48 FFMA=256 BAR=73 IMAD=91 IADD3=102 PRMT=54 SHF=141 LOP3=100
```

Interpretation:

- Quarter-output does reduce the reported resource footprint substantially:
  `REG 242 -> 214` and `SHARED 98304 -> 81920`.
- That reduction is not enough. The useful throughput collapses because the
  variant launches four output CTAs and recomputes W1/GELU for each quarter.
  Its actual-throughput view is still below the baseline and below the current
  latency=2 default.
- The SASS confirms this is not just CTA overhead: the generated CUDA Tile
  Tensor Core shape changes from `HMMA=256` in the baseline to `HMMA=160` in the
  quarter-output kernel. Halfout similarly changes to `HMMA=192`.
- Lower shared memory alone is therefore an invalid success metric for FFN12.
  A useful next FFN12 direction must preserve the one-pass W1/GELU structure and
  the `HMMA=256` pair-H32/TK64 schedule while reducing staging/resource pressure.

Decision:

- Keep `quarterout` and `quarterout_w1w2lat2` as candidates-only negative
  diagnostics.
- Do not promote quarter-output splitting into the production FFN12 path.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 W1+W2 latency=2 accumulator-order probe

Goal:

- Test whether the previously negative `accgroup` output accumulation order
  becomes useful when combined with the current positive W1+W2 `latency=2`
  schedule.
- Keep the experiment narrow: same one CTA per M tile, same full `256` output
  columns, same one-pass W1/GELU structure, same rank-2 pair-H32/TK64 MMAs.

Bench variants:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_accgroup
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_accgroup
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_accgroup --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_accgroup --warmup 30 --iters 500
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.856 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s

accgroup:
  best=1.881 ms, median=1.888 ms, useful=43.51 TF/s

W1+W2 latency=2:
  best=1.809 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.23 TF/s

W1+W2 latency=2 + accgroup:
  best=1.810 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.22 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
W1+W2 latency=2:
  REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304

W1+W2 latency=2 + accgroup:
  REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
W1+W2 latency=2:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18

W1+W2 latency=2 + accgroup:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18
```

Interpretation:

- `accgroup` alone is still negative under the non-latency baseline.
- Under the current W1+W2 `latency=2` schedule, `accgroup` compiles to exactly
  the same resource usage and same static SASS count as the non-accgroup
  variant. The small `1.809 -> 1.810 ms` difference is measurement noise.
- This means CUDA Tile codegen has already canonicalized the accumulation order
  for this latency-hinted body, so accumulator ordering is not another useful
  optimization lever for the current FFN12 kernel.

Decision:

- Keep `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_accgroup` as a
  candidates-only neutral diagnostic.
- Do not promote it into source; the current production `W1+W2 latency=2`
  expression is already the same generated kernel.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 W1+W2 latency local sweep

Goal:

- Verify whether the current W1+W2 `latency=2` staging hint is actually a local
  optimum for the FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 body.
- Follow the CUDA Tile hint constraint that `latency` is a direct load/store
  optimization hint with valid values in `[1,10]`; test only adjacent low values
  because higher historical values already had negative evidence.

Bench variants:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat1
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat3
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 20 --iters 300
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat1 --warmup 20 --iters 300
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 300
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat3 --warmup 20 --iters 300
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.856 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.10 TF/s

W1+W2 latency=1:
  best=1.921 ms, median=1.943 ms, useful=42.61 TF/s

W1+W2 latency=2:
  best=1.810 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.22 TF/s

W1+W2 latency=3:
  best=3.284 ms, median=3.292 ms, useful=24.92 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
latency=1: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
latency=2: REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
latency=3: REG=254 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
latency=1:
  total=2808 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=95 IMAD=326 IADD3=180 PRMT=116 SHF=195 LOP3=171 R2UR=13

latency=2:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18

latency=3:
  total=1792 HMMA=128 LDSM=64 LDG=80 LDGSTS=40 STS=74 STG=8
  F2F=80 FFMA=128 BAR=52 IMAD=299 IADD3=114 PRMT=48 SHF=135 LOP3=116
```

Interpretation:

- `latency=2` is the local optimum among the adjacent low-latency hints.
- `latency=1` keeps the full `HMMA=256` Tensor Core shape, but it increases
  integer/permutation/control work versus `latency=2` (`IMAD 275 -> 326`,
  `PRMT 80 -> 116`, `LOP3 145 -> 171`) and loses runtime.
- `latency=3` is a codegen cliff: it drops to `HMMA=128`, `LDSM=64`, and
  `LDGSTS=40`, matching the large runtime regression.
- The best result is therefore not explained by lower register count or shared
  memory. It is a generated-schedule effect: `latency=2` preserves the full
  Tensor Core shape while reducing non-HMMA scalar/indexing work relative to
  `latency=1`.

Decision:

- Keep FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 W1+W2 `latency=2` as the only promoted hint value.
- Keep `latency=1` and `latency=3` as candidates-only negative diagnostics.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 split W1/W2 latency probe

Goal:

- Determine whether the positive W1+W2 `latency=2` result is driven primarily
  by W1-side staging, W2-side staging, or by both sides together.
- Split the body template into `MemoryLatency` for W1 loads and
  `W2MemoryLatency` for W2 loads. The new W2 latency parameter defaults to the
  old shared value, so existing variants keep identical behavior.

Bench variants:

```text
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat1       # W1=1, W2=1
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat1_w2lat2 # W1=1, W2=2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2       # W1=2, W2=2
fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat2_w2lat1 # W1=2, W2=1
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat1 --warmup 20 --iters 300
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat1_w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 300
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat2_w2lat1 --warmup 30 --iters 500
```

Timing:

```text
W1=1, W2=1:
  best=1.916 ms, median=1.943 ms, useful=42.72 TF/s

W1=1, W2=2:
  best=1.843 ms, median=1.996 ms, useful=44.41 TF/s

W1=2, W2=2:
  best=1.810 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.23 TF/s

W1=2, W2=1:
  best=1.814 ms, median=1.819 ms, useful=45.11 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
W1=1, W2=1: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1=1, W2=2: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1=2, W2=1: REG=255 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1=2, W2=2: REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
W1=1, W2=1:
  total=2808 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=95 IMAD=326 IADD3=180 PRMT=116 SHF=195 LOP3=171 R2UR=13

W1=1, W2=2:
  total=2792 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=94 IMAD=311 IADD3=163 PRMT=115 SHF=210 LOP3=146 R2UR=22

W1=2, W2=1:
  total=2760 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=97 IMAD=317 IADD3=150 PRMT=76 SHF=213 LOP3=152 R2UR=18

W1=2, W2=2:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18
```

Interpretation:

- W1-side `latency=2` is the main requirement. Moving W1 from `1 -> 2` with
  W2 fixed at `1` improves `1.916 -> 1.814 ms`, while moving only W2 from
  `1 -> 2` improves only to `1.843 ms`.
- W2-side `latency=2` still adds a small real improvement when W1 is already at
  `2`: `1.814 -> 1.810 ms`.
- The combined `2/2` variant is fastest despite being the only mixed low-latency
  variant with `STACK=8`. Therefore this is not a register/stack win. It is a
  generated schedule win: the `2/2` code has the lowest total static count and
  much lower `IMAD` (`317 -> 275`) than `2/1`, while preserving `HMMA=256`.

Decision:

- Keep production FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 on W1=2, W2=2.
- Keep split-latency variants as candidates-only diagnostics; no source change.

### 2026-06-04 - QKV split-contig A/B/store latency split diagnostic

Goal:

- Explain the bottom-level source of the positive QKV split-contiguous
  `latency=2` result without changing the production path.
- Split the CUDA Tile `cutile::hint(... latency=2)` sites into A-load, B-load,
  and store hints. This follows the CUDA Tile API semantics: the hint attaches
  to a specific load/store call site and is a compiler optimization hint, not a
  semantic or scheduling guarantee.

Bench variants:

```text
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig              # A=0, B=0, S=0
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2         # A=2, B=2, S=2
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b0_s0     # A-only
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a0_b2_s0     # B-only
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a0_b0_s2     # store-only
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b1_s2
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a1_b2_s2
qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b2_s0
```

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b0_s0 --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a0_b2_s0 --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a0_b0_s2 --warmup 30 --iters 500
```

Timing:

```text
A=0, B=0, S=0:
  best=1.421 ms, median=1.425 ms, 43.19 TF/s

A=2, B=2, S=2:
  best=1.293 ms, median=1.305 ms, 47.47 TF/s

A=2, B=0, S=0:
  best=1.415 ms, median=1.431 ms, 43.37 TF/s

A=0, B=2, S=0:
  best=1.364 ms, median=1.381 ms, 45.00 TF/s

A=0, B=0, S=2:
  best=1.421 ms, median=1.424 ms, 43.20 TF/s
```

Short 200-iter cross-check:

```text
A=2, B=2, S=2:
  best=1.245 ms, median=1.299 ms, 49.31 TF/s

A=2, B=1, S=2:
  best=1.245 ms, median=1.305 ms, 49.31 TF/s

A=1, B=2, S=2:
  best=1.289 ms, median=1.323 ms, 47.63 TF/s

A=2, B=2, S=0:
  best=1.244 ms, median=1.303 ms, 49.34 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
A=0, B=0, S=0: REG=174 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=2, S=2: REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=0, S=0: REG=178 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=0, B=2, S=0: REG=156 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=0, B=0, S=2: REG=174 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=1, S=2: REG=164 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=1, B=2, S=2: REG=166 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=2, S=0: REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
A=0, B=0, S=0:
  total=1088 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=2, S=2:
  total=1144 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=0, S=0:
  total=1104 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=84 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=0, B=2, S=0:
  total=1112 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=83 IMAD=144 IADD3=84 PRMT=1 SHF=64 LOP3=84

A=0, B=0, S=2:
  total=1088 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=1, S=2:
  total=1104 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=82 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=1, B=2, S=2:
  total=1136 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=81 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=2, S=0:
  total=1144 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79
```

Interpretation:

- The positive result is not a Tensor Core fallback issue. Every variant keeps
  `HMMA=256`, `LDSM=96`, `LDGSTS=80`, `STS=88`, and `STG=24`.
- It is also not explained by one independent call site:
  A-only is neutral/negative, store-only is neutral, and B-only gives only a
  small gain. The high-throughput family requires A and B load hints together;
  removing the store hint (`A=2,B=2,S=0`) does not hurt in the short cross-check.
- Register count alone is not a sufficient model. B-only has the lowest
  register count (`REG=156`) but is still slower than the combined A/B-hinted
  variants. The best explanation is a CUDA Tile codegen/scheduling interaction
  around the combined A/B staging path under the fixed `M32 x N256 x TK16`
  high-shared-memory CTA.

Decision:

- Keep the production source on the simpler promoted `LoadLatency=2` path for
  QKV split-contiguous producer. The split A/B/store knobs remain bench-only
  diagnostics because no split variant is a proven source-level win over the
  already-promoted all-site `latency=2`.
- The next QKV work should target the remaining structural limit: about `96KB`
  shared memory per CTA and high CTA residency pressure, or fuse the consumer
  while preserving coalesced split-contiguous stores.

### 2026-06-04 - QKV split-contig N-width shared-footprint diagnostic

Goal:

- Test whether the remaining QKV split-contiguous gap is primarily caused by
  the `96KB` shared-memory footprint per CTA.
- Keep the source-relevant BF16 B(K,N) layout, split-contiguous Q/K/V stores,
  `M32 x TK16`, and all-site `latency=2`; vary only output tile width `TN`.
- Generalized the bench-only split-contiguous store mapping from the hard-coded
  `TN=256` case to `component_tiles=(N/3)/TN`, so Q/K/V output placement remains
  correct for `TN=32/64/128/256`.

Official CUDA Tile context:

- The CUDA Tile documentation describes tile kernels as block/tile-level code:
  programmers express tile loads, operations, and stores, while the compiler
  maps the tile operations to block threads and hardware features such as Tensor
  Cores. Therefore the relevant evidence here is generated resources/SASS plus
  timing, not source shape alone.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x32x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x64x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x128x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
  --shape infer_qkv --variant qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
TN32:
  grid=(2439,48), best=3.369 ms, median=3.373 ms, 18.22 TF/s

TN64:
  grid=(2439,24), best=2.098 ms, median=2.107 ms, 29.26 TF/s

TN128:
  grid=(2439,12), best=1.714 ms, median=1.721 ms, 35.82 TF/s

TN256:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.275 ms, median=1.305 ms, 48.12 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
TN32:  REG= 69 STACK=0 SHARED=34816
TN64:  REG= 90 STACK=0 SHARED=53248
TN128: REG= 96 STACK=0 SHARED=90112
TN256: REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count per CTA:

```text
TN32:
  total=576 HMMA=32 LDSM=32 LDGSTS=32 STS=36 STG=3
  F2F=4 BAR=97 IMAD=47 IADD3=91 PRMT=0 SHF=71 LOP3=91

TN64:
  total=672 HMMA=64 LDSM=48 LDGSTS=32 STS=36 STG=6
  F2F=8 BAR=97 IMAD=46 IADD3=94 PRMT=17 SHF=71 LOP3=83

TN128:
  total=856 HMMA=128 LDSM=64 LDGSTS=48 STS=52 STG=12
  F2F=16 BAR=97 IMAD=100 IADD3=94 PRMT=0 SHF=73 LOP3=90

TN256:
  total=1144 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79
```

Interpretation:

- This disproves the narrow hypothesis that "QKV is slow mainly because
  `SHARED=98304`, so making the tile smaller should win." `TN32` drops shared
  memory to `34816` bytes and registers to `69`, yet falls to `18.22 TF/s`.
- The bottom-level cause is work-density loss and fixed tile-schedule overhead.
  `BAR=97` is essentially constant per CTA across all `TN`, while `HMMA` per CTA
  scales down with `TN`. Because grid.y grows from `6` at `TN256` to `48` at
  `TN32`, the same total Tensor Core work is split across 8x more CTAs and pays
  the per-CTA barrier/control overhead 8x more often.
- Lower shared memory and lower register count can improve residency, but for
  this CUDA Tile QKV shape the extra residency does not compensate for the loss
  of per-CTA Tensor Core density and the amplified fixed schedule cost.

Decision:

- Do not promote `TN32`, `TN64`, or `TN128` split-contiguous QKV producer
  variants. Keep `M32 x N256 x TK16 + latency=2`.
- The next QKV attempt should preserve `TN256` work density. If reducing shared
  memory remains the target, it has to do so without splitting the N tile into
  smaller CTAs. More promising directions are changing the internal staging
  schedule or fusing the consumer while keeping coalesced split-contiguous
  stores.

### 2026-06-05 - QKV split-contig A2/B1/store-off latency diagnostic

Goal:

- Close the remaining small gap in the QKV split-contiguous latency split matrix.
- Prior diagnostics showed:
  - `A=2,B=2,S=2` and `A=2,B=2,S=0` are the fastest family.
  - `A=2,B=1,S=2` is close but slightly behind.
  - store-only latency hints are neutral.
- Add the missing bench-only `A=2,B=1,S=0` variant to test whether `B=1` plus
  removing store hints is a cleaner generated schedule.

Official CUDA Tile doc check:

- CUDA Tile C++ call-site hints attach to specific tile load/store operations,
  and the compiler maps the tile-level program to machine code.
- This makes the generated SASS/resource evidence the deciding signal; the
  numeric semantics are unchanged.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8

for v in \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b1_s2 \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b2_s0 \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_a2_b1_s0; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
    --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 30 --iters 500
done
```

Timing:

```text
A=0, B=0, S=0:
  best=1.410 ms, median=1.414 ms, 43.54 TF/s, roof70=62.2%

A=2, B=2, S=2:
  best=1.280 ms, median=1.308 ms, 47.97 TF/s, roof70=68.5%

A=2, B=1, S=2:
  best=1.299 ms, median=1.319 ms, 47.25 TF/s, roof70=67.5%

A=2, B=2, S=0:
  best=1.261 ms, median=1.310 ms, 48.69 TF/s, roof70=69.6%

A=2, B=1, S=0:
  best=1.276 ms, median=1.316 ms, 48.09 TF/s, roof70=68.7%
```

Resource usage:

```text
A=0, B=0, S=0: REG=174 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=2, S=2: REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=1, S=2: REG=164 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=2, S=0: REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A=2, B=1, S=0: REG=164 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
A=0, B=0, S=0:
  instr=2176 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=2, S=2:
  instr=2288 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=1, S=2:
  instr=2208 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=82 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=2, S=0:
  instr=2288 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

A=2, B=1, S=0:
  instr=2208 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=82 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79
```

Interpretation:

- The missing `A=2,B=1,S=0` variant is not a new winner. It reaches
  `48.09 TF/s`, below the best tested `A=2,B=2,S=0` at `48.69 TF/s`.
- Store hints remain a no-op for this family: `A=2,B=1,S=0` and
  `A=2,B=1,S=2` have identical resource and SASS counts, with only timing noise
  separating them.
- Register count alone is again insufficient. The `B=1` family uses one fewer
  register and fewer static barrier-related instructions than `B=2`, yet it is
  slower. The better `B=2` result is a generated-schedule interaction, not a
  simple lower-resource path.

Decision:

- Do not promote `A=2,B=1,S=0`.
- Keep the source default on the already-promoted all-site `latency=2` path for
  simplicity; `A=2,B=2,S=0` remains a bench-only diagnostic candidate, not a
  proven source-level replacement.
- Future QKV work should preserve the `TN256` work density and focus on
  structural staging/shared-memory changes or consumer fusion, not more small
  store-hint combinations.

### 2026-06-05 - QKV split-contig three-component launch probe

Goal:

- Test whether the source-relevant split-contiguous QKV producer is slowed
  mainly by the device-side `tile_n` component branch that selects Q/K/V output.
- Keep `TM32 x TN256 x TK16`, split-contiguous output, BF16 Tensor Core MMA, and
  the same `TN256` per-CTA tile shape.
- Replace one kernel over `grid.y=6` with three component-specialized launches,
  each over `grid.y=2`, so Q/K/V stores are direct and branch-free.

Official CUDA Tile doc check:

- CUDA Tile C++ maps tile-level source to machine code; tile source structure
  and launch structure are both part of the performance surface.
- This experiment intentionally trades one branchy larger launch for three
  simpler launches and must be judged by end-to-end kernel timing plus SASS.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8

for v in \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_components \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_components_lat2; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
    --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 30 --iters 500
done
```

Timing:

```text
single split-contig:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.409 ms, median=1.413 ms, 43.58 TF/s, roof70=62.3%

single split-contig latency=2:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.248 ms, median=1.307 ms, 49.19 TF/s, roof70=70.3%

three-component split-contig:
  component_grid=(2439,2), launches=3, best=1.437 ms, median=1.487 ms,
  42.72 TF/s, roof70=61.0%

three-component split-contig latency=2:
  component_grid=(2439,2), launches=3, best=1.356 ms, median=1.373 ms,
  45.27 TF/s, roof70=64.7%
```

Resource usage:

```text
single split-contig:                 REG=174 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
single split-contig latency=2:       REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
component split-contig:              REG=178 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
component split-contig latency=2:    REG=164 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count per CTA:

```text
single split-contig:
  instr=2176 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

component split-contig:
  instr=1872 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=8
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=69 IADD3=76 PRMT=2 SHF=61 LOP3=77

single split-contig latency=2:
  instr=2288 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

component split-contig latency=2:
  instr=1984 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=8
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=69 IADD3=76 PRMT=2 SHF=61 LOP3=77
```

Interpretation:

- Component-specialized kernels improve the per-CTA generated code:
  `STG=24 -> 8`, `IMAD=143 -> 69`, and total instruction count drops while
  keeping `HMMA=256`.
- The overall path still gets slower. The structural cost is outside a single
  CTA: three launches replace one launch, each launch covers only two `TN256`
  component tiles, and the full A matrix is read three times instead of once.
- Shared memory remains `98304` per CTA, so the experiment does not solve the
  high-shared-memory residency limit. It only removes store-side branch/control
  work, which is not the dominant end-to-end limiter.

Decision:

- Do not promote three-component QKV launches.
- Keep the single split-contiguous producer with `latency=2` as the source
  default.
- Future QKV work must reduce the internal staging/shared-memory footprint or
  fuse producer/consumer without rereading A three times. Merely specializing
  Q/K/V stores into separate launches loses too much launch and input-read
  efficiency.

### 2026-06-05 - QKV split-contig grid.z component probe

Goal:

- Follow up the negative three-component launch probe with a one-launch
  alternative.
- Use `grid.z=3` for Q/K/V component and `grid.y=2` for each component's
  `TN256` tiles. This preserves one kernel launch and the same total CTA count
  as the single split-contiguous producer, while making component identity a
  block index instead of a `tile_n` range.
- Test whether this removes enough store/control overhead without rereading A
  through three separate launches.

Implementation:

- Added `qkv_bkn_static_tiled_split_contig_zcomponent_kernel`.
- Added bench-only variants:
  - `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_zcomponents`
  - `qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_zcomponents_lat2`

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm -j8

for v in \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_lat2 \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_zcomponents \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_zcomponents_lat2 \
  qkv_t32x256x16bkn_split_contig_components_lat2; do
  ./build-min/bench_bf16_cutile_mma_gemm \
    --shape infer_qkv --variant "$v" --warmup 30 --iters 500
done
```

Timing:

```text
single split-contig:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.400 ms, median=1.412 ms,
  43.83 TF/s, roof70=62.6%

single split-contig latency=2:
  grid=(2439,6), best=1.298 ms, median=1.307 ms,
  47.28 TF/s, roof70=67.5%

grid.z component split-contig:
  grid=(2439,2,3), best=1.413 ms, median=1.436 ms,
  43.43 TF/s, roof70=62.0%

grid.z component split-contig latency=2:
  grid=(2439,2,3), best=1.299 ms, median=1.367 ms,
  47.26 TF/s, roof70=67.5%

three-launch component split-contig latency=2:
  component_grid=(2439,2), launches=3, best=1.359 ms, median=1.374 ms,
  45.16 TF/s, roof70=64.5%
```

Resource usage:

```text
single split-contig:                 REG=174 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
single split-contig latency=2:       REG=165 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
grid.z component:                    REG=174 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
grid.z component latency=2:          REG=163 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
three-launch component latency=2:    REG=164 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count per CTA:

```text
single:
  instr=2176 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

grid.z component:
  instr=2192 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=79 IMAD=146 IADD3=80 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

single latency=2:
  instr=2288 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=143 IADD3=82 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

grid.z component latency=2:
  instr=2320 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=24
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=146 IADD3=80 PRMT=1 SHF=63 LOP3=79

three-launch component latency=2:
  instr=1984 HMMA=256 LDSM=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=88 STG=8
  F2F=32 BAR=97 IMAD=69 IADD3=76 PRMT=2 SHF=61 LOP3=77
```

Interpretation:

- `grid.z` fixes the biggest problem of the three-launch component probe: it
  avoids three separate launches and recovers from `45.16 TF/s` to `47.26 TF/s`
  with latency hints.
- It still does not beat the single split-contiguous `latency=2` path. Runtime
  component selection from `blockIdx.z` does not let the compiler eliminate the
  Q/K/V store branch the way template-specialized component kernels do:
  `STG` remains `24`, and `IMAD` is slightly higher than the single kernel.
- Shared memory remains `98304`, so the structural residency limit is unchanged.
  The small register reduction (`165 -> 163`) is not enough to offset the extra
  component-index/control work.

Decision:

- Do not promote `grid.z` component QKV.
- Keep the single split-contiguous QKV producer with all-site `latency=2` as the
  source default.
- If we want component specialization, it must be compile-time specialization
  without three separate launches and without rereading A. CUDA Tile as used here
  does not get that from a runtime `grid.z` component branch.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 store-latency split diagnostic

Goal:

- Explain whether the positive FFN12 pair-H32/TK64 `latency=2` result comes
  from W1/W2 load staging, final output stores, or a combination.
- Keep the same one-pass W1/GELU/W2 dataflow, `M32`, `THidden=32`, `TK64`,
  `HMMA=256`, and output shape. This is bench-only and does not change source
  inference.

Bench implementation:

- Extended `ffn12_fused_split2_pairh32_bf16_body` with independent
  `StoreLatencyHint` / `StoreMemoryLatency` template parameters.
- Added variants:
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_storelat2`
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_storelat2`

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_storelat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_storelat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.855 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.12 TF/s

W1/W2 load latency=2:
  best=1.809 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.24 TF/s

store latency=2 only:
  best=1.853 ms, median=1.858 ms, useful=44.16 TF/s

W1/W2 load latency=2 + store latency=2:
  best=1.811 ms, median=1.816 ms, useful=45.20 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline:                    REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
store latency=2 only:         REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1/W2 load latency=2:         REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
W1/W2 load + store latency=2: REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  total=2760 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=86 IMAD=352 IADD3=177 PRMT=115 SHF=188 LOP3=175 R2UR=0

store latency=2 only:
  total=2760 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=86 IMAD=352 IADD3=177 PRMT=115 SHF=188 LOP3=175 R2UR=0

W1/W2 load latency=2:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18

W1/W2 load latency=2 + store latency=2:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18
```

Interpretation:

- Store latency hints are a no-op for this generated FFN12 shape. Store-only
  compiles to the same resource usage and same static SASS counts as baseline.
- Adding store hints on top of W1/W2 load hints also compiles to the same SASS
  as W1/W2 load hints alone. The timing difference is measurement noise.
- The FFN12 `latency=2` win is therefore specifically a W1/W2 load-staging
  codegen effect: it preserves `HMMA=256` while lowering total static
  instruction count and integer/permutation work. It is not caused by output
  store scheduling.

Decision:

- Keep production behavior unchanged for now. This bench proves the output store
  hint is not a performance lever, but removing it from source would be a code
  cleanup rather than a new speed improvement.
- Future FFN12 work should continue targeting W1/W2 staging and long-lived
  accumulator/resource pressure while preserving the one-pass `HMMA=256`
  schedule.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 A-load vs W1-weight latency split diagnostic

Goal:

- Split the W1-side `latency=2` effect further: determine whether the FFN12
  positive result comes from the input activation tile load, the W1 weight tile
  loads, or their combination.
- Keep the same one-pass W1/GELU/W2 dataflow and `HMMA=256` target. This is
  bench-only; source inference remains unchanged.

Bench implementation:

- Added independent `ALoadLatencyHint` and `W1WeightLatencyHint` template
  parameters to `ffn12_fused_split2_pairh32_bf16_body`.
- Existing variants keep default behavior because both parameters default to
  `W1LatencyHint`.
- Added variants:
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_alat2_w2lat2`
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1weightlat2_w2lat2`
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat2_w2lat0`

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_alat2_w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1weightlat2_w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1lat2_w2lat0 --warmup 20 --iters 200
```

Timing:

```text
baseline:
  best=1.854 ms, median=1.857 ms, useful=44.13 TF/s

A + W1 weight + W2 latency=2:
  best=1.810 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.21 TF/s

A-only + W2 latency=2:
  best=1.961 ms, median=2.008 ms, useful=41.74 TF/s

W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2:
  best=1.805 ms, median=1.821 ms, useful=45.34 TF/s

W1-side latency=2, W2 no hint:
  best=3.320 ms, median=3.323 ms, useful=24.65 TF/s
```

Longer 500-iter check:

```text
A + W1 weight + W2 latency=2:
  best=1.810 ms, median=1.817 ms, useful=45.21 TF/s

W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2:
  best=1.804 ms, median=1.818 ms, useful=45.37 TF/s
```

Resource usage:

```text
baseline:                         REG=242 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
A + W1 weight + W2 latency=2:      REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304
A-only + W2 latency=2:             REG=254 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2:     REG=252 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
W1-side latency=2, W2 no hint:     REG=253 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Static SASS count:

```text
baseline:
  total=2760 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=86 IMAD=352 IADD3=177 PRMT=115 SHF=188 LOP3=175 R2UR=0

A + W1 weight + W2 latency=2:
  total=2696 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=96 IMAD=275 IADD3=161 PRMT=80 SHF=209 LOP3=145 R2UR=18

A-only + W2 latency=2:
  total=2720 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=93 IMAD=317 IADD3=190 PRMT=116 SHF=180 LOP3=179 R2UR=0

W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2:
  total=2712 HMMA=256 LDSM=128 LDG=96 LDGSTS=80 STS=124 STG=8
  F2F=96 FFMA=256 BAR=100 IMAD=314 IADD3=174 PRMT=118 SHF=183 LOP3=173 R2UR=0

W1-side latency=2, W2 no hint:
  total=1896 HMMA=128 LDSM=64 LDG=80 LDGSTS=40 STS=74 STG=8
  F2F=80 FFMA=128 BAR=54 IMAD=342 IADD3=112 PRMT=48 SHF=144 LOP3=118 R2UR=0
```

Interpretation:

- W2 latency hint is a necessary guardrail. Removing it while keeping W1-side
  latency hints collapses the generated Tensor Core schedule from `HMMA=256` to
  `HMMA=128` and throughput falls to `24.65 TF/s`.
- A-load-only hint is negative even with W2 hinted. It keeps `HMMA=256`, but the
  generated schedule has worse integer/control mix and slower timing.
- W1-weight-only + W2 hint is the best tested split in this microbench:
  `1.804 ms` best in the 500-iter check versus `1.810 ms` for the existing
  all-W1-side + W2 hint. It also avoids the `STACK=8` frame and uses fewer
  registers (`252` vs `255`).
- The positive effect is therefore primarily W1 weight load staging plus W2
  load staging. Hinting the repeated A activation load is not needed and may
  slightly hurt codegen for this A10G shape.

Decision:

- Keep this as a bench-only positive candidate for now. The measured gain over
  the current production-style W1/W2 hint is small, so promotion needs a source
  A/B with Nsight isolation and end-to-end timing before changing defaults.
- The next FFN12 source experiment should add an opt-in W1-weight-only + W2
  latency path, not more broad latency values.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 W1-weight-only latency source opt-in

Goal:

- Validate whether the bench-positive `W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2` split
  also improves the real source inference kernel.
- Keep the existing production default unchanged unless source-level evidence
  shows a real win.

Source implementation:

- Added opt-in environment switch:
  `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_WEIGHT_LAT2=1`.
- The switch is gated by the existing `CUDASEP_DISABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_LAT2`
  path, so disabling pair-H32 latency also disables this candidate.
- The opt-in source instantiation uses:
  `MemoryLatency=2`, `ALoadLatencyHint=false`,
  `W1WeightLatencyHint=true`, `StoreLatencyHint=false`.
- Default source behavior remains the existing all-W1-side + W2 latency path.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min cudasep_infer -j8

./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/ffn12_weightlat_candidate_default_rerun \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_WEIGHT_LAT2=1 \
./build-min/cudasep_infer \
  --model mel-band-roformer-deux/becruily_deux.csm \
  --input test_clean.wav \
  --output outputs/ffn12_weightlat_candidate_opt_rerun \
  --bf16 --chunk-batch-size 1 --stem -1

nsys stats --report cuda_gpu_kern_sum \
  bench/nsys_ffn12_weightlat_default_clean.sqlite
nsys stats --report cuda_gpu_kern_sum \
  bench/nsys_ffn12_weightlat_opt_clean.sqlite

cuobjdump --dump-resource-usage build-min/cudasep_infer | \
  rg -n -A6 -B2 "ffn12_fused256_split2_pairh32_cutile_kernel"
```

Current `--bf16` end-to-end timing:

```text
default:
  infer=1753.6 ms, RTF=22.576043

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_WEIGHT_LAT2=1:
  infer=1839.0 ms, RTF=21.528516
```

Existing Nsight source A/B:

```text
default FFN12 main kernel:
  total=177.406616 ms, calls=96, avg=1.847986 ms

CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_WEIGHT_LAT2=1 FFN12 main kernel:
  total=178.272460 ms, calls=96, avg=1.857005 ms
```

Source resource usage for the relevant GeluMode 6, BF16-input, TK64,
non-residual pair-H32 instances:

```text
default latency=2, round-output path:
  REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304

W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2, round-output path:
  REG=252 STACK=0 SHARED=98304

default latency=2, no-round-output path:
  REG=255 STACK=8 SHARED=98304

W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2, no-round-output path:
  REG=252 STACK=0 SHARED=98304
```

Earlier source SASS count for the no-round-output instances:

```text
default latency=2:
  total=2696 IMAD=276 PRMT=75 LOP3=145

W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2:
  total=2712 IMAD=315 PRMT=116 LOP3=173
```

Interpretation:

- The source opt-in keeps the same shared-memory footprint and does not indicate
  a Tensor Core fallback. The microbench-positive resource change (`STACK=8` to
  `STACK=0`, fewer registers) is real.
- That lower resource usage does not translate to the real inference kernel.
  The Nsight-isolated FFN12 group is slower, and the current `--bf16`
  end-to-end rerun is also slower.
- The likely bottom cause is CUDA Tile codegen/scheduling for this full source
  instantiation: the W1-weight-only spelling reduces stack pressure but emits a
  worse integer/permutation/control mix than the default all-W1-side + W2
  latency form.

Decision:

- Do not promote `CUDASEP_ENABLE_FFN12_PAIRH32_WEIGHT_LAT2=1` to the default.
- Keep the opt-in branch as a source diagnostic knob for now because it proves
  that lower register/stack usage alone is not the right success metric.
- Continue FFN12 optimization by changing the dataflow/staging shape while
  preserving `HMMA=256`, rather than only changing which loads carry
  `latency=2` hints.

### 2026-06-04 - FFN12 source-matching latency plus output no-round diagnostic

Goal:

- Align the FFN12 microbench with the current source default shape more closely:
  `TK64`, pair-H32, `W1/W2 latency=2`, and output accumulator no-round.
- Recheck whether the source opt-in `W1-weight-only + W2 latency=2` remains a
  bench-positive candidate once output no-round is also enabled.
- Recheck whether adding store hints to the source-matching shape changes
  generated code or runtime.

Bench implementation:

- Added candidates-only variants:
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_outnoround`
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_outnoround_storelat2`
  - `fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1weightlat2_w2lat2_outnoround`
- These are bench-only diagnostics and do not change source inference defaults.

Commands:

```bash
ninja -C build-min bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile -j8

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_outnoround --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_outnoround --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1weightlat2_w2lat2_outnoround --warmup 20 --iters 200
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_outnoround_storelat2 --warmup 20 --iters 200

./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_outnoround --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1w2lat2_outnoround_storelat2 --warmup 30 --iters 500
./build-min/bench_bf16_ffn12_candidates_cutile \
  --variant fused_h32_poly9_split2_pairh32_tk64_w1weightlat2_w2lat2_outnoround --warmup 30 --iters 500
```
